profile.domain: Telecom MVNO SIM Lifecycle Management API

project_goal.value: Expose carrier-grade REST API for real-time SIM card lifecycle management enabling MVNO operators to activate, suspend, port, and assign data plans to SIM cards
project_goal.rationale: Core MVNO enablement need for programmatic SIM operations

target_users.value: MVNO operators consuming via downstream billing, CRM, and provisioning microservices
target_users.rationale: B2B API consumers operating carrier-grade systems

project_class.rationale: Pure API service with no frontend; downstream consumers own their UIs

capabilities.rationale: HLR provisioning and webhook callbacks require realtime; partner auth required

complexity_level.rationale: HLR integration, number portability regulatory flows, and quota webhooks add significant complexity

risk_level.rationale: Subscriber PII, carrier interconnect agreements, and CPNI data require high risk classification

data_sensitivity.rationale: Subscriber records and carrier topology data are confidential under interconnect agreements

external_exposure.rationale: API consumed exclusively by contracted MVNO operator systems

access_model.value: mTLS with operator-scoped JWT for all endpoints; per-operator API key rotation every 90 days
access_model.rationale: Partner-facing carrier API requires mutual authentication and operator isolation

feature_scope.value: SIM activation with ICCID validation and MSISDN assignment, suspension, reactivation, number portability via NPC, data plan assignment, quota webhook callbacks, operator audit log
feature_scope.rationale: Full SIM lifecycle operations required for MVNO platform

mvp_scope.value: SIM activation, suspension, and data plan assignment with HLR provisioning and operator-scoped auth
mvp_scope.rationale: Core lifecycle operations deliver immediate MVNO value

security_baseline.value: mTLS for all partner connections, AES-256 at rest, TLS 1.3 in transit, IP allowlist per operator, rate limiting 1000 req/min per operator
security_baseline.rationale: Carrier-grade security baseline for CPNI-regulated API

frontend_stack.value: none
frontend_stack.rationale: API service has no UI; operators use their own portals

backend_stack.value: Go 1.22 with Gin
backend_stack.rationale: High throughput, low latency requirements match Go runtime characteristics

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for transactional records, Redis 7 for idempotency cache and session state
data_platform.rationale: ACID guarantees for SIM state transitions; Redis for idempotency key deduplication

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate in us-east-1 with multi-AZ deployment
hosting_target.rationale: Managed container platform meeting carrier HA requirements

privacy_retention_policy.value: Subscriber operational records retained 7 years per CPNI regulations; audit logs retained 5 years
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: FCC CPNI and carrier interconnect agreement retention obligations

future_scope.value: eSIM profile management, real-time charging integration, and bulk SIM provisioning batch API
future_scope.rationale: Year 2 MVNO platform expansion

constraints.value: 99.95% uptime SLA, p99 latency under 200ms for activation, HLR provisioning must complete within 30 seconds
constraints.rationale: Carrier-grade SLA commitments to MVNO operators

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM, structured JSON logging with correlation IDs, PagerDuty for SLA breach alerts
observability_baseline.rationale: Carrier operations require real-time visibility into provisioning pipeline health

execution_preference.value: Kanban with 2-week planning cycles, SLA-gated releases
execution_preference.rationale: Carrier API releases require careful SLA impact assessment

compliance_context.value: CPNI regulations, CTIA guidelines, and bilateral carrier interconnect agreements; FCC Part 64 data handling requirements
compliance_context.rationale: CPNI compliance mandatory for subscriber data handling; interconnect agreements bind data sharing

project.goal: Carrier-grade SIM lifecycle REST API for MVNO operators
project.target_users: MVNO operator systems via downstream microservices
project.timeline: Q2 production deployment

backend.api: RESTful with OpenAPI 3.1 and versioned routes under /v1

security.transport: TLS 1.3 mandatory, IP allowlist per operator
security.secrets: AWS Secrets Manager with 90-day key rotation

data.db: PostgreSQL 16 with row-level security per operator
data.cache: Redis 7 for idempotency keys and rate limit counters

devops.ci: GitHub Actions with carrier SLA gate

reasoner_reviews.product: The MVNO SIM lifecycle scope is well-defined with activation, suspension, porting, and data plan assignment covering the critical operator use cases. Webhook callback architecture for quota events is essential and must be treated as a first-class feature, not an afterthought. The number portability flow has regulatory dependencies on NPC response times that must be reflected in the API async design.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Go 1.22 with Gin is appropriate for the sub-200ms p99 requirement at carrier scale. The HLR integration is the critical dependency; I recommend an abstraction layer behind a HLRClient interface to support multiple HLR vendors and enable mock injection in testing. The NPC portability flow must be async with polling or webhook notification since regulatory validation can take hours. Previous plan lacked the async porting state machine design.
reasoner_reviews.security: mTLS for all partner connections is correctly specified but the previous plan did not define certificate lifecycle management — operator certificate rotation, revocation via OCSP, and the CA trust hierarchy must be documented. CPNI requires audit logging of every subscriber data access by operator ID, not just state change events. The ISS-001 gap in operator-scoped data isolation at the database layer must be resolved.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 99.95% SLA with 200ms p99 activation is achievable on ECS Fargate but requires careful capacity planning. HLR provisioning latency variance is outside our control; the API must distinguish between gateway timeout and HLR processing latency in error responses to avoid misleading operators.

backend.service_design: Go microservice with domain packages: sim_lifecycle, porting, data_plans, webhooks, hlr_client, audit. Clean interface for HLRClient enabling vendor swap and test mocking.
backend.api_patterns: RESTful /v1 with idempotency-key header on all mutation endpoints; async porting endpoints return 202 with Location header for status polling
backend.background_jobs: Webhook delivery worker with exponential backoff retry up to 72 hours; NPC status polling job for in-flight port requests
backend.failure_handling: HLR circuit breaker with 10-second timeout; idempotent SIM state machine rejects duplicate activation ICCIDs via Redis dedup

security.auth_design: mTLS at ALB termination; JWT claims carry operator_id and allowed_operations scope; Go middleware validates both
security.authorization_model: Operator-scoped row-level security in PostgreSQL; each query filtered by operator_id from JWT claims
security.secrets_management: AWS Secrets Manager for HLR credentials and NPC API keys; 90-day rotation with automated reload
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Append-only subscriber_access_log table capturing operator_id, subscriber_id, action, timestamp for CPNI compliance; forwarded to Datadog for SIEM ingestion

data.entities: sim_cards (iccid PK, msisdn, operator_id, status ENUM active/suspended/ported/cancelled, data_plan_id, hlr_provisioned_at), subscribers (id, iccid FK, operator_subscriber_id, created_at), port_requests (id, iccid FK, donor_msisdn, npc_reference, status ENUM pending/validated/completed/rejected, initiated_at, completed_at), data_plans (id, operator_id, name, quota_mb, rate_plan_id, validity_days), activation_events (id, iccid FK, operator_id, event_type, payload_json, created_at)
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 with RLS policies per operator_id; partitioned activation_events by month; Redis for idempotency key TTL 24h
data.retention_and_deletion: 7-year retention for subscriber records; activation_events partitions dropped after 7 years; automated S3 Glacier archival at year 5

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate cluster in us-east-1a/1c/1d; ALB with mTLS termination; Auto Scaling 4-20 tasks on CPU 60% target
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: Go test with race detector, golangci-lint, Docker build, ECR push, ECS rolling deploy with 2-task minimum healthy; SLA gate checks p99 latency in staging before promoting to production
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM with Go tracer; custom metrics: sim_activation_latency_ms, hlr_timeout_count, port_request_age_hours, webhook_delivery_failures; PagerDuty P1 alert on HLR circuit breaker open
devops.rollback_strategy: ECS service rollback to previous task definition on health check failure within 5 minutes

ISS-001.title: Operator data isolation missing at database layer
ISS-001.detail: Previous plan described RLS conceptually but no PostgreSQL policy definitions or query-level enforcement were included in the data model or API design sections.

history.note: Initial identification: no RLS policy syntax or enforcement evidence in plan

ISS-002.title: NPC portability flow lacks async state machine design
ISS-002.detail: The number portability workflow described synchronous completion but NPC regulatory validation takes hours; the API contract must define async response codes and polling endpoints.

history.note: Missing 202 Accepted pattern and port_request status polling endpoint

focus_issues.title: Operator data isolation missing at database layer
focus_issues.detail: PostgreSQL RLS policies and query-level operator_id enforcement absent from previous plan.

focus_issues.title: NPC portability flow lacks async state machine design
focus_issues.detail: Portability API must use async 202 pattern with polling; previous plan described synchronous flow.

latest_recommendations: Add explicit PostgreSQL RLS policy definitions scoped to operator_id in the data model
Design porting endpoint as async with 202 response and GET /v1/port-requests/:id polling
Include operator certificate lifecycle and OCSP revocation in security section

previous_plan.title: MVNO SIM Lifecycle Management API v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: Go-based carrier REST API for MVNO SIM lifecycle operations with HLR integration and operator-scoped auth.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Go 1.22 Gin service on ECS Fargate with PostgreSQL 16 and Redis 7; mTLS + JWT auth; HLR integration for provisioning.
previous_plan.data_model: sim_cards, subscribers, port_requests, data_plans tables with operator_id foreign keys but no RLS policies defined.

target_output.thinking_summary: Resolving ISS-001 by adding explicit PostgreSQL RLS policy definitions ensuring operator_id-scoped query isolation at the database layer. Resolving ISS-002 by redesigning the number portability endpoint as async with 202 Accepted and a dedicated status polling resource, reflecting NPC regulatory processing latency. mTLS certificate lifecycle and CPNI audit logging get explicit treatment given the high risk level and CPNI compliance context.

fix_report.action_taken: Added explicit PostgreSQL RLS policy definitions to data_model section: CREATE POLICY operator_isolation ON sim_cards USING (operator_id = current_setting('app.operator_id')::uuid). All query paths now set app.operator_id from JWT claims before execution. api_design and security_and_compliance sections updated to describe enforcement mechanism.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Auditor confirms database-layer operator isolation is demonstrably enforced with RLS policy syntax present

fix_report.action_taken: Redesigned portability endpoint: POST /v1/port-requests returns 202 Accepted with Location: /v1/port-requests/:id header. Added GET /v1/port-requests/:id returning status ENUM pending/validated/completed/rejected and NPC reference. NPC polling background job described. Workflow section updated with async portability state machine.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Portability flow correctly reflects NPC regulatory async processing; no more synchronous completion assumption

target_output.title: MVNO SIM Lifecycle Management API — Carrier Grade
target_output.executive_summary: A carrier-grade Go REST API enabling MVNO operators to manage full SIM card lifecycles including activation with HLR provisioning, suspension, async number portability via NPC, and data plan assignment. Deployed on AWS ECS Fargate with mTLS partner authentication, operator-scoped PostgreSQL RLS, Redis idempotency, and CPNI-compliant audit logging meeting FCC Part 64 requirements.
target_output.architecture_overview: Go 1.22 Gin service on ECS Fargate (us-east-1, multi-AZ) sitting behind an ALB that terminates mTLS from MVNO operator systems. Service decomposes into sim_lifecycle, porting, data_plans, webhooks, hlr_client, and audit domain packages. HLR provisioning calls are wrapped by an HLRClient interface supporting circuit breaking and timeout handling. PostgreSQL 16 enforces operator data isolation via row-level security policies binding queries to operator_id from JWT claims. Redis 7 provides idempotency key deduplication and rate limit counters. Webhook worker delivers quota exhaustion callbacks to operator endpoints with exponential backoff retry.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Go 1.22, Gin, pgx/v5, go-redis/v9, otelgo for distributed tracing. Database: PostgreSQL 16 with RLS policies, partitioned activation_events table. Cache: Redis 7 for idempotency TTL and rate counters. Infra: ECS Fargate, ALB with mTLS termination, AWS Secrets Manager, CloudWatch, Datadog APM. CI/CD: GitHub Actions with golangci-lint, Go race detector, ECR, ECS rolling deploy.
target_output.functional_feature_map: SIM Activation: ICCID validation, HLR provisioning, MSISDN assignment, idempotency enforcement. Suspension/Reactivation: atomic SIM state transitions with HLR sync. Number Portability: async NPC submission, regulatory validation polling, atomic subscriber migration on approval. Data Plan Assignment: quota configuration and rate plan binding with HLR update. Quota Webhook Delivery: operator endpoint callbacks with retry. Operator Audit Log: CPNI-compliant subscriber access logging.
target_output.system_components: ALB with mTLS listener (operator certificate trust store); ECS Fargate cluster (4-20 tasks, CPU 60% target autoscale); Go API service with Gin router and operator_id middleware; HLRClient module with circuit breaker (10s timeout, 5-failure threshold); PostgreSQL 16 with RLS policies and monthly activation_events partitions; Redis 7 for idempotency keys (24h TTL) and rate limit counters; SQS-backed webhook delivery worker with exponential backoff; Secrets Manager for HLR credentials and NPC API keys.
target_output.workflows: SIM Activation: MVNO POST /v1/sims/activate with ICCID and operator JWT; idempotency key checked in Redis; ICCID validated against sim_cards table; HLRClient provisions SIM on HLR within 30s timeout; MSISDN assigned from operator pool; sim_cards row inserted with RLS operator_id binding; activation_event logged; 201 response with assigned MSISDN. Number Portability (Async): MVNO POST /v1/port-requests with ICCID and donor MSISDN; port_requests row created with status=pending; NPC API called to register portability request; 202 Accepted returned with Location: /v1/port-requests/:id; NPC polling job tracks status transitions pending->validated->completed; on completion subscriber record atomically migrated and HLR updated; webhook notifies operator. Quota Exhaustion Webhook: Policy enforcement node publishes quota event; webhook worker reads from SQS; operator endpoint called with event payload; exponential backoff on failure up to 72h.
target_output.data_model: sim_cards (iccid VARCHAR PK, msisdn VARCHAR UNIQUE, operator_id UUID NOT NULL, status ENUM active/suspended/ported/cancelled, data_plan_id UUID FK, hlr_provisioned_at TIMESTAMPTZ, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). PostgreSQL RLS: CREATE POLICY operator_isolation ON sim_cards USING (operator_id = current_setting('app.operator_id')::uuid); policies identical on port_requests and activation_events. subscribers (id UUID PK, iccid VARCHAR FK, operator_subscriber_id VARCHAR, created_at). port_requests (id UUID PK, iccid VARCHAR FK, donor_msisdn VARCHAR, npc_reference VARCHAR, status ENUM pending/validated/completed/rejected, initiated_at, completed_at). data_plans (id UUID PK, operator_id UUID, name VARCHAR, quota_mb INT, rate_plan_id VARCHAR, validity_days INT). activation_events (id UUID PK, iccid VARCHAR FK, operator_id UUID, event_type VARCHAR, payload_json JSONB, created_at) PARTITION BY RANGE (created_at). rate_plans (id UUID PK, operator_id UUID, plan_code VARCHAR, per_mb_rate NUMERIC).
target_output.api_design: RESTful /v1 with Idempotency-Key header on all mutations. POST /v1/sims/activate {iccid, operator_subscriber_id, data_plan_id} -> 201 {msisdn, iccid, status}. POST /v1/sims/:iccid/suspend -> 200 {status}. POST /v1/sims/:iccid/reactivate -> 200 {status}. POST /v1/port-requests {iccid, donor_msisdn, target_msisdn} -> 202 {id, status, location}. GET /v1/port-requests/:id -> 200 {id, status ENUM, npc_reference, completed_at}. PUT /v1/sims/:iccid/data-plan {data_plan_id} -> 200 {quota_mb, validity_days}. GET /v1/audit-log?operator_id=&from=&to= -> 200 paginated CPNI audit entries. All endpoints enforce operator_id from JWT claims against RLS context variable.
target_output.security_and_compliance: mTLS: ALB listener configured with operator CA bundle; operator certificates rotated every 365 days with OCSP stapling; revocation via OCSP responder URL embedded in certs. JWT: RS256, operator_id and allowed_operations claims; 1-hour expiry; Go middleware sets app.operator_id session variable for RLS before each query. CPNI: subscriber_access_log captures operator_id, iccid, action, requesting_user, justification, timestamp; append-only with no UPDATE/DELETE privileges; forwarded to Datadog SIEM. IP allowlist: per-operator CIDR ranges enforced at ALB security group. Rate limiting: 1000 req/min per operator enforced via Redis sliding window. FCC Part 64: subscriber data access restricted to authorized operator scope; CPNI disclosure procedures documented in runbook.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: ECS Fargate us-east-1a/1c/1d; ALB with mTLS, WAF (SQLi/XSS rules), and access logs to S3. GitHub Actions CI: golangci-lint, go test -race, 85% coverage gate, docker build, ECR push, ECS rolling deploy (minimum 2 healthy tasks). Staging SLA gate: automated p99 latency check against 200ms threshold before production promotion. Secrets Manager: HLR credentials, NPC API keys, DB password — 90-day rotation with Lambda rotator. Blue/green rollback via ECS service rollback to previous task definition. Aurora Multi-AZ with automated failover for PostgreSQL. Redis ElastiCache Multi-AZ with automatic failover.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM with Go otel tracer; trace IDs propagated in all HLR and NPC calls. Custom metrics: sim_activation_duration_ms (p50/p95/p99), hlr_circuit_breaker_state, port_request_age_hours (histogram), webhook_delivery_attempt_count, operator_rate_limit_rejections. PagerDuty P1: HLR circuit breaker open >60s, activation p99 >200ms, ECS task count <2. PagerDuty P2: webhook delivery failure rate >5%, port request stuck in pending >4 hours. Structured JSON logs with correlation_id, operator_id, iccid fields for log-based alerting.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: ~$800/month baseline for 4 Fargate tasks + Aurora Multi-AZ + Redis. HLR call volume drives primary scale; ECS autoscaling to 20 tasks at 1000 concurrent activations. Redis idempotency keys capped at 1M entries before LRU eviction. Activation_events monthly partitioning limits index bloat; S3 Glacier archival reduces hot storage cost at year 5.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (10 weeks): SIM activation with HLR integration, suspension/reactivation, operator mTLS auth, RLS enforcement, CPNI audit logging, CI/CD pipeline, Datadog integration. Phase 2 (6 weeks): Async number portability with NPC integration and polling endpoints, data plan assignment with quota sync. Phase 3 (4 weeks): Quota webhook delivery worker, operator audit log export API, bulk activation batch endpoint, eSIM groundwork.
target_output.development_guidelines: Go 1.22 strict mode; golangci-lint with errcheck, staticcheck, gosec enabled in CI. HLRClient always mocked in unit tests; integration tests use containerized HLR simulator. RLS policies tested with operator_id cross-contamination test suite. All database migrations via golang-migrate with rollback scripts. ADR required for any HLR vendor change.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: HLR vendor outage during activation surge. Mitigation: circuit breaker queues requests for retry; operators receive 503 with Retry-After header. Risk: NPC regulatory changes affecting portability SLA. Mitigation: NPC adapter abstracted behind interface for swap without API contract change. Tradeoff: RLS over application-layer operator filtering chosen for defense-in-depth at cost of session variable overhead per query. Risk: CPNI audit log growth exceeding PostgreSQL performance at high volume — mitigation: forward to Datadog SIEM and archive to S3 after 90 days.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Async portability confirmed: 202 + polling pattern selected over webhook-only to support operators without public callback endpoints. RLS selected over schema-per-operator: schema-per-operator rejected due to migration complexity at 50+ MVNO operator scale. Certificate rotation period set to 365 days with OCSP; shorter rotation deemed operationally disruptive for carrier-grade partners.

metadata.notes: Carrier MVNO SIM lifecycle API; revision resolves operator DB isolation (ISS-001) and async NPC portability flow (ISS-002).

profile.domain: Network Configuration Drift Detection and Remediation

project_goal.value: Continuously audit multi-vendor router and switch configurations against versioned golden templates, detect drift, and automatically remediate via NETCONF/RESTCONF with human approval gating for production changes
project_goal.rationale: Core mission replacing fragmented manual audit scripts run ad hoc by NOC engineers

target_users.value: NOC engineers and network operations managers in hybrid campus and WAN environments
target_users.rationale: Primary operators identified in discovery workshops

project_class.rationale: Batch-driven headless tool with no browser UI; driven by schedulers and NETCONF transports

capabilities.rationale: Requires NETCONF/RESTCONF integration layer, scheduled jobs, and internal DevOps tooling hooks

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-vendor normalization, diff engine, automated remediation, and ticketing integrations are non-trivial

risk_level.rationale: Automated writes to production network devices carry outage risk; NERC CIP governs configuration changes

data_sensitivity.rationale: Vendor credentials, device topology, and running configs are sensitive operational data

external_exposure.rationale: System is air-gapped from public internet and accessed only from the NOC network

access_model.value: Keycloak RBAC with noc_engineer, network_admin, and readonly_auditor roles; remediation execution requires network_admin
access_model.rationale: Least-privilege access for destructive NETCONF write operations

feature_scope.value: Device registration and credential vault, scheduled NETCONF polling, golden template versioning in Git, drift detection diff engine, remediation workflow with approval gate, ticketing system integration, post-remediation verification, audit logging
feature_scope.rationale: Full lifecycle from drift discovery to confirmed remediation

mvp_scope.value: NETCONF polling for Cisco IOS-XE and Junos devices, template diff engine, drift report generation, manual approval via JIRA ticket, basic Keycloak auth
mvp_scope.rationale: Covers the two dominant vendors in the existing fleet for initial pilot

security_baseline.value: Vault-managed device credentials with short-lived tokens, mTLS between service pods, all NETCONF sessions over SSHv2, audit log of every config read and write operation
security_baseline.rationale: NERC CIP and internal change management policy requirements

frontend_stack.value: None — headless automation service; operational interface via CLI and JIRA
frontend_stack.rationale: No browser UI required; NOC operators interact via ticket workflow and CLI status commands

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI for internal control API; Celery workers for batch jobs
backend_stack.rationale: Team Python expertise; ncclient and netconf-client libraries available for NETCONF

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for drift reports, device registry, and job state; Redis 7 for Celery broker and job result cache
data_platform.rationale: Relational model for structured audit history; Redis for reliable task queue

hosting_target.value: Bare-metal Kubernetes cluster in on-premises NOC datacenter
hosting_target.rationale: No cloud egress allowed; all network devices are reachable only from on-prem

privacy_retention_policy.value: Running configuration snapshots retained 12 months; audit logs retained 3 years per NERC CIP requirements
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Regulatory retention mandate for critical infrastructure change records

future_scope.value: YANG model validation, Arista EOS and Nokia SR OS vendor plugins, automated rollback without human gate for low-risk drift categories
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 vendor coverage expansion and risk-tiered automation

constraints.value: Must operate entirely on-premises with no cloud dependencies; Kubernetes cluster has 12 worker nodes with 4 vCPU / 16 GB RAM each
constraints.rationale: Air-gap requirement from network security policy

observability_baseline.value: Grafana dashboards, Prometheus metrics from FastAPI and Celery workers, structured JSON logging to on-prem Loki, AlertManager for Celery queue depth and failed remediation jobs
observability_baseline.rationale: Existing on-prem observability stack used across NOC tooling

execution_preference.value: Two-week sprints; trunk-based development with feature flags for vendor plugin rollout
execution_preference.rationale: Small team workflow; feature flags allow partial vendor support to be deployed without full enablement

compliance_context.value: NERC CIP-010 configuration change management and CIP-007 system security management standards apply; all automated remediation events must be logged with before/after config snapshots
compliance_context.rationale: Critical infrastructure protection regulations govern automated configuration changes to network devices

project.goal: Detect and remediate configuration drift on multi-vendor network devices
project.target_users: NOC engineers and network operations managers
project.timeline: Q2 pilot on Cisco and Juniper fleet

backend.framework: FastAPI control API with Celery workers
backend.api: Internal REST for job management and status queries

security.transport: mTLS between pods, SSHv2 for all NETCONF sessions
security.secrets: HashiCorp Vault for device credential storage

data.db: PostgreSQL 16 for drift reports, device registry, job state
data.cache: Redis 7 as Celery broker and task result store

devops.environments: dev namespace, staging namespace, production namespace with separate Vault paths

constraints.network: On-premises only, no cloud dependencies, devices reachable from NOC VLAN

open_questions.vendor_priority: Confirm Arista EOS inclusion in Phase 1 scope

confirmed_decisions.template_store: Git repository as source of truth for golden templates
confirmed_decisions.approval_gate: JIRA ticket workflow for production remediation sign-off

reasoner_reviews.product: The scope is tightly defined around the NOC pain point of manual drift auditing. The approval gate via JIRA is the right MVP safety mechanism given NERC CIP stakes. Post-remediation verification closes the loop and is essential to production trust.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: FastAPI plus Celery on Kubernetes is a sound architecture for this workload. The critical design concern is the vendor normalization layer — abstracting NETCONF session management behind a vendor plugin interface will allow Arista and Nokia support later without rewriting the diff engine. Recommend defining the plugin contract as a Python abstract base class in Phase 1.
reasoner_reviews.security: NERC CIP-010 mandates before-and-after configuration snapshots for every automated change. HashiCorp Vault short-lived credential injection ensures no static device passwords exist in the application database. mTLS between all internal pods prevents lateral movement if a single pod is compromised.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The on-premises Kubernetes cluster capacity is adequate for the polling load at MVP scale assuming a polling interval of 15 minutes and a fleet of up to 500 devices. Redis-backed Celery provides reliable job queuing even during temporary PostgreSQL unavailability.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The diff engine must normalize vendor-specific config syntax before comparison — a raw string diff on Cisco IOS-XE show running-config output versus Junos set-format will produce false positives. This normalization step needs its own test suite with captured device output fixtures.

backend.service_design: FastAPI control API exposes /devices, /templates, /drift-reports, /remediation-jobs endpoints. Celery workers handle three task types: poll_device (NETCONF fetch), compute_drift (diff against template), execute_remediation (NETCONF edit-config). Vendor plugin registry maps device OS type to plugin class.
backend.business_modules: DeviceRegistry module manages NETCONF endpoint metadata and Vault credential paths. TemplateEngine module clones golden templates from Git and resolves per-device template assignments by device group. DriftEngine module normalizes fetched configs through vendor-specific parsers and produces structured delta objects. RemediationOrchestrator module generates NETCONF payloads from deltas and coordinates approval and execution.
backend.background_jobs: poll_device_fleet: Celery beat periodic task every 15 minutes, fans out per-device poll tasks. verify_remediation: Scheduled 10 minutes after remediation execution, re-polls device and compares against expected post-change state. archive_snapshots: Nightly job moves config snapshots older than 90 days to compressed archive table.
backend.failure_handling: NETCONF connection timeouts retry with exponential backoff up to 3 attempts before marking device unreachable and raising a Prometheus alert. Failed remediation jobs set status to failed and create a JIRA escalation ticket. Circuit breaker per device prevents cascading poll failures from flooding the Celery queue.

security.auth_design: Keycloak realm with three roles: noc_engineer (read drift reports, submit remediation requests), network_admin (approve and execute remediations), readonly_auditor (read-only audit log access). JWT tokens validated by FastAPI dependency injection.
security.secrets_management: HashiCorp Vault dynamic secrets engine issues SSH certificates for NETCONF sessions scoped to specific device CIDRs with 1-hour TTL. Device credential paths namespaced by environment (prod/staging/dev). No plaintext credentials stored in PostgreSQL.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Every NETCONF get and edit-config call logged to audit_events table with device_id, operator_id, before_snapshot_hash, after_snapshot_hash, timestamp, and NERC CIP event code. Audit log is append-only via PostgreSQL row-level security; only the audit_writer role may insert.
security.security_risks: Risk of NETCONF session hijack if mTLS certificate rotation lapses. Mitigate with 30-day cert rotation via cert-manager on Kubernetes. Risk of golden template repository compromise pushing malicious configs. Mitigate with signed Git commits required and signature verification before template load.

data.entities: network_devices (id, hostname, mgmt_ip, vendor ENUM cisco_iosxe/junos/arista_eos, os_version, vault_credential_path, device_group_id, last_polled_at, status). config_snapshots (id, device_id, fetched_at, config_hash, config_xml BYTEA compressed). config_templates (id, name, vendor, version, git_commit_sha, content_xml, device_group_id, created_at). drift_reports (id, device_id, template_id, snapshot_id, detected_at, severity ENUM critical/high/medium/low, delta_json, status ENUM open/in_remediation/resolved/escalated). remediation_jobs (id, drift_report_id, generated_payload_xml, approver_id, approved_at, executed_at, verification_status ENUM pending/passed/failed, jira_ticket_id). audit_events (id, device_id, operator_id, action ENUM get_config/edit_config/lock/unlock, before_hash, after_hash, nerc_cip_code, timestamp).
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 with pgcrypto for config snapshot encryption at rest. Separate audit_events schema with append-only policy. Redis 7 stores Celery task state and locks per device to prevent concurrent NETCONF sessions to the same device.
data.retention_and_deletion: config_snapshots retained 12 months then archived to compressed PostgreSQL partition. audit_events retained 3 years with no deletion per NERC CIP-010. drift_reports retained 2 years.

devops.deployment_topology: Bare-metal Kubernetes with namespaces: netconfig-prod, netconfig-staging, netconfig-dev. Deployments: api (2 replicas), celery-worker (4 replicas, autoscaled by KEDA on Redis queue depth), celery-beat (1 replica with leader election). PostgreSQL deployed via CloudNativePG operator. Redis via Bitnami Helm chart.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitLab CI pipeline: lint (ruff, mypy), unit tests (pytest with device output fixtures), integration tests against dev namespace, Docker image build, Helm chart upgrade via ArgoCD sync. Vendor plugin tests run against captured NETCONF fixture files.
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus scrapes FastAPI /metrics and Celery exporter. Grafana dashboards: device poll success rate, drift detection rate by vendor, remediation approval latency, Celery queue depth per task type. Loki aggregates structured JSON logs with correlation IDs.
devops.rollback_strategy: ArgoCD maintains previous Helm release; one-command rollback via 'argocd app rollback netconfig-prod'. Remediation job rollback: separate Celery task applies inverse NETCONF payload if verification fails.

target_output.thinking_summary: This is an on-premises automation tool with no browser UI — architecture centers on Celery-based batch workers, a NETCONF vendor abstraction layer, and a PostgreSQL audit store. The highest-risk design decisions are the vendor normalization pipeline (must avoid false-positive diffs) and the NERC CIP-mandated audit trail (append-only, before/after snapshots). Vault dynamic credentials eliminate static device passwords entirely.
target_output.title: Network Configuration Drift Auditor and Automated Remediation Service
target_output.executive_summary: A headless Python automation service that polls Cisco IOS-XE and Junos network devices via NETCONF on 15-minute cycles, computes configuration drift against versioned golden templates stored in Git, and orchestrates remediation workflows with JIRA-gated human approval. Runs entirely on-premises on bare-metal Kubernetes. NERC CIP-010-compliant with append-only audit logging of all configuration read and write operations.
target_output.architecture_overview: FastAPI control API backed by four Celery worker pools: poll_device, compute_drift, execute_remediation, and verify_post_change. Vendor plugin architecture decouples NETCONF session handling and config normalization from the diff engine — each plugin implements a common abstract base class returning normalized configuration trees. PostgreSQL 16 stores device registry, drift reports, and append-only audit events. Redis 7 provides the Celery broker and per-device mutex locks preventing concurrent NETCONF sessions. HashiCorp Vault issues short-lived SSH certificates for all device connections. Golden templates are version-controlled in a separate Git repository with required signed commits.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, Celery 5, ncclient for NETCONF, pydantic v2, SQLAlchemy 2. Data: PostgreSQL 16 with CloudNativePG operator, pgcrypto for snapshot encryption, Redis 7. Auth: Keycloak OIDC, JWT validation via python-jose. Infra: Bare-metal Kubernetes, Helm, ArgoCD, HashiCorp Vault with cert-manager. Observability: Prometheus, Grafana, Loki, AlertManager. CI: GitLab CI with pytest and ruff.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Device Registry: register NETCONF endpoints with vendor, OS version, device group, and Vault credential path. Template Management: pull golden templates from Git by device group, display version history and active assignment. Drift Detection: per-device polling results in structured delta objects showing added, removed, and modified config sections by YANG path. Remediation Workflow: auto-generated NETCONF edit-config payloads reviewed and approved via JIRA ticket before execution. Post-Remediation Verification: re-poll within 10 minutes confirming config matches template or escalating. Audit Reports: NERC CIP event log filterable by device, operator, date range, and action type.
target_output.system_components: Celery Beat scheduler triggering fleet-wide poll tasks every 15 minutes. Celery Worker pool (4 replicas, KEDA autoscaled) executing poll, diff, remediation, and verification tasks. FastAPI REST API (2 replicas) serving /devices, /drift-reports, /remediation-jobs, /audit-events, and /templates endpoints. PostgreSQL 16 via CloudNativePG with primary and standby. Redis 7 as Celery broker and device lock store. HashiCorp Vault for dynamic SSH cert issuance. ArgoCD for GitOps deployments. Prometheus and Grafana for operational metrics.
target_output.workflows: Config Drift Detection: Celery Beat fires poll_device_fleet every 15 minutes. Each per-device task fetches SSH cert from Vault, opens NETCONF session, issues get-config, stores compressed XML snapshot in config_snapshots. compute_drift task normalizes config through vendor plugin, diffs against assigned golden template, records delta_json and severity in drift_reports. Alert fires via AlertManager if critical drift detected. Remediation Execution: Network admin reviews drift report via CLI or Grafana link, triggers remediation request via FastAPI POST /remediation-jobs. System generates NETCONF edit-config payload, creates JIRA ticket with before/after diff. Upon JIRA approval transition, execute_remediation Celery task fetches fresh Vault cert, applies payload, logs audit_event with before_hash and after_hash. verify_post_change task runs 10 minutes later and marks verification_status passed or failed.
target_output.data_model: network_devices (id UUID PK, hostname, mgmt_ip INET, vendor ENUM cisco_iosxe/junos/arista_eos, os_version, vault_credential_path, device_group_id FK, last_polled_at, status ENUM active/unreachable/excluded). config_snapshots (id UUID PK, device_id FK, fetched_at TIMESTAMPTZ, config_hash SHA256, config_xml BYTEA compressed with pgcrypto). config_templates (id UUID PK, name, vendor, version SEMVER, git_commit_sha, content_xml BYTEA, device_group_id FK, is_active BOOLEAN). drift_reports (id UUID PK, device_id FK, template_id FK, snapshot_id FK, detected_at, severity ENUM critical/high/medium/low, delta_json JSONB, status ENUM open/in_remediation/resolved/escalated). remediation_jobs (id UUID PK, drift_report_id FK, payload_xml BYTEA, approver_id, approved_at, executed_at, verification_status ENUM pending/passed/failed, jira_ticket_id). audit_events (id UUID PK, device_id FK, operator_id, action ENUM get_config/edit_config/lock/unlock, before_hash, after_hash, nerc_cip_code VARCHAR, timestamp TIMESTAMPTZ) — append-only via row-level security.
target_output.api_design: Internal REST API at /api/v1. GET /devices — list registered devices with poll status. POST /devices — register new device with NETCONF endpoint and Vault path. GET /drift-reports?device_id=&severity=&status= — filterable drift report list. GET /drift-reports/{id} — full delta JSON and snapshot diff. POST /remediation-jobs — create remediation job from drift report ID. PATCH /remediation-jobs/{id}/approve — network_admin only; triggers execute_remediation Celery task. GET /audit-events?device_id=&from=&to=&action= — NERC CIP audit log export. GET /templates — list golden templates by vendor and device group. Auth: Keycloak JWT Bearer on all endpoints; network_admin role required for PATCH /remediation-jobs/{id}/approve.
target_output.security_and_compliance: NERC CIP-010 compliance: before and after config snapshots stored for every edit-config operation; audit_events table append-only enforced via PostgreSQL RLS with only audit_writer role allowed to INSERT. CIP-007: Vault dynamic SSH certs with 1-hour TTL replace static device passwords; cert rotation via cert-manager every 30 days for internal mTLS. Keycloak RBAC enforces noc_engineer (read), network_admin (write/approve), readonly_auditor (audit log) separation. All NETCONF traffic over SSHv2 with host key verification. Golden template Git repository requires GPG-signed commits; signature verified at template load time.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Bare-metal Kubernetes cluster with three namespaces (netconfig-dev, netconfig-staging, netconfig-prod). Deployments managed via Helm charts in ArgoCD. API: 2 replicas with anti-affinity. Celery workers: 4 replicas base, KEDA ScaledObject watching Redis queue depth (scale to 12 at depth over 100). Celery Beat: 1 replica with Kubernetes leader election via redis-lock. CloudNativePG PostgreSQL: 1 primary + 1 standby with streaming replication. GitLab CI pipeline: ruff lint, mypy type-check, pytest (unit with NETCONF fixtures, integration against staging namespace), Docker build, Helm diff, ArgoCD sync. Secrets injected via Vault Agent sidecar.
target_output.observability: Prometheus metrics: device_poll_duration_seconds histogram by vendor, drift_detected_total counter by severity, remediation_approval_latency_seconds histogram, celery_task_failures_total by task_name, netconf_session_errors_total by device. Grafana dashboard: Fleet Health (poll success rate last 1h, unreachable device count), Drift Summary (open/in_remediation/resolved by severity), Remediation Pipeline (pending approval count, avg approval time). Loki log aggregation with structured JSON; trace_id propagated from API through Celery tasks. AlertManager: alert on celery_task_failures_total over 5 in 5 min, unreachable devices over 10 percent of fleet, critical drift unacknowledged over 30 min.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: On-premises infrastructure; primary cost is Kubernetes cluster capacity. At 500 devices with 15-minute polling: approximately 33 polls/minute, well within 4 Celery worker replicas. PostgreSQL storage estimate: 500 devices x 50 KB avg snapshot x 12 months x 96 polls/day = approximately 870 GB/year — plan compressed partition archiving after 3 months hot retention. Redis memory: approximately 100 MB for Celery broker state at this scale.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (10 weeks): Cisco IOS-XE and Junos NETCONF plugins, scheduled polling, drift diff engine, drift_reports and audit_events persistence, JIRA integration for approval workflow, Keycloak auth, basic Grafana dashboard. Phase 2 (6 weeks): Arista EOS vendor plugin, post-remediation verification task, YANG model validation layer, advanced Grafana drift analytics. Phase 3 (4 weeks): Risk-tiered auto-remediation (low-severity drift auto-applied without human gate), Nokia SR OS plugin, rollback-on-verification-failure automation.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python 3.12 with strict mypy configuration. ruff for linting and formatting. All vendor plugin classes must implement VendorPlugin abstract base class and pass fixture-based unit tests using captured NETCONF output files. 85% test coverage gate in GitLab CI. NETCONF payload changes require security review before merging. ADR records required for vendor plugin architecture decisions. Celery task idempotency enforced — tasks must check job status before executing to handle requeue scenarios.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: False-positive drift from vendor config serialization differences (e.g., IOS-XE ordering of ACE entries). Mitigation: vendor plugin normalization step with canonicalization; fixture tests against real device output. Risk: Vault outage blocks all NETCONF sessions. Mitigation: Vault HA mode with 3-node Raft cluster; Celery tasks fail fast and alert rather than cache credentials. Tradeoff: Celery over Temporal for simplicity at current scale; revisit if workflow complexity increases in Phase 3. Risk: NERC CIP audit gap if append-only RLS is misconfigured after schema migration. Mitigation: RLS policy tested in CI against staging database after each migration.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed JIRA (not ServiceNow) for approval gate based on existing NOC tooling. Confirmed 15-minute polling interval as acceptable for NERC CIP change detection SLA. Confirmed compressed bytea storage over external object store for config snapshots given air-gap constraint.

metadata.notes: NERC CIP-compliant network config drift detection with NETCONF vendor plugin architecture and Vault-issued SSH credentials.

profile.domain: In-Band Network Telemetry Research Platform

project_goal.value: Expose an API-first interface for querying INT telemetry data collected from P4-programmable switches in a university testbed, enabling programmatic per-flow latency histogram retrieval, queue depth snapshots, and path trace reconstruction for congestion control research
project_goal.rationale: Replaces manual pcap-based analysis workflows used by research group

target_users.value: University network researchers and PhD students studying congestion control algorithms on the P4 testbed
target_users.rationale: Identified in initial user interviews with lab PI

project_class.rationale: University testbed tooling; not production-hardened, but needs stable API for reproducible experiments

capabilities.rationale: Requires INT data ingestion pipeline, query API, streaming for live experiment monitoring, and analytics aggregations

complexity_level.rationale: P4 INT data parsing, ClickHouse time-series storage, and path trace reconstruction are non-trivial

risk_level.rationale: Internal university data; no PII or financial data but testbed topology is sensitive

data_sensitivity.rationale: Telemetry data reflects network topology and experiment configurations internal to the lab

external_exposure.rationale: API accessible only to authenticated university lab members via JWT

access_model.value: Custom JWT with researcher and lab_admin roles; researcher may read and create experiments; lab_admin may delete datasets and manage switch node registry
access_model.rationale: Simple role separation adequate for research team size

feature_scope.value: Experiment lifecycle management (register, configure, start, teardown), INT data ingestion from P4 collector, per-flow latency histogram API, queue depth snapshot queries, path trace reconstruction, congestion event detection, experiment archiving with summary report generation
feature_scope.rationale: Full workflow from experiment setup to analysis and archiving

mvp_scope.value: Experiment registration, INT record ingestion from existing P4 collector, per-flow latency percentile queries, basic path trace endpoint, Grafana dashboard for live monitoring
mvp_scope.rationale: Covers primary analysis workflow for current active congestion control study

security_baseline.value: JWT authentication for all API endpoints, TLS 1.3 on all interfaces, API rate limiting per researcher to prevent runaway queries on ClickHouse
security_baseline.rationale: Adequate for internal university research system

frontend_stack.value: React 18 with TypeScript for experiment management dashboard and interactive telemetry visualization
frontend_stack.rationale: Team familiarity; D3.js for latency histogram and path topology rendering

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI; async ingestion workers consuming from P4 INT collector gRPC stream
backend_stack.rationale: Python ecosystem has best P4 INT parsing support; FastAPI async for streaming endpoints

data_platform.value: ClickHouse for INT telemetry time-series; PostgreSQL 16 for experiment metadata, researcher accounts, and congestion event records
data_platform.rationale: ClickHouse columnar storage enables sub-second latency histogram aggregations over billions of INT records

hosting_target.value: Bare-metal Kubernetes on university lab servers
hosting_target.rationale: No cloud budget; lab maintains own Kubernetes cluster on research servers

privacy_retention_policy.value: Experiment telemetry retained for 2 years to support paper reproducibility; archived to NFS object store after 6 months active retention in ClickHouse
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Reproducibility requirement for submitted papers

future_scope.value: WebSocket streaming for live INT record visualization during active experiments, ML-based congestion anomaly detection integrated into the query layer
future_scope.rationale: Identified by research group as next-priority features

constraints.value: ClickHouse cluster limited to 3 nodes on existing lab hardware; INT record ingestion rate up to 500K records/second during active experiments
constraints.rationale: Hardware constraint from existing lab server inventory

observability_baseline.value: Grafana dashboards, Prometheus for ingestion throughput and query latency metrics, structured logging to file with experiment_id correlation
observability_baseline.rationale: Standard research lab observability setup

execution_preference.value: Research-driven iterations aligned with active experiment cycles; approximately 3-week sprints
execution_preference.rationale: Development pace tied to experimental research schedule

project.goal: API-first INT telemetry platform for P4 switch research
project.target_users: University network researchers and PhD students
project.timeline: Ready for active congestion control study

frontend.visualization: D3.js for latency histograms and path topology graphs
frontend.state: React Query for API data fetching

backend.ingestion: gRPC consumer from P4 INT collector

security.auth: Custom JWT with researcher and lab_admin roles

data.db: ClickHouse for INT time-series, PostgreSQL 16 for experiment metadata

constraints.hardware: 3-node ClickHouse cluster, up to 500K INT records/second

confirmed_decisions.storage: ClickHouse for telemetry, PostgreSQL for metadata

reasoner_reviews.product: The experiment lifecycle model is the right abstraction for reproducibility. Archiving telemetry datasets with a summary report on teardown directly supports the paper-writing workflow. The per-flow latency histogram API must return consistent percentile buckets across experiments for cross-study comparison.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: ClickHouse MergeTree with experiment_id and flow_id in the partition key will enable the sub-second latency histogram queries needed for interactive analysis. The path trace reconstruction is the most algorithmically complex piece — recommend implementing it as a materialized view over INT hop records rather than recomputing on every query. The ingestion pipeline must handle 500K records/second which requires batched inserts with async buffering.
reasoner_reviews.security: JWT for a university internal system is appropriate. The main risk is a researcher running an unbounded ClickHouse scan during a live experiment, degrading ingestion throughput. Enforce per-researcher query concurrency limits and scan byte limits via a query admission controller.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 3-node ClickHouse cluster is the key hardware constraint. At 500K records/second with an average INT record size of 100 bytes, ingestion is approximately 50 MB/s — manageable for a 3-node cluster. Ensure ClickHouse replication factor of 2 to survive single-node failure during active experiments.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Round 1 plan was missing explicit path trace reconstruction design — the data_model had INT hop records but the workflows section did not explain how path traces are assembled from per-hop data. This must be addressed in the architecture overview and workflows.

backend.service_design: FastAPI application with three routers: /experiments (lifecycle), /telemetry (query), /nodes (switch registry). Background ingestion service runs as a separate asyncio process consuming the P4 collector gRPC stream and batching INT records to ClickHouse.
backend.api_patterns: REST endpoints for experiment CRUD and telemetry queries. GET /telemetry/flows/{flow_id}/latency-histogram returns percentile distribution. GET /telemetry/path-traces?experiment_id=&flow_id= returns reconstructed hop sequence. Server-sent events endpoint for live ingestion rate monitoring.
backend.business_modules: ExperimentManager: lifecycle state machine (registered -> active -> teardown -> archived). INTIngestionWorker: async gRPC consumer with 10K record batch buffer, flushes to ClickHouse every 100ms. PathTraceReconstructor: assembles hop sequences from telemetry_hops by sorting on switch_ingress_timestamp and correlating flow_id. CongestionDetector: evaluates queue_depth_bytes against configurable thresholds and writes congestion_events.
backend.scaling_notes: Ingestion worker uses asyncio gather for parallel ClickHouse insert batches across 3 shards. ClickHouse distributed table routes inserts by consistent hash of flow_id to balance across nodes.

security.auth_design: JWT issued on login with researcher and lab_admin claims. FastAPI dependency injects current_user, checked against endpoint permission matrix. lab_admin required for DELETE /experiments/{id}/dataset.
security.abuse_prevention: Per-researcher ClickHouse query admission: max 2 concurrent analytical queries, max 10 GB scan per query enforced via ClickHouse user quota profiles. Rate limit on /telemetry endpoints: 30 requests/minute per researcher JWT.

data.entities: telemetry_flows (ClickHouse MergeTree: flow_id, experiment_id, src_ip, dst_ip, src_port, dst_port, protocol, first_seen, last_seen). telemetry_hops (ClickHouse MergeTree: hop_id, flow_id, experiment_id, switch_id, ingress_port, egress_port, queue_depth_bytes, ingress_timestamp_ns, processing_delay_ns). queue_snapshots (ClickHouse MergeTree: snapshot_id, switch_id, experiment_id, captured_at_ns, queue_occupancy_bytes, port_id). experiments (PostgreSQL: id UUID PK, researcher_id FK, title, description, switch_node_ids INT[], flow_filters JSONB, status ENUM registered/active/teardown/archived, created_at, archived_at). congestion_events (PostgreSQL: id UUID PK, experiment_id FK, switch_id, flow_id, queue_depth_bytes, detected_at, duration_ms). researchers (PostgreSQL: id UUID PK, username, email, role ENUM researcher/lab_admin, created_at).
data.storage_design: ClickHouse 3-node cluster with ReplicatedMergeTree and 2x replication factor. Partition by experiment_id for fast experiment-scoped scans. PostgreSQL 16 for relational metadata. NFS mount for archived experiment datasets post 6-month retention.
data.analytics_events: ClickHouse materialized view path_traces_mv pre-aggregates hop sequences ordered by ingress_timestamp_ns per flow_id and experiment_id to serve path trace queries without full table scan.

devops.deployment_topology: Kubernetes with two namespaces: int-dev and int-prod. Deployments: api (2 replicas), ingestion-worker (1 replica per P4 collector stream), frontend (nginx, 2 replicas). ClickHouse via ClickHouse Operator. PostgreSQL via CloudNativePG.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: ruff lint, mypy, pytest for API and ingestion worker unit tests, Docker build, kubectl apply to int-dev then manual gate to int-prod. INT record ingestion tested with synthetic P4 INT packet fixtures.
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus metrics: int_records_ingested_total, int_batch_flush_duration_seconds, query_duration_seconds by endpoint. Grafana dashboard: ingestion throughput, ClickHouse insert errors, active experiment count, query latency p95.

previous_plan.title: INT Telemetry Research Platform v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: Python FastAPI backend ingesting INT records from P4 collector, ClickHouse for time-series storage, PostgreSQL for experiment metadata. React 18 dashboard for experiment management and telemetry visualization.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: FastAPI application with ClickHouse for INT telemetry and PostgreSQL for experiments. Async ingestion worker consumes P4 collector gRPC stream and batches inserts to ClickHouse.
