S3 for CDR files and dispute evidence with SSE-S3. Datadog APM sidecar on each Fargate task. Auth0 tenant with RBAC and MFA policy.
target_output.workflows: CDR Settlement Processing: Billing analyst uploads CDR file via POST /cdr-batches. API stores file to S3 staging bucket and creates cdr_batches record. EventBridge triggers spring-batch-worker on billing cycle close. Spring Batch settlement job: ItemReader streams CSV from S3 in 10K-record chunks. ItemProcessor looks up rate_per_minute from rate_decks by trunk_group_id and call_type, computes billable_minutes and amount. ItemWriter inserts chunk to cdr_records. On Spring Batch restart, job repository resumes from last committed chunk_id. After all chunks complete, InvoiceGenerator aggregates sum(amount) and sum(billable_minutes) per partner, inserts settlement_invoices with status=draft and created_by=system. SOX Invoice Approval: Wholesale billing analyst reviews invoice draft in InvoiceReviewTable, calls PATCH /invoices/{id}/submit (transitions to pending_approval). Billing manager opens pending_approval queue, reviews, calls PATCH /invoices/{id}/finalize — endpoint validates finalized_by != invoice.created_by (HTTP 409 if same user), transitions to finalized, writes financial_audit_log entry. Routing Rule Rollback: Carrier relations manager selects historical snapshot, calls POST /routing-rules/{agreement_id}/rollback body {target_snapshot_id}. RoutingVersionManager executes PostgreSQL transaction: UPDATE routing_rule_snapshots SET is_active=false, rolled_back_at=NOW() WHERE is_active=true AND agreement_id=X; UPDATE routing_rule_snapshots SET is_active=true WHERE id=target_snapshot_id. financial_audit_log entry written with entity_type=routing_rule, action=rolled_back.
target_output.data_model: carrier_partners (id UUID PK, partner_name, partner_code VARCHAR UNIQUE, country_code CHAR(2), sftp_config_encrypted JSONB, status ENUM active/inactive). interconnect_agreements (id UUID PK, partner_id FK, agreement_reference VARCHAR UNIQUE, effective_from DATE, effective_to DATE, settlement_currency CHAR(3), billing_cycle_day INT, status ENUM draft/active/expired). rate_decks (id UUID PK, agreement_id FK, version INT, effective_from DATE, rates JSONB, created_by UUID, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). routing_rule_snapshots (id UUID PK, agreement_id FK, snapshot_version INT, ruleset_json JSONB NOT NULL, promoted_at TIMESTAMPTZ, promoted_by UUID, rolled_back_at TIMESTAMPTZ, rolled_back_by UUID, is_active BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT false). cdr_batches (id UUID PK, partner_id FK, billing_cycle_id FK, file_s3_key, file_size_bytes BIGINT, total_records INT, processed_records INT, status ENUM uploaded/processing/complete/failed). cdr_records (id UUID PK, batch_id FK, call_datetime TIMESTAMPTZ, trunk_group_id, call_type ENUM voice_termination/voice_origination/transit, duration_seconds INT, billable_minutes DECIMAL(12,6), rate_per_minute DECIMAL(10,6), amount DECIMAL(14,4)). settlement_invoices (id UUID PK, partner_id FK, billing_cycle_id FK, total_billable_minutes DECIMAL(14,4), total_amount DECIMAL(14,4), currency CHAR(3), status ENUM draft/pending_approval/finalized/disputed, created_by UUID, finalized_by UUID, finalized_at TIMESTAMPTZ — finalized_by must differ from created_by per SOX). dispute_cases (id UUID PK, invoice_id FK, raised_by_partner_id FK, status ENUM initiated/evidence_exchange/under_review/resolved/closed, sla_deadline TIMESTAMPTZ). dispute_evidence (id UUID PK, dispute_id FK, uploaded_by UUID, file_s3_key, uploaded_at). financial_audit_log (id UUID PK, entity_type ENUM invoice/rate_deck/routing_rule, entity_id UUID, action ENUM created/updated/finalized/rolled_back, changed_by UUID, before_value JSONB, after_value JSONB, timestamp TIMESTAMPTZ) — INSERT-only via PostgreSQL RLS, audit_writer role.
target_output.api_design: REST API at /api/v1. GET POST /carrier-partners — partner management. GET POST /agreements — interconnect agreement CRUD. GET POST /rate-decks?agreement_id= — rate deck versioning. GET /routing-rules/{agreement_id}/snapshots — version history. POST /routing-rules/{agreement_id}/rollback — carrier_relations role; body {target_snapshot_id}. POST /cdr-batches — upload CDR file to S3 and create batch record. GET /invoices?status=pending_approval — billing queue. PATCH /invoices/{id}/submit — wholesale_billing_analyst role. PATCH /invoices/{id}/finalize — billing_manager role; enforces finalized_by != created_by returning HTTP 409 on violation. POST /disputes — raise dispute against invoice_id. PATCH /disputes/{id}/status — status transitions with role checks. POST /disputes/{id}/evidence — file upload to S3. GET /analytics/settlement-summary?from=&to= — monthly aggregation by partner. GET /admin/rate-decks — system_admin only.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Auth0 SSO with four RBAC roles: carrier_relations, wholesale_billing_analyst, billing_manager, system_admin. MFA enforced for wholesale_billing_analyst, billing_manager, and system_admin via Auth0 MFA policy. Spring Security @PreAuthorize on service methods. SOX segregation: PATCH /invoices/{id}/finalize validates request.userId != invoice.createdBy and returns HTTP 409 Conflict if same user attempts finalization. financial_audit_log: PostgreSQL RLS with audit_writer role (INSERT only); application uses audit_writer connection for all financial event writes; no UPDATE or DELETE granted. AES-256 encryption at rest on RDS via KMS. S3 CDR files and dispute evidence encrypted with SSE-S3. TLS 1.3 enforced by ALB. WAF rules block SQL injection and XSS.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: ECS Fargate us-east-1 with two services: wholesale-api (2 tasks min, 6 max, auto-scale CPU 70%, Datadog APM sidecar) and spring-batch-worker (1 task, triggered by EventBridge rule on billing_cycle_close event). RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ db.r6g.large with automated 7-day backups and manual snapshot before each schema migration. ElastiCache Redis 7 cluster backup enabled. S3 bucket with versioning and Glacier lifecycle for CDR files older than 90 days. Jenkins pipeline: mvn verify (TestContainers PostgreSQL for integration tests, H2 for unit tests), Docker build, ECR push, ECS rolling update. Flyway migrations applied as ECS one-off task before service update. Rollback: previous ECS task definition retained for immediate rollback.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM on all Fargate tasks with trace propagation from API through Spring Batch jobs. Custom metrics: cdr_records_processed_per_batch histogram, invoice_generation_duration_seconds, dispute_resolution_days histogram by partner, routing_rollback_count counter. Dashboards: Settlement Pipeline (batch status, CDR processing rate, invoice draft count), SOX Compliance (pending_approval invoice count, finalization latency, audit_log entry rate), Dispute SLA (cases by status, approaching SLA deadline count). PagerDuty alerts: Spring Batch settlement job failure, invoice finalization error rate over 1%, cdr_processing_exceptions over 1000 in one batch.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: RDS db.r6g.large Multi-AZ: approximately $350/month. ElastiCache Redis cache.t3.medium: approximately $50/month. ECS Fargate 2 baseline tasks: approximately $80/month. S3 CDR storage at 5 GB per partner per month for 20 partners = 100 GB/month, approximately $2.50. Datadog APM: existing corporate contract. Total approximately $500/month. Spring Batch auto-scales via ECS task launch per billing cycle event — no idle compute cost. S3 Intelligent-Tiering for CDR files older than 30 days reduces storage cost by approximately 40%.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (10 weeks): Carrier partner and interconnect agreement CRUD, rate deck versioning, manual CDR batch upload to S3, Spring Batch SettlementEngine (10K chunk processing, restart-from-checkpoint via database job repository), invoice draft generation, SOX two-step approval workflow (wholesale_billing_analyst submit, billing_manager finalize with segregation enforcement), financial_audit_log with INSERT-only RLS, Auth0 SSO and MFA, basic dispute initiation. Phase 2 (6 weeks): Routing rule snapshots with atomic rollback via PostgreSQL transaction, dispute evidence attachment exchange, SLA tracking and dashboard, advanced analytics (settlement summary, dispute resolution metrics), routing quality monitor batch job. Phase 3 (4 weeks): Automated SFTP CDR pull from carrier SFTP servers, partner portal API for dispute status query, bulk rate deck import tool.
target_output.development_guidelines: Java 21 with strict Checkstyle configuration. Spring Boot test slices (DataJpaTest with TestContainers PostgreSQL, WebMvcTest for controller layer). 80% test coverage gate on service and batch layer. Spring Batch job unit tests with in-memory H2 and sample CDR fixture files. Flyway migrations reviewed by DBA before staging deployment. financial_audit_log write path has integration test verifying INSERT-only enforcement. routing_rule_snapshots rollback logic has unit tests for transaction atomicity using TestContainers. SOX invoice segregation enforced in WebMvcTest with same-user finalization scenario asserting HTTP 409.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Spring Batch job failure mid-batch leaves partial cdr_records for a billing cycle. Mitigation: restart-from-checkpoint via database job repository; InvoiceGenerator validates processed_records == total_records before generating draft. Risk: SOX audit_log RLS bypass via direct DBA access. Mitigation: RDS IAM authentication with least-privilege; DBA access requires separate approval and is logged via CloudTrail. Risk: routing rule rollback during active traffic causes brief misrouting while transaction propagates to routing plane. Mitigation: accepted tradeoff; routing plane synchronization is on 5-minute cycle, out of scope for this system. Tradeoff: Spring Boot over lighter frameworks for team familiarity and Spring Batch ecosystem; acceptable overhead at this scale.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed billing_manager as separate Auth0 role over senior wholesale_billing_analyst flag for SOX role segregation clarity. Confirmed immutable routing_rule_snapshots over soft-delete versioning for audit trail completeness. Confirmed Flyway over Liquibase for team familiarity.

metadata.notes: Round 3 wholesale voice interconnect platform; resolves routing rule rollback schema and SOX two-step invoice approval segregation.

profile.domain: Multi-Vendor RAN Alarm Correlation and NOC Ticket Automation

project_goal.value: Correlate alarm data from multi-vendor RAN management systems, suppress redundant child alarms, enrich root-cause alarms with topology context, and forward actionable events to NOC ticketing system
project_goal.rationale: NOC receives thousands of raw alarms per day with high redundancy; correlation and suppression reduces alert fatigue and allows engineers to focus on root causes

target_users.value: NOC engineers responding to ticketed incidents; operations managers reviewing correlation accuracy and suppression rates; tool administrators managing correlation rules
target_users.rationale: NOC is the primary operational consumer; administrators maintain rule accuracy

project_class.rationale: Headless correlation and enrichment engine with integrations to NMS systems and ITSM; no primary user-facing application

capabilities.rationale: Real-time alarm ingestion, multi-vendor NMS integrations, batch feedback processing, realtime correlation engine

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-vendor alarm format normalization, correlation window management, topology enrichment, feedback-based rule weight adjustment

risk_level.rationale: Incorrect suppression could silently drop critical alarms; over-correlation could create inaccurate tickets; operational risk but no safety or financial impact

data_sensitivity.rationale: RAN alarm data is internal operational telemetry; no personal or regulated data

external_exposure.rationale: Alarm ingestion from internal NMS systems; ITSM integration is internal; no external exposure

access_model.value: Keycloak RBAC: noc-engineer (read correlation results, view tickets), rules-admin (manage correlation rules), operations-manager (suppression rate and accuracy reports)
access_model.rationale: Separation between operations consumers and rule administrators

feature_scope.value: SNMP trap and REST adapter receivers for multi-vendor NMS, alarm normalization to canonical format, deduplication within 60-second window, topology enrichment with cell tower parent-child relationships, rule-based correlation clustering within 5-minute windows, child alarm suppression, root-cause alarm enrichment and ITSM forwarding, NOC engineer feedback loop updating rule weights, correlation accuracy reporting
feature_scope.rationale: Full correlation pipeline from alarm ingestion to ticket creation with feedback learning

mvp_scope.value: SNMP trap receiver for Ericsson OSS, alarm normalization, basic rule-based correlation with 5-minute window, topology enrichment, ITSM ticket creation via ServiceNow REST API
mvp_scope.rationale: Ericsson OSS is the dominant NMS vendor covering 60% of RAN; delivers immediate NOC alert reduction

security_baseline.value: Keycloak RBAC for API access, TLS 1.3 for all API and ITSM communications, SNMP community strings in Kubernetes Secrets, no alarm data logged to stdout (topology and alarm details excluded from log lines)
security_baseline.rationale: Internal platform with privileged access to NOC ticketing and NMS integration credentials

frontend_stack.value: none — automation tool; operators access via ITSM tickets and Grafana dashboards
frontend_stack.rationale: automation_tool class; no primary user-facing UI

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI; asyncio for concurrent NMS adapter connections; Celery for batch feedback processing
backend_stack.rationale: asyncio handles concurrent SNMP trap reception from multiple NMS endpoints; Celery for async feedback batch jobs

data_platform.value: MongoDB 7 for alarm correlations and topology data; Redis 7 for correlation window state and deduplication keys
data_platform.rationale: MongoDB document model fits variable alarm attribute structures across vendors; Redis for sub-millisecond window state

hosting_target.value: Bare-metal Kubernetes on self-hosted infrastructure
hosting_target.rationale: Self-hosted infrastructure for internal operational tooling

privacy_retention_policy.value: Alarm correlations retained 90 days; NOC feedback records retained 1 year; raw alarm events retained 7 days for replay
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: 90-day correlation history for accuracy analysis; short raw alarm retention to manage storage

future_scope.value: ML-based correlation using embedding similarity for novel alarm patterns; Huawei and Nokia NMS adapters; real-time correlation accuracy dashboard
future_scope.rationale: ML correlation is Phase 2 after rule-based system is validated with production data

constraints.value: Correlation latency from alarm receipt to ITSM ticket creation must be <2 minutes; SNMP trap receiver must handle 5000 traps/minute peak; suppression must never drop alarms without logging suppression reason
constraints.rationale: NOC SLA requires timely ticket creation; suppression auditability required to validate no false negatives

observability_baseline.value: Grafana + Prometheus for correlation pipeline metrics; alerting on ITSM ticket creation failures, SNMP trap reception rate drops, and correlation accuracy degradation
observability_baseline.rationale: Pipeline health monitoring for NOC tool supporting 24/7 operations

execution_preference.value: GitLab CI; Helm on bare-metal Kubernetes; iterative rule library development
execution_preference.rationale: Existing GitLab CI toolchain; self-hosted Kubernetes

project.goal: Multi-vendor RAN alarm correlation, child suppression, topology enrichment, and NOC ITSM ticket creation
project.timeline: Ericsson OSS MVP Q2; multi-vendor coverage Q3

backend.framework: FastAPI + asyncio for NMS adapters; Celery for feedback jobs
backend.protocols: SNMP v2c trap reception; ServiceNow REST API for ITSM

data.cache: Redis 7 for window state and dedup

reasoner_reviews.product: The suppression audit log is critical for NOC acceptance — engineers must be able to verify that a specific alarm was suppressed and see the root-cause alarm it was linked to. Without this, the tool will be distrusted and operators will disable suppression.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Correlation window state in Redis must handle window overlap between batches. Use Redis ZADD sorted sets keyed by correlation_window_id with alarm IDs as members and receipt timestamp as score. Window close triggered by watermark advancement or explicit close timer. MongoDB alarm_correlations document structure should embed suppressed_child_alarm_ids array to enable efficient suppression audit queries.
reasoner_reviews.security: SNMP community strings are sensitive credentials — rotate quarterly and store in Kubernetes Secrets via External Secrets Operator. ServiceNow integration credentials (OAuth2 client credentials) must also be rotated via Kubernetes Secrets. No alarm details in application logs — correlation engine logs should use alarm_id references only.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 5000 traps/minute = ~83 traps/second peak; asyncio SNMP trap receiver with pysnmp can handle this on a single process. 2-minute end-to-end latency requires: trap reception <5s, normalization <10s, correlation window close <5 minutes, enrichment <10s, ITSM creation <30s — total budget within 6 minutes. Tighten window close timer to 3 minutes for faster processing.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Round 2 plan improved correlation window management and enrichment latency but the feedback loop for updating rule weights was still underspecified — what triggers a weight update and what is the update function?

backend.service_design: Five service modules: snmp_trap_receiver (asyncio pysnmp UDP server, multi-vendor OID normalization), rest_alarm_adapter (FastAPI endpoint for NMS REST push adapters), correlation_engine (asyncio event loop consuming normalized alarms from Redis queue, window management, rule evaluation, child suppression), enrichment_service (MongoDB topology lookup, parent-child enrichment), itsm_forwarder (ServiceNow REST API client, ticket creation with retry), feedback_processor (Celery batch job, NOC engineer resolution annotations -> rule weight updates)
backend.api_patterns: Internal async message passing via Redis lists (alarms_normalized queue, alarms_correlated queue). External: ServiceNow REST API for ticket creation. FastAPI admin API: GET /correlations (list recent), GET /correlations/{id}/suppressed-alarms, POST /rules (create/update correlation rule), GET /suppression-audit
backend.failure_handling: ITSM ticket creation failure queued to Redis retry list with exponential backoff up to 30 minutes; dead-letter MongoDB collection for alarms with persistent ITSM failures; correlation window close on timeout even if SNMP session is active to prevent unbounded window growth

data.entities: alarm_events (id UUID, source_nms, vendor, alarm_type, network_element_id, severity ENUM critical/major/minor/warning, alarm_text, received_at, normalized_at). alarm_correlations (id UUID, root_alarm_id FK, suppressed_child_alarm_ids UUID[], correlation_rule_id, topology_context JSONB containing {cell_id, site_id, parent_bsc_id, parent_msc_id}, enriched_at, itsm_ticket_id, status ENUM active/resolved, created_at). correlation_rules (id UUID, name, trigger_alarm_type, child_alarm_types[], time_window_seconds, topology_scope ENUM cell/site/bsc, weight FLOAT, active). suppression_audit (id UUID, suppressed_alarm_id FK, root_correlation_id FK, suppression_rule_id, suppression_reason, suppressed_at — immutable append-only).
data.consistency_notes: suppression_audit is append-only; no UPDATE or DELETE; MongoDB collection with no update permissions for application service account.

devops.deployment_topology: Bare-metal Kubernetes: snmp-trap-receiver Pod (UDP hostNetwork, port 162), correlation-engine Deployment 2 replicas, enrichment-service Deployment 2 replicas, itsm-forwarder Deployment 1 replica, feedback-processor Celery CronJob, MongoDB StatefulSet, Redis Cluster.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitLab CI: pytest for correlation engine unit tests (alarm correlation rule evaluation with fixture alarm sets), mypy, Docker build, Helm upgrade. Molecule tests for SNMP trap receiver with mock OID fixtures.
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus: alarms_received_per_min, alarms_normalized_per_min, correlations_created_per_hour, itsm_tickets_created_per_hour, suppression_rate_pct, itsm_creation_failure_rate. Grafana: Correlation Pipeline Health, Suppression Audit, NOC Ticket Creation Rate.

ISS-001.title: Feedback loop rule weight update function undefined
ISS-001.detail: Rounds 1-2 described a feedback loop where NOC engineer resolution annotations update correlation rule weights but never defined the weight update function, initial weights, or convergence criteria.

history.note: Feedback loop mentioned but not specified
Still no weight update algorithm defined

ISS-002.title: Suppression audit query performance for per-alarm lookups undefined
ISS-002.detail: Round 2 added suppression_audit collection but did not specify MongoDB indexes for efficient per-alarm lookup queries by NOC engineers investigating why a specific alarm was suppressed.

history.note: No suppression audit specified
Suppression audit added but no index specification for query performance

focus_issues.title: Feedback loop rule weight update function undefined
focus_issues.detail: Weight update algorithm, initial weights, and convergence criteria needed for feedback processor.

focus_issues.title: Suppression audit MongoDB index specification missing
focus_issues.detail: Index on suppressed_alarm_id for per-alarm audit lookup queries by NOC engineers.

latest_recommendations: Define weight update as: new_weight = old_weight + learning_rate * (feedback_signal - old_weight) where feedback_signal=1.0 for confirmed root cause correlation, 0.0 for false correlation, learning_rate=0.1; cap weight in [0.1, 1.0]

Add MongoDB compound index on suppression_audit: {suppressed_alarm_id: 1, suppressed_at: -1} for per-alarm lookup; {root_correlation_id: 1, suppressed_at: -1} for per-correlation suppressed child listing

convergence_state.reason: Feedback weight algorithm and suppression audit index unresolved

previous_audits.summary: Good correlation engine and enrichment design with suppression audit in Round 2; two remaining gaps prevent production readiness: feedback weight update algorithm is unclear and suppression audit queries will be slow without proper MongoDB indexes.
strengths: asyncio SNMP trap receiver correctly handles 5000 traps/minute
Suppression audit append-only collection provides required auditability
Correlation window watermark management correctly prevents unbounded growth
concerns: Feedback processor weight update algorithm not defined — NOC engineers cannot predict how feedback affects future correlations
suppression_audit query performance for per-alarm lookups depends on index that has not been specified
recommendations: Use exponential moving average weight update: new_weight = alpha * feedback + (1-alpha) * old_weight with alpha=0.1; document in operational runbook
Add compound MongoDB indexes on suppression_audit for suppressed_alarm_id and root_correlation_id lookups

issue_updates.title: Feedback weight update algorithm
issue_updates.detail: EMA with alpha=0.1 recommended but not yet implemented

issue_updates.title: Suppression audit index missing
issue_updates.detail: No MongoDB index on suppressed_alarm_id or root_correlation_id

previous_plan.title: RAN Alarm Correlation Engine — Round 2
previous_plan.executive_summary: Python FastAPI asyncio alarm correlation engine on bare-metal Kubernetes with SNMP trap receiver, MongoDB for correlations, Redis for window state, ServiceNow ITSM integration, and suppression audit collection.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: asyncio SNMP trap receiver normalizes alarms to MongoDB. Correlation engine uses Redis sliding window. Enrichment adds topology context. ITSM forwarder creates ServiceNow tickets. Suppression audit logs all suppressed alarms.
previous_plan.data_model: alarm_events, alarm_correlations, correlation_rules, suppression_audit in MongoDB.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: Keycloak RBAC, TLS 1.3, SNMP credentials in Kubernetes Secrets, no alarm details in logs.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 3 resolves both issues: feedback processor weight update is now a documented exponential moving average (EMA) formula with alpha=0.1, initial weight 0.5, bounded to [0.1, 1.0], applied per correlation_rule on each NOC annotation batch. Suppression audit MongoDB indexes specified: compound {suppressed_alarm_id: 1, suppressed_at: -1} for per-alarm lookup and {root_correlation_id: 1} for per-correlation child listing. Both are focused operational improvements that complete the production-readiness of the correlation engine.

fix_report.action_taken: Defined feedback processor weight update as EMA: new_weight = (1 - alpha) * old_weight + alpha * feedback_signal where alpha=0.1, feedback_signal=1.0 for NOC annotation 'confirmed_root_cause', 0.0 for 'false_correlation', 0.5 for 'partial_match'. Initial weight for new rules = 0.5. Weight bounded to [0.1, 1.0] — floor prevents complete rule suppression, ceiling prevents overconfidence. Celery feedback_processor batch job runs nightly, processes all annotations from past 24 hours, updates correlation_rules.weight in MongoDB, logs weight delta per rule to ops runbook.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Feedback loop has a transparent, predictable update function; NOC engineers understand how their annotations influence future correlations; weight drift monitored via Grafana

fix_report.action_taken: Added MongoDB indexes to suppression_audit collection: (1) compound index {suppressed_alarm_id: 1, suppressed_at: -1} for per-alarm lookups by NOC engineers (O(log n) vs O(n) full scan), (2) compound index {root_correlation_id: 1, suppressed_at: -1} for listing all child alarms suppressed by a given correlation. Both indexes specified in Mongoose schema and applied via MongoDB create_index() in application startup migration.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Per-alarm suppression audit queries return in <50ms regardless of collection size; NOC engineers can efficiently verify suppression reason for any alarm

target_output.title: RAN Alarm Correlation Engine — Feedback Weight Algorithm and Suppression Audit Index Hardening
target_output.executive_summary: Python FastAPI asyncio automation tool on bare-metal Kubernetes correlating multi-vendor RAN alarms with rule-based clustering in 5-minute windows using Redis, topology-enriched root-cause forwarding to ServiceNow, and an append-only MongoDB suppression audit trail. Feedback processor uses a documented EMA weight update (alpha=0.1) to refine correlation rule confidence from NOC engineer annotations. MongoDB suppression_audit compound indexes on suppressed_alarm_id and root_correlation_id enable sub-50ms per-alarm audit lookups.
target_output.architecture_overview: asyncio SNMP trap receiver (pysnmp UDP server on port 162 with hostNetwork) normalizes trap OIDs from Ericsson OSS to canonical alarm_events and pushes to Redis alarms_normalized queue. Correlation engine consumes queue, clusters alarms within 5-minute windows using rule-based matching against correlation_rules weighted by EMA feedback scores, suppresses child alarms, writes alarm_correlations and suppression_audit to MongoDB. Enrichment service augments root-cause alarms with cell/site/BSC topology context from MongoDB network_elements. ITSM forwarder creates ServiceNow incident tickets with enriched context. Nightly feedback_processor Celery job updates correlation_rules.weight using EMA from NOC annotation records.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, asyncio, pysnmp 6 for SNMP trap reception, motor (async MongoDB driver), aioredis. Batch: Celery 5 with Redis broker. Database: MongoDB 7 (alarm_correlations, suppression_audit, correlation_rules), Redis 7 Cluster (window state, dedup cache, Celery broker). ITSM: ServiceNow REST API v2 via httpx async client. Infrastructure: bare-metal Kubernetes, Helm, GitLab CI. Monitoring: Grafana + Prometheus.
target_output.functional_feature_map: SNMP Trap Reception: asyncio pysnmp UDP server on port 162, multi-vendor OID lookup table for Ericsson OSS 1.3.6.1.4.1.193.x OIDs, canonical alarm normalization, 60-second deduplication window via Redis SETEX on alarm_hash key. Correlation Engine: Redis ZADD window per correlation group key, 5-minute window close timer, rule evaluation against correlation_rules sorted by weight DESC, child alarm identification via matching alarm_types and topology scope, MongoDB alarm_correlations write with suppressed_child_alarm_ids[], suppression_audit append per suppressed alarm. Topology Enrichment: MongoDB lookup of network_elements by network_element_id, attaches cell_id, site_id, parent_bsc_id to correlation document. ITSM Forwarding: ServiceNow REST POST /api/now/table/incident with enriched correlation context, retry queue in Redis on failure. Feedback Processing: nightly Celery job reads noc_annotations collection (NOC engineer resolution annotations), applies EMA weight update per rule, updates correlation_rules.weight, logs delta.
target_output.system_components: snmp-trap-receiver Pod (hostNetwork, UDP 162, pysnmp asyncio, Ericsson OID normalization), rest-alarm-adapter Deployment (FastAPI REST endpoint for NMS push adapters), correlation-engine Deployment 2 replicas (asyncio event loop, Redis window state, MongoDB writes), enrichment-service Deployment 2 replicas (MongoDB topology lookup), itsm-forwarder Deployment 1 replica (ServiceNow httpx client, Redis retry queue), feedback-processor CronJob nightly (Celery, EMA rule weight update), MongoDB 7 StatefulSet 3-replica replica set, Redis 7 Cluster 3 shards (window state + dedup + Celery broker).
target_output.workflows: Alarm Correlation Workflow: SNMP trap received on UDP 162 by pysnmp asyncio handler. OID lookup table maps trap OID to (vendor, alarm_type, severity). Canonical alarm_event created and deduplicated via Redis SETEX alarm_hash_key 60s. Pushed to alarms_normalized Redis list. Correlation engine pops from list, looks up correlation_rules with trigger_alarm_type matching. Computes correlation_group_key from (trigger_alarm_type, topology_scope, network_element_id). ZADD correlation_window:{group_key} score=received_at member=alarm_id with TTL 10 minutes. On window close (5 minutes elapsed): ZRANGEBYSCORE retrieves all alarm_ids in window. Evaluate child_alarm_types and topology_scope match. Write alarm_correlations document with root_alarm_id and suppressed_child_alarm_ids[]. For each suppressed alarm: append to suppression_audit with suppression_rule_id. Push enriched root alarm to alarms_correlated Redis list. Enrichment service pops, looks up network_elements, attaches topology context. ITSM forwarder creates ServiceNow incident. Feedback Weight Update (nightly): Celery feedback_processor reads noc_annotations created in past 24 hours grouped by correlation_rule_id. For each rule: compute EMA: new_weight = 0.9 * current_weight + 0.1 * mean(feedback_signals). Clamp to [0.1, 1.0]. Update correlation_rules.weight in MongoDB. Log: {rule_id, old_weight, new_weight, annotation_count, delta}.
target_output.data_model: alarm_events (MongoDB): {id UUID, source_nms, vendor, alarm_type, network_element_id, severity, alarm_text, received_at, normalized_at, dedup_hash}. alarm_correlations: {id UUID, root_alarm_id, suppressed_child_alarm_ids: UUID[], correlation_rule_id, topology_context: {cell_id, site_id, parent_bsc_id, parent_msc_id}, enriched_at, itsm_ticket_id, status, created_at}. index: {root_alarm_id: 1}, {itsm_ticket_id: 1}, {status: 1, created_at: -1}. correlation_rules: {id UUID, name, trigger_alarm_type, child_alarm_types: [], time_window_seconds, topology_scope, weight: Float [0.1-1.0] default 0.5, active}. suppression_audit (append-only): {id UUID, suppressed_alarm_id, root_correlation_id, suppression_rule_id, suppression_reason, suppressed_at}. indexes: {suppressed_alarm_id: 1, suppressed_at: -1}, {root_correlation_id: 1, suppressed_at: -1}. noc_annotations: {id UUID, itsm_ticket_id, correlation_id FK, annotator_user_id, feedback_type ENUM confirmed_root_cause/false_correlation/partial_match, annotated_at}.
target_output.api_design: Admin API (FastAPI, Keycloak JWT): GET /api/v1/correlations?status=active&limit=50 returns recent correlations with suppression counts. GET /api/v1/correlations/{id} returns full correlation with topology context and suppressed child alarm list. GET /api/v1/suppression-audit?alarm_id={id} returns suppression records for a specific alarm (uses {suppressed_alarm_id: 1, suppressed_at: -1} index). POST /api/v1/rules (rules-admin role: create/update correlation rule). GET /api/v1/rules/{id}/weight-history returns EMA weight change log. POST /api/v1/annotations/{itsm_ticket_id} (noc-engineer: submit feedback annotation for ITSM ticket).
target_output.security_and_compliance: Keycloak RBAC with JWT validation on all FastAPI routes. Role-based endpoint access: noc-engineer (GET correlations, suppression-audit, POST annotations), rules-admin (POST rules, GET weight-history), operations-manager (read-only dashboards). SNMP v2c community strings in Kubernetes Secrets via External Secrets Operator; rotated quarterly. ServiceNow OAuth2 client credentials in Kubernetes Secrets. suppression_audit MongoDB collection: application service account has insert-only permissions — no update or delete via MongoDB RBAC. Correlation and alarm data references by ID in logs only — no alarm_text or topology details in log lines.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Bare-metal Kubernetes. snmp-trap-receiver: single Pod with hostNetwork=true on SNMP receiver node, UDP port 162 bound to NMS management VLAN NIC. correlation-engine and enrichment-service: 2 replicas each, HPA on Redis queue depth. GitLab CI: pytest for correlation engine (rule evaluation unit tests with fixture alarm sets), mypy, Docker build, Helm upgrade. MongoDB replica set 3 nodes with PVC. Redis Cluster 3 shards. MongoDB indexes applied via application startup migration using motor create_index() — idempotent.
target_output.observability: Prometheus metrics: alarms_received_per_min (alert if <100 during NOC hours — indicates SNMP connectivity issue), correlations_created_per_hour, suppression_rate_pct = suppressed / (suppressed + forwarded), itsm_ticket_creation_failure_count (alert >5/hour), feedback_weight_delta_max (monitor rule weight convergence). Grafana dashboards: Pipeline Health (ingestion rate, correlation rate, ITSM creation rate), Suppression Audit (suppression rate trend, top suppressed alarm types), Rule Weight Tracker (per-rule weight history line chart — shows EMA convergence). Alerts: SNMP trap rate <100/min during business hours (P2 — potential NMS connectivity loss), ITSM creation failures >5/hour (P2), correlation accuracy <70% based on NOC annotations (P3 — investigate rules).
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Bare-metal Kubernetes (existing infrastructure) — no additional compute cost. MongoDB 3-replica set on existing servers. Redis Cluster on existing servers. Software: all open-source. Pipeline scales to 10,000 traps/minute with 4 correlation-engine replicas (HPA on Redis queue depth >500). MongoDB alarm_correlations collection estimated 50K documents/day at 5-minute windows, 90-day retention = ~4.5M documents; well within single replica set capacity.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (complete): Ericsson OSS SNMP trap receiver, alarm normalization, rule-based correlation, topology enrichment, ServiceNow ITSM ticket creation, suppression audit. Phase 2 (Round 2): Multi-vendor NMS adapters (Huawei OMC, Nokia NetAct REST adapters), correlation window optimization, enrichment latency reduction. Phase 3 (Round 3 hardening): EMA feedback weight update algorithm, suppression_audit MongoDB indexes, correlation accuracy Grafana dashboard, feedback annotation API. Phase 4 (future): ML-based correlation using alarm embedding similarity for novel patterns; real-time correlation accuracy dashboard.
target_output.development_guidelines: suppression_audit collection must be insert-only — enforce via MongoDB user with insert permission only on this collection; verify in startup health check. Correlation rule evaluation must produce deterministic results for the same alarm set — no random tiebreaking; sort rules by weight DESC then id ASC for stable ordering. Feedback processor must be idempotent — track processed annotation IDs in a processed_annotations set; do not apply the same annotation twice. EMA weight update must log old and new weight for every rule updated — this is the operational evidence for rule weight changes.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: EMA weight convergence too slow (alpha=0.1) for rapidly evolving network event patterns. Mitigation: admin API allows manual weight override for urgent rule corrections; EMA alpha configurable per rule via correlation_rules.learning_rate field. Risk: SNMP v2c community string interception on management VLAN. Mitigation: SNMP v3 with authPriv is future Phase 4 enhancement; management VLAN isolation is current control. Tradeoff: rule-based correlation over ML for Phase 1 — rule-based provides transparent, auditable suppression decisions acceptable to NOC; ML correlation deferred to Phase 4 after sufficient labeled data from feedback loop accumulates. Tradeoff: MongoDB over PostgreSQL for alarm_correlations — document model handles variable topology_context and suppressed_child_alarm_ids array natively without join tables; accepted higher operational complexity of MongoDB replica set.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: EMA alpha value confirmed at 0.1 — provides slow, stable convergence suitable for operational rule weights that should not oscillate. Suppression audit index confirmed as compound {suppressed_alarm_id, suppressed_at} for NOC alarm-specific lookups. Multi-vendor adapter scope for Phase 3 confirmed: Huawei OMC and Nokia NetAct REST API adapters (not SNMP) added in Phase 2; ZTE NM in Phase 4. ITSM ticket format confirmed: ServiceNow incident with short_description from root_alarm enriched context, description containing topology context JSON, work_notes listing suppressed child alarm count.

metadata.notes: Round 3 RAN alarm correlation engine resolving EMA feedback weight algorithm and MongoDB suppression audit index specification.

profile.domain: On-Device ML Broadband Congestion Prediction Consumer Mobile App

project_goal.value: Consumer mobile app using on-device ML to predict broadband congestion probability in the next 30 minutes and surface proactive recommendations to defer downloads, adjust streaming quality, or switch to mobile data
project_goal.rationale: ISP and end-consumer interest in proactive congestion management; privacy-by-design on-device inference minimizes personal data transmission

target_users.value: Home broadband subscribers on iOS and Android seeking proactive guidance before congestion impacts their experience
target_users.rationale: Mass-market consumer app; users are non-technical and require simple actionable recommendations

project_class.rationale: React Native consumer app with on-device TFLite inference and backend model management pipeline

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, ai_llm, analytics
capabilities.rationale: React Native frontend, Python FastAPI backend, PostgreSQL data, Firebase Auth, on-device TFLite model (ai_llm capability), usage analytics

complexity_level.rationale: On-device ML with A/B model testing, privacy-preserving federated measurement collection, and model lifecycle management

risk_level.rationale: Incorrect predictions inconvenience users but cause no harm; low financial or safety impact

data_sensitivity.rationale: Network usage measurements linked to device identity are personal data under GDPR and CCPA

external_exposure.rationale: Consumer app served via public APIs

access_model.value: Firebase Auth for consumer account creation; anonymous mode available for users who decline data sharing; no PII required beyond device token for model update delivery
access_model.rationale: Privacy-first design; most functionality available without identity; data sharing opt-in

feature_scope.value: On-device 5-minute measurement collection (latency, jitter, throughput), TFLite model inference for 30-minute congestion probability, proactive recommendation cards, anonymized measurement upload for model retraining, A/B testing framework for model challenger vs champion comparison, model OTA update delivery, opt-in data sharing with granular consent, GDPR right-to-erasure, analytics dashboard for model performance monitoring
feature_scope.rationale: Full lifecycle from measurement collection through on-device inference to model improvement loop

mvp_scope.value: On-device measurement collection, TFLite champion model inference, congestion probability display, anonymized measurement upload with opt-in consent
mvp_scope.rationale: Core prediction and measurement loop delivers value before A/B testing complexity

security_baseline.value: Firebase Auth for authenticated endpoints, HTTPS for all API calls, GDPR consent gate before any measurement upload, measurement data anonymized before transmission (device fingerprint replaced with ephemeral session token), right-to-erasure implemented as deletion of all session tokens associated with user account
security_baseline.rationale: GDPR requires explicit consent for measurement data collection; personal data minimization

frontend_stack.value: React Native 0.74 with TypeScript; Expo managed workflow; TFLite React Native bridge for on-device inference; Expo EAS for build and OTA
frontend_stack.rationale: React Native cross-platform; TFLite bridge for on-device ML; Expo EAS for OTA model bundle updates

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI; PostgreSQL 16 for model metadata and consent records; GCS for model artifacts
backend_stack.rationale: FastAPI for measurement ingestion and model serving APIs; PostgreSQL for consent and model version tracking

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 on Cloud Run; Redis 7 for A/B test configuration cache; GCS for TFLite model artifacts; BigQuery for aggregated measurement analytics
data_platform.rationale: PostgreSQL for consent records; BigQuery for large-scale measurement analytics; GCS for model artifact storage

hosting_target.value: GCP Cloud Run for FastAPI; Cloud SQL PostgreSQL; GCS for model artifacts; Firebase for auth and push notifications
hosting_target.rationale: Cloud Run serverless scales to consumer traffic without cluster management; Firebase integrates natively with React Native

privacy_retention_policy.value: Anonymized measurements retained 90 days for model retraining; aggregated analytics in BigQuery retained 2 years; consent records retained 5 years for GDPR compliance evidence; right-to-erasure deletes all measurement records linked to user session tokens
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: GDPR retention minimization; consent records retained for accountability

future_scope.value: ISP partnership API for actual congestion ground-truth labels improving model accuracy; personalized recommendations based on individual usage patterns; widget for iOS/Android home screen
future_scope.rationale: Ground-truth labels from ISP significantly improve prediction accuracy; personalization is Phase 3

constraints.value: On-device inference must complete in <100ms on 3-year-old mid-range devices; TFLite model size <5MB for OTA delivery; GDPR consent required before any measurement upload; app must function fully without data sharing enabled
constraints.rationale: Consumer device performance requirement; OTA bundle size limit; GDPR law

observability_baseline.value: Firebase Crashlytics for mobile crash reporting; Grafana + Prometheus on Cloud Run for backend metrics; BigQuery for model performance analytics
observability_baseline.rationale: Firebase-native crash reporting; Cloud Run metrics for backend; BigQuery for ML metrics

execution_preference.value: Expo EAS Build and Submit; GitHub Actions CI for backend; Cloud Run deployment via GitHub Actions
execution_preference.rationale: EAS managed build pipeline; standard GitHub Actions for backend

compliance_context.value: GDPR Articles 5, 6, 7, 17 apply to measurement data collection; CCPA for California users; privacy-by-design via on-device inference minimizes transmitted personal data; consent records document legal basis for processing
compliance_context.rationale: Personal network usage data under GDPR; on-device inference reduces data minimization obligation

llm_integration.value: TFLite classification model (not LLM) for on-device congestion probability prediction; model trained offline on anonymized measurement datasets; OTA model update delivery via GCS pre-signed URLs
llm_integration.rationale: On-device TFLite is the ai_llm capability — edge ML model, not a generative LLM

project.goal: Consumer mobile app with on-device TFLite congestion prediction and backend model management pipeline
project.timeline: MVP Q2; A/B testing Q3; ISP ground-truth integration Q4

frontend.ml: TFLite React Native bridge for on-device inference

backend.storage: GCS for model artifacts; BigQuery for measurement analytics

reasoner_reviews.product: The anonymous mode is critical for GDPR compliance and user trust — the core prediction functionality must work without any data upload. Ensure the recommendation cards are actionable and specific, not generic. 'Your connection may be congested in 20 minutes — consider pausing your large download' is better than 'Congestion predicted'.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: TFLite model input features should include: latency_p50_ms, latency_p95_ms, jitter_ms, throughput_down_mbps, time_of_day_sin, time_of_day_cos, day_of_week, rolling_24h_congestion_count. Cyclic encoding of time features prevents discontinuity at midnight. Model output: congestion_probability_float, confidence_score_float. A/B testing framework should use a deterministic per-device cohort assignment (hash(device_token) % 100) to avoid reassigning users between model versions on every launch.
reasoner_reviews.security: Ephemeral session token for measurement anonymization must be rotated on each app launch — never persist beyond a single session. GDPR right-to-erasure endpoint must delete all measurement_records keyed by any session token ever associated with the user's account, not just current session.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: TFLite model <5MB requires quantized model (INT8 or float16) — full FP32 model may exceed limit. Target model size 2-3MB post-quantization. Inference <100ms on mid-range device requires benchmark on Pixel 5 equivalent.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Rounds 1-3 progressively implemented on-device inference, consent management, and A/B testing framework. Round 4 focus: ensure right-to-erasure implementation is complete (all session tokens, not just current) and A/B cohort assignment is deterministic and stable across app restarts.

frontend.app_structure: React Native Expo managed. Navigation: React Navigation bottom tabs (Home with congestion card, History, Settings with consent management). Zustand stores: predictionStore (current congestion probability, recommendation, last_inference_at), consentStore (data_sharing_enabled, session_token), modelStore (active model version, inference latency). Background task: Expo Task Manager 5-minute measurement collection job.
frontend.pages_and_flows: HomeScreen: congestion probability gauge (0-100%), recommendation card with specific action, last updated timestamp. HistoryScreen: 24-hour congestion prediction accuracy chart (if consent enabled). SettingsScreen: data sharing toggle with GDPR consent explanation, account management, erasure request button.
frontend.state_management: Zustand for prediction, consent, model state. React Query for remote calls to backend APIs. TFLite React Native bridge (react-native-fast-tflite) for model inference.
frontend.frontend_risks: TFLite React Native bridge cold start latency on first inference. Mitigation: pre-warm model on app foreground event.

backend.service_design: FastAPI with modules: measurement_ingestion (receive anonymized measurement batches from consented devices), model_manager (model version CRUD, A/B test config, GCS pre-signed URL generation), erasure_handler (GDPR right-to-erasure endpoint), consent_manager (record consent decisions)
backend.api_patterns: RESTful: POST /v1/measurements (batch upload, requires data_sharing_enabled consent), GET /v1/models/active (returns active model version and GCS download URL for OTA), GET /v1/ab-test/assignment?device_token_hash={hash} (returns champion or challenger model assignment), POST /v1/consent (record consent decision), DELETE /v1/users/{user_id}/data (GDPR erasure)
backend.failure_handling: Measurement ingestion idempotent via session_token + window_start deduplication; model download URL expires after 24h; Cloud Run scales to zero during off-peak hours

data.entities: consent_records (id UUID, user_id Firebase UID, data_sharing_enabled BOOL, consent_given_at, consent_version VARCHAR, gdpr_lawful_basis ENUM explicit_consent), session_tokens (id UUID, user_id, token VARCHAR, created_at, active BOOL — rotated per app launch), measurement_records (id UUID, session_token FK, window_start TIMESTAMP, latency_p50_ms FLOAT, latency_p95_ms FLOAT, jitter_ms FLOAT, throughput_down_mbps FLOAT, congestion_probability_predicted FLOAT, created_at), model_versions (id UUID, model_name, version SEMVER, gcs_uri, tflite_size_kb INT, ab_test_cohort ENUM champion/challenger, validation_accuracy FLOAT, deployed_at), ab_test_assignments (device_token_hash VARCHAR PK, assigned_model_version_id FK, assigned_at)
data.retention_and_deletion: measurement_records: DELETE WHERE created_at < NOW() - INTERVAL 90 DAY (scheduled nightly). GDPR erasure: DELETE FROM session_tokens WHERE user_id = {user_id}; DELETE FROM measurement_records WHERE session_token IN (SELECT token FROM deleted session_tokens). consent_records: retained 5 years (GDPR accountability).

devops.deployment_topology: Cloud Run services: api (FastAPI, min-instances=1 for latency), PostgreSQL Cloud SQL us-central1, GCS bucket (model artifacts, public read on /models/ path), BigQuery dataset (measurement analytics). Expo EAS Build + Submit for iOS and Android.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: pytest (unit tests, Testcontainers PostgreSQL), Docker build, Cloud Run deploy. Expo EAS on main branch merge. Model deployment: upload quantized .tflite to GCS, update model_versions record, BigQuery validation query must show validation_accuracy >= champion before challenger promotion.
devops.observability_stack: Firebase Crashlytics (crash-free rate, inference latency P90). Grafana on Cloud Run: measurement_ingestion_rate, model_download_requests. BigQuery ML metrics: prediction accuracy vs actual congestion events (when ISP ground-truth available in future).

ISS-001.title: GDPR right-to-erasure deletes only current session token, not all historical tokens
ISS-001.detail: Round 3 erasure implementation deleted measurement_records for current session_token only; GDPR Article 17 requires deletion of all personal data including measurements linked to all past session tokens for the user.

history.note: No erasure implementation
Erasure added but only current session
Still deletes only current session token

ISS-002.title: A/B cohort assignment re-randomizes on each app launch
ISS-002.detail: Round 3 A/B cohort computed hash(device_token) % 100 on each launch without persisting the assignment, causing users to switch between champion and challenger models across sessions, invalidating A/B test statistical validity.

history.note: No A/B testing
A/B added but no assignment persistence
Assignment computed fresh each launch — not stable

focus_issues.title: GDPR erasure incomplete — only current session token deleted
focus_issues.detail: All historical session tokens for user must be deleted to satisfy GDPR Article 17.

focus_issues.title: A/B cohort assignment not stable across app restarts
focus_issues.detail: Assignment must be persisted to ab_test_assignments table keyed by device_token_hash for cross-launch stability.

latest_recommendations: Erasure endpoint must collect ALL session_token values ever linked to user_id from session_tokens table (including active=false historical tokens), then DELETE measurement_records WHERE session_token IN (collected tokens), then DELETE session_tokens WHERE user_id = {user_id}

A/B assignment: on first launch, compute cohort = hash(device_token_hash) % 100, persist to ab_test_assignments table; on subsequent launches, GET /v1/ab-test/assignment returns persisted assignment without recomputation

convergence_state.reason: GDPR erasure incompleteness and A/B stability gap unresolved after Round 3

previous_audits.summary: Strong on-device ML architecture with TFLite and Expo EAS OTA; GDPR erasure implementation is legally insufficient covering only current session, and A/B assignment instability invalidates test statistics.
strengths: On-device TFLite inference correctly preserves privacy-by-design
Ephemeral session token rotation per launch correctly limits linkability
Consent gate before measurement upload correctly implements GDPR lawful basis
concerns: Erasure deletes current session only — historical session tokens and linked measurements remain, violating GDPR Article 17
A/B test model assignment recomputed each launch — users switch models, invalidating A/B statistical analysis
blocking_issues: ISS-001: GDPR Article 17 violation — erasure request does not delete all personal data
recommendations: Erasure endpoint: SELECT token FROM session_tokens WHERE user_id={uid}, then batch DELETE from measurement_records using collected tokens, then DELETE session_tokens WHERE user_id={uid}
Persist A/B assignment to PostgreSQL ab_test_assignments on first launch; subsequent launches query persisted assignment — never recompute

issue_updates.title: GDPR erasure incomplete
issue_updates.detail: measurement_records for historical sessions not deleted

issue_updates.title: A/B assignment unstable
issue_updates.detail: Assignment recomputed each launch invalidates experiment

previous_plan.title: Broadband Congestion Prediction App — Round 3
previous_plan.executive_summary: React Native Expo app with on-device TFLite congestion prediction, anonymized measurement upload, A/B model testing, and Cloud Run FastAPI backend. Firebase Auth, GDPR consent management, partial erasure (current session only — needs fix).
previous_plan.architecture_overview: React Native TFLite inference. 5-minute Expo background measurement collection. FastAPI measurement ingestion and model management. PostgreSQL for consent and model versions. GCS for TFLite model artifacts.
previous_plan.data_model: consent_records, session_tokens, measurement_records, model_versions, ab_test_assignments in PostgreSQL.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: Firebase Auth, GDPR consent gate, ephemeral session token per launch, partial erasure.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 4 resolves two final issues: GDPR erasure now collects all historical session_tokens for the user (including active=false), bulk-deletes measurement_records, then deletes session_tokens in a PostgreSQL transaction — satisfying GDPR Article 17 completely. A/B test assignment is now persisted on first launch to ab_test_assignments and returned on subsequent launches without recomputation — ensuring each device stays in the same model cohort for the experiment duration. Both fixes are self-consistent with existing data model and API design.

fix_report.action_taken: GDPR erasure endpoint DELETE /v1/users/{user_id}/data now executes within a single PostgreSQL transaction: (1) SELECT token FROM session_tokens WHERE user_id = {user_id} (includes active=false historical tokens), (2) DELETE FROM measurement_records WHERE session_token = ANY(collected_tokens), (3) DELETE FROM session_tokens WHERE user_id = {user_id}, (4) UPDATE consent_records SET erased_at = NOW() WHERE user_id = {user_id}. Transaction commits atomically — no partial erasure. Erasure confirmation logged with count of session_tokens deleted and measurement_records deleted.
fix_report.expected_outcome: GDPR Article 17 satisfied — all measurement data linked to user across all sessions deleted in atomic transaction; consent record erasure timestamp preserved as GDPR accountability evidence

fix_report.action_taken: A/B assignment persistence: on first app launch, React Native calls GET /v1/ab-test/assignment?device_token_hash={hash}. Backend checks ab_test_assignments WHERE device_token_hash = {hash}. If no record: computes cohort = hash(device_token_hash_bytes) % 100, inserts ab_test_assignments record with assigned_model_version_id, returns assignment. If record exists: returns existing assignment without recomputation. Assignment persists until current A/B test experiment ends and ab_test_assignments table is cleared for next experiment.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Each device maintains stable model cohort assignment for the full A/B test duration; cross-session model switches eliminated; A/B statistical analysis validity restored

target_output.title: Broadband Congestion Prediction App v1.0 — GDPR Erasure Completeness and A/B Assignment Stability
target_output.executive_summary: Consumer React Native app using on-device TFLite inference to predict home broadband congestion probability 30 minutes in advance, surfacing specific deferral and quality adjustment recommendations. Python FastAPI backend on GCP Cloud Run manages model OTA delivery, A/B testing with stable per-device cohort assignments, and fully GDPR-compliant right-to-erasure deleting all historical session-linked measurements in a single atomic PostgreSQL transaction. Ephemeral session tokens rotate per launch for anonymization; data sharing requires explicit GDPR consent.
target_output.architecture_overview: React Native Expo app collects network performance measurements every 5 minutes via Expo background task, runs on-device TFLite inference (INT8 quantized, <3MB) to compute congestion probability and recommendation, and displays result to user. With user consent, anonymized measurements are batched and uploaded to FastAPI backend with ephemeral session token (rotated each launch, not linked to Firebase UID on backend). Backend stores measurements keyed by session token in PostgreSQL for model retraining. Model OTA delivery uses GCS pre-signed URLs; TFLite model bundle updates delivered via Expo EAS Update without app store review. A/B test cohort assignments persisted per device_token_hash to ensure stable model assignment across launches. GDPR erasure atomically deletes all session tokens and linked measurements for a user.
target_output.technology_stack: Mobile: React Native 0.74, Expo SDK 51, TypeScript, react-native-fast-tflite (TFLite bridge), Expo Task Manager (background measurement collection), Expo EAS Build + EAS Update, Firebase Auth, Firebase Crashlytics. Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI 0.111, Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16, GCS (model artifacts), BigQuery (measurement analytics). A/B Testing: PostgreSQL ab_test_assignments for stable per-device cohort. Model: TFLite INT8 quantized classification model <3MB. CI/CD: GitHub Actions, Expo EAS, Cloud Run deploy.
target_output.functional_feature_map: On-Device Measurement Collection: Expo background task every 5 minutes collects latency P50/P95, jitter, throughput downstream via native network speed test. TFLite Inference: react-native-fast-tflite loads INT8 quantized model from Expo FileSystem; input vector [latency_p50, latency_p95, jitter, throughput, time_sin, time_cos, day_of_week, rolling_24h_count]; output [congestion_probability, confidence]. Recommendation Engine: rules applied to congestion_probability: >0.7 = high-urgency card, 0.4-0.7 = advisory card, <0.4 = clear. Measurement Upload: if consent_enabled, batch last 6 measurements (30 minutes) with session_token and upload to POST /v1/measurements. Model OTA: background check GET /v1/models/active; if version > local, download from GCS pre-signed URL via Expo FileSystem, validate SHA256, swap active model. A/B Assignment: GET /v1/ab-test/assignment on first launch, persisted in ab_test_assignments; subsequent launches return cached Zustand value. GDPR: consent toggle in Settings; erasure request calls DELETE /v1/users/{uid}/data.
target_output.system_components: React Native app (Expo background measurement task, TFLite inference engine, Firebase Auth, Expo EAS OTA), FastAPI Cloud Run (min-instances=1, measurement ingestion, model management, A/B assignment, consent, erasure), Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 (consent_records, session_tokens, measurement_records, model_versions, ab_test_assignments), GCS model-artifacts bucket (TFLite .tflite files, public-read on /models/ prefix), BigQuery measurement analytics dataset, Firebase Auth + Crashlytics, Cloud Run HTTPS ingress.
target_output.workflows: GDPR Erasure Workflow: User taps 'Delete all my data' in Settings. React Native calls DELETE /v1/users/{firebase_uid}/data with Firebase ID token. FastAPI erasure_handler: BEGIN TRANSACTION; SELECT array_agg(token) FROM session_tokens WHERE user_id = {firebase_uid} (collects all tokens including active=false historical); DELETE FROM measurement_records WHERE session_token = ANY(token_array); DELETE FROM session_tokens WHERE user_id = {firebase_uid}; UPDATE consent_records SET erased_at = NOW(), data_erased = true WHERE user_id = {firebase_uid}; COMMIT. Return {deleted_sessions, deleted_measurements} counts. If transaction fails: ROLLBACK, return 500. A/B Assignment Workflow: App launches. Zustand checks if ab_model_assignment in persistent store. If not: call GET /v1/ab-test/assignment?device_token_hash={sha256(device_id)}. Backend: SELECT assigned_model_version_id FROM ab_test_assignments WHERE device_token_hash = {hash}. If exists: return cached assignment. If not: cohort = int.from_bytes(sha256_bytes[:2], 'big') % 100; if cohort < 10: challenger else champion; INSERT ab_test_assignments (device_token_hash, assigned_model_version_id, assigned_at); return assignment. App saves to Zustand persistent store. On subsequent launches: use stored Zustand value without API call.
target_output.data_model: consent_records (id UUID, user_id VARCHAR, data_sharing_enabled BOOL, consent_given_at, consent_version, gdpr_lawful_basis, erased_at TIMESTAMP, data_erased BOOL DEFAULT false). session_tokens (id UUID, user_id VARCHAR, token VARCHAR UNIQUE, created_at, active BOOL DEFAULT true — set false on next launch rotation). measurement_records (id UUID, session_token VARCHAR FK session_tokens.token, window_start TIMESTAMP, latency_p50_ms FLOAT, latency_p95_ms FLOAT, jitter_ms FLOAT, throughput_down_mbps FLOAT, congestion_probability_predicted FLOAT, created_at). model_versions (id UUID, model_name, version SEMVER, gcs_uri, tflite_size_kb INT, quantization_type ENUM fp32/fp16/int8, ab_test_cohort ENUM champion/challenger, validation_accuracy FLOAT, deployed_at). ab_test_assignments (device_token_hash VARCHAR PK, assigned_model_version_id UUID FK model_versions.id, cohort_percentile INT, assigned_at). Indexes: session_tokens(user_id), measurement_records(session_token, created_at), ab_test_assignments is primary key indexed.
target_output.api_design: POST /v1/measurements body {session_token, window_start, measurements[{latency_p50_ms, latency_p95_ms, jitter_ms, throughput_down_mbps, congestion_probability_predicted}]} requires consent header. GET /v1/models/active returns {version, gcs_download_url (24h pre-signed), sha256, tflite_size_kb}. GET /v1/ab-test/assignment?device_token_hash={hash} returns {model_version, cohort ENUM champion/challenger, persisted BOOL}. POST /v1/consent body {firebase_id_token, data_sharing_enabled, consent_version} returns {consent_id}. DELETE /v1/users/{user_id}/data requires Firebase ID token, returns {deleted_sessions, deleted_measurements, erased_at}.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Firebase ID token validation on all authenticated endpoints via Firebase Admin SDK. Measurement ingestion: session_token is ephemeral per-launch UUID (not linked to Firebase UID in backend storage — only user_id stored in session_tokens for erasure linking); backend cannot reconstruct identity from measurement_records alone. GDPR Article 17 erasure: atomic PostgreSQL transaction deletes all session_tokens and measurement_records for user_id; consent_records erased_at timestamp preserved as GDPR accountability evidence per Article 5(2). GDPR Article 7 consent: explicit opt-in with versioned consent text; withdrawal via Settings toggle calls DELETE /v1/users/{uid}/data then POST /v1/consent with data_sharing_enabled=false. CCPA: right-to-opt-out via same consent toggle. A/B test device_token_hash is SHA256 of Expo device ID — non-reversible hash; not stored with user identity.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Cloud Run api service: min-instances=1 (avoids cold start for measurement uploads), max-instances=10, CPU=1, memory=512MB. Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 db-f1-micro for MVP; upgrade to db-g1-small at 10K MAU. GCS model-artifacts bucket: versioned storage, public-read on /models/{version}/ path for TFLite download. GitHub Actions: pytest + Testcontainers PostgreSQL, Docker build, Cloud Run deploy. Expo EAS: production build on main tag; EAS Update for JS bundle OTA (model bundle delivered via GCS URL not EAS Update — model is a binary file, not JS). Scheduled nightly Cloud Run Job: DELETE measurement_records WHERE created_at < NOW() - INTERVAL 90 DAYS; rotate expired sessions.
target_output.observability: Firebase Crashlytics: crash-free session rate (alert <99%), TFLite inference latency P90 (custom event, alert >100ms). Cloud Run metrics: measurement_ingestion_rate_per_min, model_download_requests_per_hour, erasure_requests_count. BigQuery analytics: daily prediction accuracy (congestion_probability vs actual reported congestion events from opt-in users), A/B champion vs challenger accuracy comparison (after 10K assignments per cohort). Grafana dashboard: A/B Test Progress (assignments per cohort, prediction accuracy by cohort), Consent Funnel (consent rate, erasure rate).
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Cloud Run api ~$30/month (min 1 instance + measurement upload traffic at 100K DAU). Cloud SQL db-f1-micro ~$10/month MVP. GCS model storage <$5/month. BigQuery ingestion ~$10/month at 100K DAU sending 12 measurements/day. Firebase Auth free tier at 100K MAU. Total ~$55/month at MVP scale. At 1M MAU: Cloud Run scales to 5-10 instances ($150/month), Cloud SQL upgrades to db-g1-small ($50/month), BigQuery ~$100/month. Total ~$300/month at 1M MAU.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (complete): on-device TFLite inference, measurement collection, consent management, backend measurement ingestion, model OTA. Phase 2 (Rounds 2-3): A/B testing framework, Firebase Crashlytics, erasure implementation. Phase 3 (Round 4 — current): GDPR erasure completeness (all historical sessions), A/B assignment persistence and stability. Phase 4 (future): ISP partnership API for ground-truth congestion labels, personalized recommendations, iOS/Android home screen widget.
target_output.development_guidelines: All PostgreSQL operations touching user personal data (erasure, consent) must use explicit transactions — never rely on auto-commit for multi-table personal data operations. TFLite model updates must validate SHA256 before swapping active model to prevent corrupted model deployment. A/B experiment end must include a cleanup step: bulk-update ab_test_assignments champion column to promote winning model, clear table for next experiment. Never log session_token values — use session_token_hash for log correlation.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: TFLite model accuracy degrades on iOS due to CoreML vs TFLite runtime differences on Apple Silicon. Mitigation: run inference benchmarks on both platforms in CI using iOS Simulator; react-native-fast-tflite uses platform-native delegate automatically. Risk: Cloud Run cold start (>1s) degrades measurement upload latency for early morning first-request spike. Mitigation: min-instances=1 prevents cold start for authenticated measurement upload endpoint. Tradeoff: ephemeral session token per launch over persistent device ID — stronger privacy protection at cost of less precise per-device measurement attribution; accepted for GDPR compliance. Tradeoff: TFLite over Core ML / ONNX — cross-platform single model format simplifies ML pipeline; Core ML would provide better iOS performance but requires separate model training and distribution.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: GDPR erasure scope confirmed: all session_tokens (active=true and active=false) and all linked measurement_records deleted atomically; consent_records erased_at set but record retained for GDPR accountability. A/B assignment stable across launches confirmed: persisted to ab_test_assignments; Zustand persistent store caches assignment on device to avoid API calls on subsequent launches. Model OTA delivery confirmed: GCS public-read pre-signed URL (24h TTL) for TFLite binary download — not delivered via EAS Update which handles JS bundles only. Erasure confirmation response confirmed: {deleted_sessions: N, deleted_measurements: M, erased_at: timestamp} for user-visible confirmation.

metadata.notes: Round 4 congestion prediction app — GDPR erasure completeness and A/B assignment persistence both resolved; compliance_context and llm_integration conditional fields present.

profile.domain: Quantum Key Distribution Network Simulation Research Prototype

project_goal.value: Research prototype simulating QKD networks using BB84 protocol over modeled fiber spans, allowing researchers to study secure key generation rates, QBER under channel noise, and eavesdropping detection probability as a function of channel conditions
project_goal.rationale: Physical QKD testbeds are expensive and unavailable to most research groups; simulation platform enables reproducible protocol experiments at low cost

target_users.value: Quantum network researchers and graduate students running BB84 simulation experiments to study channel noise impacts and eavesdropping detection
target_users.rationale: Academic research group; users are expert operators of quantum network simulation tools

project_class.rationale: Experimental research platform for quantum protocol simulation; not production network software

capabilities.rationale: Python simulation backend, InfluxDB time-series storage, React analytics dashboard

complexity_level.rationale: BB84 protocol simulation with photon transmission modeling, basis reconciliation, privacy amplification, eavesdropping injection, and QBER computation

risk_level.rationale: Research prototype; simulation failures affect only experiments, not production systems

data_sensitivity.rationale: Research simulation results are internal lab data; not personal or regulated

external_exposure.rationale: Research dashboard accessible to authenticated lab members; not publicly accessible

access_model.value: Custom JWT for research dashboard API; no self-service registration; lab administrator provisions accounts
access_model.rationale: Small authenticated research team; controlled access to simulation results

feature_scope.value: QKD network topology configuration with fiber span lengths and noise profiles, BB84 protocol simulation with photon transmission, basis reconciliation, error correction, privacy amplification, secure key generation rate computation, eavesdropping injection with QBER impact modeling, simulation result storage in InfluxDB, research dashboard with key rate vs QBER charts, experiment reproducibility via stored configuration, channel condition parameter sweeps
feature_scope.rationale: Complete BB84 simulation workflow from topology configuration through key generation to results analysis

mvp_scope.value: Single fiber span BB84 simulation, QBER computation, key generation rate output, InfluxDB storage, Grafana dashboard for results
mvp_scope.rationale: Single-span simulation validates all core BB84 mechanics before multi-node topology support

security_baseline.value: Custom JWT for dashboard API access, TLS 1.3 for HTTPS, no credentials in Git repository, basic auth for Grafana
security_baseline.rationale: Research prototype security baseline; not hardened for production

frontend_stack.value: React 18 with TypeScript; Chart.js for result visualization
frontend_stack.rationale: React 18 with Chart.js for interactive QBER and key rate visualization

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI; numpy for photon simulation; scipy for statistical analysis
backend_stack.rationale: Python scientific computing ecosystem for quantum protocol simulation; numpy for photon state vectors and basis matrices

data_platform.value: InfluxDB 2.7 for simulation time-series results; PostgreSQL 16 for experiment metadata
data_platform.rationale: InfluxDB for QBER and key rate time-series across parameter sweeps; PostgreSQL for experiment configuration records

hosting_target.value: Bare-metal Kubernetes on university lab infrastructure
hosting_target.rationale: Research lab owns bare-metal servers; no cloud cost

privacy_retention_policy.value: Experiment results retained indefinitely for paper reproducibility; raw simulation run logs retained 30 days
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Research reproducibility requires permanent experiment result retention

future_scope.value: Multi-node QKD network topology with trusted relay nodes; decoy state BB84 protocol variant; comparison with E91 entanglement-based protocol
future_scope.rationale: Multi-node and E91 are publishable extensions for follow-on papers

constraints.value: BB84 simulation must model polarization-based photon transmission with depolarization noise model; QBER computation must be numerically accurate to 0.1%; simulation must be deterministic for reproducibility given the same random seed
constraints.rationale: Scientific reproducibility requires deterministic simulation; QBER accuracy critical for meaningful eavesdropping detection analysis

observability_baseline.value: Grafana + Prometheus for prototype health; InfluxDB for experiment results; structured logging
observability_baseline.rationale: University lab standard monitoring stack

execution_preference.value: GitHub Actions CI; bare-metal Kubernetes; iterative experiment-driven development
execution_preference.rationale: Research context with agile experiment-driven iteration

project.goal: BB84 QKD protocol simulation with QBER analysis, eavesdropping injection, and key rate computation on bare-metal Kubernetes
project.target_users: Quantum network researchers and graduate students
project.timeline: Single-span MVP in 6 weeks; multi-node Phase 2

backend.framework: FastAPI; numpy for photon simulation; scipy for statistics

reasoner_reviews.product: The parameter sweep capability is essential for paper results — researchers need to run BB84 across a range of fiber span lengths and noise levels and compare key rates systematically. Ensure experiment configuration is fully serializable for reproducibility.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: BB84 simulation accuracy requires correct photon basis selection probabilities (50/50 for standard BB84), depolarization noise model (channel error rate e_c = QBER for simple depolarization), sifting ratio (50% of bits discarded on basis mismatch), cascade error correction model, and privacy amplification reducing key length by h(QBER) per qubit. QBER threshold for eavesdropping detection is typically 11% for intercept-resend attacks. NumPy random generator seeded with experiment seed for deterministic results.
reasoner_reviews.security: JWT tokens for dashboard access. Simulation runs execute in-process — no sandboxing needed for a research prototype. Experiment configuration JSON should be stored as-is in PostgreSQL for exact replay.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Deterministic simulation requires numpy.random.default_rng(seed) not the global random state — global state is not reproducible across Python versions. QBER computation: QBER = errors / sifted_bits where errors counted from basis-matched positions with wrong polarization outcome.

backend.service_design: FastAPI application with simulation_engine module (BB84 protocol steps as pure Python functions with numpy), experiment_api (CRUD for experiments, run simulation job, retrieve results), parameter_sweep_runner (Celery task for async sweep jobs across noise/span ranges)
backend.api_patterns: RESTful: POST /experiments (register config), POST /experiments/{id}/run (trigger simulation), GET /experiments/{id}/results (QBER, key_rate, sifted_key_length), POST /experiments/{id}/inject-eavesdrop (configure intercept-resend attack fraction), GET /experiments/{id}/sweep-results (parameter sweep table).
backend.failure_handling: Simulation engine exceptions caught at API layer; partial sweep results stored on failure with incomplete flag; experiment config always persisted before run to enable replay

data.entities: experiments (id UUID, name, fiber_span_km FLOAT, attenuation_db_per_km FLOAT, noise_type ENUM depolarization/phase_flip, noise_probability FLOAT, eavesdrop_fraction FLOAT DEFAULT 0, random_seed INT, status ENUM configured/running/completed/failed, created_at). simulation_runs (id UUID, experiment_id FK, run_number INT, total_photons INT, sifted_key_bits INT, qber FLOAT, raw_key_rate_bps FLOAT, secure_key_rate_bps FLOAT, privacy_amp_ratio FLOAT, ran_at). sweep_configs (id UUID, base_experiment_id FK, sweep_param ENUM span_km/noise_probability/eavesdrop_fraction, sweep_values FLOAT[], status). InfluxDB: measurement bb84_results, tags={experiment_id, run_id, noise_type}, fields={qber, raw_key_rate, secure_key_rate, sifted_bits, eavesdrop_detected BOOL}.
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL for experiment config and run metadata; InfluxDB for per-run time-series (enables Grafana charting over sweep parameter dimension).

devops.deployment_topology: Bare-metal Kubernetes: simulation-api Deployment 2 replicas, celery-worker Deployment 1 replica, InfluxDB StatefulSet, PostgreSQL StatefulSet, Grafana + Prometheus.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: pytest for BB84 simulation unit tests (deterministic results with fixed seed, QBER accuracy tests), mypy --strict, Docker build, Helm upgrade.
devops.observability_stack: Grafana dashboard: BB84 Key Rate vs QBER scatter plot, QBER vs Fiber Span line chart, eavesdropping detection rate vs fraction. Prometheus: simulation_run_duration_seconds, api_request_rate.

target_output.thinking_summary: QKD simulation research prototype using Python numpy for BB84 protocol modeling.
