Key decisions: numpy.random.default_rng(seed) for deterministic reproducible simulations, depolarization noise model with QBER = errors/sifted_bits, eavesdropping modeled as intercept-resend adding QBER = eavesdrop_fraction/4, privacy amplification reducing key by h_binary(QBER) fraction. Weak quality plan — several sections at minimum detail to demonstrate quality distribution. research_prototype class with no FrontendAgent in active_specialists per manifest (spec says frontend listed but no frontend specialist in active_specialists for this row per manifest entry which lists backend/security/data/devops/frontend — however capabilities only include backend/data/analytics, so frontend specialist is not expected).
target_output.title: BB84 QKD Network Simulation Prototype
target_output.executive_summary: Python research prototype simulating BB84 quantum key distribution over configurable fiber spans with depolarization noise modeling, eavesdropping injection via intercept-resend attack, and QBER analysis. Stores simulation results in InfluxDB for Grafana visualization and React dashboard charts. FastAPI backend on bare-metal Kubernetes enables experiment configuration, deterministic replay, and parameter sweep automation.
target_output.architecture_overview: FastAPI application on bare-metal Kubernetes hosts the BB84 simulation engine as a Python module using numpy for photon state computation. Researchers configure experiments via REST API specifying fiber span, noise probability, eavesdropping fraction, and random seed. Celery workers execute parameter sweep jobs asynchronously. Results stored in InfluxDB for time-series analysis and PostgreSQL for experiment metadata. React 18 dashboard queries the FastAPI results API to render QBER and key rate charts.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, numpy 1.26 for photon simulation, scipy for statistical analysis, Celery for sweep jobs. Storage: InfluxDB 2.7, PostgreSQL 16. Frontend: React 18, TypeScript, Chart.js. Infrastructure: bare-metal Kubernetes, Helm 3. CI/CD: GitHub Actions. mpmath for arbitrary-precision floating-point arithmetic in QBER calculations where IEEE 754 rounding introduces unacceptable error at low eavesdrop fractions. Celery Redis broker for sweep job queue. pytest-benchmark for BB84 simulation throughput regression tracking.
target_output.functional_feature_map: BB84 Simulation: photon transmission over fiber span with attenuation-based photon loss probability, depolarization noise model, basis reconciliation sifting, QBER computation, cascade error correction model, privacy amplification with binary entropy function. Eavesdropping Injection: intercept-resend attack model adding QBER contribution = eavesdrop_fraction / 4. Parameter Sweep: Celery task iterating over span_km or noise_probability or eavesdrop_fraction arrays and running simulation at each value.
target_output.system_components: simulation-api Deployment (FastAPI, BB84 engine, 2 replicas), celery-worker Deployment (sweep jobs), InfluxDB StatefulSet, PostgreSQL StatefulSet, Grafana, Prometheus, React dashboard Nginx Pod. BB84 photon-state engine module (numpy-based, deterministic with seed for reproducibility) isolated as a pure Python subpackage so it can be imported by Jupyter notebooks independently of the FastAPI server. Parameter sweep orchestrator tracks job state in PostgreSQL and dispatches Celery tasks per sweep step.
target_output.workflows: BB84 Experiment Workflow: Researcher calls POST /experiments with fiber_span_km=100, attenuation_db_per_km=0.2, noise_probability=0.01, eavesdrop_fraction=0.05, random_seed=42. API creates experiment record. Researcher calls POST /experiments/{id}/run. Simulation engine: rng = numpy.random.default_rng(42). Generates N photon polarization states (0/90/45/135 degrees). Alice selects random bases (rectilinear/diagonal). Bob selects random bases. Photon transmitted through fiber: survival probability = 10^(-attenuation*span/10). Depolarization noise flips polarization with probability noise_probability. Eavesdrop intercept-resend adds QBER contribution = eavesdrop_fraction/4. Basis reconciliation: keep photons where Alice and Bob bases match. QBER = errors/sifted_bits. Privacy amplification: secure_key_rate = raw_key_rate * (1 - 2*h_binary(QBER)). Results written to InfluxDB and simulation_runs. Parameter Sweep: POST /experiments/{id}/sweep with sweep_param=noise_probability, sweep_values=[0.0, 0.01, 0.02, 0.05, 0.1]. Celery task iterates, runs simulation for each value, stores all results.
target_output.data_model: experiments (id UUID, name, fiber_span_km, attenuation_db_per_km, noise_probability, eavesdrop_fraction, random_seed, status, created_at). simulation_runs (id UUID, experiment_id FK, total_photons, sifted_key_bits, qber, raw_key_rate_bps, secure_key_rate_bps, privacy_amp_ratio, ran_at). sweep_configs (id UUID, base_experiment_id FK, sweep_param, sweep_values FLOAT[], status). InfluxDB: measurement bb84_results, fields qber/raw_key_rate/secure_key_rate/sifted_bits/eavesdrop_detected. InfluxDB tag set includes experiment_id and sweep_step for multi-series charting of parameter sweeps. PostgreSQL sweep_configs JSONB column stores arbitrary sweep parameter grids for future extension beyond fiber_span and noise_probability.
target_output.api_design: POST /experiments (create config), POST /experiments/{id}/run (trigger), GET /experiments/{id}/results (run summary), POST /experiments/{id}/inject-eavesdrop {fraction} (update config), POST /experiments/{id}/sweep {sweep_param, sweep_values} (async Celery job), GET /experiments/{id}/sweep-results (sweep table). JWT required on all endpoints.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Custom JWT HS256 tokens. TLS 1.3 on FastAPI via HTTPS ingress. No credentials in Git. Grafana basic auth. Experiment configuration JSON stored verbatim in PostgreSQL for exact reproduction. No external network access from simulation engine.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Bare-metal Kubernetes 1.29. simulation-api 2 replicas. celery-worker 1 replica. InfluxDB StatefulSet PVC 50GB. PostgreSQL StatefulSet PVC 20GB. GitHub Actions: pytest (BB84 determinism test with fixed seed, QBER accuracy test), Docker build, Helm upgrade. Helm chart values.yaml exposes BB84_PHOTONS_PER_RUN and CELERY_CONCURRENCY as configurable overrides for varying server hardware. PersistentVolumeClaims use local-path StorageClass on bare-metal to avoid NFS latency during InfluxDB write bursts. GitHub Actions matrix tests Python 3.11 and 3.12 to catch numpy ABI breaks on dependency updates.
target_output.observability: Grafana: QBER vs fiber span line chart, key rate vs QBER scatter. Prometheus: simulation_run_duration_seconds. InfluxDB retention: experiment results indefinite. Structured logging from FastAPI. Prometheus simulation_queue_depth gauge tracks pending Celery sweep tasks to surface backlog during large parameter grid runs. FastAPI /metrics endpoint exposes histogram buckets for BB84 photon simulation duration segmented by fiber_span_km quantile.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero cloud cost. Existing bare-metal hardware. InfluxDB storage grows at ~1MB per 1000 simulation runs; manageable on 50GB PVC. Simulation compute: single BB84 run with 10^6 photons takes ~2 seconds on modern CPU.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP: single fiber span BB84, QBER computation, key rate output, InfluxDB, Grafana dashboard. Phase 2: multi-node QKD topology with trusted relay nodes, React dashboard with topology visualization. Phase 3: decoy state BB84 variant, E91 entanglement protocol comparison.
target_output.development_guidelines: BB84 simulation functions must be pure functions with no global state — all randomness via numpy.random.default_rng(seed) passed as argument. QBER must be computed only from sifted bits (basis-matched positions); never from all photons. Privacy amplification must apply binary entropy h(QBER) = -QBER*log2(QBER) - (1-QBER)*log2(1-QBER); handle QBER=0 edge case returning 1.0 key fraction. Simulation unit tests must include deterministic seed tests asserting exact QBER output.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: depolarization noise model oversimplifies real fiber channel characteristics. Mitigation: document model limitations in paper methodology section; Phase 2 adds polarization mode dispersion model. Risk: InfluxDB StatefulSet PVC fills during large parameter sweeps. Mitigation: sweep jobs estimate storage before running; alert at 80% PVC usage. Tradeoff: simplified cascade error correction model over full Cascade protocol implementation — full Cascade requires interactive communication rounds between Alice and Bob which adds simulation complexity; simplified model sufficient for QBER accuracy comparisons. Tradeoff: InfluxDB over TimescaleDB — InfluxDB native Flux queries for sweep parameter dimension analysis simpler to configure; accepted higher operational overhead of separate time-series database.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Photon count per simulation run confirmed at 10^6 for statistical significance; runtime ~2s on CPU. QBER accuracy requirement of 0.1% met with 10^6 photons (statistical error ~0.03%). Eavesdropping model confirmed as intercept-resend: each eavesdropped photon has 50% chance of disturbing polarization, contributing QBER = eavesdrop_fraction / 4 in expectation. Privacy amplification confirmed: Shor-Preskill bound, secure_key_rate = raw_key_rate * max(0, 1 - 2*h_binary(QBER)) where QBER includes channel noise and eavesdropping contributions.

metadata.notes: Round 1 QKD simulation research prototype first-pass — deliberately weak quality with thin system_components, functional_feature_map, and observability sections.

profile.domain: Telecom SMB Fleet Management

project_goal.rationale: Core product need for SMB cost control over 5-50 company SIMs

target_users.rationale: Primary personas identified in discovery research

security_baseline.rationale: Financial data and carrier credential protection baseline

project.goal: Real-time SMB mobile fleet cost monitoring with cap enforcement and overage alerts
project.timeline: MVP in 12 weeks

frontend.state: React Query for server state, Zustand for cap configuration local state

security.auth: Cognito user pools, JWT bearer tokens
security.secrets: AWS Secrets Manager for carrier API credentials

data.cache: ElastiCache Redis 7 for cap state and alert dedup

reasoner_reviews.product: MVP scope is well-scoped for pilot. The 15-minute polling cadence means near-real-time alerts but not instant; need to set correct user expectations in the onboarding flow. Billing cycle summary reports add significant retention value beyond the alert feature.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The polling-then-alert architecture is the right choice given carrier API rate limits. A Redis sorted-set approach for tracking running costs per SIM enables O(1) cap threshold checks without querying PostgreSQL on every usage update. Batching carrier API calls by operator account helps stay within rate limits.
reasoner_reviews.security: Carrier API credentials must never reach the mobile client. All carrier polling happens server-side with credentials in Secrets Manager. The Redis cap state layer should be the authoritative source to avoid race conditions in concurrent alert evaluations.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: $3000/month is achievable for the MVP scale. Fargate with auto-scaling for the polling worker, a single RDS multi-AZ instance, and ElastiCache Redis fits within budget.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The 15-minute polling gap means a SIM that crosses the hard cap intra-interval will not trigger a carrier-side block until the next poll. The plan should document this limitation and recommend the 80% soft-alert threshold as the primary user-visible feature.

backend.service_design: Two Fastify services: api-service handling mobile client requests and polling-worker handling scheduled carrier API ingestion. Polling worker runs as a separate ECS task with per-account rate-limit tracking in Redis.
backend.api_patterns: REST /api/v1 with JWT auth. Key endpoints: GET /fleet, GET /fleet/sims/:iccid/usage, PUT /fleet/sims/:iccid/caps, GET /billing-cycles/:id/report. Webhook receiver for carrier push events when supported.
backend.background_jobs: Carrier polling job runs every 15 minutes per registered operator account; usage reconciliation job runs nightly to close billing cycle records and generate summary reports.
backend.failure_handling: Carrier API failures trigger exponential backoff with jitter up to 60-minute retry ceiling. Failed alert deliveries are retried via SNS DLQ. PostgreSQL connection pool with circuit breaker to prevent thundering herd on DB restart.

frontend.app_structure: React Native with Expo managed workflow, tab navigator with Fleet, Alerts, and Reports tabs.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Login/MFA flow, Fleet overview list, SIM detail with usage gauge and cap slider, Alert history, Billing cycle report, Cap configuration modal.
frontend.state_management: React Query for fleet usage data with 60-second stale time, Zustand for pending cap changes before submission, push notification handler for FCM alert display.
frontend.performance_notes: Usage gauge components memoized to prevent re-render on unrelated state updates. SIM list virtualized with FlashList for accounts with 50+ SIMs.

security.auth_design: Cognito user pool with hosted UI, JWT access tokens with 1-hour expiry, refresh tokens stored in secure keychain on device.
security.authorization_model: account_owner role can configure caps and view all SIMs; fleet_viewer role is read-only dashboard access scoped to assigned SIM groups.
security.secrets_management: Carrier API credentials stored exclusively in AWS Secrets Manager, injected into polling worker via ECS task role at runtime. Never passed to mobile client.
security.abuse_prevention: Rate limiting on login endpoint via Fastify rate-limit plugin, Cognito account lockout after 5 failed attempts.

data.entities: corporate_accounts (id UUID PK, company_name, cognito_pool_id, carrier_operator_id, billing_cycle_start_day), fleet_sims (id UUID PK, corporate_account_id FK, iccid CHAR(20) UNIQUE, msisdn, assigned_user_name, data_cap_mb INT, roaming_cap_eur NUMERIC, status ENUM active/suspended/deactivated), usage_records (id UUID PK, sim_id FK, recorded_at TIMESTAMPTZ, data_used_mb INT, roaming_cost_eur NUMERIC, polling_cycle_id UUID), cost_caps (id UUID PK, sim_id FK, cap_type ENUM data/roaming, threshold_value NUMERIC, alert_pct INT DEFAULT 80, updated_at), overage_alerts (id UUID PK, sim_id FK, alert_type ENUM approaching_cap/cap_reached, triggered_at TIMESTAMPTZ, delivered_at TIMESTAMPTZ, sns_message_id), billing_cycles (id UUID PK, corporate_account_id FK, cycle_start DATE, cycle_end DATE, total_data_mb INT, total_cost_eur NUMERIC, status ENUM open/closed).
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL for all persistent records; Redis sorted sets keyed by billing_cycle_id storing running cost per SIM for O(1) cap threshold evaluation.
data.retention_and_deletion: usage_records retained 13 months then archived to S3 in Parquet format; corporate_account data anonymized 30 days after account closure.

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate cluster in eu-west-1 with separate task definitions for api-service and polling-worker. ALB routes mobile client traffic to api-service. CloudWatch Events triggers polling-worker on 15-minute schedule.
devops.environments: dev (single task, shared RDS), staging (mirrors production topology for carrier API integration testing), production (multi-AZ RDS, auto-scaling api-service 2-8 tasks, dedicated polling-worker task).
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: lint, Jest unit tests, integration tests against staging DB, Docker build, ECR push, ECS rolling deploy. Expo EAS build for mobile app with TestFlight and Play Internal Testing distribution.
devops.observability_stack: CloudWatch metrics for ECS CPU/memory, RDS connections, Redis hit rate. Custom metrics: polling cycle duration per carrier, alert delivery latency, cap threshold evaluations per minute. Alarms via SNS to PagerDuty for polling failures and alert delivery DLQ depth.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a mobile fleet cost management app for SMBs constrained by 15-minute carrier API polling. The Redis sorted-set pattern for running cost tracking is the key architectural insight enabling fast cap threshold evaluation. Separating the api-service and polling-worker into independent ECS tasks allows each to scale independently. EU hosting in eu-west-1 is required for GDPR data residency.
target_output.title: SMB Corporate Mobile Fleet Cost Manager
target_output.executive_summary: A React Native mobile app backed by Node.js/Fastify on AWS ECS Fargate that enables SMB owners to monitor per-SIM data and roaming usage across their corporate mobile fleet in near-real-time, configure cost caps per SIM or group, and receive FCM push alerts before overage charges occur. The backend polls carrier billing APIs every 15 minutes, maintains running cost state in Redis for sub-second cap evaluation, and persists usage records in PostgreSQL for billing cycle reporting. Hosted in AWS eu-west-1 for GDPR compliance.
target_output.architecture_overview: The system splits into two independent ECS Fargate services: an api-service handling all React Native client requests via an Application Load Balancer, and a polling-worker triggered every 15 minutes by CloudWatch Events to poll registered carrier accounts. The polling-worker fetches per-SIM usage deltas from the carrier API, writes incremental usage_records to PostgreSQL, and updates Redis sorted sets tracking running costs per SIM within the current billing cycle. The api-service evaluates cap thresholds against Redis and publishes SNS messages that fan out to FCM for push delivery when thresholds are crossed. All carrier API credentials reside exclusively in AWS Secrets Manager, injected into the polling-worker at runtime via ECS task role.
target_output.technology_stack: Mobile: React Native with TypeScript targeting iOS 16+ and Android 13+, Expo managed workflow, React Query, Zustand, Firebase Cloud Messaging. API Service: Node.js 20, Fastify with Zod validation, Cognito JWT auth middleware. Polling Worker: Node.js 20, carrier API client with exponential backoff. Data: PostgreSQL 16 RDS multi-AZ eu-west-1, ElastiCache Redis 7. Infrastructure: ECS Fargate, ALB, CloudWatch Events, SNS, Secrets Manager, ECR, GitHub Actions, Expo EAS.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Fleet Overview: real-time per-SIM usage gauges showing data consumed versus cap and roaming cost versus roaming cap, updated on app foreground resume via React Query refetch. Cap Configuration: account_owner sets monthly data cap in MB and roaming cost cap in EUR per SIM or SIM group via slider modal, persisted to cost_caps table and Redis. Push Alerts: SNS-to-FCM delivery when a SIM reaches 80% and 100% of configured cap, with alert history in overage_alerts. Billing Cycle Reports: end-of-cycle summary comparing actual spend against caps highlighting top 5 highest-cost SIMs.
target_output.system_components: ALB routes HTTPS traffic from React Native app to api-service tasks. ECS api-service: Fastify REST API serving fleet dashboard, cap management, and alert history endpoints. ECS polling-worker: scheduled Node.js process that iterates registered carrier accounts and pulls per-SIM usage deltas. PostgreSQL RDS multi-AZ: persistent store for corporate_accounts, fleet_sims, usage_records, cost_caps, overage_alerts, billing_cycles. ElastiCache Redis 7: sorted sets tracking running data usage in MB and roaming cost in EUR per SIM within active billing cycle. SNS plus FCM: alert fanout from cap threshold evaluation to device push notification delivery.
target_output.workflows: Polling and Alert Flow: CloudWatch Events triggers polling-worker every 15 minutes; worker iterates corporate_accounts, calls carrier operator API per account, fetches per-ICCID usage delta since last poll, inserts usage_records into PostgreSQL, increments Redis sorted set scores for each SIM, then calls a cap evaluation routine checking each SIM running cost against configured thresholds; any SIM crossing 80% or 100% triggers SNS publish with sim_id and alert_type; SNS routes to FCM delivering push notification to the account_owner registered device tokens. Billing Cycle Close: a nightly job identifies billing_cycles with cycle_end equal to current date, aggregates all usage_records for the cycle into totals, writes summary to billing_cycles, marks status=closed, and resets Redis running-cost entries for the next cycle.
target_output.data_model: corporate_accounts (id UUID PK, company_name, cognito_user_pool_id, carrier_operator_id, carrier_account_ref, billing_cycle_start_day INT 1-28, created_at). fleet_sims (id UUID PK, corporate_account_id FK, iccid CHAR(20) UNIQUE, msisdn, assigned_user_name, data_cap_mb INT, roaming_cap_eur NUMERIC(8,2), status ENUM active/suspended/deactivated). usage_records (id UUID PK, sim_id FK, polling_cycle_id UUID, recorded_at TIMESTAMPTZ, data_used_mb INT, roaming_cost_eur NUMERIC(8,2)). cost_caps (id UUID PK, sim_id FK, cap_type ENUM data/roaming, threshold_value NUMERIC, alert_pct INT DEFAULT 80, updated_at). overage_alerts (id UUID PK, sim_id FK, alert_type ENUM approaching_cap/cap_reached, triggered_at TIMESTAMPTZ, delivered_at TIMESTAMPTZ, sns_message_id). billing_cycles (id UUID PK, corporate_account_id FK, cycle_start DATE, cycle_end DATE, total_data_mb INT, total_cost_eur NUMERIC(10,2), status ENUM open/closed).
target_output.api_design: REST /api/v1 with Cognito JWT bearer auth. GET /fleet returns fleet_sims list with current usage from Redis. GET /fleet/sims/:iccid/usage returns paginated usage_records. PUT /fleet/sims/:iccid/caps with body {data_cap_mb, roaming_cap_eur, alert_pct} updates cost_caps and refreshes Redis cap reference. GET /alerts returns overage_alerts paginated. GET /billing-cycles returns list of closed cycles. GET /billing-cycles/:id/report returns aggregated usage breakdown per SIM. All responses include X-Request-ID correlation header.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Cognito user pools with JWT access tokens (1-hour TTL), refresh tokens in device secure keychain. RBAC: account_owner full access; fleet_viewer read-only scoped to assigned SIM groups enforced in Fastify auth middleware. TLS 1.3 enforced on ALB. Carrier API credentials exclusively in AWS Secrets Manager, injected via ECS task role. Redis contains no PII, only numeric usage scores keyed by sim_id UUID. GDPR: privacy notice at registration, data export endpoint, account deletion with 30-day anonymization window. CCPA: do-not-sell flag persisted in corporate_accounts. 13-month usage data retention with automated S3 archival via Lambda lifecycle job.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: ECS Fargate eu-west-1: api-service auto-scales 2-8 tasks on CPU 60% target with ALB health checks on /health. polling-worker runs as single scheduled task triggered by CloudWatch Events every 15 minutes with 10-minute execution timeout. RDS PostgreSQL 16 multi-AZ with automated backups, 7-day retention, PgBouncer sidecar for connection pooling. ElastiCache Redis 7 single-AZ with persistence disabled. GitHub Actions CI: ESLint, Jest unit plus integration tests with test DB, Docker build, ECR push, ECS rolling deploy with CodeDeploy health gate. Expo EAS CI builds on tag push, distributes to TestFlight and Play Internal Testing.
target_output.observability: CloudWatch metrics: ECS api-service CPU/memory/request-count, polling-worker invocation duration, RDS FreeableMemory/DBConnections, Redis CacheMisses. Custom application metrics emitted via CloudWatch PutMetricData: polling_cycle_duration_seconds per carrier account, cap_evaluations_per_cycle, alert_delivery_latency_ms, failed_carrier_polls_total. Alarms: polling-worker failure SNS to on-call, SNS DLQ depth greater than 5, RDS connection saturation greater than 80%, Redis evictions greater than 0.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $1800/month for MVP at 500 corporate accounts with 10K SIMs: ECS Fargate api-service ~$300, polling-worker ~$100, RDS multi-AZ db.t3.medium ~$90, ElastiCache cache.t3.micro ~$30, ALB ~$20, data transfer ~$50. Scales linearly with account count. At 5000 accounts the polling-worker will require horizontal sharding by carrier_operator_id. Reserved Fargate capacity reduces compute cost 30% at steady state.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (10 weeks): Cognito auth and RBAC, carrier API integration for one pilot carrier, fleet dashboard with usage polling, per-SIM cap configuration, push alerts via SNS and FCM, PostgreSQL schema and Redis cap state, CI/CD pipeline and ECS deployment. Phase 2 (6 weeks): billing cycle close job and monthly report, fleet_viewer role and SIM group cap assignment, multi-carrier support, usage export endpoint for GDPR data portability. Phase 3 (4 weeks): automated carrier-side spend limit enforcement via carrier API, department cost allocation analytics.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode across api-service, polling-worker, and React Native app. Zod schemas define both runtime validation and TypeScript types for all API request/response contracts. Jest unit tests for cap evaluation logic, usage aggregation, and billing cycle close routines with coverage gate at 80%. Expo EAS preview builds on every PR for mobile regression testing. No carrier API credentials in environment variables; always inject via ECS task role or Secrets Manager.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: carrier API polling latency means a SIM can exceed its hard cap within a 15-minute window before the system detects it. Mitigation: set default alert_pct to 80% so users receive warnings with headroom and document the polling window in onboarding. Risk: carrier API breaking changes or credential expiry silently halt usage ingestion. Mitigation: polling-worker failure alarm with 15-minute breach threshold. Tradeoff: Redis sorted-set cap evaluation over per-query PostgreSQL aggregation improves alert responsiveness but requires Redis reconstruction logic on cache miss.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected Fastify over Express for polling-worker due to lower overhead on high-frequency scheduled tasks. Confirmed Redis as cap state store over in-memory process state to survive polling-worker restarts. Chose FCM over APNs-direct to simplify cross-platform push delivery. Selected eu-west-1 as primary region to satisfy GDPR data residency for EU customers.

metadata.notes: SMB mobile fleet cost management with carrier API polling, Redis cap state, and GDPR-compliant EU hosting.

profile.domain: Network Research Documentation

project_goal.value: Static documentation and benchmarking results site for an open-source ML traffic classification library, hosting methodology docs, interactive benchmark charts, and a release changelog
project_goal.rationale: Community-facing documentation hub to support library adoption in network research

target_users.rationale: Primary audience identified in open-source project charter

project.goal: Documentation and benchmark visualization site for open-source ML traffic classification library
project.timeline: Ship with library v1.0 release

frontend.content: MDX pages for docs, JSON data files for benchmarks

data.benchmark_data: Versioned JSON result files in /data/benchmarks/ directory

constraints.cost: Zero hosting cost on Firebase free tier

reasoner_reviews.product: The scope is exactly right for an open-source docs site. The benchmark chart filtering by protocol category is the highest-value feature for researchers evaluating the library. Consider adding a copy citation button on methodology pages to support academic use.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Astro is the correct choice for this use case. Use Astro content collections for typed benchmark JSON loading at build time. The CI job that runs the benchmark suite on release and regenerates JSON files is the most important automation to get right.
reasoner_reviews.security: CSP headers via Firebase Hosting firebase.json configuration. No server-side code means no injection surface. Ensure the benchmark JSON files do not contain any path or user data from the CI environment that could leak internal information.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Firebase Hosting free tier supports 10GB/month bandwidth which is ample for a research documentation site. Build time under 3 minutes is achievable with Astro partial hydration and static rendering.

frontend.app_structure: Astro project with content collections: /docs (MDX pages), /benchmarks (JSON data), /changelog (MD). Chart.js benchmark components hydrated client-side only on benchmark pages using Astro client:load directive.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Home with library overview and quick-start code block, Benchmarks page with protocol category filter and Chart.js bar and radar charts, Methodology pages (MDX), Changelog page parsed from CHANGELOG.md, API Reference (auto-linked to source).
frontend.performance_notes: Astro static output with zero JavaScript on docs pages. Chart.js loaded only on benchmark page via dynamic import. Firebase CDN caches all assets globally.
frontend.accessibility_notes: Chart.js charts include aria-label and data-table fallback for screen readers. Color palette chosen for WCAG AA contrast on benchmark comparison charts.

devops.deployment_topology: Firebase Hosting single-site deployment. GitHub Actions workflow: build on PR to preview channel, deploy to production on library release tag push.
devops.ci_cd_design: On library release tag: run benchmark suite via pytest, generate updated benchmark JSON files, commit to repo, trigger Astro build, deploy to Firebase production channel via firebase-tools action. On PR: Astro build check and Firebase preview channel deploy with PR comment containing preview URL.
devops.observability_stack: Google Analytics 4 GA4 tag embedded in Astro layout component. Firebase Hosting dashboard for bandwidth and request metrics. No custom alerting required for static site.
devops.cost_controls: All resources within Firebase free tier: 10GB storage, 10GB/month bandwidth. No paid Firebase services activated.

security.auth_design: No authentication; fully public site.
security.abuse_prevention: Firebase Hosting DDoS protection included at no cost. Rate limiting handled by Firebase infrastructure.
security.privacy_controls: Google Analytics 4 with IP anonymization enabled, cookie consent banner for EU visitors, no additional tracking pixels.

data.entities: benchmark_results (JSON: library_version, protocol_category, model_architecture, accuracy_pct, f1_score, inference_latency_ms, test_dataset_size), protocol_categories (JSON: id, name, description, example_protocols list), library_releases (CHANGELOG.md parsed: version, date, changes list)
data.schema_notes: All benchmark data is static JSON in /src/data/benchmarks/. Astro content collections provide TypeScript types at build time. No runtime database.
data.analytics_events: GA4 custom events: benchmark_filter_applied (protocol_category), chart_view (chart_type), doc_page_view (page_slug), changelog_version_clicked (version).

target_output.thinking_summary: Static website with no backend - Astro is the right framework for documentation with MDX support. The key workflow is the CI benchmark automation that generates fresh JSON data files on library release and triggers a rebuild. No database, no server-side runtime, no auth. The only runtime integration is Google Analytics 4 via client-side script tag.
target_output.title: ML Traffic Classification Library Documentation Site
target_output.executive_summary: A fully static documentation and benchmarking results website for an open-source ML-based traffic classification library, built with Astro and deployed to Firebase Hosting. Benchmark result JSON files are generated by CI on each library release and committed to the repository, triggering an Astro rebuild that publishes fresh Chart.js visualizations comparing classification accuracy and inference latency across protocol categories. Zero server-side runtime; all content statically rendered at build time.
target_output.architecture_overview: Purely static site architecture: Astro generates HTML at build time from MDX documentation pages, typed benchmark JSON content collections, and a parsed CHANGELOG.md. No server-side runtime exists at request time. GitHub Actions orchestrates two CI paths: on library release tags, the benchmark suite runs, generates updated JSON result files, and deploys to Firebase Hosting production; on PRs, a build check deploys to a Firebase preview channel. Firebase Hosting provides global CDN delivery with automatic HTTPS, CSP headers via firebase.json, and DDoS protection at no cost on the free tier.
target_output.technology_stack: Site framework: Astro 4 with TypeScript, content collections for type-safe benchmark JSON loading. Charts: Chart.js 4 loaded via Astro client:load on benchmark page only. Content: MDX for documentation pages, marked for CHANGELOG parsing. Deployment: Firebase Hosting via firebase-tools GitHub Actions action. Analytics: Google Analytics 4 with IP anonymization. CI: GitHub Actions. Build: Astro static adapter outputting to /dist.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Benchmark Comparison Page: filterable bar and radar charts showing accuracy, F1 score, and inference latency per model architecture across HTTP, DNS, TLS, QUIC, and streaming protocol categories. Methodology Docs: MDX pages explaining feature extraction approach, dataset curation process, and evaluation protocol with embedded code examples. Getting-Started Guide: installation, basic usage, and a quick-start PCAP classification example. Release Changelog: auto-generated from CHANGELOG.md with anchor-linked version headings. API Reference: external link to auto-generated TypeDoc output hosted on GitHub Pages.
target_output.system_components: Astro build process: compiles MDX, loads benchmark JSON content collections, renders all pages to static HTML/CSS/JS in /dist. Firebase Hosting: serves /dist via global CDN with HTTPS, custom domain, and CSP header injection from firebase.json. GitHub Actions benchmark runner: pytest benchmark suite produces /src/data/benchmarks/{version}.json files. Google Analytics 4: client-side GA4 script in Astro layout head with custom event tracking for chart interactions.
target_output.workflows: Release-Triggered Rebuild: maintainer pushes a semver tag to GitHub; Actions workflow runs the Python benchmark suite against the new library version, writes result JSON to /src/data/benchmarks/v{version}.json, commits the file, runs astro build, and deploys to Firebase Hosting production channel via firebase-tools deploy. Researchers visit the Benchmarks page, select a protocol category from the filter dropdown, and Chart.js updates the accuracy and latency charts client-side without a page reload, comparing all model architectures in the selected category. PR Preview Deploy: contributor opens a PR with documentation changes; Actions builds the Astro site and deploys to a Firebase preview channel, posting the preview URL as a PR comment for reviewer validation before merge.
target_output.data_model: benchmark_results JSON schema: {library_version: string, protocol_category: ENUM http/dns/tls/quic/streaming/voip, model_architecture: string, accuracy_pct: number, f1_score: number, precision: number, recall: number, inference_latency_ms: number, test_dataset_size: int, benchmark_date: string}. protocol_categories JSON: {id: string, name: string, description: string, example_protocols: string[]}. All data is static files in /src/data/ versioned in Git alongside library releases. No database or runtime data store.
target_output.api_design: No server-side API. All data access is at build time via Astro content collections using getCollection('benchmarks') which returns typed benchmark result objects. Client-side Chart.js rendering reads pre-loaded JSON embedded in the page HTML at build time. The only external call at runtime is the Google Analytics 4 measurement protocol hit on page view and custom events.
target_output.security_and_compliance: HTTPS enforced by Firebase Hosting with automatic certificate management. Content-Security-Policy header set in firebase.json: default-src 'self', script-src 'self' https://www.googletagmanager.com, style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'. No server-side code eliminates injection and SSRF attack surfaces. Google Analytics 4 configured with IP anonymization (anonymize_ip: true) and no ad personalization signals. EU cookie consent banner on first visit using a lightweight client-side widget. No user accounts, no form submissions, no PII collected beyond anonymous GA4 session data.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Firebase Hosting project with two channels: production (library release tags) and preview (PR builds). firebase.json configures headers for CSP, X-Frame-Options DENY, and cache-control max-age=3600 for HTML and max-age=31536000 for fingerprinted assets. GitHub Actions release workflow: checkout, setup Python, run pytest benchmark suite, write JSON, astro build, firebase deploy. PR workflow: astro build only, firebase hosting channel deploy preview. Rollback: Firebase Hosting supports one-click rollback to previous production deploy via the Firebase console.
target_output.observability: Google Analytics 4 dashboard tracks page views by page_slug, sessions by country, and custom events: benchmark_filter_applied with protocol_category parameter, chart_interaction with chart_type, doc_section_viewed. Firebase Hosting console monitors bandwidth consumed and request volume against free tier limits. No alerting required; static site has no uptime dependency beyond Firebase infrastructure SLA.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero recurring hosting cost on Firebase Hosting free tier: 10GB storage and 10GB/month bandwidth, ample for a research documentation site. Only cost is GitHub Actions minutes for CI: approximately 15 minutes per release build. Firebase Hosting CDN scales automatically to any traffic volume within the free tier. If traffic exceeds free tier thresholds, the Blaze pay-as-you-go plan charges $0.026/GB above 10GB/month.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 with library v1.0 (2 weeks): Astro project scaffold, methodology docs and getting-started guide in MDX, benchmark comparison charts for HTTP/DNS/TLS/QUIC/streaming categories, changelog page, Firebase Hosting deployment, GitHub Actions release CI. Phase 2 post-v1.0 (3 weeks): API reference TypeDoc integration, accessibility audit and WCAG AA fix pass, citation copy button on methodology pages, Google Analytics 4 event tracking refinement. Phase 3 (future): WebAssembly-compiled library playground for interactive in-browser classification experiments.
target_output.development_guidelines: Astro TypeScript strict mode. Benchmark JSON files must validate against the defined schema via a CI schema-check step before deploy. Chart.js components wrapped in Astro client:load to ensure zero JavaScript shipped to non-benchmark pages. MDX pages require frontmatter with title and description fields enforced by Astro content collection schema. PR preview deploy must pass before any documentation merge. No tracking pixels beyond GA4; no third-party fonts that leak visitor data.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: benchmark suite runtime increases as new model architectures are added, potentially breaking the 3-minute build constraint. Mitigation: cache Python dependencies in Actions and run benchmarks in parallel by protocol category. Risk: Firebase free tier bandwidth limit reached during a viral launch or paper citation event. Mitigation: monitor Firebase dashboard; upgrade to Blaze tier at $0.026/GB overage, which is negligible. Tradeoff: committing generated benchmark JSON files to the repository increases repo size over time; accepted for simplicity over an external artifact store.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected Astro over Next.js for zero-runtime static output on documentation pages. Confirmed Firebase Hosting over GitHub Pages for better CDN performance and preview channel support. Chose Chart.js over D3.js for lower bundle size on benchmark pages. Decided to commit benchmark JSON to repo rather than fetch from GitHub Releases at build time to simplify Astro content collections integration.

metadata.notes: Static documentation site for ML traffic classification library with CI-driven benchmark chart regeneration on release.

profile.domain: Telecom OSS/BSS Microservices Decomposition

project_goal.value: Decompose a monolithic OSS/BSS integration platform into independently deployable microservices each owning a single domain (provisioning, inventory, ticketing, mediation) to enable per-service scaling and reduce blast radius of outages
project_goal.rationale: Current monolith deployments cause full-platform outages during partial failures and cannot scale provisioning independently during peak onboarding campaigns

target_users.value: Platform engineering teams operating OSS/BSS services and NOC operators monitoring service health
target_users.rationale: Internal platform consumers who currently share deployment risk across unrelated OSS domains

project_class.rationale: Decomposition project is a platform architecture initiative, not a user-facing application

capabilities.rationale: Service mesh, deployment topology, CI/CD per service, and strangler-fig migration patterns are the core concerns

complexity_level.rationale: Distributed microservices with mTLS service mesh, consumer-driven contract tests, and live traffic migration at carrier scale

risk_level.rationale: Production topology changes to carrier-grade OSS platform require CAB approval and carry service continuity risk

data_sensitivity.rationale: OSS/BSS data includes subscriber provisioning state and network topology data classified as confidential

external_exposure.rationale: All microservices communicate within the EKS cluster; no public internet exposure

access_model.value: Service-to-service mTLS via Istio; custom JWT for human operator access to deployment tooling
access_model.rationale: Zero-trust service mesh with no implicit trust between services

feature_scope.value: Strangler-fig migration for provisioning, inventory, ticketing, and mediation domains; Istio service mesh with mTLS; per-service ArgoCD applications; consumer-driven contract tests; circuit breaker policies
feature_scope.rationale: Full decomposition scope with service governance controls

mvp_scope.value: Provision service extraction complete with Istio mTLS, ArgoCD GitOps deployment, and traffic routing parity verified via shadow mode
mvp_scope.rationale: Provisioning domain chosen first due to highest scaling pressure during customer onboarding

security_baseline.value: Istio mTLS for all inter-service calls, RBAC on Kubernetes namespaces, secrets in AWS Secrets Manager via External Secrets Operator, no monolith credentials shared with extracted services
security_baseline.rationale: Zero-trust service mesh security baseline for carrier-grade platform

frontend_stack.value: None - infrastructure project with no user-facing frontend
frontend_stack.rationale: Infrastructure decomposition requires no frontend

backend_stack.value: Go 1.22 with Gin for extracted microservices; Istio 1.20 service mesh on EKS
backend_stack.rationale: Go chosen for low-memory footprint per service pod; Istio for mTLS and traffic management

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 per service (database-per-service pattern), Redis 7 for shared session state during migration period
data_platform.rationale: Database-per-service enforces domain isolation; shared Redis is a transitional coupling to be eliminated post-migration

hosting_target.value: AWS EKS in us-east-1, ArgoCD for GitOps, Istio service mesh
hosting_target.rationale: EKS for Kubernetes-native service deployment; ArgoCD for declarative GitOps per service

privacy_retention_policy.value: OSS/BSS data follows existing 7-year telecom regulatory retention; no change to data retention as part of decomposition
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Decomposition is a topology change; retention policies are unchanged

future_scope.value: Event-driven choreography via Kafka replacing synchronous inter-service REST calls, per-service autoscaling HPA policies based on domain-specific metrics
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 evolution after service boundaries are stable

constraints.value: No prolonged customer service interruption during migration; CAB approval required for all production topology changes; strangler-fig must allow rollback at any phase
constraints.rationale: Carrier-grade SLA commitments and change management policy

observability_baseline.value: Grafana + Prometheus for service mesh golden signals; distributed tracing via Jaeger; ArgoCD sync status dashboards
observability_baseline.rationale: Existing observability stack extended for microservices visibility

execution_preference.value: Domain-by-domain extraction with full validation gate before proceeding to next domain
execution_preference.rationale: Sequential domain extraction reduces simultaneous migration risk

compliance_context.value: Internal CAB change management approval required for all production topology changes; carrier-grade SLA commitments govern maximum allowed service degradation windows
compliance_context.rationale: High risk level and confidential data sensitivity trigger compliance context requirement

project.goal: Strangler-fig decomposition of monolithic OSS/BSS into domain microservices on EKS with Istio
project.timeline: 18-month full decomposition, provisioning domain in 6 months

security.auth: Istio mTLS for service-to-service, custom JWT for operators
security.secrets: External Secrets Operator from AWS Secrets Manager

data.migration: Flyway for schema migrations per service

constraints.migration: Strangler-fig with traffic shifting via Istio VirtualService
constraints.rollback: Required at every traffic shift increment

reasoner_reviews.product: Provisioning domain extraction first is the right prioritization given peak onboarding load. The shadow-mode validation approach is critical for proving parity before shifting production traffic. Ensure the contract test suite covers all integration points the monolith currently exposes to downstream billing and CRM services.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The database-per-service pattern is non-negotiable for true domain isolation, but the shared Redis during migration is an acceptable transitional coupling if it has a defined sunset date. Istio VirtualService traffic shifting with 5%/25%/50%/100% increments gives fine-grained rollback capability. Consumer-driven contract tests via Pact should gate every service promotion to staging.
reasoner_reviews.security: mTLS via Istio PeerAuthentication policies in STRICT mode per namespace is the right baseline. Ensure the External Secrets Operator service account has least-privilege IAM role with only the Secrets Manager paths for its namespace. Circuit breaker Envoy filters should be defined at the service mesh level, not duplicated in application code.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Round 2 plan left the circuit breaker policy definitions vague. The current plan must specify concrete Envoy outlier detection thresholds (consecutive_5xx, interval, base_ejection_time) per domain service rather than referencing generic circuit breaker patterns. Also, the monolith strangler routing configuration was not explicitly shown - the plan must include the Istio VirtualService weight progression and the rollback trigger conditions.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The OSS/BSS monolith cannot be taken offline during decomposition — all microservice extractions must operate in shadow mode alongside the monolith until traffic cutover is validated. The shared Oracle database cannot be migrated until all services depending on each schema are independently deployed and validated. Timeline of 18 months constrains the decomposition to three domains per quarter; provisioning, billing, and inventory are the first three given their peak load criticality.

backend.service_design: Four bounded-context microservices: provisioning-service (SIM/subscriber provisioning state machine), inventory-service (network resource registry), ticketing-service (trouble ticket lifecycle), mediation-service (CDR/event normalization). Each owns its PostgreSQL schema and exposes gRPC internal API plus REST for operator tooling.
backend.api_patterns: gRPC for inter-service calls with proto contracts versioned in a shared api-contracts repository. REST /api/v1 for human operator tools. Consumer-driven contract tests via Pact for all gRPC consumer/provider pairs.
backend.failure_handling: Envoy outlier detection per service: consecutive_5xx=5, interval=30s, base_ejection_time=30s, max_ejection_percent=50. Retry policies: 3 retries with exponential backoff for idempotent GET operations; no auto-retry for state-mutating calls.
backend.scaling_notes: HPA per service on custom Prometheus metrics: provisioning-service scales on pending_activations_queue_depth, inventory-service on lookup_request_rate.

security.auth_design: Istio PeerAuthentication in STRICT mTLS mode per namespace. Service accounts with minimal RBAC, no pod-to-pod calls outside mesh.
security.secrets_management: External Secrets Operator with per-namespace ExternalSecret CRDs pulling from AWS Secrets Manager. Secrets rotated via Secrets Manager Lambda rotation; ESO reconciles within 60 seconds.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Istio access logs forwarded to Prometheus/Grafana via Envoy telemetry. All operator tool actions logged with user JWT subject and action type.

data.entities: service_manifests (id UUID PK, service_name, version, git_sha, deployed_at, environment), api_contracts (id UUID PK, consumer_service, provider_service, pact_version, verified_at, status ENUM passing/failing), circuit_breaker_policies (id UUID PK, service_name, consecutive_5xx_threshold, ejection_interval_s, max_ejection_pct), deployment_topologies (id UUID PK, environment, service_name, replica_count, traffic_weight_pct, updated_at), migration_phases (id UUID PK, domain_name, phase ENUM shadow/5pct/25pct/50pct/100pct/legacy_decom, started_at, completed_at, rollback_count)
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL per service in dedicated RDS instance. Migration metadata stored in a central migration_control PostgreSQL DB accessible only by the platform engineering team.
data.migration_notes: Flyway versioned migrations per service. Monolith and microservice share read replica during shadow phase only; write path split at first traffic shift increment.

devops.deployment_topology: EKS cluster with dedicated namespaces per domain service: provisioning, inventory, ticketing, mediation, monolith. Istio sidecar injection enabled per namespace. ArgoCD App-of-Apps pattern: one root Application per environment pointing to per-service Application manifests in a GitOps repo.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: Go build, unit tests, Pact contract tests, Docker build, ECR push. ArgoCD: syncs from GitOps repo on merge to main. Traffic shifts executed via kubectl patch on Istio VirtualService, gated by Prometheus golden signal check (error rate less than 0.1%, p99 latency within 10% of baseline).
devops.rollback_strategy: Istio VirtualService weight revert to previous split via ArgoCD rollback. Automated rollback triggered if error rate exceeds 0.5% within 5 minutes of a traffic shift.
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus scraping Envoy sidecar metrics per service pair. Grafana dashboards for per-domain golden signals and ArgoCD sync status. Jaeger distributed tracing for cross-service call chains. Alerting: PagerDuty via Alertmanager for error_rate greater than 0.1% and sync drift greater than 5 minutes.

ISS-001.title: Circuit breaker policies lack concrete Envoy outlier detection thresholds
ISS-001.detail: Round 2 plan referenced circuit breakers generically without specifying consecutive_5xx, interval, base_ejection_time, or max_ejection_percent values for each domain service. Runtime cannot generate correct Istio EnvoyFilter resources without these.

history.note: Initial audit flagged missing circuit breaker specifics
Round 2 plan still references generic patterns

ISS-002.title: Strangler-fig traffic routing configuration not specified
ISS-002.detail: The Istio VirtualService weight progression and rollback trigger conditions were not included in the deployment plan. Without explicit traffic shift percentages and automated rollback conditions, the migration cannot be executed safely.

history.note: Missing from initial plan
Still absent from round 2 plan

ISS-003.title: Consumer-driven contract test coverage not defined for all gRPC service pairs
ISS-003.detail: Round 1 mentioned Pact but did not specify which consumer/provider pairs require contracts. Round 2 added the api_contracts table and gRPC proto repository.

history.note: Contract test scope undefined
api-contracts repo and Pact integration added to CI

focus_issues.title: Circuit breaker policies lack concrete Envoy outlier detection thresholds
focus_issues.detail: Must specify consecutive_5xx, interval, base_ejection_time, and max_ejection_percent for each extracted domain service.

focus_issues.title: Strangler-fig traffic routing configuration not specified
focus_issues.detail: Istio VirtualService weight progression (shadow/5%/25%/50%/100%) and automated rollback trigger conditions must be explicitly specified.

latest_recommendations: Add concrete Envoy outlier detection thresholds per service to the security and deployment sections
Define the Istio VirtualService weight progression and automated rollback error rate thresholds explicitly in deployment_and_operations

previous_plan.title: OSS/BSS Microservices Decomposition via Strangler-Fig v2
previous_plan.executive_summary: Decomposes the monolithic OSS/BSS integration platform into four domain microservices using a strangler-fig pattern on EKS with Istio mTLS service mesh and ArgoCD GitOps. Provisioning domain extracted first.
previous_plan.deployment_and_operations: EKS with Istio PeerAuthentication STRICT per namespace. ArgoCD App-of-Apps for per-service GitOps. Traffic shifting via Istio VirtualService. Circuit breakers implemented via Envoy filters. Rollback via ArgoCD.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: Istio mTLS STRICT mode. External Secrets Operator for Secrets Manager. Consumer-driven Pact contracts gate staging promotion. CAB approval for production topology changes.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 3 must resolve ISS-001 and ISS-002 by adding explicit Envoy outlier detection thresholds and the complete VirtualService traffic shift progression with automated rollback conditions. The plan should show exact numeric values for circuit breaker policies and the precise traffic weight schedule for the provisioning domain extraction as the reference migration pattern.

fix_report.action_taken: Added explicit Envoy outlier detection configuration per domain service to security_and_compliance and deployment_and_operations: consecutive_5xx=5, interval=30s, base_ejection_time=30s, max_ejection_percent=50 for provisioning-service and inventory-service; consecutive_5xx=3, base_ejection_time=60s for ticketing-service and mediation-service which have lower tolerance for retry storms
fix_report.expected_outcome: Platform engineering team can generate correct Istio EnvoyFilter resources directly from the plan; auditor can verify circuit breaker policy completeness against carrier-grade SLA requirements

fix_report.action_taken: Added complete VirtualService weight progression for provisioning domain extraction: shadow mode (0% production traffic, parallel writes), 5% shift with 30-minute observation window, 25% shift with 2-hour observation window, 50% shift with 24-hour observation window, 100% cutover; added automated rollback trigger: if error_rate greater than 0.5% on new service within 5 minutes of any shift increment, VirtualService weights revert to previous split and PagerDuty alert fires
fix_report.expected_outcome: Migration team has a concrete runbook for each traffic shift increment with unambiguous rollback conditions; NOC can monitor the shift progression via Grafana VirtualService weight dashboard

target_output.title: OSS/BSS Microservices Decomposition via Strangler-Fig
target_output.executive_summary: Decomposes the monolithic OSS/BSS integration platform into four independently deployable Go microservices on AWS EKS using a strangler-fig pattern with Istio 1.20 service mesh enforcing mTLS in STRICT mode. Each domain service (provisioning, inventory, ticketing, mediation) owns a dedicated PostgreSQL 16 database. Traffic is migrated via Istio VirtualService weight progression (shadow, 5%, 25%, 50%, 100%) with automated rollback triggered if the new service error rate exceeds 0.5% within 5 minutes of a shift. ArgoCD App-of-Apps manages GitOps deployment per service. Consumer-driven Pact contract tests gate every staging promotion.
target_output.architecture_overview: The decomposition uses the strangler-fig pattern: the existing monolith remains fully operational throughout migration, with Istio VirtualService routing rules incrementally shifting traffic from the monolith to each extracted microservice. The EKS cluster hosts five namespaces: provisioning, inventory, ticketing, mediation, and monolith. Istio sidecar injection is enabled in all namespaces with PeerAuthentication in STRICT mTLS mode, ensuring all inter-service calls are mutually authenticated. Each microservice is deployed as an ArgoCD Application sourced from a GitOps repository, with rollout status gated by Prometheus golden signal checks before traffic shifts proceed. The monolith namespace is decommissioned domain-by-domain as each extraction reaches 100% traffic.
target_output.technology_stack: Microservices: Go 1.22 with Gin, gRPC for inter-service calls, protobuf contracts in api-contracts repo. Service Mesh: Istio 1.20, Envoy sidecar, PeerAuthentication STRICT mTLS. GitOps: ArgoCD App-of-Apps, GitHub Actions for CI. Data: PostgreSQL 16 per service on RDS, Flyway migrations, Redis 7 ElastiCache for transitional shared session state. Secrets: External Secrets Operator with AWS Secrets Manager. Observability: Prometheus, Grafana, Jaeger, Alertmanager, PagerDuty. Contract Testing: Pact broker for gRPC consumer/provider pairs.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Provisioning Service: manages SIM and subscriber provisioning state machine, exposes gRPC ActivateSIM, SuspendSIM, PortSubscriber RPCs. Inventory Service: network resource registry supporting CRUD for physical and logical network elements, exposes gRPC QueryResource, ReserveResource RPCs. Ticketing Service: trouble ticket lifecycle (open, assign, escalate, close), gRPC CreateTicket, UpdateTicket RPCs. Mediation Service: CDR and event normalization from multiple source formats, gRPC SubmitEvent RPC with schema validation.
target_output.system_components: EKS Cluster: five namespaces (provisioning, inventory, ticketing, mediation, monolith) with Istio sidecar injection. Istio Control Plane: PeerAuthentication STRICT mTLS per namespace, VirtualService per domain with weighted routes, EnvoyFilter for circuit breaker outlier detection. ArgoCD: App-of-Apps root Application per environment, per-service Application CRDs. PostgreSQL RDS: per-service database in dedicated RDS instance. External Secrets Operator: per-namespace ExternalSecret CRDs pulling from AWS Secrets Manager. Pact Broker: self-hosted in EKS for contract verification. Prometheus/Grafana/Jaeger: observability stack scraping Envoy sidecars.
target_output.workflows: Provisioning Domain Traffic Shift: ArgoCD applies VirtualService update setting provisioning-service weight=5, monolith weight=95; Prometheus monitor runs for 30 minutes checking error_rate less than 0.5% and p99_latency within 10% of monolith baseline; if healthy, next shift to 25/75 with 2-hour window, then 50/50 with 24-hour window, then 100/0; if error_rate greater than 0.5% within 5 minutes of any shift, GitHub Actions job patches VirtualService back to previous weights and fires PagerDuty alert. Consumer-Driven Contract Verification: on PR merge to any microservice, GitHub Actions runs Pact tests publishing results to Pact Broker; ArgoCD sync to staging is blocked until all consumer contracts for that service show status=passing.
target_output.data_model: service_manifests (id UUID PK, service_name, version SEMVER, git_sha CHAR(40), deployed_at, environment ENUM dev/staging/production). api_contracts (id UUID PK, consumer_service, provider_service, pact_version, proto_sha CHAR(40), verified_at, status ENUM passing/failing). circuit_breaker_policies (id UUID PK, service_name, consecutive_5xx_threshold INT, interval_seconds INT, base_ejection_time_seconds INT, max_ejection_percent INT). deployment_topologies (id UUID PK, environment, service_name, replica_count, traffic_weight_pct INT, virtual_service_version, updated_at). migration_phases (id UUID PK, domain_name, phase ENUM shadow/5pct/25pct/50pct/100pct/legacy_decom, started_at, completed_at, rollback_count INT, approved_by).
target_output.api_design: gRPC APIs versioned in api-contracts/proto/{service}/v1/. Provisioning service: ActivateSIM returns ActivateResponse, SuspendSIM returns SuspendResponse, PortSubscriber returns PortResponse. Inventory service: QueryResource returns ResourceList, ReserveResource returns ReservationConfirmation. All gRPC calls authenticated via Istio mTLS peer certificate; service accounts mapped to Kubernetes RBAC roles. Operator REST API /api/v1/services/:name/traffic-weight for NOC dashboard integration.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Istio PeerAuthentication STRICT mTLS in all five namespaces; no plaintext service-to-service calls permitted. EnvoyFilter outlier detection per service: provisioning-service and inventory-service (consecutive_5xx=5, interval=30s, base_ejection_time=30s, max_ejection_percent=50); ticketing-service and mediation-service (consecutive_5xx=3, interval=30s, base_ejection_time=60s, max_ejection_percent=30) reflecting lower tolerance for retry storms on ticket creation and CDR ingestion. External Secrets Operator service account IAM role restricted to secretsmanager:GetSecretValue on paths prefixed with oss-bss/{namespace}/. Network Policies restrict inter-namespace traffic to declared service dependency graph only. CAB change record required for every VirtualService traffic shift above 5%.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: EKS cluster us-east-1 with Istio installed via Helm. ArgoCD App-of-Apps: root Application per environment (dev/staging/production) pointing to GitOps repo path /apps/{env}/. Each domain service has a Helm chart with configurable replica_count, HPA min/max, and resource requests/limits. Provisioning domain traffic shift runbook: shadow mode (VirtualService weight provisioning=0, monolith=100 with mirroring enabled), then 5/95 for 30 minutes, 25/75 for 2 hours, 50/50 for 24 hours, 100/0 for 48-hour stabilization before monolith namespace deletion. Automated rollback: GitHub Actions job watches Prometheus error_rate metric for provisioning-service; if greater than 0.5% sustained for 3 minutes post-shift, patches VirtualService to previous weights and creates PagerDuty incident. Production topology changes require CAB approval ticket number in PR description; merge blocked by Actions check.
target_output.observability: Prometheus scrapes Envoy sidecar metrics per pod: istio_requests_total, istio_request_duration_milliseconds labeled by source and destination service. Grafana dashboards: per-domain golden signals (request rate, error rate, p50/p95/p99 latency), VirtualService weight progression timeline, Pact contract verification status, ArgoCD sync state per service. Jaeger traces cross-service gRPC call chains with 10% sampling rate. Alertmanager rules: error_rate greater than 0.1% (warning), greater than 0.5% (critical with PagerDuty), ArgoCD sync drift greater than 5 minutes, circuit breaker ejection_percentage greater than 40%.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: EKS node group: 6x m5.xlarge instances baseline (~$800/month). ArgoCD and Pact Broker run as shared cluster services. Per-service RDS: 4x db.t3.medium PostgreSQL (~$360/month total). HPA scales each service 2-10 pods on domain-specific metrics; provisioning-service scales on pending_activations_queue_depth Prometheus metric. Total estimated platform cost ~$2000/month, replacing shared monolith infrastructure with per-service isolation.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (6 months): provisioning-service extraction with full traffic shift to 100% and monolith provisioning module disabled. Phase 2 (4 months): inventory-service extraction using same strangler-fig pattern. Phase 3 (4 months): ticketing-service and mediation-service extracted in parallel by two teams. Phase 4 (2 months): monolith namespace decommissioned, Redis shared session state eliminated, event-driven Kafka choreography design initiated for Phase 2 roadmap.
target_output.development_guidelines: Go services must pass golangci-lint with staticcheck and errcheck enabled. gRPC proto changes require backward-compatible additions only; breaking changes require a new proto version directory. Pact contract tests run before every staging ArgoCD sync via Actions. HPA custom metrics require a Prometheus recording rule with naming convention {service}_{metric}_per_second. CAB ticket number in PR description is enforced by a GitHub Actions check against the JIRA API. No direct kubectl apply to production; all changes via ArgoCD GitOps.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Istio sidecar overhead adds 1-3ms per hop latency; measured at current monolith inter-module call volume before migration to establish baseline. Risk: shared Redis session state during migration creates a distributed coupling; mitigated by defining Redis sunset date at provisioning extraction completion and tracking redis_key_namespace_usage metric. Tradeoff: database-per-service increases RDS instance count and operational overhead but is non-negotiable for domain isolation. Tradeoff: sequential domain extraction is slower than parallel but reduces simultaneous migration risk for carrier-grade services.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected gRPC over REST for inter-service calls due to proto schema enforcement and Pact compatibility. Confirmed Envoy outlier detection over application-level circuit breaker libraries to avoid duplicating logic. Chose ArgoCD App-of-Apps over Flux for GitOps due to team familiarity and better multi-environment Application support. Rollback automated at 0.5% error rate threshold based on carrier SLA budget.

metadata.notes: Round 3 revision resolving ISS-001 (concrete Envoy circuit breaker thresholds) and ISS-002 (VirtualService traffic shift progression with automated rollback). Previous rounds had flawed circuit breaker specs and missing strangler-fig routing config.

profile.domain: ISP NetFlow Anomaly Detection AI System

project_goal.value: AI system that ingests billions of NetFlow records daily from a tier-1 ISP backbone to detect DDoS amplification, BGP hijacking, and large-scale scanning campaigns, with an analyst investigation workspace and RTBH mitigation workflow
project_goal.rationale: Core operational need: SOC analysts need near-real-time anomaly detection at backbone scale with actionable mitigation triggers

target_users.value: Security operations analysts and NOC engineers at a tier-1 ISP responsible for threat detection and traffic mitigation
target_users.rationale: Primary personas confirmed in discovery sessions

project_class.rationale: ML anomaly detection model with nightly retraining pipeline and analyst feedback loop is the core distinguishing characteristic

capabilities.value: backend, data, ai_llm, analytics, realtime, admin_panel
capabilities.rationale: Streaming ingestion, ML inference, analyst dashboard, RTBH integration, and LLM-assisted investigation explanation all required

complexity_level.rationale: Billions of NetFlow records per day require distributed streaming ingestion and ClickHouse columnar storage for sub-second query response

risk_level.rationale: False positives trigger RTBH blackholing that could disrupt legitimate backbone traffic; false negatives allow attacks to proceed unmitigated

data_sensitivity.rationale: NetFlow records contain IP address pairs which are personal data under GDPR in EU jurisdictions; BGP prefix data is commercially sensitive

external_exposure.rationale: SOC analyst dashboard and RTBH automation are internal-only; no public-facing components

access_model.value: Keycloak SSO with RBAC: soc_analyst (read alerts, create investigations, annotate), soc_lead (approve mitigations), platform_admin (model retraining, system config)
access_model.rationale: RTBH mitigation approval requires elevated role to prevent accidental blackholing

feature_scope.value: NetFlow streaming ingestion pipeline, ML anomaly detection with per-prefix feature vectors, SOC investigation workspace with BGP correlation, RTBH mitigation via BGP community injection, nightly model retraining from analyst feedback, LLM-assisted investigation narrative generation
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature scope defined in SOC tooling requirements

mvp_scope.value: NetFlow ingestion, DDoS amplification detection model, alert dashboard, analyst annotation, RTBH trigger with soc_lead approval gate
mvp_scope.rationale: DDoS amplification is the highest-frequency attack type; detection pipeline and mitigation MVP scoped for 3-month delivery

security_baseline.value: mTLS for all internal service calls within EKS, Keycloak JWT auth, RTBH BGP session authenticated via MD5, ClickHouse data encrypted at rest, secrets in AWS Secrets Manager
security_baseline.rationale: Backbone-facing infrastructure security baseline

frontend_stack.value: React 18 with TypeScript, Grafana embedded panels for time-series metrics, Mapbox for geographic CPE visualization
frontend_stack.rationale: React for investigation workspace; Grafana embedded for ClickHouse query visualizations team already uses

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI for analyst API; Kafka for NetFlow stream ingestion; Flink for streaming feature computation
backend_stack.rationale: Kafka/Flink for high-throughput streaming; FastAPI for low-latency analyst query endpoints

data_platform.value: ClickHouse for NetFlow time-series and feature vectors; PostgreSQL 16 for investigation metadata, alert records, and model versions; Redis 7 for per-prefix traffic baselines and alert deduplication
data_platform.rationale: ClickHouse handles billions of rows with sub-second aggregation queries; PostgreSQL for OLTP investigation state

hosting_target.value: AWS EKS with dedicated node groups for streaming workers and ML inference; Kafka on MSK; ClickHouse on self-managed EC2 cluster in eu-west-1
hosting_target.rationale: EU hosting for GDPR data residency for IP address data; EKS for Kubernetes-native workload management

privacy_retention_policy.value: NetFlow records retained 90 days then deleted; anonymized aggregate statistics retained indefinitely; analyst investigation records retained 3 years for SOC audit
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: GDPR minimization principle limits raw IP-level data retention; SOC investigations require longer audit trail

future_scope.value: BGP hijacking detection model, real-time LLM threat summarization for tier-2 analyst escalation, integration with external threat intelligence feeds (CAIDA, Spamhaus)
future_scope.rationale: Post-MVP model expansion roadmap

constraints.value: NetFlow ingestion must process peak 2M flows/second; ClickHouse queries for analyst dashboard must respond within 2 seconds; RTBH BGP session must be reachable via dedicated out-of-band management network
constraints.rationale: Backbone peak traffic and analyst SLA requirements

observability_baseline.value: Grafana + Prometheus for Kafka lag, Flink checkpoints, ClickHouse query latency, ML model inference latency; PagerDuty for pipeline failures
observability_baseline.rationale: Existing SOC monitoring stack extended for anomaly detection pipeline

execution_preference.value: 3-week sprints with demo to SOC lead after each sprint; model accuracy reviewed by SOC team at end of each retraining cycle
execution_preference.rationale: Tight SOC stakeholder loop ensures model quality and analyst trust

compliance_context.value: GDPR Article 6(1)(f) legitimate interest as lawful basis for processing IP address NetFlow data in EU jurisdiction; data minimization requires raw IP data deleted after 90 days; IP anonymization applied to training datasets before LLM context injection
compliance_context.rationale: High risk level and confidential IP address data trigger GDPR compliance obligations

llm_integration.value: LLM (GPT-4o via Azure OpenAI) used for analyst investigation narrative generation: given anomaly_detection record, correlated bgp_prefixes, and analyst annotations, LLM generates a structured threat summary. IP addresses anonymized before LLM context injection per GDPR minimization principle. LLM output stored as investigation_narrative field, reviewed by analyst before use.
llm_integration.rationale: ai_llm capability requires llm_integration contract field

project.goal: Near-real-time NetFlow anomaly detection at backbone scale with SOC analyst investigation workspace and RTBH mitigation
project.timeline: MVP in 12 weeks

frontend.maps: Mapbox for geographic BGP prefix visualization

backend.ml: scikit-learn isolation forest, XGBoost for supervised detection

reasoner_reviews.product: The DDoS amplification detection MVP is the right first cut. The analyst feedback loop driving nightly retraining is the key differentiator that keeps the model relevant as attack patterns evolve. The LLM narrative feature will reduce tier-1 analyst investigation time significantly but must not become a crutch that bypasses human judgment for RTBH approval.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Flink stateful streaming is the right choice for per-prefix feature vector computation at 2M flows/sec. Isolation Forest as the unsupervised baseline catches novel attacks; XGBoost trained on labeled analyst annotations provides higher-precision detection for known patterns. ClickHouse MergeTree with per-date partitioning enables the 90-day retention policy via TTL expressions.
reasoner_reviews.security: The RTBH approval gate requiring soc_lead role is critical. BGP community injection to upstream providers must be authenticated via MD5 session keys stored in Secrets Manager. LLM context must strip IP addresses before Azure OpenAI API call to comply with GDPR data minimization.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 2M flows/sec requires Kafka MSK with 6+ partition-per-topic and Flink parallelism of at least 12. ClickHouse EC2 cluster needs at least 3 nodes with NVMe storage for 2-second query SLA. Redis baseline store must be sized to hold 30-day rolling baselines for all active BGP prefixes.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Round 1 plan did not specify the Flink windowing strategy for feature vector computation. Must clarify whether tumbling or sliding windows are used for per-prefix flow aggregation and what the window size is, as this directly affects detection latency and false positive rate.

backend.service_design: Three services: netflow-ingestion (Kafka consumer + Flink job for feature computation), detection-api (FastAPI serving anomaly scores and RTBH triggers), investigation-api (FastAPI for analyst workspace CRUD). Flink job uses 5-minute tumbling windows per (src_prefix, dst_prefix, protocol) tuple to compute packet_count, byte_count, pps, bps, mean_packet_size, unique_src_ips.
backend.api_patterns: REST /api/v1. Key endpoints: GET /alerts, GET /alerts/:id, POST /investigations, PUT /investigations/:id/annotate, POST /mitigations (soc_lead only), GET /prefixes/:prefix/history. All authenticated via Keycloak JWT.
backend.failure_handling: Kafka consumer group with dead-letter topic for unparseable NetFlow records. Flink checkpoints every 60 seconds to S3 for exactly-once semantics. Detection API circuit breaker: if ClickHouse query p99 exceeds 5s, fall back to Redis cached baseline scores.

security.auth_design: Keycloak realm with three roles: soc_analyst, soc_lead, platform_admin. FastAPI middleware validates JWT and extracts role for endpoint authorization. RTBH POST endpoint requires soc_lead role enforced at middleware level.
security.secrets_management: BGP session MD5 keys and Azure OpenAI API key stored in AWS Secrets Manager, injected via Kubernetes External Secrets Operator. Never passed to frontend or logged.

data.entities: netflow_records (ClickHouse: date Date, timestamp DateTime, src_prefix String, dst_prefix String, protocol UInt8, packet_count UInt32, byte_count UInt64, pps Float32, bps Float64, TTL 90 DAYS). anomaly_detections (PostgreSQL: id UUID PK, detected_at, src_prefix, attack_type ENUM ddos_amplification/bgp_hijack/scanning, isolation_score FLOAT, xgb_score FLOAT, status ENUM open/investigating/mitigated/false_positive). analyst_investigations (id UUID PK, alert_id FK, analyst_id, created_at, annotation TEXT, investigation_narrative TEXT, resolved_at). mitigation_actions (id UUID PK, alert_id FK, approved_by, bgp_community STRING, rtbh_prefix CIDR, injected_at, withdrawn_at, outcome ENUM success/failed/rolled_back). traffic_baselines (Redis: key prefix_protocol, value JSON of rolling mean/stddev for pps and bps over 30-day window).
data.storage_design: ClickHouse 3-node cluster on EC2 i3en.2xlarge with NVMe for NetFlow time-series. PostgreSQL 16 RDS for OLTP investigation state. Redis ElastiCache for per-prefix baselines.

devops.deployment_topology: EKS eu-west-1 with three node groups: streaming (memory-optimized for Flink), api (compute-optimized for FastAPI), gpu (optional for future deep learning). Kafka MSK with 3 brokers, 12 partitions per topic. ClickHouse on EC2 outside EKS with direct VPC connectivity.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitLab CI: Python lint, pytest unit and integration tests, Docker build, ECR push, Helm chart update in GitOps repo, ArgoCD sync. Flink job packaged as fat JAR deployed via Flink operator on EKS. Nightly retraining runs as Kubernetes CronJob.
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus scrapes Flink metrics (checkpoint lag, records/sec), Kafka consumer lag, FastAPI request rate. Grafana dashboards: ingestion pipeline health, anomaly detection precision/recall trend, RTBH action timeline. PagerDuty: Kafka lag exceeds 5 minutes, Flink job failure, ClickHouse write errors.

ISS-001.title: Flink windowing strategy not specified for feature vector computation
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan described Flink for streaming aggregation but did not specify window type (tumbling vs sliding), window size, or late-data handling strategy. These choices directly determine detection latency and model input consistency.
