previous_plan.title: FWA CPE Signal Quality Monitoring Dashboard v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: React + Go dashboard backed by ClickHouse for FWA CPE signal metrics with Leaflet map and WebSocket live updates.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: React frontend, Go Gin API, ClickHouse for time-series, Redis for latest metric cache, GKE Autopilot hosting.

target_output.thinking_summary: Weak quality round 2 revision: ISS-001 addressed by adding WebSocket hub pattern specification, but overall plan sections will be thinner than strong quality. The fix_report resolves the hub architecture. Data model uses manifest key_entities: cpe_devices, signal_metrics, base_stations, upgrade_candidates. No compliance_context needed for internal data.

fix_report.action_taken: Added WebSocket hub pattern to system_components and workflows: a Go Hub struct maintains a map of registered client channels (max 200 per replica); a broadcast channel receives metric update payloads; hub goroutine fans out to all registered client send channels; clients with full send buffers are disconnected to prevent slow-consumer backpressure; when ClickHouse p99 exceeds 3 seconds, hub broadcasts last Redis-cached metrics with {stale: true} flag in the message payload
fix_report.expected_outcome: Engineering team has a concrete WebSocket concurrency model; staleness handling prevents dashboard showing blank metrics during ClickHouse slowdown

target_output.title: FWA CPE Signal Quality Monitoring Dashboard
target_output.executive_summary: A React and Go web application for an FWA ISP that streams real-time RSRP, SINR, and throughput metrics from CPE devices to support engineers via WebSocket, backed by ClickHouse for time-series queries and Redis for latest-metric caching. Hosted on GKE Autopilot.
target_output.architecture_overview: The signal-api Go service handles both REST queries and WebSocket connections. A background goroutine ingests incoming CPE metric updates into ClickHouse and publishes the latest value to Redis. A Hub goroutine maintains up to 200 registered WebSocket client channels per replica and fans out metric broadcasts. ClickHouse serves historical range queries for time-series charts. Redis sorted sets serve instant sector heatmap rendering.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: React 18 TypeScript, Recharts, Leaflet, React Query, Zustand. API: Go 1.22 Gin with WebSocket via gorilla/websocket. Time-series: ClickHouse. Cache and realtime: Redis 7 Memorystore. Hosting: GKE Autopilot, Cloud SQL PostgreSQL for CPE registry. CI/CD: GitHub Actions. Mapbox GL JS for CPE geographic heatmap overlays keyed by RSRP tier. Cloud Armor WAF rules protecting the signal API from unauthenticated scraping of tower coverage data.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Signal Dashboard: Leaflet CPE map colored by SINR band with slider filter. CPE Detail: Recharts RSRP/SINR/throughput time-series with 1h/6h/24h zoom. Base Station View: sector heatmap from Redis sorted set SINR scores. Upgrade Candidates: engineer-created records with sector context. Weekly Report: auto-generated base station ranking by CPEs below SINR threshold.
target_output.system_components: signal-api Go service: REST endpoints for CPE and metric queries, WebSocket Hub with 200-client cap per replica and per-client buffered channel (size 32), broadcast goroutine with slow-consumer disconnect. ClickHouse: signal_metrics MergeTree partitioned by date. Redis Memorystore: sorted sets per sector for SINR scores, hash per CPE for latest metric values. PostgreSQL Cloud SQL: cpe_devices, base_stations, upgrade_candidates.
target_output.workflows: CPE Metric Ingestion: CPE devices push RSRP, SINR, throughput to collection gateway every 60 seconds; gateway writes batch to ClickHouse signal_metrics and updates Redis hash for cpe_id; Hub broadcast goroutine receives update and sends to all registered WebSocket clients; if client send channel full, client is disconnected. Support Engineer Triage: engineer opens map, sets SINR threshold slider to -10 dB, map re-renders CPE markers below threshold in red; clicks base station sector for drill-down; reviews CPE time-series for persistent degradation; creates upgrade_candidate record tagging the sector.
target_output.data_model: signal_metrics (ClickHouse: date, collected_at, cpe_id, base_station_id, sector_id, rsrp_dbm Float32, sinr_db Float32, throughput_mbps Float32, TTL 730 DAYS). cpe_devices (PostgreSQL: cpe_id UUID PK, base_station_id FK, sector_id, latitude, longitude, status). base_stations (PostgreSQL: id UUID PK, name, latitude, longitude, sector_count). upgrade_candidates (PostgreSQL: id UUID PK, base_station_id, sector_id, reason, reported_by, created_at, resolved_at). Redis: hash cpe:{id}:latest = {rsrp, sinr, throughput, ts}; sorted set sector:{base_id}:{sector_id}:sinr, score=sinr_db.
target_output.api_design: GET /api/v1/cpe returns CPE list with latest signal band. GET /api/v1/cpe/:id/metrics?start&end returns ClickHouse time-series. GET /api/v1/base-stations/:id/summary returns sector SINR distribution from Redis. POST /api/v1/upgrade-candidates creates record. GET /api/v1/reports/weekly returns base station ranking. WebSocket /ws/metrics streams {cpe_id, rsrp, sinr, throughput, ts, stale: bool} payloads.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Custom JWT HMAC-SHA256, 8-hour access tokens. WebSocket upgrade validates JWT. support_engineer: read-only; planning_manager: write upgrade_candidates. TLS on GKE internal load balancer. Read-only ClickHouse service user for signal-api. No customer PII in signal metrics; cpe_id is an internal device identifier.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GKE Autopilot: signal-api Deployment 2 replicas with HPA on CPU 60%, Cloud SQL PostgreSQL, Redis Memorystore. ClickHouse on GCE e2-standard-8 with persistent SSD. GitHub Actions CI: go test, Docker build, Artifact Registry push, kubectl apply. Rolling deploy with health check on /health endpoint. ClickHouse backup job runs nightly via Kubernetes CronJob to GCS bucket with 30-day retention. Alerting: Cloud Monitoring custom metric fires when WebSocket connection count drops to zero for more than 60 seconds, indicating ingest pipeline failure.
target_output.observability: Prometheus: ClickHouse query duration histogram (2-second SLA), WebSocket client count, Hub broadcast latency. Cloud Monitoring: GKE pod restarts, ClickHouse disk usage. Grafana: signal ingestion rate, WebSocket client count trend, SINR threshold breach count by base station.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: GKE Autopilot signal-api ~$150/month, Cloud SQL PostgreSQL ~$80, Redis Memorystore ~$60, ClickHouse GCE e2-standard-8 ~$200, data transfer ~$30. Total ~$520/month. Scales by adding signal-api replicas behind internal load balancer; each replica supports 200 WebSocket clients.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (8 weeks): ClickHouse schema and CPE metric ingestion, signal-api REST and WebSocket Hub, React dashboard with map and time-series, upgrade candidate workflow, GitHub Actions CI, GKE deploy. Phase 2 (4 weeks): weekly upgrade ranking report, base station sector heatmap from Redis, SINR trend alerting. Phase 3: predictive degradation ML model, firmware version correlation.
target_output.development_guidelines: Go services use golangci-lint. WebSocket Hub goroutine must not block on slow client sends; buffered channels and disconnect-on-full enforced. ClickHouse queries must include date partition filter to avoid full-table scans. React components consuming WebSocket subscribe/unsubscribe in useEffect cleanup.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: ClickHouse query SLA breach causes dashboard to show stale Redis metrics; stale flag in WebSocket payload mitigates confusion. Risk: CPE metric ingestion pipeline failure causes signal_metrics gaps; monitor ingestion rate per base station. Tradeoff: Redis sorted sets for sector heatmap duplicate ClickHouse data but provide sub-millisecond map rendering vs 2-second ClickHouse query.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Chose Recharts over Highcharts for open-source licensing. Chose Redis sorted sets for sector heatmap over on-demand ClickHouse aggregation to meet map render latency requirement. Selected GKE Autopilot over standard GKE to reduce node management overhead for internal tooling.

metadata.notes: Round 2 weak quality: ISS-001 WebSocket hub pattern resolved; overall sections are thinner than strong quality. FWA CPE signal quality monitoring dashboard.

profile.domain: PCAP Feature Extraction CLI for ML Traffic Classification

project_goal.value: CLI tool that preprocesses raw PCAP files into structured feature vectors for ML traffic classification models, supporting flow-level statistics, inter-arrival times, and payload byte distributions with optional IP anonymization
project_goal.rationale: Network engineers need reproducible PCAP preprocessing to feed consistent feature vectors into traffic classification model training pipelines

target_users.value: Network engineers and ML researchers preprocessing PCAP captures for model training
target_users.rationale: Primary users are the research team running model training pipelines

project_class.rationale: Command-line tool invoked against PCAP files or directories; no UI, no server

capabilities.rationale: PCAP parsing, flow reconstruction, feature computation, and CSV/Parquet output are the sole capabilities

complexity_level.rationale: Bidirectional flow reconstruction from raw packets and multiple feature extraction profiles add complexity beyond simple parsing

risk_level.rationale: Local CLI tool with no network services, no authentication required

data_sensitivity.rationale: PCAP files contain internal network captures; anonymization feature available for research sharing

external_exposure.rationale: CLI runs locally on the engineer's workstation or CI runner; no network exposure

access_model.value: No authentication required; local file system access only
access_model.rationale: Local CLI tool with no shared access model

feature_scope.value: Bidirectional flow reconstruction from PCAP, feature extraction profiles (flow-level stats, inter-arrival times, payload byte histograms), CSV and Parquet output formats, optional IP anonymization via hashing, extraction config YAML files
feature_scope.rationale: Feature set defined in ML pipeline requirements doc

mvp_scope.value: Flow-level feature extraction (packet count, byte count, duration, pps, bps, mean/stddev inter-arrival time) from single PCAP or directory of PCAPs, CSV output, optional IP hash anonymization
mvp_scope.rationale: Core feature set sufficient for initial model training pipeline integration

security_baseline.value: No network services; read-only PCAP file access; anonymization via SHA-256 hashing of IP addresses
security_baseline.rationale: CLI security baseline requires no server-side controls

frontend_stack.value: None - CLI tool with no UI
frontend_stack.rationale: CLI project class has no frontend

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with Click for CLI, Scapy for PCAP parsing, pandas for feature DataFrame, pyarrow for Parquet output
backend_stack.rationale: Scapy is the standard Python PCAP parsing library; Click for ergonomic CLI; pandas/pyarrow for tabular output

data_platform.value: None - output is CSV or Parquet files written to local filesystem
data_platform.rationale: No database required; output files feed into external ML training pipelines

hosting_target.value: Local execution on engineer workstation or CI runner; distributed via pip package
hosting_target.rationale: CLI tool packaged as a pip-installable package

privacy_retention_policy.value: Input PCAP files are not modified or retained by the tool; output feature files are the engineer's responsibility
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Tool does not store or transmit data

future_scope.value: Payload byte distribution feature profile, inter-arrival time histograms, streaming PCAP processing for large captures, config-driven feature profile selection via YAML
future_scope.rationale: Post-MVP feature extraction expansion

constraints.value: Must process 1GB PCAP in under 5 minutes on a 4-core workstation; output CSV must be compatible with pandas read_csv without additional preprocessing
constraints.rationale: Typical PCAP capture size and model training pipeline compatibility requirement

observability_baseline.value: CLI progress output via tqdm progress bar; verbose flag for per-file processing stats
observability_baseline.rationale: Local CLI observability via stdout

execution_preference.value: Single developer iterative development; pytest unit tests for feature extraction functions
execution_preference.rationale: Small scope CLI tool; single developer sufficient

project.goal: CLI PCAP feature extractor for ML traffic classification model training
project.timeline: MVP in 4 weeks

reasoner_reviews.product: The IP anonymization feature is essential for the research data sharing use case. The YAML config profile is a good future-proofing decision that lets researchers define custom feature sets without code changes.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Scapy is the right choice for bidirectional flow reconstruction. The flow key should be a 5-tuple (src_ip, dst_ip, src_port, dst_port, protocol) with normalized direction (lower IP first) to ensure bidirectional matching. Using Python multiprocessing for per-file parallelism can meet the 5-minute performance constraint on a 4-core workstation.
reasoner_reviews.security: SHA-256 hashing of IP addresses is the correct anonymization approach. Use a fixed per-dataset salt stored in the output metadata to allow consistent anonymization across PCAP batches from the same capture set.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 1GB PCAP at 64-byte average packet size is approximately 16M packets. Scapy single-threaded parsing is slow; multiprocessing.Pool across files in a directory will meet the constraint.

backend.service_design: Single Python package pcapextract with CLI entry point. Modules: parser (Scapy PCAP reading, bidirectional flow reconstruction), features (flow stat computation), anonymizer (SHA-256 IP hashing with salt), writer (pandas DataFrame to CSV/Parquet).

security.auth_design: No authentication; local CLI.

data.entities: flow_records (in-memory pandas DataFrame: flow_id, src_ip, dst_ip, src_port, dst_port, protocol ENUM tcp/udp/icmp/other, first_seen, last_seen, duration_ms, fwd_packets, bwd_packets, total_bytes, pps, bps, mean_iat_ms, stddev_iat_ms, label STRING). pcap_files (filesystem: path, size_bytes, packet_count, flow_count, extraction_duration_s written to per-file stats JSON). output_datasets (filesystem: CSV or Parquet files with feature columns matching flow_records schema).

devops.deployment_topology: Python package distributed via pip. pyproject.toml with entry_point scripts.pcapextract = 'pcapextract.cli:main'. GitHub Actions: pytest, ruff lint, build wheel, publish to internal PyPI on tag.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions on push: ruff lint, pytest with sample PCAP fixtures (synthetically generated pcap files using Scapy in test setup). Release on tag: build wheel and publish.
devops.observability_stack: tqdm progress bar for per-file and per-directory processing. --verbose flag prints per-flow count, per-file extraction duration, and output file size. No external monitoring.

target_output.thinking_summary: A CLI tool with no frontend, no database, and no authentication. Weak quality means some sections will be at minimum word counts. The key architectural decisions are: bidirectional 5-tuple flow reconstruction with Scapy, multiprocessing for parallel PCAP processing to meet the 5-minute SLA, SHA-256 IP anonymization with per-dataset salt file, and pandas/pyarrow for output.
target_output.title: PCAP Feature Extraction CLI for ML Traffic Classification
target_output.executive_summary: A Python 3.12 CLI tool using Click and Scapy that parses raw PCAP files, reconstructs bidirectional flows by 5-tuple, computes flow-level feature vectors, and outputs CSV or Parquet files for ML traffic classification model training. Supports optional SHA-256 IP anonymization with a per-dataset salt file for research data sharing.
target_output.architecture_overview: Single Python package pcapextract with four modules: parser handles PCAP reading and bidirectional flow reconstruction via Scapy; features computes flow statistics from reconstructed flows; anonymizer applies SHA-256 hashing to IP addresses using a per-dataset salt; writer outputs pandas DataFrames to CSV or Parquet via pyarrow. CLI entry point via Click. Parallel PCAP processing uses multiprocessing.Pool across files in a directory to meet the 1GB-in-5-minutes performance constraint.
target_output.technology_stack: Python 3.12, Click for CLI, Scapy for PCAP parsing and flow reconstruction, pandas for in-memory feature DataFrames, pyarrow for Parquet serialization, tqdm for progress display, multiprocessing.Pool for parallel file processing. Packaged as pip wheel via pyproject.toml. pytest with synthetic PCAP fixtures generated via scapy's PacketList for unit tests covering each flow feature extractor. hypothesis for property-based testing of the IP address normalisation and port bucketing logic.
target_output.functional_feature_map: extract command: reads single PCAP or directory, reconstructs bidirectional flows, computes flow_stats profile features, optionally anonymizes IPs, writes CSV or Parquet output. IP Anonymization: --anonymize flag replaces src_ip and dst_ip with SHA-256(ip+salt) hex strings; salt auto-generated if no --salt-file provided. Verbose mode: prints per-file packet count, flow count, extraction duration, and output row count.
target_output.system_components: cli.py: Click command group with extract, profiles, version subcommands. parser.py: Scapy rdpcap loader, 5-tuple flow key normalization, bidirectional accumulator, 120-second idle timeout. features.py: flow stat computation (duration_ms, pps, bps, mean_iat_ms, stddev_iat_ms). anonymizer.py: SHA-256 with salt. writer.py: pandas to CSV via to_csv and to Parquet via pyarrow.
target_output.workflows: Single PCAP Extraction: engineer runs pcapextract extract --input capture.pcap --output features.csv --format csv --profile flow_stats; parser reads all packets, accumulates per-flow packet lists keyed by normalized 5-tuple, closes flows on 120-second idle timeout; features module computes stats per flow; writer exports DataFrame to CSV. Batch Directory Extraction: multiprocessing.Pool maps extraction function across all .pcap files in directory; results concatenated into single output file; tqdm shows per-file progress in main process.
target_output.data_model: flow_records (in-memory pandas DataFrame): flow_id UUID, src_ip STRING, dst_ip STRING, src_port INT, dst_port INT, protocol ENUM tcp/udp/icmp/other, first_seen FLOAT epoch, last_seen FLOAT epoch, duration_ms FLOAT, fwd_packets INT, bwd_packets INT, total_bytes INT, pps FLOAT, bps FLOAT, mean_iat_ms FLOAT, stddev_iat_ms FLOAT, label STRING. When --anonymize: src_ip and dst_ip replaced with SHA-256 hex digest. pcap_files: per-file stats JSON written alongside output file.
target_output.api_design: CLI interface only. pcapextract extract --input PATH --output PATH --format csv|parquet --profile flow_stats [--anonymize] [--salt-file PATH] [--label LABEL] [--verbose]. pcapextract profiles prints available extraction profile names and their computed feature columns. pcapextract version prints package version.
target_output.security_and_compliance: No network services or authentication. SHA-256 IP anonymization with per-dataset salt ensures consistent anonymization across batch runs. Salt stored in output_dir/salt.txt with warning if salt file already exists. Original IPs never written to output when --anonymize is active. Input PCAPs read-only; tool never modifies source files.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Distributed as pip wheel. pyproject.toml defines entry point pcapextract pointing to pcapextract.cli:main. GitHub Actions on push: ruff lint, pytest with synthetic PCAP fixtures. Tag release builds wheel and publishes to internal PyPI server. No server deployment. Docker image (python:3.12-slim + libpcap-dev) published to internal registry for containerised ML pipeline integration. The image allows pcapextract to run inside Kubeflow pipeline steps without per-node libpcap installation. Version pinned in the internal PyPI mirror to ensure reproducible ML experiment environments.
target_output.observability: tqdm progress bar per file with packet count and flow count displayed on completion. --verbose flag logs per-file extraction duration in seconds and output row count to stderr. Per-file stats JSON written to output directory for pipeline integration.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero infrastructure cost; runs on engineer workstation. Performance scales linearly with CPU cores via multiprocessing.Pool. 1GB PCAP processes in under 5 minutes on 4-core workstation using 4 parallel workers.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (4 weeks): Scapy parser with bidirectional flow reconstruction, flow_stats feature profile, CSV output, IP anonymization, multiprocessing parallel processing, Click CLI, pytest suite, pip packaging. Phase 2 (3 weeks): Parquet output via pyarrow, inter-arrival time histogram feature profile, YAML config-driven feature profile selection. Phase 3: streaming mode for large PCAPs exceeding RAM, payload byte histogram profile.
target_output.development_guidelines: ruff for linting with isort integration. Feature computation functions must be pure functions with no side effects to enable pytest unit testing without real PCAP files. Test fixtures generate synthetic Scapy packets using Scapy PacketList. CLI commands must not import Scapy at module level to keep --help fast; import inside command function.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Scapy PCAP parsing speed is slower than libpcap-based tools; multiprocessing mitigates for directory batches but single large PCAPs are still single-threaded. Mitigation: document limitation; future phase adds streaming chunk mode. Tradeoff: pandas in-memory DataFrame limits maximum single-PCAP flow count to available RAM; 1GB PCAP typically produces under 500K flows which is manageable on 8GB workstations.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Chose Scapy over dpkt for richer protocol parsing and better bidirectional flow reconstruction support. Chose 5-minute 4-core performance target as acceptable for batch preprocessing; real-time streaming is a future phase. Chose SHA-256 with salt file over deterministic hashing to prevent reverse-lookup attacks on IP addresses in shared research datasets.

metadata.notes: CLI tool for PCAP feature extraction; no frontend, no database, no auth. Weak quality with some thin sections.

profile.domain: Telecom DC Consolidation to OpenStack Private Cloud

project_goal.value: Consolidate fragmented on-premises server estate across 12 regional data centers into a centralized OpenStack private cloud, decommissioning legacy bare-metal servers and reducing co-location and power costs by an estimated 40%
project_goal.rationale: 12 regional DCs have uncoordinated growth, excess stranded capacity, and rising co-lo costs; centralization and virtualization target 40% cost reduction

target_users.value: Infrastructure engineering team and DC operations staff executing the consolidation program
target_users.rationale: Internal program with no external user-facing components

project_class.rationale: DC consolidation is a platform infrastructure program with Ansible automation, not a user-facing application

capabilities.rationale: OpenStack provisioning, Ansible automation, migration batch orchestration, and cost tracking are the core capabilities

complexity_level.rationale: 12 regional DCs, hundreds of physical servers, live workload migration with service continuity requirements

risk_level.rationale: Live workload migration with service continuity risk; storage volume replication errors can cause data loss; network cutover can cause extended outages

data_sensitivity.rationale: Server inventory, network topology, and workload configurations are confidential internal infrastructure data; some workloads may have data residency requirements

external_exposure.rationale: All consolidation tooling and OpenStack management plane are on internal network only

access_model.value: Keycloak RBAC: infrastructure_engineer (execute migrations), dc_lead (approve migration waves), platform_admin (OpenStack admin, Ansible vault)
access_model.rationale: Wave approval gate prevents unauthorized production server decommissioning

feature_scope.value: Discovery phase inventorying all 12 DC physical servers and workloads, compatibility assessment with OpenStack, migration wave planning, Ansible-driven VM provisioning on OpenStack, live block migration storage replication, traffic cutover during maintenance windows, service health validation, server decommissioning, cost savings dashboard
feature_scope.rationale: Full consolidation lifecycle from discovery to decommission

mvp_scope.value: Discovery and wave planning for first 2 DCs, Ansible OpenStack VM provisioning, storage live migration for 20 pilot workloads, health check validation, cost savings baseline measurement
mvp_scope.rationale: Pilot 2 DCs to validate migration automation before scaling to all 12

security_baseline.value: Ansible vault for all credentials; encryption at rest for all OpenStack Cinder volumes; no plain-text passwords in playbooks or CI; Keycloak for management API auth
security_baseline.rationale: Internal security policy mandates encryption at rest for consolidated storage per compliance_context

frontend_stack.value: None - infrastructure project; no user-facing frontend
frontend_stack.rationale: Infrastructure consolidation program has no UI requirement

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with Ansible for infrastructure automation; FastAPI for internal migration management API
backend_stack.rationale: Ansible for declarative infrastructure automation; FastAPI for migration state API consumed by GitLab CI pipelines

data_platform.value: MySQL 8 for migration state tracking, server inventory, and cost records; no real-time database required
data_platform.rationale: MySQL for migration orchestration state; existing MySQL operations team familiarity

hosting_target.value: OpenStack private cloud as the target platform; GitLab CI for pipeline orchestration on existing bare-metal Kubernetes
hosting_target.rationale: OpenStack is the consolidation target; existing GitLab CI for Ansible playbook execution

privacy_retention_policy.value: Server inventory and migration records retained 5 years for asset management; cost comparison invoices retained 7 years for finance audit
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Asset management and finance audit retention requirements

future_scope.value: Automated workload compatibility scoring, continuous cost optimization recommendations, capacity planning dashboard for OpenStack cloud
future_scope.rationale: Post-consolidation operational tooling roadmap

constraints.value: Maximum 4-hour maintenance window per migration batch; rollback must be possible within 30 minutes of cutover; no more than 5 simultaneous live migrations per OpenStack hypervisor
constraints.rationale: Service continuity SLA and OpenStack hypervisor I/O bandwidth limits

observability_baseline.value: Grafana + Prometheus for OpenStack VM metrics; GitLab CI pipeline status; cost comparison dashboard in Grafana
observability_baseline.rationale: OpenStack and GitLab standard monitoring

execution_preference.value: Sequential DC-by-DC migration with full validation before proceeding to next DC
execution_preference.rationale: Reduces simultaneous migration risk; allows lessons-learned application between DCs

compliance_context.value: Internal security policy mandates encryption at rest for all consolidated OpenStack Cinder storage volumes; workloads with data residency requirements (EU subscriber data) must remain in EU-region OpenStack availability zones; all production server decommissioning requires infrastructure steering committee sign-off via ITSM change record
compliance_context.rationale: High risk and confidential data sensitivity trigger compliance context

project.goal: Consolidate 12 regional DCs into centralized OpenStack private cloud with Ansible automation
project.timeline: Pilot 2 DCs in 16 weeks

backend.migrations: Ansible OpenStack collection for VM and volume provisioning

security.encryption: Cinder volume encryption at rest mandatory

data.db: MySQL 8 for migration state and inventory

reasoner_reviews.product: Sequential DC-by-DC approach is the right strategy for a 12-DC program. The cost savings dashboard comparing co-lo and power invoices before and after each wave is the key stakeholder artifact that justifies continued investment.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Ansible OpenStack collection (openstack.cloud) provides idempotent VM and Cinder volume provisioning. Live block migration via OpenStack cold migration with Cinder volume replication is the safest approach. Migration wave grouping should order workloads by criticality descending to migrate the least critical first.
reasoner_reviews.security: Ansible vault must encrypt all OpenStack credentials and Keycloak service account tokens. GitLab CI must use GitLab Vault integration; no secrets in CI environment variables. Cinder volume encryption via LUKS backend is the correct OpenStack approach.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 5 concurrent migrations per hypervisor is a hard constraint. The Ansible playbook must check current live migration count on the target hypervisor before triggering each migration. The 30-minute rollback window requires pre-staged rollback playbooks that can revert VM and volume state on the source hardware.

backend.service_design: migration-api (FastAPI): REST endpoints for discovery triggers, migration wave management, health check status, cost tracking. Ansible playbooks in GitLab repo executed by GitLab CI manual pipelines or migration-api Celery tasks.
backend.api_patterns: REST /api/v1: GET /servers returns inventory, POST /migration-waves creates wave, PUT /migration-waves/:id/approve (dc_lead), POST /migration-waves/:id/execute triggers Ansible, GET /cost-report returns savings comparison.
backend.failure_handling: Ansible check mode for dry-run before production execution. Health check playbook verifies VM ping, service port response, and application health endpoint within 4-hour maintenance window. If health check fails, rollback.yml runs automatically via GitLab CI downstream pipeline.

security.auth_design: Keycloak realm: infrastructure_engineer, dc_lead, platform_admin. FastAPI JWT middleware. dc_lead approval required for wave execution.
security.secrets_management: Ansible vault encrypts OpenStack RC credentials, SSH keys for DC servers, Keycloak service account token. GitLab CI uses GitLab-integrated Vault for vault password injection. No credentials in GitLab CI environment variables.

data.entities: data_centers (id INT PK, name, city, country, co_lo_monthly_cost, power_kw, server_count, status ENUM active/draining/decommissioned). physical_servers (id UUID PK, dc_id FK, hostname, cpu_cores, ram_gb, storage_tb, os, workload_count, openstack_compatible BOOL, migration_batch_id FK). vm_instances (id UUID PK, server_id FK, openstack_instance_id, target_hypervisor, vcpus, ram_gb, status ENUM provisioning/active/decommissioned). migration_waves (id UUID PK, dc_id FK, status ENUM planned/approved/executing/completed/rolled_back, planned_start, actual_start, actual_end, itsm_change_ref, approved_by). cost_records (id UUID PK, dc_id FK, month DATE, co_lo_cost, power_cost, server_count_active, notes).
data.storage_design: MySQL 8 single-node on existing management Kubernetes. Alembic for schema migrations. No real-time data store required.

devops.deployment_topology: migration-api on bare-metal Kubernetes management cluster. GitLab CI runners on management network with OpenStack API reachability and SSH access to DC servers. OpenStack target cluster on consolidated DC hardware.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitLab CI pipeline per migration wave: manual trigger by dc_lead, runs discovery check, provision-vms, migrate-volumes, cutover, health-check stages in sequence. Rollback stage triggered on health-check failure. Pipeline approval via GitLab protected environment requiring dc_lead role.
devops.rollback_strategy: Rollback playbook deletes provisioned OpenStack VMs, restores source server routing, re-enables source server services. Must complete within 30-minute window. GitLab pipeline monitoring ensures rollback starts within 5 minutes of health check failure detection.
devops.observability_stack: Grafana + Prometheus for OpenStack VM CPU/memory, migration task duration, live migration count per hypervisor. Cost dashboard compares monthly co_lo_cost and power_cost from cost_records table. PagerDuty: migration health check failure, rollback triggered.

target_output.thinking_summary: First pass infrastructure project: DC consolidation to OpenStack. No frontend, Ansible-driven automation, MySQL for migration state, Keycloak RBAC. compliance_context required for encryption at rest and data residency. Moderate quality means most sections are good but 1-2 may be slightly thin. Key insight: Ansible OpenStack collection for idempotent VM and Cinder volume provisioning with migration_wave state machine.
target_output.title: Telecom Regional DC Consolidation to OpenStack Private Cloud
target_output.executive_summary: An Ansible-driven infrastructure program consolidating a telecom operator's server estate across 12 regional data centers into a centralized OpenStack private cloud, targeting 40% reduction in co-location and power costs. Discovery playbooks inventory all physical servers, generate compatibility assessments, and group workloads into migration waves ordered by criticality. Each wave provisions equivalent VMs on OpenStack via the openstack.cloud Ansible collection, replicates Cinder storage volumes, performs traffic cutover in 4-hour maintenance windows, and validates service health before decommissioning source hardware. A FastAPI migration management API tracks wave state and serves cost savings reports to the infrastructure steering committee.
target_output.architecture_overview: The program has three logical layers: automation (Ansible playbooks in GitLab executing against DC servers and OpenStack API), orchestration (migration-api FastAPI service tracking migration_waves state machine in MySQL), and target platform (OpenStack Yoga private cloud). GitLab CI pipelines execute Ansible stages sequentially per migration wave: discovery, provision-vms, migrate-volumes, cutover, health-check, with a rollback stage triggered automatically on health check failure. All credentials are Ansible vault encrypted with vault password injected via GitLab-integrated Vault. Cinder volume encryption at rest via LUKS is mandatory for all migrated volumes.
target_output.technology_stack: Automation: Python 3.12, Ansible 9 with openstack.cloud collection, Ansible Vault. Orchestration: Python 3.12 FastAPI, Celery for async pipeline triggers. Data: MySQL 8 for migration state and inventory, Alembic for schema migrations. Auth: Keycloak RBAC. CI/CD: GitLab CI with protected environment approval gate. Observability: Grafana + Prometheus. Target platform: OpenStack Yoga on consolidated bare-metal.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Discovery: SSH-based lshw inventory across all 12 DCs, OpenStack compatibility assessment (vCPU ratio, supported OS, network driver), compatibility_score written to physical_servers table. Wave Planning: group servers into migration waves by DC and criticality, create migration_waves records, ITSM change request integration. VM Provisioning: idempotent openstack.cloud.server playbook creating VMs with matching vCPU and RAM on target hypervisor, respecting max-5-concurrent-migrations constraint. Live Migration: Cinder block migration with progress monitoring. Health Check: ping, port check, application health endpoint validation within 4-hour window. Cost Dashboard: Grafana chart of co_lo_cost + power_cost per DC month-on-month from cost_records.
target_output.system_components: migration-api FastAPI service: REST endpoints for discovery, wave management, cost reporting, Celery task dispatch. GitLab CI pipelines: per-wave pipeline with manual dc_lead approval gate, sequential Ansible stage execution, automatic rollback on health check failure. Ansible playbook repository: discovery.yml, provision-vms.yml, migrate-volumes.yml, cutover.yml, health-check.yml, rollback.yml. MySQL 8: data_centers, physical_servers, vm_instances, migration_waves, cost_records. OpenStack Yoga: Nova for VM scheduling, Cinder for block storage with LUKS encryption, Neutron for network segment mapping.
target_output.workflows: Migration Wave Execution: dc_lead approves migration_wave in migration-api, which creates ITSM change record via ServiceNow API; GitLab CI pipeline triggered; discovery stage verifies source servers healthy; provision-vms stage creates OpenStack VMs respecting hypervisor concurrency limit (Ansible loop with check on Nova live-migration-count before each VM); migrate-volumes stage initiates Cinder block migration with progress polling; cutover stage reassigns network segment and disables source server NIC; health-check stage waits 15 minutes then validates VM ping, port 443, and /health endpoint; on success, marks wave completed and raises decommission ticket; on failure, rollback stage deletes OpenStack VMs, re-enables source NIC, marks wave=rolled_back. Cost Tracking: monthly GitLab CI job reads DC invoices from shared drive, inserts cost_records rows, Grafana dashboard renders savings trend.
target_output.data_model: data_centers (id INT PK, name, city, country, co_lo_monthly_cost DECIMAL, power_kw DECIMAL, status ENUM active/draining/decommissioned). physical_servers (id UUID PK, dc_id FK, hostname, cpu_cores INT, ram_gb INT, storage_tb DECIMAL, os, openstack_compatible BOOL, compatibility_score TINYINT, migration_batch_id FK). vm_instances (id UUID PK, server_id FK, openstack_instance_id VARCHAR, target_hypervisor, vcpus INT, ram_gb INT, status ENUM provisioning/active/decommissioned). migration_waves (id UUID PK, dc_id FK, status ENUM planned/approved/executing/completed/rolled_back, itsm_change_ref, approved_by, planned_start, actual_end). cost_records (id UUID PK, dc_id FK, month DATE, co_lo_cost DECIMAL, power_cost DECIMAL, server_count_active INT).
target_output.api_design: REST /api/v1 with Keycloak JWT. GET /data-centers returns DC list with status. POST /data-centers/:id/discover triggers discovery playbook. GET /migration-waves returns wave list with status filter. POST /migration-waves creates wave for DC. PUT /migration-waves/:id/approve requires dc_lead role, sets ITSM change ref. POST /migration-waves/:id/execute triggers GitLab CI pipeline. GET /cost-report returns savings comparison by DC and month.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Keycloak RBAC: infrastructure_engineer executes non-destructive playbooks; dc_lead approves wave execution; platform_admin manages OpenStack and Ansible vault. Ansible vault encrypts all OpenStack RC credentials, DC SSH keys, ServiceNow API token; vault password injected via GitLab-integrated HashiCorp Vault. Cinder LUKS encryption at rest enabled via OpenStack volume type with encrypted backend for all migrated volumes. EU-region workloads provisioned exclusively to EU availability zones via AZ constraint in provision-vms.yml. ITSM change record required for all production wave executions, enforced in migration-api approve endpoint. Decommission of physical servers requires infrastructure steering committee sign-off recorded in migration_waves.approved_by.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: migration-api deployed on existing bare-metal Kubernetes management cluster as 2-replica Deployment with internal LoadBalancer. MySQL on management Kubernetes via CloudNativePG or existing MySQL RDS equivalent. GitLab CI runners on management network with OpenStack API endpoint and DC server SSH connectivity. OpenStack Yoga installed on consolidated DC bare-metal via Ansible openstack-ansible deployment. Rolling wave execution: pilot 2 DCs in months 1-4, scale to remaining 10 DCs over 18 months. Each wave: 4-hour maintenance window, automated health check, 30-minute rollback window.
target_output.observability: Prometheus scrapes: Ansible task duration via custom callback plugin, live migration count per hypervisor from Nova API, OpenStack VM CPU/memory. Grafana dashboards: migration wave status Gantt chart, live migrations per hypervisor vs 5-limit, cost savings trend per DC (co_lo + power monthly comparison). PagerDuty: health check failure triggering rollback, live migration limit exceeded, GitLab pipeline stuck for more than 30 minutes.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Infrastructure cost: OpenStack bare-metal CapEx replaces co-lo OpEx; target 40% savings on current $2M annual co-lo and power spend. Migration tooling operational cost: GitLab CI minutes, Kubernetes management cluster. Scales to all 12 DCs by parameterizing Ansible inventory; no architecture changes required for additional DCs.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 Pilot (16 weeks): discovery automation for 2 pilot DCs, Ansible VM provisioning and Cinder migration playbooks, health check and rollback playbooks, migration-api with wave management, ITSM integration, cost_records tracking, GitLab CI pipeline. Phase 2 (12 weeks): scale to remaining 10 DCs, parallel wave execution for non-overlapping DCs with hypervisor concurrency enforcement, capacity planning dashboard. Phase 3 (ongoing): automated workload compatibility scoring for future server additions, continuous cost optimization recommendations.
target_output.development_guidelines: Ansible playbooks must run in check mode successfully before production execution. All OpenStack API calls use openstack.cloud collection modules for idempotency; no raw Nova/Cinder REST calls from Ansible. Ansible inventory generated dynamically from physical_servers MySQL table via a dynamic inventory script. pytest-ansible for playbook unit testing in CI. GitLab CI protected environments require dc_lead approval; no manual pipeline execution in production without approval.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Cinder live block migration takes longer than 4-hour maintenance window for large volumes. Mitigation: pre-migrate storage volumes in the preceding week using Cinder volume replication before the maintenance window cutover. Risk: OpenStack hypervisor capacity insufficient for all VMs if sizing is incorrect. Mitigation: capacity check in provision-vms.yml verifies available hypervisor resources before provisioning. Tradeoff: MySQL chosen over PostgreSQL due to existing team expertise; acceptable for migration management OLTP workload.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Chose OpenStack Yoga as the stable LTS release over newer versions for multi-year support alignment. Selected Ansible openstack.cloud collection over direct SDK calls for idempotency guarantees. Decided to use GitLab CI protected environments for wave approval rather than a separate approval workflow tool.

metadata.notes: DC consolidation to OpenStack with Ansible automation, migration wave state machine, Cinder LUKS encryption compliance, and cost savings tracking.

profile.domain: GitOps SD-WAN Policy Management Research Prototype

project_goal.value: Research prototype applying GitOps principles to SD-WAN overlay policy management, allowing researchers to define routing intents as declarative YAML manifests in Git and observe an automated reconciliation controller applying changes to a multi-site Mininet testbed
project_goal.rationale: GitOps for network policy management is an under-explored research area; the prototype provides a reproducible experimental platform for measuring convergence time and drift conditions

target_users.value: Network researchers studying GitOps control loops for SD-WAN and programmable networking
target_users.rationale: Academic research group with 5-10 researchers using the prototype

project_class.rationale: Experimental platform for reproducible networking research; not a production product

capabilities.rationale: Go reconciliation controller, ArgoCD-style GitOps, Mininet testbed integration, and convergence analytics are the core capabilities

complexity_level.rationale: Kubernetes custom controller with etcd state, Mininet northbound API integration, and convergence measurement pipeline

risk_level.rationale: Research prototype with authenticated access; no production network exposure but requires protecting testbed control plane

data_sensitivity.rationale: Routing intent manifests and testbed topology are internal research data

external_exposure.rationale: Researcher dashboard accessible via authenticated web interface on university network

access_model.value: Custom JWT with researcher and admin roles; researchers can commit manifests and view experiments; admins manage testbed topology
access_model.rationale: Simple JWT sufficient for research prototype; no external auth provider needed

feature_scope.value: Git repository-driven routing intent manifest submission, schema validation of YAML intents against SD-WAN policy schema, reconciliation controller applying intents to Mininet testbed, convergence time measurement, drift detection between intended and observed state, experiment rollback via Git revert, audit trail of reconciliation events
feature_scope.rationale: Full GitOps control loop with measurement instrumentation for research purposes

mvp_scope.value: Single-site Mininet testbed, YAML intent schema validation, reconciliation controller detecting Git commits and applying overlay tunnel policies, convergence time measurement, reconciliation event log
mvp_scope.rationale: Single-site prototype sufficient for initial convergence time research paper

security_baseline.value: Custom JWT for researcher API access; Mininet testbed control plane isolated from external network; etcd data not publicly accessible; Git repository requires authentication
security_baseline.rationale: Research prototype security baseline for university network environment

frontend_stack.value: React 18 with TypeScript for researcher dashboard showing reconciliation event feed and convergence metrics
frontend_stack.rationale: React for interactive experiment monitoring and convergence chart visualization

backend_stack.value: Go 1.22 with Gin for reconciliation controller API and researcher dashboard API
backend_stack.rationale: Go for Kubernetes-style controller-runtime patterns and low-memory controller process

data_platform.value: etcd for routing intent desired state and observed network state; PostgreSQL 16 for experiment metadata and convergence measurement records
data_platform.rationale: etcd for Kubernetes-native key-value state store matching GitOps controller patterns; PostgreSQL for analytical query capability on convergence measurements

hosting_target.value: Bare-metal Kubernetes on university research cluster; Mininet testbed on dedicated bare-metal servers
hosting_target.rationale: University research cluster with Kubernetes; Mininet requires bare-metal Linux for network namespace operations

privacy_retention_policy.value: Experiment data retained for 3 years for research reproducibility; routing intent manifests retained in Git indefinitely as research artifacts
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Academic research data retention requirement for paper reproducibility

future_scope.value: Multi-site Mininet topology, integration with hardware SD-WAN controllers for live testbed, statistical analysis dashboard for paper publication, automated experiment batch runner
future_scope.rationale: Research roadmap beyond initial prototype

constraints.value: Reconciliation controller must detect Git commit within 30 seconds of push; convergence measurement precision must be at least 100ms resolution; testbed limited to 10 virtual sites in Mininet
constraints.rationale: Research measurement precision requirements and university testbed hardware limits

observability_baseline.value: Grafana + Prometheus for controller loop latency and convergence time distribution; ArgoCD-style sync status in researcher dashboard
observability_baseline.rationale: Standard Kubernetes observability stack on university research cluster

execution_preference.value: Iterative research development with biweekly group demos; prioritize measurement instrumentation quality for paper publication
execution_preference.rationale: Academic research cadence

project.goal: GitOps SD-WAN policy reconciliation research prototype with convergence measurement
project.timeline: Prototype in 12 weeks

backend.testbed: Mininet northbound REST API for policy application

data.state_store: etcd for desired and observed state

reasoner_reviews.product: Convergence measurement precision and audit trail completeness are the primary research outputs. The reconciliation event audit trail must include both the intended state at commit time and the observed state after policy application to support the drift analysis in the paper.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Using Kubernetes controller-runtime with a custom CRD RoutingIntent resource and etcd as the state store matches the GitOps controller pattern exactly. The Git polling interval can be reduced to 10 seconds using a GitHub webhook for sub-30-second detection. The reconciliation controller should emit reconciliation_events with nanosecond timestamps for 100ms measurement resolution.
reasoner_reviews.security: Custom JWT for a research prototype is appropriate. etcd should have TLS client certificates for controller access. Mininet testbed should be isolated to a separate VLAN with controller-only access to the Mininet northbound API.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Rounds 1-2 did not specify the RoutingIntent CRD schema or the reconciliation controller state machine. Round 3 must define the CRD fields, the controller reconcile loop logic (observed vs desired diff computation), and the Mininet northbound API call sequence for policy application.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The Mininet testbed operates in an isolated VM with no production network access — the prototype must function entirely offline with no external API calls. The RoutingIntent CRD reconciliation loop must complete within 5 seconds of a git commit to qualify as a research-grade convergence measurement. Kubernetes controller-runtime v0.17+ is required for the structured logging API used in convergence event audit trail output.

backend.service_design: Two Go services: intent-controller (Kubernetes controller-runtime watching RoutingIntent CRDs, reconciling with Mininet testbed) and research-api (Gin serving researcher dashboard REST endpoints and JWT auth). intent-controller also handles Git polling webhook receipt.

security.auth_design: Custom JWT HMAC-SHA256 issued by research-api login endpoint. Researcher role: read events, submit intents. Admin role: manage testbed topology, reset experiments. Kubernetes etcd access via TLS client certs for intent-controller only.

data.entities: routing_intents (etcd: key /intents/{id}, value RoutingIntent CRD YAML). network_states (etcd: key /observed/{site_id}, value JSON of current tunnel and routing table state). experiments (PostgreSQL: id UUID PK, title, researcher_id, created_at, status ENUM running/completed/archived). reconciliation_events (PostgreSQL: id UUID PK, experiment_id FK, intent_version, event_type ENUM applied/drifted/timeout/rollback, intended_state_hash, observed_state_hash, convergence_ms INT, recorded_at TIMESTAMPTZ). policy_versions (PostgreSQL: id UUID PK, experiment_id FK, git_commit_sha CHAR(40), manifest_yaml TEXT, applied_at, rolled_back_at).
data.storage_design: etcd 3-node cluster on Kubernetes for real-time desired and observed state with 1-second heartbeat. PostgreSQL 16 for experiment metadata and convergence analytics queries for paper publication.

devops.deployment_topology: Bare-metal Kubernetes on university cluster: intent-controller Deployment (1 replica, Kubernetes RBAC for CRD access), research-api Deployment (2 replicas), PostgreSQL StatefulSet, etcd 3-node StatefulSet. Mininet testbed on 2 dedicated bare-metal Linux servers with Mininet northbound REST API on management interface.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: go test, golangci-lint, Docker build, push to GitHub Container Registry. ArgoCD syncs research cluster from GitOps manifests repo. Mininet testbed reset job in Kubernetes CronJob running nightly.
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus scrapes: controller reconcile loop duration, reconcile queue depth, Mininet API call latency, convergence_ms histogram. Grafana dashboards: reconciliation event timeline, convergence time CDF for paper charts, Git detection latency distribution.

previous_plan.title: GitOps SD-WAN Reconciliation Research Prototype v2
previous_plan.executive_summary: Go reconciliation controller watching RoutingIntent CRDs in etcd, applying changes to Mininet testbed via northbound API. GitHub webhook for sub-30-second manifest detection. React researcher dashboard.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: intent-controller Go service watches etcd for RoutingIntent changes, reconciles with Mininet northbound API. research-api serves researcher dashboard. etcd for state; PostgreSQL for analytics.
previous_plan.system_components: intent-controller: CRD watch, Git webhook, Mininet integration. research-api: JWT auth, experiment and event endpoints. etcd: desired and observed state. PostgreSQL: experiments and convergence measurements.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 3 resolves ISS-001 by defining the RoutingIntent CRD schema with sites[], policy_version, experiment_id in spec and observed_state_hash, convergence_ms, phase in status. Resolves ISS-002 by specifying the reconcile loop: hash desired spec SHA-256, compare to etcd observed_state_hash, compute per-site diff, call Mininet northbound REST API per changed site, poll observed state every 100ms until hash matches or 60-second timeout, record convergence_ms.

fix_report.action_taken: Defined RoutingIntent CRD spec fields: sites (array of {site_id string, overlay_tunnel_type enum gre/vxlan/ipsec, dst_prefix CIDR, next_hop IP, metric int}), policy_version string, experiment_id UUID; status fields: observed_state_hash SHA-256 hex, last_reconciled_at RFC3339, convergence_ms int, phase enum Pending/Reconciling/Converged/Drifted; added CRD OpenAPI validation schema for overlay_tunnel_type enum and CIDR format
fix_report.expected_outcome: CRD is implementable directly from the spec; Kubernetes API server validates YAML manifests against OpenAPI schema before reconciliation

fix_report.action_taken: Specified reconcile loop: (1) hash RoutingIntent spec JSON with SHA-256 to produce desired_state_hash; (2) read etcd key /observed/{experiment_id} for current observed_state_hash; (3) if hashes equal, set phase=Converged and return; (4) compute per-site diff between desired sites[] and observed sites list; (5) for each changed site, call Mininet northbound REST PUT /sites/{site_id}/policy with tunnel_type, dst_prefix, next_hop, metric; (6) start 100ms polling loop reading Mininet GET /sites/{site_id}/state; (7) recompute observed_state_hash on each poll; (8) when observed_state_hash equals desired_state_hash, record convergence_ms as elapsed time, update etcd observed key, set phase=Converged; (9) on 60-second timeout, set phase=Drifted and record reconciliation_event with event_type=timeout
fix_report.expected_outcome: Controller implementation has unambiguous reconcile loop logic; convergence_ms measurements are deterministic and comparable across experiments; researcher can reproduce results from the audit trail

target_output.title: GitOps SD-WAN Overlay Policy Reconciliation Research Prototype
target_output.executive_summary: A research prototype implementing a Kubernetes-style GitOps control loop for SD-WAN overlay policy management. Researchers define routing intents as RoutingIntent YAML CRDs in a Git repository; a Go reconciliation controller detects commits via GitHub webhook, validates YAML against the CRD OpenAPI schema, applies policy changes to a multi-site Mininet testbed via northbound REST API, and measures convergence time at 100ms resolution. Reconciliation events and convergence measurements are stored in PostgreSQL for analytical queries supporting research paper publication. A React dashboard presents the reconciliation event timeline and convergence time distribution.
target_output.architecture_overview: Two Go services run on bare-metal Kubernetes: intent-controller implements a Kubernetes controller-runtime reconcile loop watching RoutingIntent CRDs stored in etcd, receives GitHub webhook push events, validates intent manifests, applies policies to Mininet testbed sites via REST, and measures convergence by polling observed state at 100ms intervals until desired and observed state hashes match. research-api serves the React researcher dashboard with JWT auth, experiment management, and reconciliation event queries. etcd stores real-time desired and observed state; PostgreSQL stores experiment metadata and convergence measurements for analytics.
target_output.technology_stack: Controller: Go 1.22 with controller-runtime, Kubernetes client-go. API: Go 1.22 Gin with custom JWT. State store: etcd 3-node cluster. Analytics: PostgreSQL 16. Frontend: React 18 TypeScript, Recharts, React Query, Monaco editor for YAML. Testbed: Mininet on bare-metal Linux with custom northbound REST API. CI/CD: GitHub Actions, ArgoCD. Observability: Grafana, Prometheus.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Intent Submission: researcher commits RoutingIntent YAML to Git; GitHub webhook triggers manifest validation; valid CRDs applied to Kubernetes API. Reconciliation: controller detects desired state change, computes per-site diff, applies Mininet policy commands, measures convergence time at 100ms resolution. Dashboard: live reconciliation event feed, convergence_ms histogram by policy_version, testbed topology graph with current overlay tunnels. Rollback: Git revert creates new commit; controller reconciles back to previous intent version; rollback convergence_ms recorded.
target_output.system_components: intent-controller: GitHub webhook receiver (POST /webhook/github), manifest YAML validator against RoutingIntent CRD OpenAPI schema, Kubernetes API client applying CRDs, controller-runtime reconcile loop with 100ms polling, Mininet northbound REST client, etcd state writer, PostgreSQL reconciliation_events writer. research-api: JWT auth endpoints, GET /experiments, GET /experiments/:id/events, GET /experiments/:id/convergence-stats, WebSocket /ws/events for real-time feed. etcd: /intents/{id} desired state, /observed/{experiment_id} observed state. PostgreSQL: experiments, reconciliation_events, policy_versions.
target_output.workflows: GitOps Reconciliation Loop: researcher pushes RoutingIntent YAML commit to GitHub; GitHub sends webhook to intent-controller POST /webhook/github; controller validates YAML against OpenAPI schema, rejects invalid with error annotation on commit status API; applies valid RoutingIntent CRD to Kubernetes API; controller-runtime reconcile queue receives object; reconcile function hashes desired spec JSON (SHA-256), reads /observed/{experiment_id} from etcd, computes per-site diff; for each changed site calls Mininet PUT /sites/{site_id}/policy with {tunnel_type, dst_prefix, next_hop, metric}; starts 100ms polling loop reading Mininet GET /sites/{site_id}/state; after each poll recomputes observed_state_hash; when observed_state_hash equals desired_state_hash records convergence_ms and writes reconciliation_event with event_type=applied; if 60-second timeout elapses sets phase=Drifted and records event_type=timeout. Rollback Workflow: researcher runs git revert on experiment branch; push triggers webhook; controller detects new desired state matching previous policy_version; reconcile loop runs as above; rollback convergence_ms recorded separately for comparative analysis.
target_output.data_model: routing_intents (etcd key /intents/{id}: RoutingIntent CRD YAML with spec.sites[], spec.policy_version, spec.experiment_id, status.observed_state_hash, status.convergence_ms, status.phase ENUM Pending/Reconciling/Converged/Drifted). network_states (etcd key /observed/{experiment_id}: JSON {state_hash: string, sites: [{site_id, tunnel_type, dst_prefix, next_hop, observed_at_ns}]}). experiments (PostgreSQL: id UUID PK, title, researcher_id, git_repo_url, created_at, status ENUM running/completed/archived). reconciliation_events (PostgreSQL: id UUID PK, experiment_id FK, intent_version, git_commit_sha CHAR(40), event_type ENUM applied/drifted/timeout/rollback, intended_state_hash VARCHAR, observed_state_hash VARCHAR, convergence_ms INT, recorded_at TIMESTAMPTZ). policy_versions (PostgreSQL: id UUID PK, experiment_id FK, git_commit_sha, manifest_yaml TEXT, applied_at, rolled_back_at).
target_output.api_design: Researcher REST /api/v1 with custom JWT. POST /auth/login returns JWT. GET /experiments returns list. POST /experiments creates experiment with git_repo_url. GET /experiments/:id/events returns paginated reconciliation_events with event_type filter. GET /experiments/:id/convergence-stats returns {mean_ms, p50_ms, p95_ms, p99_ms, timeout_count} aggregated from reconciliation_events. GET /experiments/:id/topology returns current observed testbed state from etcd. POST /webhook/github (no auth) receives GitHub push webhooks; validates HMAC-SHA256 signature.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Custom JWT HMAC-SHA256 for researcher and admin roles. GitHub webhook authenticated via HMAC-SHA256 signature validation using secret stored in Kubernetes Secret. etcd accessed only by intent-controller via TLS client certificates; not exposed externally. Mininet northbound API accessible only from intent-controller pod via Kubernetes NetworkPolicy. Kubernetes RBAC: intent-controller ServiceAccount has create/update/get on RoutingIntent CRD only. No external network access from controller pods.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Bare-metal Kubernetes university cluster: intent-controller Deployment (1 replica with leader election disabled for prototype), research-api Deployment (2 replicas with internal LoadBalancer). PostgreSQL 16 StatefulSet with 50GB PVC. etcd 3-node StatefulSet. Mininet testbed: 2 dedicated bare-metal Ubuntu 22.04 servers running Mininet with custom northbound REST API on port 8080, accessible via Kubernetes Service with ExternalName. ArgoCD syncs Kubernetes manifests from GitOps repo. GitHub Actions: go test, golangci-lint, Docker build, push to GHCR.
target_output.observability: Prometheus scrapes: controller reconcile queue depth, reconcile loop duration histogram, Mininet API call latency histogram, convergence_ms Prometheus histogram (used for Grafana percentile charts). Grafana dashboards: reconciliation event timeline, convergence time CDF suitable for paper figures, Git detection latency distribution, controller queue depth trend. Custom metric: drift_timeout_rate = timeout_count / total_reconcile_count for monitoring testbed reliability.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero external cost; runs on existing university research cluster. Scales to 10 Mininet sites within testbed hardware constraints. Multi-site expansion requires additional bare-metal servers for Mininet; controller scales by increasing polling parallelism per experiment.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (12 weeks): RoutingIntent CRD definition and validation webhook, intent-controller with reconcile loop and convergence measurement, single-site Mininet testbed integration, GitHub webhook receiver, PostgreSQL reconciliation_events, React dashboard with event feed and convergence histogram, ArgoCD deployment. Phase 2 (6 weeks): multi-site Mininet topology (up to 10 sites), rollback convergence measurement, convergence time CDF dashboard for paper publication, batch experiment runner. Phase 3: hardware SD-WAN controller integration, automated experiment batch runner, statistical analysis export for paper figures.
target_output.development_guidelines: Go controller must be tested with envtest (controller-runtime integration testing framework) to avoid requiring a live Kubernetes cluster in CI. Mininet northbound API must have a mock implementation for controller unit tests. RoutingIntent CRD OpenAPI validation schema must reject YAML with invalid CIDR notation or unknown overlay_tunnel_type at admission time. convergence_ms must be measured from the timestamp when the Mininet API call completes (policy applied) to when observed_state_hash matches desired_state_hash, not from Git commit time, to isolate the control loop latency from Git detection latency.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: 100ms polling of Mininet observed state adds load to testbed; mitigated by limiting concurrent reconcile operations to one per experiment. Risk: Mininet northbound API latency exceeds 60-second timeout for complex multi-site policies; mitigated by increasing timeout to 120 seconds for Phase 2 multi-site experiments with documented trade-off for paper. Tradeoff: etcd chosen over PostgreSQL for real-time state to match Kubernetes controller patterns; etcd adds operational complexity but enables authentic GitOps controller semantics for research validity.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Specified SHA-256 hashing of spec JSON for state hash comparison to ensure deterministic hash across identical specs regardless of YAML key ordering. Chose 100ms Mininet polling interval over push notification to avoid modifying the testbed southbound API. Decided convergence_ms measures control loop latency (Mininet-apply to observed-converge) separately from Git detection latency for cleaner paper methodology.

metadata.notes: Round 3 strong quality: resolves RoutingIntent CRD schema (ISS-001) and reconciliation controller state machine (ISS-002) for GitOps SD-WAN research prototype.

profile.domain: Carrier Real-Time Charging (OCS) Microservices Decomposition

project_goal.value: Decompose monolithic OCS into event-driven microservices replacing synchronous Diameter flows with Kafka-based async architecture for independent quota and balance deduction scaling
project_goal.rationale: Monolith cannot scale quota and deduction components independently; Diameter synchronous latency causes charging failures under peak load

target_users.value: Platform engineering teams managing carrier real-time charging infrastructure; NOC teams monitoring subscriber billing accuracy
target_users.rationale: Internal platform consumers, not end subscribers

project_class.rationale: Platform decomposition with no user-facing frontend; focused on topology, service mesh, and deployment architecture

capabilities.rationale: Event-driven backend services with real-time Kafka processing and GitOps-based deployment

complexity_level.rationale: Billion-level charging event throughput, strict ordering guarantees, and zero-revenue-loss failover requirements

risk_level.rationale: Revenue-critical system; any charging inaccuracy results in direct financial loss and regulatory exposure

data_sensitivity.rationale: Subscriber balance and deduction records are financial transaction data subject to SOX retention

external_exposure.rationale: OCS is an internal carrier platform; Diameter interface is network-internal, not internet-facing

access_model.value: Keycloak RBAC for operator console access; mTLS for service-to-service communication; Diameter interface restricted to PGW IP ranges
access_model.rationale: Service mesh enforces zero-trust between charging microservices

feature_scope.value: Diameter adapter ingesting CCR messages to Kafka, quota management service, balance deduction service with idempotent writes, shadow-mode parallel testing framework, diameter response emitter, billing reconciliation batch jobs
feature_scope.rationale: Full decomposition scope covering all OCS functional domains

mvp_scope.value: Diameter adapter plus quota management service running in shadow mode alongside legacy OCS with parity validation
mvp_scope.rationale: Shadow mode proves correctness before any traffic cutover risk

security_baseline.value: mTLS between all microservices via Istio, encrypted Kafka topics, secrets in AWS Secrets Manager, immutable container images, RBAC for Kubernetes namespaces
security_baseline.rationale: Financial data and revenue accuracy demand zero-trust service security

frontend_stack.value: none - internal platform with no frontend; operations via kubectl and Grafana dashboards
frontend_stack.rationale: infrastructure_project class; no user-facing UI required

backend_stack.value: Java 21 with Spring Boot 3; Spring Kafka for consumer/producer; Resilience4j for circuit breakers
backend_stack.rationale: Team Java expertise; Spring Kafka native integration with Kafka Streams for stateful processing

data_platform.value: MongoDB 7 for subscriber balance documents with atomic updates; Redis 7 for idempotency key cache and quota grant state
data_platform.rationale: MongoDB document model fits subscriber balance with embedded quota grants; Redis sub-millisecond deduplication

hosting_target.value: AWS EKS with Istio service mesh; MSK for managed Kafka; MongoDB Atlas
hosting_target.rationale: Managed Kafka and MongoDB reduce operational burden for high-availability charging platform

privacy_retention_policy.value: Charging event records retained 7 years for SOX audit; balance snapshots retained 3 years; idempotency keys expire after 24 hours
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: SOX financial retention requirements for telecoms

future_scope.value: 5G NR SA charging interface (CHF/HTTP2); real-time fraud scoring integration; self-serve quota top-up API for subscriber portals
future_scope.rationale: 5G SA requires HTTP/2-based CHF replacing Diameter; fraud integration is Phase 2

constraints.value: Zero revenue loss tolerance during cutover; shadow mode must run for minimum 4 weeks before live traffic migration; Diameter interface backward compatibility required
constraints.rationale: Revenue-critical system cannot tolerate charging gaps

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM on all Spring Boot services; Kafka consumer lag dashboards; charging decision latency P99 SLO <50ms; alerting on deduction error rate >0.01%
observability_baseline.rationale: Revenue accuracy requires sub-basis-point error rate monitoring

execution_preference.value: Strangler-fig incremental extraction; shadow mode validation gates before each service cutover; Jenkins CI/CD with GitOps ArgoCD deployment
execution_preference.rationale: Incremental cutover minimizes blast radius for revenue-critical decomposition

compliance_context.value: SOX audit retention for all charging event records; carrier regulatory compliance for charging accuracy under national telecoms authority oversight; PCI DSS not applicable as no card data processed
compliance_context.rationale: Financial transaction records and carrier regulatory exposure require SOX controls

project.goal: Replace monolithic OCS Diameter flows with event-driven microservices for independent quota and deduction scaling
project.target_users: Platform engineering and NOC operations teams
project.timeline: Shadow mode MVP in Q2; full cutover Q4

backend.patterns: Event-driven; idempotent consumers; CQRS for balance reads vs deductions

security.auth: Keycloak RBAC for operator access; mTLS via Istio for service mesh
security.transport: TLS 1.3 on Kafka; mTLS between all pods

data.cache: Redis 7 for idempotency keys and quota state
data.streaming: AWS MSK Kafka 3.6 with 96 partitions on charging-events topic

open_questions.cutover_ordering: Determine per-service or per-IMSI cohort cutover strategy

reasoner_reviews.product: The shadow-mode approach is the correct risk mitigation for a revenue-critical decomposition. Parity validation between legacy OCS and new microservices must cover both positive charging decisions and edge cases like negative balance handling and quota exhaustion rollover.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Kafka partitioning strategy is critical: partition by subscriber IMSI to preserve ordering for balance deductions per subscriber. 96 partitions on the charging-events topic supports current throughput with headroom. Consider separate topics for quota grants vs deduction events to allow independent consumer scaling.
reasoner_reviews.security: mTLS via Istio service mesh is mandatory given financial data in transit. Ensure Kafka topic-level ACLs restrict producer and consumer access by service identity. MongoDB Atlas encryption at rest with customer-managed KMS keys required for SOX.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Shadow mode 4-week minimum is appropriate. Define parity thresholds -- e.g., <0.001% charging decision divergence before cutover approval. Rollback plan must restore Diameter path to legacy OCS within 5 minutes.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Round 1 plan lacked detail on the idempotency key lifecycle for deduction events, and the shadow mode traffic comparison logic was underspecified. These must be addressed in this revision.

backend.service_design: Five microservices: diameter-adapter (Netty-based Diameter stack publishing CCR events to Kafka), quota-manager (stateful consumer tracking granted units per session), balance-deductor (idempotent consumer applying atomic MongoDB deduction), diameter-responder (emits CCA messages based on Kafka grant/rejection events), reconciliation-worker (batch Spring Batch job for nightly balance reconciliation)
backend.api_patterns: Internal Kafka event contracts with Avro schemas in Confluent Schema Registry; REST actuator endpoints for health and metrics per service
backend.failure_handling: Resilience4j circuit breakers on MongoDB writes; dead-letter topic for unparseable Diameter messages; consumer group rebalance monitoring with automatic lag alerting

security.auth_design: Keycloak service accounts for each microservice; Kubernetes ServiceAccount tokens projected for pod identity
security.authorization_model: Istio AuthorizationPolicy restricting inter-service traffic: only diameter-adapter may produce to charging-events topic; only balance-deductor may write to subscriber-balances collection
security.secrets_management: AWS Secrets Manager with External Secrets Operator syncing MongoDB credentials and Kafka SASL keys to Kubernetes Secrets; 30-day rotation

data.entities: subscriber_balances (subscriber_id, balance_amount, currency, quota_grants[], last_updated, version), quota_grants (grant_id, session_id, granted_units, unit_type, expiry_ts, status ENUM active/exhausted/expired), deduction_records (deduction_id, subscriber_id, session_id, units_consumed, balance_before, balance_after, charged_at), idempotency_keys (key hash, result, created_at TTL 24h in Redis)
data.storage_design: MongoDB Atlas M40 cluster with 3 replicas; writeConcern majority for all balance writes; Redis Cluster for idempotency key deduplication
data.migration_notes: Bulk subscriber balance migration from legacy OCS Oracle DB via AWS DMS with dual-write validation phase

devops.deployment_topology: EKS cluster with dedicated node groups per service tier: high-memory for balance-deductor, compute-optimized for quota-manager, standard for adapter and responder
devops.ci_cd_design: Jenkins multi-branch pipeline: unit tests with Testcontainers Kafka and MongoDB, Avro schema compatibility checks against Schema Registry, Docker build, Helm chart lint, ArgoCD sync
devops.rollback_strategy: ArgoCD app rollback to previous Helm release within 2 minutes; Kafka consumer group offset reset to checkpoint before failed deployment if deduction errors spike

previous_audits.summary: Strong architectural decomposition strategy with good Kafka topology but critical gaps in idempotency key lifecycle and shadow mode acceptance criteria leave revenue accuracy risk unaddressed.

Strangler-fig migration approach appropriate for revenue-critical system
