previous_plan.title: OCS Microservices Decomposition -- Round 1
previous_plan.executive_summary: Incremental decomposition of monolithic OCS using strangler-fig pattern with Kafka event bus replacing Diameter synchronous flows. Shadow mode validation before any live traffic migration.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Diameter adapter translates CCR messages to Kafka events; quota manager and balance deductor consume events independently; diameter responder emits CCA based on grant decisions. MongoDB stores subscriber balances.
previous_plan.data_model: subscriber_balances, quota_grants, deduction_records in MongoDB. Redis for session state.
previous_plan.deployment_and_operations: EKS deployment with Jenkins CI and ArgoCD GitOps. Shadow mode routing at diameter-adapter layer.

target_output.thinking_summary: Addressing two focused issues from Round 1: idempotency key TTL must be anchored to Kafka max redelivery window to eliminate double-deduction risk on consumer restart, and shadow mode requires a quantitative parity threshold with automated comparison to create an objective cutover gate. The architecture is otherwise sound -- IMSI partitioning, MongoDB atomic writes, and Istio mTLS are correctly specified. This revision adds concrete operational definitions to both gaps.

fix_report.action_taken: Specified Redis idempotency key TTL as max.poll.interval.ms (300s) + 60s buffer = 360s, covering the maximum Kafka redelivery window. Added deduplication logic: balance-deductor checks Redis before each MongoDB write; on key hit, returns cached result without re-executing deduction. Keys store deduction_id, result, and balance_after as JSON.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Zero double-deduction risk on consumer restart or partition rebalance within the 360-second deduplication window

fix_report.action_taken: Defined shadow mode parity as automated side-by-side comparison of session_id, charging_decision, and granted_units tuples between legacy OCS Diameter CCA responses and new microservice events, with <0.001% divergence over a rolling 24-hour window as the objective cutover gate. Added a shadow-comparator service that subscribes to both legacy CCA captures and new grant events.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Objective data-driven cutover gate replaces subjective judgment; parity dashboard visible to engineering and operations leadership

target_output.title: OCS Microservices Decomposition -- Idempotency and Shadow Parity Hardening
target_output.executive_summary: Event-driven decomposition of a carrier OCS replacing Diameter synchronous charging flows with a Kafka-based microservice architecture on AWS EKS. Five Spring Boot services handle Diameter adaptation, quota management, balance deduction with 360-second idempotency windows, and automated shadow mode parity validation requiring <0.001% charging decision divergence before any IMSI cohort is cut over from the legacy OCS.
target_output.architecture_overview: Diameter CCR messages from PGW nodes arrive at a Netty-based diameter-adapter service that parses and publishes charging request events to an MSK Kafka topic partitioned by subscriber IMSI for strict per-subscriber ordering. Quota-manager and balance-deductor services consume independently, with balance-deductor performing atomic MongoDB writes protected by Redis idempotency keys with 360-second TTL. A diameter-responder service subscribes to grant/reject events and emits Diameter CCA messages back to the PGW. A shadow-comparator service runs in parallel during migration, consuming both legacy OCS CCA captures and new microservice grant events to compute parity metrics. All service communication is secured by Istio mTLS within the EKS cluster.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Java 21, Spring Boot 3.2, Spring Kafka, Resilience4j, Netty 4.1 for Diameter stack. Messaging: AWS MSK Kafka 3.6, 96 partitions on charging-events topic, Avro schemas in Confluent Schema Registry. Storage: MongoDB Atlas M40 3-replica cluster with writeConcern majority; Redis 7 Cluster for idempotency keys. Infrastructure: AWS EKS with Istio 1.20, AWS Secrets Manager with External Secrets Operator. CI/CD: Jenkins multibranch, ArgoCD GitOps, Helm 3 charts.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Diameter Adapter: CCR-I/CCR-U/CCR-T parsing, IMSI extraction, event publishing to charging-events topic, dead-letter routing for malformed messages. Quota Manager: session-scoped quota grant tracking, unit reservation, expiry management. Balance Deductor: idempotent atomic balance deduction with Redis deduplication, negative balance guard, overdraft prevention. Diameter Responder: CCA-I/CCA-U/CCA-T emission based on Kafka grant events. Shadow Comparator: real-time parity computation between legacy CCA captures and new microservice decisions. Reconciliation Worker: nightly Spring Batch job cross-referencing deduction records against MSK event log.
target_output.system_components: MSK Kafka cluster (96 partitions, charging-events + grant-events + cca-captures topics), diameter-adapter Deployment (Netty Diameter stack, 6 replicas, compute-optimized), quota-manager StatefulSet (in-memory session state with Kafka Streams), balance-deductor Deployment (MongoDB writes, Redis idempotency, 12 replicas, memory-optimized), diameter-responder Deployment (Kafka consumer to Diameter CCA emitter), shadow-comparator Deployment (parity metrics producer), reconciliation-worker CronJob (nightly), MongoDB Atlas M40 cluster, Redis 7 Cluster (3 shards), Istio service mesh with PeerAuthentication enforcing STRICT mTLS.
target_output.workflows: Charging Event Flow: PGW sends Diameter CCR-I to diameter-adapter. Adapter parses IMSI and service-identifier, publishes ChargingRequest Avro event to partition IMSI%96 on charging-events. Quota-manager consumer grants units and publishes QuotaGrant event. Balance-deductor consumer computes deduction_id = SHA256(session_id+sequence_number), checks Redis for existing key -- on hit returns cached result, on miss executes MongoDB findOneAndUpdate with $inc on balance_amount using writeConcern majority, writes result to Redis with 360s TTL. Diameter-responder publishes CCA-I with granted units to PGW. Shadow Mode Parity: Shadow-comparator subscribes to cca-captures topic (legacy OCS CCA tapped via Diameter proxy) and grant-events topic, joins on session_id with 5-second window, compares granted_units and result tuples. Parity ratio computed over rolling 24-hour window; cutover gate requires <0.001% divergence over minimum 4-week window.
target_output.data_model: subscriber_balances (subscriber_id STRING PK, msisdn, balance_amount DECIMAL128, currency, quota_grants ARRAY with grant_id/session_id/granted_units/unit_type/expiry_ts/status, last_updated, version INT for optimistic concurrency). deduction_records (deduction_id STRING PK, subscriber_id, session_id, sequence_number, units_consumed, balance_before, balance_after, charged_at, kafka_offset). idempotency_keys in Redis: key=SHA256(session_id+sequence_number), value with deduction_id/result/balance_after, TTL=360s. balance_snapshots (subscriber_id, snapshot_at, balance_amount, snapshot_type ENUM nightly/pre_cutover) for SOX audit trail.
target_output.api_design: Internal Kafka event contracts (Avro): ChargingRequest {session_id, imsi, msisdn, service_identifier, requested_units, ccr_type ENUM initial/update/terminate}, QuotaGrant {grant_id, session_id, granted_units, expiry_ts, result ENUM granted/denied/partial}, DeductionConfirmed {deduction_id, session_id, units_consumed, balance_after}. REST health endpoints: GET /actuator/health (all services), GET /actuator/metrics. Shadow comparator REST: GET /api/v1/parity/summary (rolling 24h stats), GET /api/v1/parity/divergences (recent mismatches for investigation).
target_output.security_and_compliance: Istio PeerAuthentication mode STRICT enforces mTLS on all pod-to-pod communication within charging namespaces. Kafka topic ACLs via MSK IAM: diameter-adapter producer on charging-events, quota-manager consumer on charging-events producer on grant-events, balance-deductor consumer on grant-events writer to MongoDB. MongoDB Atlas encryption at rest with AWS KMS customer-managed key; field-level encryption for balance_amount using CSFLE. Keycloak RBAC for operator console with roles: charging-operator (read), charging-admin (read+write), charging-auditor (audit log read). SOX audit trail: all deduction_records immutable append-only; balance_snapshots retained 3 years; deduction_records retained 7 years in S3 Glacier after 90 days hot storage.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: EKS cluster us-east-1 with three node groups: compute-optimized (c6i.4xlarge) for diameter-adapter and quota-manager, memory-optimized (r6i.4xlarge) for balance-deductor handling MongoDB connection pool, standard (m6i.2xlarge) for diameter-responder and shadow-comparator. Horizontal Pod Autoscaler on balance-deductor: scale 8-24 replicas on Kafka consumer lag >10k messages. Jenkins multibranch pipeline: unit tests with Testcontainers (Kafka + MongoDB), Avro schema compatibility check against Schema Registry (BACKWARD_TRANSITIVE), Docker build, Helm lint, ArgoCD sync with progressive delivery. Cutover runbook: enable shadow mode T-4 weeks, daily parity report review, cutover approval gate at <0.001% divergence, IMSI cohort migration 5% per day, legacy OCS standby for 30 days post-cutover.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM with trace propagation across Kafka consumer/producer boundaries via W3C traceparent header injection. Dashboards: charging-events consumer lag per partition, balance-deductor P99 write latency (SLO <50ms), quota-manager grant rate per second, idempotency key hit rate (Redis). Alerts: consumer lag >50k events on any partition (P1), balance-deductor error rate >0.01% (P1), MongoDB writeConcern timeout rate >0.1% (P2), shadow parity divergence >0.001% (P1 blocks cutover). Kafka topic retention 24 hours for replay; separate audit-log topic retained 7 days for SOX evidence.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: EKS node cost approximately $4,200/month baseline (3 node groups, reserved instances). MSK M5.4xlarge 3-broker approximately $1,800/month. MongoDB Atlas M40 approximately $600/month. Redis Cluster approximately $400/month. Total approximately $7,000/month. At 2x current subscriber load, balance-deductor scales to 24 replicas (+$800/month); Kafka partitions can be doubled to 192 with no consumer changes due to IMSI partitioning strategy.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 -- Shadow Mode (8 weeks): Deploy diameter-adapter, quota-manager, balance-deductor, diameter-responder in shadow namespaces. Tap Diameter traffic via proxy. Deploy shadow-comparator. Establish parity dashboard. Begin 4-week minimum parity observation. Phase 2 -- Pilot Cutover (4 weeks): Migrate 5% IMSI cohort per day using diameter-adapter routing rules. Monitor parity, consumer lag, and deduction error rate continuously. Legacy OCS active for non-migrated subscribers. Phase 3 -- Full Cutover and Legacy Decommission (4 weeks): Complete IMSI migration. Decommission Diameter proxy tap. Run reconciliation worker for 30 days. Archive legacy OCS Oracle DB to S3 Glacier.
target_output.development_guidelines: Avro schema changes must pass BACKWARD_TRANSITIVE compatibility check against Schema Registry before merge. Kafka consumer implementations must be idempotent -- use deduction_id deduplication pattern as standard. Integration tests use Testcontainers with real Kafka and MongoDB; no mocks for storage layer. MongoDB writes must use writeConcern majority with wtimeout 5000ms; never use w:0. All charging event handlers must be instrumented with Datadog custom metrics via micrometer.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Consumer rebalance during peak charging hour causes lag spike exceeding idempotency window. Mitigation: Set max.poll.interval.ms=300s; session.timeout.ms=60s; use static group membership (group.instance.id) to avoid unnecessary rebalances. Risk: MongoDB Atlas M40 write throughput saturation during flash crowd events. Mitigation: Enable MongoDB Atlas auto-scaling with M60 burst; Redis quota state reduces MongoDB write amplification. Tradeoff: Avro schema evolution over Protobuf -- Confluent Schema Registry ecosystem integration outweighs binary size advantage for this Java-native stack. Tradeoff: MongoDB document model over PostgreSQL for subscriber balances -- embedded quota_grants array enables atomic quota+balance reads without joins, critical for <50ms P99 target.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: IMSI cohort cutover ordering resolved: migrate by IMSI hash bucket (0-19 in Phase 2, 20-99 in Phase 3) to evenly distribute load migration. SOX retention approach confirmed: append-only deduction_records collection with MongoDB changestream archival to S3 Glacier after 90 days. Dead-letter queue handling confirmed: unparseable Diameter messages go to charging-dlq topic with 7-day retention for manual replay after parser fix.

metadata.notes: Round 2 OCS decomposition revision addressing idempotency TTL and shadow mode parity thresholds for revenue-critical carrier charging platform.

profile.domain: NIST SP 800-53 Network Infrastructure Compliance Automation

project_goal.value: Continuously scan telecom network device configurations against NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 control library, generate compliance gap reports, and automate remediation of hardening deficiencies including insecure SSH ciphers, missing ACLs, and NTP misconfiguration
project_goal.rationale: Manual compliance audits are quarterly and miss configuration drift; automated continuous scanning is required for FedRAMP readiness

target_users.value: Security engineers reviewing compliance gaps and approving remediations; network operations staff executing approved hardening changes; compliance managers reviewing audit reports for regulatory submissions
target_users.rationale: Three distinct user personas with different permissions and workflows

project_class.rationale: Backend-only compliance scanning and remediation engine with admin panel for approvals; no customer-facing frontend

capabilities.rationale: Scheduled scan jobs, device integrations via SSH/NETCONF, backend compliance engine, and admin panel for remediation approvals

complexity_level.rationale: Complex control rule library with 300+ NIST controls, multi-vendor device adapters, and approval workflow state machine

risk_level.rationale: Automated remediation on live network devices carries risk of misconfiguration causing outages; approval gates and rollback required

data_sensitivity.rationale: Device configuration snapshots contain security-relevant details (ACLs, keys, cipher lists) classified as confidential

external_exposure.rationale: Tool operates entirely within internal network management zone; no internet-facing endpoints

access_model.value: Azure AD SSO with RBAC roles: compliance-viewer (read-only), compliance-engineer (scan + report), remediation-approver (approve/reject tasks), compliance-admin (full access including exception management)
access_model.rationale: Separation of duties between scanning, remediation approval, and exception management is a NIST AC-5 requirement

feature_scope.value: Continuous device configuration scanning, NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 control rule library with 300+ checks, compliance gap reports by control family, automated remediation task generation, dual-authorization approval workflow, pre/post configuration diff capture, time-limited exception management with escalation, FedRAMP audit report export
feature_scope.rationale: Full compliance lifecycle from detection through remediation and reporting

mvp_scope.value: SSH-based scanning for Cisco IOS and Juniper JunOS devices, AC and SC control families, automated gap reports, manual remediation task creation with approval workflow
mvp_scope.rationale: Cover the two highest-risk control families on the two primary device platforms first

security_baseline.value: Azure AD authentication with MFA; TLS 1.3 for all API endpoints; PostgreSQL encryption at rest; SSH private keys for device access stored in Azure Key Vault; all remediation actions logged with user identity
security_baseline.rationale: Tool handling confidential device configs and executing live remediations requires strong auth and audit trail

frontend_stack.value: none - admin panel implemented as server-rendered FastAPI admin interface with Jinja2 templates; no SPA framework
frontend_stack.rationale: Lightweight admin panel for internal operators; SPA complexity not warranted

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI; Celery for async scan and remediation tasks; Paramiko and ncclient for SSH and NETCONF device connections
backend_stack.rationale: FastAPI for REST API and admin panel; Celery for distributed scan job execution; Paramiko proven for SSH automation

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for control definitions, scan results, remediation tasks, exception records, and audit logs; no cache layer required
data_platform.rationale: Relational model appropriate for structured compliance data with referential integrity between controls, scans, and remediation tasks

hosting_target.value: Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS); Azure DevOps for CI/CD; Azure Monitor for observability
hosting_target.rationale: Existing Azure infrastructure for internal tools; AKS provides container orchestration for scan worker scaling

privacy_retention_policy.value: Configuration snapshots retained 2 years for audit evidence; scan results retained 3 years; remediation audit logs retained 5 years for FedRAMP evidence
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: FedRAMP continuous monitoring requires multi-year evidence retention

future_scope.value: NETCONF/YANG-based scanning for Nokia and Ericsson RAN equipment; real-time drift detection via syslog streaming; integration with ServiceNow ITSM for remediation ticket routing
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 expands beyond SSH-based Cisco/Juniper to RAN equipment and event-driven detection

constraints.value: All remediations require dual-authorization approval before execution; automated remediations limited to low-risk control classes (SI-2 ciphers, NTP); high-risk controls (ACL changes) require manual execution with pre-approval only
constraints.rationale: ACL changes can disrupt traffic; automation limited to safe non-traffic-affecting hardening

observability_baseline.value: Azure Monitor with alerts on scan job failures, remediation execution errors, and exception expiry; compliance score trending dashboard per device group and control family
observability_baseline.rationale: Compliance posture trending is a key metric for FedRAMP continuous monitoring reports

execution_preference.value: Azure DevOps pipelines with blue/green deployment; Celery workers scaled via KEDA based on scan queue depth
execution_preference.rationale: Queue-depth autoscaling ensures scan jobs complete within SLA during large fleet scans

compliance_context.value: NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 full control set; FedRAMP Moderate baseline for government carrier customers; continuous monitoring requirements mandate monthly compliance score reporting and 72-hour remediation SLA for critical findings
compliance_context.rationale: FedRAMP authorization requires documented automated compliance scanning and evidence of continuous monitoring

project.goal: Continuous NIST SP 800-53 compliance scanning and automated remediation for telecom network infrastructure
project.target_users: Security engineers, network ops, compliance managers
project.timeline: MVP (Cisco/Juniper, AC+SC families) in Q2; full control library Q4

backend.integrations: Paramiko SSH, ncclient NETCONF for device connections

security.auth: Azure AD SSO with MFA and RBAC
security.secrets: Azure Key Vault for device SSH keys

data.db: PostgreSQL 16 for all compliance data and audit logs
data.retention: Scan results 3 years, remediation logs 5 years

devops.autoscaling: KEDA on Celery scan queue depth

constraints.remediation: Dual-authorization required; automated execution limited to low-risk controls only

open_questions.exception_workflow: Define exception approval chain: direct manager vs security committee for different control families

reasoner_reviews.product: The dual-authorization gate for remediations is correctly scoped. The distinction between auto-executable low-risk remediations (cipher list, NTP) and approval-only high-risk ones (ACLs) is the right safety model. Exception management with time-limits and re-scan notifications is essential for FedRAMP continuous monitoring evidence.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Celery with KEDA autoscaling is the right pattern for scan job bursting. SSH connection pooling per device must be managed carefully -- concurrent scan connections to the same device can trigger rate limiting or lockout on some IOS platforms. Consider a per-device connection semaphore in the scan worker pool.
reasoner_reviews.security: Azure Key Vault for device SSH private keys is mandatory. Key rotation policy must be defined -- recommend 90-day rotation with automated re-provisioning via Ansible. Configuration snapshots containing ACL details must be encrypted at rest in PostgreSQL using pgcrypto for the config_text column.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Round 1 lacked a defined rollback mechanism for automated remediations. If a cipher list change causes SSH connectivity loss to the device, the tool must have a recovery path. Pre-remediation config backup and automated rollback trigger on device unreachability are required.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The round 1 plan did not specify the pre-remediation backup and rollback mechanism, leaving a critical gap for a tool that executes live device changes.

backend.service_design: Core services: scan-api (FastAPI REST endpoints for scan scheduling, results, tasks), celery-scan-workers (distributed scan job executors connecting to devices via SSH/NETCONF), celery-remediation-workers (execute approved low-risk remediations with pre-backup), admin-panel (FastAPI Jinja2 admin for approvals and exceptions), control-rule-engine (Python library evaluating device config text against NIST control rules)
backend.api_patterns: REST API: POST /api/v1/scans (schedule scan), GET /api/v1/scans/{id}/results (gap report), POST /api/v1/remediation-tasks/{id}/approve, POST /api/v1/exceptions (create exception), GET /api/v1/reports/compliance-score (trending)
backend.failure_handling: SSH connection failures marked as scan_error with retry up to 3 times; device unreachability after remediation triggers automated rollback from pre-remediation config backup; Celery task retry with exponential backoff on transient failures

security.auth_design: Azure AD OAuth2 with MSAL; JWT tokens validated on every API request; MFA enforced via Azure AD Conditional Access policy for all compliance-related roles
security.authorization_model: PostgreSQL row-level security for scan results by device group ownership; remediation-approver role cannot approve their own submitted tasks (segregation); compliance-admin role required for exception creation
security.secrets_management: Azure Key Vault for device SSH private keys and PostgreSQL credentials; Key Vault references in AKS pod identity via Workload Identity; 90-day key rotation automated via Azure DevOps pipeline

data.entities: network_devices (device_id, hostname, platform ENUM ios/junos/nokia, ip_address, device_group, ssh_key_ref, last_scan_at), compliance_controls (control_id NIST-format, family, title, description, automation_level ENUM auto/manual/advisory, risk_tier ENUM low/medium/high), scan_results (scan_id, device_id, control_id, status ENUM pass/fail/error, config_evidence TEXT encrypted, checked_at), remediation_tasks (task_id, scan_result_id, status ENUM pending/approved/rejected/executed/rolled_back, approver_1_id, approver_2_id, executed_at, rollback_config TEXT encrypted), exception_records (exception_id, device_id, control_id, reason, approved_by, expires_at, status ENUM active/expired/revoked)
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server with HA replica; pgcrypto encryption on config_evidence and rollback_config columns; 5-year PITR retention enabled
data.migration_notes: Initial control library loaded from NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 OSCAL JSON via migration script; device inventory imported from existing IPAM system via CSV

devops.deployment_topology: AKS cluster with two node pools: api-pool (Standard_D4s_v3, 2-4 nodes) for FastAPI and admin panel, worker-pool (Standard_D8s_v3, 2-20 nodes) for Celery scan and remediation workers with KEDA scaling on Redis queue depth
devops.ci_cd_design: Azure DevOps multi-stage pipeline: unit tests with pytest and mock device fixtures, integration tests against containerized PostgreSQL, Docker build, Helm chart deploy to staging AKS, blue/green production deployment with health check gate
devops.rollback_strategy: Blue/green deployment allows instant traffic switch back to previous version; database migrations use Alembic with up/down scripts tested in CI

ISS-001.title: Pre-remediation backup and rollback mechanism undefined
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan specified automated remediation execution but provided no mechanism for capturing device configuration state before applying changes or detecting and recovering from connectivity loss caused by a remediation.

history.note: Identified in constraints reviewer: automated live device changes with no rollback path is a critical gap

ISS-002.title: Per-device SSH connection concurrency not controlled
ISS-002.detail: Celery scan workers can spawn multiple parallel scan tasks against the same device, risking SSH connection rate limiting or account lockout on Cisco IOS platforms that enforce max-login-sessions.

history.note: Architect reasoner identified IOS platform SSH concurrency risk

focus_issues.title: Pre-remediation backup and rollback mechanism undefined

focus_issues.title: Per-device SSH connection concurrency not controlled

Add per-device connection semaphore in Celery scan worker pool; set max concurrent SSH connections per device to 1

Azure Key Vault for device SSH keys is the correct secrets management approach

Add Redis-backed per-device semaphore in scan worker pool with max_connections_per_device=1 configuration

previous_plan.title: NIST 800-53 Compliance Automation -- Round 1
previous_plan.executive_summary: Automated compliance scanning tool for telecom network devices using Python FastAPI backend with Celery workers connecting via SSH to check device configurations against NIST SP 800-53 control library and execute approved remediations.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: FastAPI scan API with Celery distributed workers using Paramiko SSH to collect running configs from Cisco IOS and Juniper JunOS devices. Control rule engine evaluates config text against 300+ NIST control rules.
previous_plan.data_model: network_devices, compliance_controls, scan_results, remediation_tasks, exception_records in PostgreSQL.
previous_plan.deployment_and_operations: AKS deployment with Azure DevOps CI/CD. KEDA autoscaling on Celery worker pool.

target_output.thinking_summary: This revision addresses two issues from Round 1: the critical absence of pre-remediation backup and rollback for automated device changes, and uncontrolled SSH connection concurrency that risks IOS device lockout. The pre-remediation backup pattern -- capture 'show running-config', store encrypted, verify connectivity post-change, auto-restore on failure -- is the industry-standard safety net for network automation tools. The per-device Redis semaphore solves the concurrency issue cleanly within the Celery worker model.

fix_report.action_taken: Added pre-remediation config snapshot step to remediation-worker pipeline: (1) SSH 'show running-config' capture stored encrypted in remediation_tasks.rollback_config column, (2) automated change applied, (3) 60-second post-remediation SSH connectivity health check, (4) on health check failure: auto-restore via SSH 'configure replace' from rollback_config, (5) task status set to rolled_back with alert to remediation-approver. All steps logged in remediation audit trail.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Zero risk of permanent misconfiguration from automated remediations; all changes are fully recoverable within 60-second detection window

fix_report.action_taken: Added Redis-backed per-device connection semaphore in Celery scan worker pool. Each scan task acquires a Redis lock keyed by device_id with TTL equal to max scan timeout (300s) before opening SSH connection. If lock unavailable, task is requeued with 30-second delay. Maximum concurrent connections per device = 1. Semaphore TTL prevents stale locks from blocking after worker crash.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Eliminates risk of SSH max-sessions lockout on Cisco IOS and Juniper JunOS devices during fleet-wide scan bursts

target_output.title: NIST SP 800-53 Compliance Automation -- Remediation Safety and Scan Concurrency Hardening
target_output.executive_summary: Continuous NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 compliance scanning and automated remediation tool for telecom network infrastructure on Azure AKS. Python FastAPI backend with Celery workers connects to Cisco IOS and Juniper JunOS devices via SSH and NETCONF to evaluate 300+ control rules, generate gap reports by control family, and execute dual-authorized low-risk remediations. This revision adds pre-remediation config snapshot with automatic rollback on post-change connectivity failure and per-device Redis semaphore preventing SSH lockout during fleet-wide scans. FedRAMP Moderate baseline evidence is generated via sealed audit logs and compliance score trending dashboards.
target_output.architecture_overview: Celery scan workers acquire a per-device Redis semaphore before opening a Paramiko SSH connection, collect running configuration, and pass it to the control rule engine which evaluates each NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 control rule using regex and structured parsing patterns for Cisco IOS and Juniper JunOS syntax. Scan results and configuration evidence are stored encrypted in PostgreSQL. Failed controls generate remediation tasks routed through a dual-authorization approval workflow in the FastAPI admin panel. Approved low-risk remediations (SSH ciphers, NTP servers, login banners) are executed by remediation workers that capture a pre-change config snapshot, apply the change, run a 60-second connectivity health check, and auto-restore from snapshot on failure. Exception records with time-limits are tracked for controls that cannot be immediately remediated, with automated re-scan notifications on expiry. Azure AD with RBAC enforces segregation between scanning, approval, and exception management roles.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI 0.110, Celery 5.3 with Redis 7 broker, Paramiko 3.4 for SSH, ncclient 0.6 for NETCONF. Database: PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server with pgcrypto for config column encryption. Infrastructure: AKS with KEDA 2.13 for Celery worker autoscaling, Azure Key Vault for SSH keys via Workload Identity, Azure AD for SSO. CI/CD: Azure DevOps multi-stage pipelines, Helm 3 charts, blue/green deployment.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Compliance Scanning: SSH/NETCONF device config collection, NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 rule evaluation (300+ controls across AC/AU/CM/IA/SC/SI families), per-device and per-control-family compliance score. Gap Reporting: compliance score trending, gap report by control family, FedRAMP evidence package export. Remediation Management: task generation from failed controls, dual-authorization approval workflow with segregation enforcement, automated execution with pre/post config snapshot, auto-rollback on failure. Exception Management: time-limited exception creation, management approval, expiry notification and re-scan trigger. Fleet Management: device inventory with platform classification, device group scoping for scan jobs.
target_output.system_components: scan-api (FastAPI, 2-4 replicas, REST endpoints for scan scheduling and results), celery-scan-workers (2-20 replicas, KEDA scaling on scan queue depth, Paramiko SSH with Redis semaphore), celery-remediation-workers (2-6 replicas, pre/post config snapshot, auto-rollback logic), admin-panel (FastAPI Jinja2, 2 replicas, approval and exception workflows), control-rule-engine (Python library, shared by scan workers, 300+ NIST rule implementations), Redis 7 (Celery broker + per-device semaphore store), PostgreSQL 16 (all compliance data, encrypted config columns), Azure Key Vault (device SSH keys and DB credentials), Azure AD (SSO and RBAC).
target_output.workflows: Scheduled Compliance Scan: Celery beat triggers fleet scan job every 6 hours. For each device: acquire Redis semaphore(device_id, TTL=300s), open SSH connection via Paramiko using Key Vault SSH key, collect 'show running-config', release semaphore. Control rule engine evaluates config text against all applicable NIST controls for device platform. scan_results records written to PostgreSQL with encrypted config_evidence. Failed controls generate remediation_tasks with status=pending. Remediation Execution: remediation-approver reviews task in admin panel, first approval sets approver_1_id, second approval (different user) sets approver_2_id and status=approved. Remediation worker picks up approved low-risk tasks: (1) SSH 'show running-config' -> rollback_config encrypted in task record, (2) apply remediation command sequence, (3) 60-second SSH connectivity health check loop, (4) on success: status=executed, (5) on failure: SSH 'configure replace' from rollback_config, status=rolled_back, alert to approvers.
target_output.data_model: network_devices (device_id UUID PK, hostname, ip_address, platform ENUM ios/junos/nokia, device_group, ssh_key_vault_ref, last_scan_at, last_scan_status). compliance_controls (control_id VARCHAR NIST-format e.g. AC-2, control_family, title, automation_level ENUM auto/manual/advisory, risk_tier ENUM low/medium/high, platform_applicability ARRAY). scan_results (scan_id UUID PK, device_id FK, control_id FK, status ENUM pass/fail/error/exception, config_evidence TEXT pgcrypto-encrypted, checked_at). remediation_tasks (task_id UUID PK, scan_result_id FK, status ENUM pending/approved/rejected/executing/executed/rolled_back/failed, approver_1_id, approver_2_id, approved_at, rollback_config TEXT pgcrypto-encrypted, executed_at, rollback_triggered_at). exception_records (exception_id UUID PK, device_id FK, control_id FK, justification, approved_by, expires_at, status ENUM active/expired/revoked).
target_output.api_design: POST /api/v1/scans {device_ids[], control_families[]} -> schedule Celery scan job. GET /api/v1/scans/{scan_id}/results -> gap report with per-device per-control pass/fail status. GET /api/v1/devices/{device_id}/compliance-score -> current score by control family. POST /api/v1/remediation-tasks/{task_id}/approve {approver_comment} -> first or second approval. POST /api/v1/remediation-tasks/{task_id}/reject {reason} -> reject and notify requester. POST /api/v1/exceptions {device_id, control_id, justification, expires_at} -> create exception pending approval. GET /api/v1/reports/compliance-score?group={group}&from={ts}&to={ts} -> trending data for FedRAMP evidence. POST /api/v1/reports/fedRamp-evidence-package -> generate sealed PDF audit report.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Azure AD OAuth2 with MSAL for all API endpoints; MFA enforced via Conditional Access for compliance-engineer and higher roles. RBAC roles: compliance-viewer (GET read-only), compliance-engineer (POST scans), remediation-approver (approve/reject tasks, cannot approve own submissions), compliance-admin (exception management, report export). PostgreSQL pgcrypto encryption on config_evidence and rollback_config columns using AES-256. Azure Key Vault stores device SSH private keys with Workload Identity federation; 90-day rotation via automated Azure DevOps pipeline. All remediation task state transitions logged to PostgreSQL audit_events table with user_id, action, timestamp, ip_address -- immutable append-only via trigger. NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 compliance evidence: scan_results with config_evidence serve as CM-6 control assessment artifacts; remediation audit trail serves as CM-3 change management evidence. FedRAMP Moderate: monthly compliance score reports generated and archived for continuous monitoring evidence package.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: AKS cluster Standard_D4s_v3 api-pool (2-4 nodes autoscaled) for scan-api and admin-panel. Standard_D8s_v3 worker-pool (2-20 nodes) for Celery scan and remediation workers with KEDA ScaledObject monitoring Redis scan queue length (target: 10 tasks per replica). Azure DevOps pipeline: pytest unit tests with mock device fixtures (paramiko mocks), integration tests against containerized PostgreSQL and Redis, Docker build, Helm lint, staging AKS deployment with smoke test suite, blue/green production deploy with automated health check gate before traffic switch. Blue/green: two AKS deployments (blue/green) behind ALB; traffic shifted after readiness probes pass and compliance API health endpoint returns 200. Rollback: ALB traffic shift back to previous deployment within 2 minutes.
target_output.observability: Azure Monitor with Log Analytics workspace collecting container logs from all AKS pods. Metrics: Celery task queue depth per queue (scan/remediation), scan job completion rate, remediation execution success/rollback rate, per-control-family compliance score trending, exception count by device group. Alerts: scan job failure rate >5% in 1-hour window (P2), remediation rollback triggered (P1 -- immediate Slack + email to remediation-approver and security manager), exception expiry within 48 hours (P3 scheduled notification). Compliance dashboard: compliance score by device group and control family over 90 days, FedRAMP continuous monitoring report generated monthly.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: AKS baseline 4 nodes approximately $800/month (reserved). Worker pool peaks at 20 nodes during fleet scan ($3,000/month burst, KEDA scales down after scans complete). Azure PostgreSQL Flexible Server General Purpose 4 vCores approximately $400/month. Azure Key Vault and Monitor approximately $150/month. Total baseline approximately $1,350/month; peak scan day approximately $3,500/month. Fleet growth to 2,000 devices is handled by KEDA worker scaling without architecture changes.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 -- MVP (10 weeks): SSH scanning for Cisco IOS and Juniper JunOS, AC and SC control families (60 controls), gap reports, manual remediation task creation with dual-authorization approval. Phase 2 -- Automated Remediation (8 weeks): Low-risk automated remediations (SSH ciphers, NTP, login banners) with pre/post snapshot and rollback. KEDA worker autoscaling. Exception management workflow. Phase 3 -- Full Control Library and FedRAMP Evidence (8 weeks): All 300+ NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 controls, NETCONF scanning for Nokia equipment, FedRAMP evidence package export, compliance score trending API.
target_output.development_guidelines: Control rule implementations must include unit tests with both compliant and non-compliant config fixtures for each supported platform. Remediation commands must be tested against device platform simulators (Cisco CML or GNS3) before merge to main. SSH connection handling must always use context managers to guarantee semaphore release; bare SSH connections outside context manager pattern are rejected in code review. All PostgreSQL queries accessing config_evidence must use pgcrypto decrypt functions -- no plaintext config exposure in ORM queries.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Pre-remediation 'show running-config' on large device (50,000-line config) causes SSH timeout. Mitigation: Set Paramiko SSH read timeout to 120 seconds; use buffered streaming for large configs; test against Nexus 9K configs in CI. Risk: Redis semaphore TTL expires during slow scan on device with large config, allowing second scan worker to connect. Mitigation: Renew semaphore TTL at 50% of max scan timeout if scan still in progress; semaphore renewal heartbeat in scan worker. Tradeoff: FastAPI Jinja2 admin panel vs React SPA -- server-rendered admin is simpler to secure (no CORS, no token storage in browser) at the cost of less interactive UX; acceptable for internal compliance operators.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Exception approval chain resolved: control families with risk_tier=low require direct manager approval; risk_tier=medium and high require security committee quorum of 2 security engineers. SSH key rotation resolved: automated 90-day rotation via Azure DevOps pipeline using azure-keyvault-secrets SDK; rotation event triggers immediate rescan of all devices using that key to validate connectivity before old key deleted.

metadata.notes: Round 2 NIST 800-53 compliance automation revision addressing pre-remediation backup/rollback gap and SSH connection concurrency on live network devices.

profile.domain: Mobile Network Operator ML Feature Store and Predictive Maintenance Serving Platform

project_goal.value: Build a scalable ML feature store and model serving platform for MNO predictive maintenance use cases, providing standardized hourly feature pipelines for cell tower health signals and low-latency inference endpoints for real-time tower failure prediction
project_goal.rationale: Ad-hoc per-model feature computation creates duplicate pipelines and inconsistent training/serving skew; centralized feature store eliminates duplication and ensures consistency

target_users.value: Data scientists building and registering feature pipelines; ML engineers deploying and monitoring model versions; operations systems querying inference endpoints for real-time maintenance scheduling
target_users.rationale: Three consumer personas: pipeline authors, model operators, and operational integration consumers

project_class.rationale: Platform infrastructure for ML workloads; no user-facing product; consumed by internal data science and operations teams

capabilities.rationale: Batch feature pipelines, FastAPI serving layer, ClickHouse data platform, GKE Autopilot deployment

complexity_level.rationale: Thousands of cell towers generating telemetry at 1-minute intervals; feature store must handle tens of millions of rows per day with sub-second online query latency

risk_level.rationale: Incorrect failure predictions increase unnecessary maintenance dispatches; false negatives miss real failures; platform stability affects maintenance scheduling quality

data_sensitivity.rationale: Cell tower telemetry and predictive model outputs are internal operational data; no subscriber PII

external_exposure.rationale: Feature store and inference API consumed only by internal MNO systems and data science teams

access_model.value: Custom JWT issued by internal SSO for data science team access; service accounts with static JWT for operational system integrations querying inference endpoints
access_model.rationale: Internal platform; lightweight JWT sufficient; no OAuth2 complexity needed for service account integrations

feature_scope.value: Hourly batch feature pipelines for cell tower signals (VSWR, temperature, power levels, uptime, alarm counts); feature registry with versioning and lineage; online feature store for low-latency serving; model artifact registry; inference API with A/B testing framework for challenger model evaluation; model performance monitoring with drift detection
feature_scope.rationale: Full ML platform lifecycle from feature engineering through model deployment and monitoring

mvp_scope.value: Hourly batch pipelines for VSWR and temperature features for 5,000 pilot towers; ClickHouse feature store; FastAPI inference endpoint for single production failure prediction model; basic model versioning
mvp_scope.rationale: Validate feature store value with one model on pilot tower population before scaling to full fleet

security_baseline.value: JWT authentication on inference API; GKE Workload Identity for GCP service access; ClickHouse user-level access controls for feature data; all inter-service communication within GKE via internal load balancers
security_baseline.rationale: Internal platform with no external exposure; defense in depth via network policy and IAM

frontend_stack.value: none - internal platform; data scientists use Python SDK client and Jupyter notebooks; no web UI required for MVP
frontend_stack.rationale: infrastructure_project; data science workflows are Jupyter and CLI-based

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI for inference serving and feature registry API; Apache Airflow on GKE for batch pipeline orchestration
backend_stack.rationale: FastAPI for low-latency inference serving; Airflow standard for batch feature pipeline scheduling

data_platform.value: ClickHouse for offline and online feature store (columnar storage with sub-second queries); Redis 7 for ultra-low-latency online feature cache for inference-time lookups
data_platform.rationale: ClickHouse handles both batch feature materialization and point queries with appropriate indexes; Redis reduces inference latency below ClickHouse query overhead

hosting_target.value: GKE Autopilot on GCP; Cloud Storage for model artifacts; BigQuery for training data export
hosting_target.rationale: GKE Autopilot simplifies node management for mixed CPU/memory workloads; existing GCP footprint

privacy_retention_policy.value: Cell tower telemetry retained 18 months in ClickHouse; model artifacts retained indefinitely; feature pipeline job logs retained 90 days
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Sufficient telemetry history for seasonal model retraining; no PII, no regulatory minimum retention

future_scope.value: Real-time streaming features from Pub/Sub telemetry stream replacing hourly batch; automated model retraining triggers on drift detection; expansion to RAN KPI features beyond tower health
future_scope.rationale: Streaming features are Phase 2; current batch hourly latency acceptable for predictive maintenance use case

constraints.value: Inference API P99 latency <100ms; feature pipelines must complete within 45 minutes of hourly trigger to ensure freshness for next inference cycle; no external ML platforms (no Vertex AI managed endpoints -- cost constraint)
constraints.rationale: 100ms P99 inference SLO required for integration with real-time maintenance scheduling systems

observability_baseline.value: Grafana dashboards on Prometheus metrics for inference latency, feature pipeline completion time, model prediction distribution, and feature freshness lag; Airflow task duration alerts
observability_baseline.rationale: Feature freshness and inference latency are the key SLOs for platform reliability

execution_preference.value: GitHub Actions CI/CD; GKE Autopilot with Horizontal Pod Autoscaler for inference API
execution_preference.rationale: GitHub Actions integrates with existing data science team workflow; GKE HPA handles inference traffic variation

project.goal: ML feature store and inference serving platform for cell tower failure prediction
project.target_users: Data scientists, ML engineers, maintenance scheduling systems
project.timeline: MVP (5,000 pilot towers) in Q2; full fleet in Q4

backend.framework: FastAPI for inference API, Airflow for batch pipelines
backend.patterns: Feature store pattern with offline materialization and online serving cache

security.access: GKE Workload Identity for GCP IAM

constraints.freshness: Feature pipelines complete within 45 minutes of hourly trigger

open_questions.drift_detection: Define feature drift detection metric thresholds that trigger model retraining alerts

reasoner_reviews.product: The Redis online feature cache is the correct approach for meeting the <100ms inference P99 SLO. The 45-minute pipeline completion constraint is realistic for hourly batch over 5,000 towers initially but will need optimization when scaling to the full 50,000-tower fleet. The A/B testing framework with 10% challenger cohort is the right model promotion gate.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Training/serving skew is the primary ML platform reliability risk. The feature registry must enforce that offline pipeline feature definitions and online serving lookup keys are identical. A dedicated feature validation step in the inference pipeline that checks Redis cache hit rate and falls back to ClickHouse query is important for cache cold-start scenarios after Redis restarts.
reasoner_reviews.security: GKE Workload Identity binding for Airflow workers and inference pods eliminates long-lived service account keys. ClickHouse should enforce row-level access to prevent data scientists from querying feature data belonging to projects they do not own.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 45-minute pipeline completion window is tight for the full 50,000-tower fleet at hourly granularity. Airflow DAG should include task-level timeout alerting at 35 minutes to give operators time to investigate before the next hourly trigger. Parallelism tuning for ClickHouse insert workers will be critical.

backend.service_design: Four services: feature-registry-api (FastAPI, manages feature_pipelines and feature_sets metadata), inference-api (FastAPI, loads model artifacts from GCS, queries Redis for online features, returns failure_probability score), airflow-workers (batch feature computation DAGs for cell_tower_signals processing), model-registry-api (FastAPI, manages model_artifacts versions and A/B test configurations)
backend.api_patterns: REST: GET /api/v1/features/{tower_id}?feature_set={name}&as_of={ts} for online feature lookup; POST /api/v1/predict {tower_id, model_version} returning {failure_probability, confidence_interval, feature_freshness_ts}; GET /api/v1/models/{model_id}/performance for drift metrics
backend.failure_handling: Redis cache miss falls back to ClickHouse point query with <200ms timeout; Airflow DAG task retry 3 times with 5-minute backoff on ClickHouse insert failures; inference API circuit breaker on Redis unavailability falls back to full ClickHouse path

security.auth_design: Custom JWT with RSA-256 signing; JWT claims include team_id and feature_project scope for ClickHouse row-level access control
security.authorization_model: ClickHouse user per feature project with SELECT restricted to project-specific feature tables; inference API service account has SELECT only on online feature tables
security.secrets_management: GKE Workload Identity for GCS model artifact access and BigQuery export; JWT signing key in GCP Secret Manager with 180-day rotation

data.entities: feature_pipelines (pipeline_id, name, source_signal_type, computation_logic, output_feature_set, schedule_interval, last_run_at, last_run_status), feature_sets (feature_set_id, pipeline_id, feature_names ARRAY, entity_key_type ENUM tower_id/sector_id, created_at, version), cell_tower_signals (tower_id, measured_at, vswr FLOAT, temperature_celsius FLOAT, power_level_dbm FLOAT, uptime_hours INT, alarm_count_1h INT, alarm_count_24h INT), failure_predictions (prediction_id, tower_id, model_version, failure_probability FLOAT, confidence_interval FLOAT, predicted_at, features_freshness_ts)
data.storage_design: ClickHouse: MergeTree tables for cell_tower_signals and materialized feature sets with ORDER BY (tower_id, measured_at); Redis Hash per tower_id storing latest feature vector for inference; GCS bucket for model artifact .pkl and TFLite files
data.migration_notes: Initial 18-month backfill of cell_tower_signals from existing OSS/BSS telemetry data warehouse via ClickHouse bulk insert; estimated 500M rows

devops.deployment_topology: GKE Autopilot: feature-registry-api and inference-api as Deployments with HPA (min 2, max 12 replicas on CPU >60%); Airflow on GKE with dedicated worker node pool; ClickHouse StatefulSet 3-node cluster; Redis Deployment single node (Phase 1 simplicity)
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: pytest for API unit tests, ClickHouse integration tests with TestContainers, Airflow DAG unit tests with dag.test(), Docker build, Helm deploy to GKE staging, smoke test inference endpoint with known tower fixture
devops.rollback_strategy: Helm rollback to previous chart version; model rollback via model-registry A/B test percentage adjustment to 0% challenger traffic

target_output.thinking_summary: This is a first-pass ML platform architecture for a mobile network operator. The key design decisions are ClickHouse as the dual offline/online feature store backed by Redis for inference-time latency, Airflow for batch pipeline orchestration to meet the 45-minute freshness SLO, and a model registry with A/B testing framework as the promotion gate. Training/serving skew prevention via a unified feature registry is the central architectural concern. The moderate quality rating means most sections are solid but a couple should be slightly thinner on implementation detail.
target_output.title: MNO ML Feature Store and Predictive Maintenance Serving Platform
target_output.executive_summary: Centralized ML feature store and model serving infrastructure for a mobile network operator's cell tower failure prediction use cases. Apache Airflow batch pipelines compute hourly feature sets from cell tower telemetry (VSWR, temperature, power levels, alarm rates) and materialize them into a ClickHouse feature store backed by Redis for <100ms inference latency. A FastAPI inference API serves failure probability scores with confidence intervals consumed by maintenance scheduling systems. A model registry with A/B testing framework gates model promotions on challenger accuracy improvement over the production champion.
target_output.architecture_overview: Airflow DAGs run on GKE every hour, pulling cell tower telemetry from the source OSS systems, computing rolling-window statistics for each tower, and bulk-inserting materialized feature sets into ClickHouse. A Redis cache layer stores the latest feature vector per tower_id for sub-millisecond inference-time lookup. The FastAPI inference API loads model artifacts from GCS at startup, queries Redis for tower features on each prediction request, and falls back to a ClickHouse point query on cache miss. A feature registry API tracks pipeline definitions, feature set schemas, and entity key types to enforce consistency between offline computation and online serving, preventing training/serving skew. A model registry API manages artifact versions and A/B test configurations, routing 10% of inference traffic to challenger models for performance comparison before full promotion.
target_output.technology_stack: Batch Pipelines: Apache Airflow 2.8 on GKE, Python 3.12 operators with ClickHouse driver for feature materialization. Inference Serving: Python 3.12, FastAPI 0.110, scikit-learn and joblib for model loading, Redis 7 for online feature cache. Feature Storage: ClickHouse 24.3 StatefulSet (3-node cluster, ReplicatedMergeTree), Redis 7 Deployment. Infrastructure: GKE Autopilot on GCP, GCS for model artifacts, GitHub Actions CI/CD.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Feature Pipelines: hourly VSWR rolling-window (1h, 6h, 24h averages), temperature trend computation, power level deviation from baseline, 1h and 24h alarm rate aggregation. Feature Registry: pipeline version tracking, feature schema validation, entity key type enforcement, lineage from source signal to materialized feature. Inference API: tower failure probability scoring, confidence interval computation, feature freshness metadata in response. Model Registry: artifact versioning, A/B test configuration, performance metric comparison dashboard.
target_output.system_components: feature-registry-api (FastAPI Deployment, 2 replicas), inference-api (FastAPI Deployment, HPA 2-12 replicas), model-registry-api (FastAPI Deployment, 2 replicas), airflow-scheduler (GKE Deployment), airflow-workers (GKE Deployment, 4-20 workers), ClickHouse cluster (3-node StatefulSet, ReplicatedMergeTree), Redis 7 (Deployment, single node Phase 1), GCS bucket (model artifacts).
target_output.workflows: Hourly Feature Materialization: Airflow DAG triggered at :00 each hour. Tasks: (1) fetch raw telemetry from OSS source for last 2 hours via REST API, (2) compute tower-level VSWR/temperature/power/alarm features in pandas, (3) bulk insert into ClickHouse cell_tower_signals and materialized feature tables, (4) update Redis hash per tower_id with latest feature vector, (5) update feature_pipelines.last_run_at and status. Alert at 35-minute mark if DAG still running. Inference Request: inference-api receives POST /api/v1/predict with tower_id. Redis HGET tower:{tower_id} for feature vector (cache hit <1ms). If cache miss: ClickHouse SELECT for latest feature row (50-100ms). Load model artifact from memory (loaded at startup). Score with scikit-learn predict_proba. Return failure_probability, confidence_interval, features_freshness_ts.
target_output.data_model: cell_tower_signals: tower_id STRING, measured_at DateTime64(3), vswr FLOAT64, temperature_celsius FLOAT32, power_level_dbm FLOAT32, uptime_hours INT32, alarm_count_1h INT16, alarm_count_24h INT16 -- ClickHouse MergeTree ORDER BY (tower_id, measured_at) PARTITION BY toYYYYMM(measured_at). feature_sets_materialized: tower_id, feature_date DateTime, vswr_1h_avg, vswr_6h_avg, vswr_24h_avg, temp_trend_6h, power_deviation_pct, alarm_rate_1h, alarm_rate_24h -- MergeTree ORDER BY (tower_id, feature_date). failure_predictions: prediction_id UUID, tower_id, model_version STRING, failure_probability FLOAT32, confidence_interval FLOAT32, predicted_at DateTime -- MergeTree ORDER BY predicted_at.
target_output.api_design: POST /api/v1/predict {tower_id, model_version='production'} -> {failure_probability: 0.73, confidence_interval: 0.08, features_freshness_ts: '2026-03-15T10:00:00Z', model_version: '2.1.0'}. GET /api/v1/features/{tower_id}?feature_set=cell_health_v2 -> feature vector JSON with freshness metadata. POST /api/v1/pipelines {name, source_signal_type, schedule_interval} -> register feature pipeline. GET /api/v1/models/{model_id}/ab-test -> current A/B test split and challenger performance metrics.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Custom JWT with RSA-256 for inference API; GKE Workload Identity for GCS and BigQuery access without static service account keys. ClickHouse users scoped per feature project with row-level access via user-defined restrictions. Internal GKE network policy restricts inference-api egress to Redis and ClickHouse only. GCS model artifact bucket with uniform bucket-level access; only inference-api and model-registry-api service accounts have read access.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GKE Autopilot provisions nodes on demand for Airflow workers and inference API. HPA on inference-api scales 2-12 replicas on CPU >60% with 60-second stabilization window. Airflow workers use CeleryExecutor with Redis as broker. ClickHouse 3-node ReplicatedMergeTree with ZooKeeper for coordination. GitHub Actions: pytest, ClickHouse TestContainers, Airflow DAG test(), Docker build, Helm deploy to staging, smoke test POST /api/v1/predict with fixture tower_id expecting response <100ms.
target_output.observability: Grafana dashboards on Prometheus: inference API P50/P95/P99 latency (SLO <100ms), Redis cache hit rate (target >95%), Airflow DAG completion time per pipeline (alert >35 min), feature freshness lag (delta between latest feature_date and now), model prediction score distribution (drift detection via histogram comparison). Airflow task failure alerts to Slack ops channel.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: GKE Autopilot burst cost for 20 Airflow workers during hourly pipeline approximately $0.80/run. Inference API 4 replicas baseline approximately $600/month. ClickHouse 3-node e2-standard-8 approximately $900/month. Redis e2-standard-2 approximately $120/month. Total approximately $1,800/month baseline. Scaling to 50,000 towers: Airflow worker pool scales to 60 workers for pipeline execution; ClickHouse may need upgrade to 6-node cluster for query concurrency.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 -- Pilot Feature Store (10 weeks): ClickHouse cluster, hourly VSWR/temperature pipelines for 5,000 pilot towers, Redis cache, inference API with single production model, basic feature registry. Phase 2 -- Full Fleet and Model Registry (8 weeks): Scale pipelines to 50,000 towers, A/B testing framework, model registry with promotion gates, feature drift monitoring. Phase 3 -- Streaming Features (future): Pub/Sub real-time telemetry ingestion replacing hourly batch for sub-minute feature freshness.
target_output.development_guidelines: Feature pipeline DAGs must pass Airflow dag.test() in CI with sample telemetry fixtures before merge. Inference API must include integration test verifying end-to-end predict call with Redis cache hit and cache miss paths. ClickHouse table schema changes require backward-compatible ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN only; never DROP COLUMN without data science team sign-off. Model artifacts must be versioned with semantic versioning and registered in model-registry before deployment; direct GCS artifact path changes in inference-api config are rejected in review.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Airflow hourly DAG timing conflict -- two DAG runs overlap if previous run takes >60 minutes. Mitigation: Set max_active_runs=1 per DAG; alert at 35 minutes. Risk: Redis cache cold-start after restart causes spike in ClickHouse queries exceeding connection pool. Mitigation: Cache warm-up job on Redis startup that pre-loads latest feature vectors for top 1,000 highest-risk towers. Tradeoff: Custom ClickHouse feature store vs managed Vertex AI Feature Store -- cost constraint eliminates Vertex AI; ClickHouse dual offline/online pattern is proven at similar scale.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Drift detection thresholds resolved: feature distribution drift (>15% change in mean VSWR over rolling 7-day window) triggers email alert to model owner; prediction score distribution drift (KL divergence >0.2 vs baseline 30-day distribution) triggers model retraining investigation ticket. Model A/B test promotion criteria resolved: challenger must show >2% improvement in F1-score on last 30 days of held-out tower data over rolling evaluation window before promotion.

metadata.notes: First-pass ML feature store for MNO predictive maintenance; good overall with some sections slightly thinner on implementation specifics.

profile.domain: BGP Routing Table Convergence Performance Profiler Desktop Application

project_goal.value: Desktop application for senior network architects to profile BGP routing table convergence in lab environments, visualizing route advertisement propagation timelines, RIB processing times, and memory usage across GNS3 and EVE-NG testbed routers
project_goal.rationale: Manual BGP timer tuning is time-consuming; architects need visualization of convergence behavior across multiple routers to identify bottlenecks in routing design

target_users.value: Senior network architects working with GNS3 or EVE-NG lab environments profiling BGP convergence for routing design optimization
target_users.rationale: Specialist user persona; desktop app installed on architect workstation

project_class.rationale: Local Electron app collecting data from SSH-connected lab routers; no server backend; all processing local

capabilities.rationale: Electron frontend with Vue 3, Python subprocess backend for SSH and data collection, SQLite storage, analytics visualizations

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-router parallel SSH instrumentation, timestamp-synchronized convergence event parsing, and high-dimensional visualization of route propagation across dynamic topologies

risk_level.rationale: Local lab tool; no production network access; worst case is incomplete profiling data from a lab device

data_sensitivity.rationale: Lab router configurations and BGP convergence measurements are internal; no subscriber data

external_exposure.rationale: Desktop app communicates only with local lab environment; no cloud connectivity

access_model.value: none - local desktop application; no user authentication required; lab router SSH credentials stored in local encrypted keychain
access_model.rationale: Local-only tool; OS user access control is sufficient

feature_scope.value: Profiling session configuration pointing at GNS3/EVE-NG lab, multi-router SSH instrumentation, BGP update message capture with timestamps, convergence timeline visualization with per-hop propagation delay, RIB processing latency per router, memory consumption timeline, session save/compare for side-by-side analysis
feature_scope.rationale: Full profiling lifecycle from session setup through comparative analysis

mvp_scope.value: Profiling sessions against GNS3 labs only, up to 10 routers, BGP update capture and timeline visualization, session save and replay
mvp_scope.rationale: GNS3 is the primary lab platform; EVE-NG support and comparison mode are Phase 2

security_baseline.value: SSH credentials stored in OS keychain (macOS Keychain or Windows Credential Store); no plaintext storage; Electron contextIsolation=true and nodeIntegration=false
security_baseline.rationale: Basic desktop app security; Electron security defaults prevent renderer process from accessing Node APIs directly

frontend_stack.value: Electron 31 with Vue 3 and TypeScript; D3.js for convergence timeline and propagation visualizations
frontend_stack.rationale: Electron for cross-platform desktop; Vue 3 for reactive UI; D3.js for custom network topology and timeline visualizations

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 embedded via Electron subprocess; Paramiko for SSH connections to lab routers; custom BGP update log parser
backend_stack.rationale: Python provides mature SSH and network protocol libraries; subprocess pattern isolates Python from Electron main process

data_platform.value: SQLite for local profiling session storage and convergence event records; no cloud database
data_platform.rationale: Local desktop storage; SQLite is appropriate for profiling session data volumes

hosting_target.value: Local desktop installation; GitHub Actions for CI; distributed as electron-builder .dmg and .exe installers
hosting_target.rationale: Desktop app packaging; no cloud hosting

privacy_retention_policy.value: Local data retained as long as architect keeps the SQLite database; no automatic deletion; export to CSV for archiving
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Local tool; data lifecycle managed by user

future_scope.value: EVE-NG support, OSPF and IS-IS convergence profiling beyond BGP, cloud session storage for team sharing of profiling results
future_scope.rationale: Multi-protocol support and team collaboration are Phase 2

constraints.value: Must run on macOS and Windows; Python subprocess must bundle Python runtime to avoid dependency on installed Python; all SSH connections to lab routers only (no production IPs)
constraints.rationale: Cross-platform distribution and zero-install-dependency requirement for architect workstations

observability_baseline.value: none - local desktop tool; no telemetry or centralized monitoring; electron-log for local crash diagnostics only
observability_baseline.rationale: Local tool; diagnostic logging sufficient

execution_preference.value: GitHub Actions CI for automated testing; electron-builder for cross-platform packaging
execution_preference.rationale: Standard Electron desktop app packaging workflow

project.goal: Desktop BGP convergence profiler for lab architects using GNS3/EVE-NG
project.timeline: MVP (GNS3, 10 routers) in Q2

frontend.visualization: D3.js convergence timelines and topology graphs

open_questions.timestamp_sync: Determine clock synchronization strategy across multiple lab routers for accurate convergence timing

reasoner_reviews.product: The session compare feature is the highest-value capability for architects optimizing BGP timer configurations -- the ability to quantify improvement between different configurations side-by-side is the core use case. Ensure session comparison allows metric overlay including P50/P95 convergence time, not just timeline visualization.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Clock synchronization across lab routers is critical for accurate convergence measurement. GNS3 virtual routers share the host clock, which simplifies this significantly. For EVE-NG Phase 2, NTP synchronization to a common source before each profiling session is required. The Python subprocess IPC pattern via stdin/stdout JSON is cleaner than a local HTTP server for Electron-to-Python communication.
reasoner_reviews.security: Electron contextIsolation and nodeIntegration=false are correctly specified. SSH private keys must never be passed through the renderer process -- all SSH credential access must be in the Electron main process or Python subprocess. Ensure the preload script exposes only specific IPC channels via contextBridge, not arbitrary ipcRenderer access.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: PyInstaller bundling of Python runtime adds 80-150MB to installer size. Evaluate whether PyOxidizer or Nuitka would produce a smaller bundle. Document the Python runtime version in the release notes to help architects with security patching.

backend.service_design: Python subprocess services: ssh-collector (Paramiko SSH to each lab router, collects 'show ip bgp' and debug output, emits JSON events to stdout), bgp-parser (parses BGP update log lines into structured convergence_events with timestamps), convergence-analyzer (computes propagation delay, RIB processing time, memory delta per router from event stream)
backend.api_patterns: IPC via stdin/stdout JSON between Electron main process and Python subprocess; event types: session_started, router_connected, bgp_update_captured, convergence_event, analysis_complete, error
backend.failure_handling: SSH connection failure to individual router marks that router as unavailable in session without aborting other routers; Python subprocess crash triggers Electron error dialog with log path

security.auth_design: SSH credentials retrieved from OS keychain via keytar npm package in Electron main process; passed to Python subprocess via environment variable (not command line argument)
security.authorization_model: No app-level authorization; OS user account is the access boundary; Electron BrowserWindow sandboxed with contextIsolation
security.secrets_management: keytar stores SSH credentials in macOS Keychain or Windows Credential Manager; never stored in SQLite or app config files

data.entities: profiling_sessions (session_id, name, lab_platform ENUM gns3/eve_ng, topology_config JSON, created_at, status), routers (router_id, session_id, hostname, ip_address, platform ENUM ios/junos/eos), bgp_sessions (bgp_session_id, router_id, peer_ip, asn, state), convergence_events (event_id, session_id, router_id, event_type ENUM update_received/rib_processed/route_advertised, route_prefix, timestamp_us, sequence_number), rib_snapshots (snapshot_id, router_id, captured_at, route_count, memory_mb)
data.storage_design: SQLite with WAL mode; convergence_events indexed on (session_id, timestamp_us) for timeline queries; sessions stored in ~/.bgp-profiler/sessions.db
data.migration_notes: SQLite schema migrations via drizzle-orm; auto-run on app startup

devops.deployment_topology: Local desktop installation; Python subprocess bundled via PyInstaller into single-file executable embedded in Electron app resources; electron-builder produces macOS .dmg (ARM64 and x64) and Windows .exe NSIS installer
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: Vue 3 unit tests with vitest, Python subprocess unit tests with pytest (mock SSH fixtures), electron-builder cross-platform build matrix, release artifacts uploaded to GitHub Releases
devops.rollback_strategy: Users downgrade via reinstalling previous version from GitHub Releases; SQLite schema rollback not supported -- forward-only migrations

frontend.state_management: Pinia store for session state and live event stream; D3.js visualization components directly consume reactive Pinia getters
frontend.ui_modules: SessionSetup: router list form with SSH credential entry and GNS3 API URL. ProfilingDashboard: live router connection status indicators, BGP update event log streaming. TimelineVisualization: D3.js Gantt-style chart showing per-router convergence events on shared time axis. RouterMetrics: per-router RIB size and memory timeline. SessionCompare: overlay mode for two sessions showing convergence time delta.
frontend.accessibility_notes: Timeline zoom-to-convergence-event on click; router color coding consistent across all visualization panels for cross-reference

target_output.thinking_summary: This is a flawed first-pass plan for a BGP convergence profiler desktop app. The intentional flaw is a technology mismatch: the plan uses a cloud database (PostgreSQL on Supabase) instead of SQLite as specified in the contract, and recommends a server-side REST API instead of the Electron subprocess IPC pattern. This creates an inconsistency between the desktop_app/local_only profile and the plan's architecture. Additionally, the plan omits the D3.js timeline visualization detail specified in the contract and uses a React frontend instead of Vue 3.
target_output.title: BGP Convergence Profiler -- Network Architect Lab Tool
target_output.executive_summary: Desktop application enabling senior network architects to instrument multi-router GNS3 lab environments, inject BGP route change events, and visualize convergence propagation timelines, RIB processing latencies, and memory usage across up to 10 routers. Profiling sessions are stored in a PostgreSQL database and accessed via a local REST API server, allowing future web UI extensions. Built with Electron and React, with a Node.js backend service handling SSH connections to lab routers.
target_output.architecture_overview: The application runs a local Express.js HTTP server on port 7429 that acts as the backend for the Electron frontend. SSH connections to GNS3 lab routers are made from the Node.js server process using the ssh2 npm package. Convergence events are stored in a PostgreSQL database hosted on Supabase for cloud synchronization across architect workstations. The React frontend communicates with the local Express server via REST API calls. BGP update log parsing is handled in Node.js with custom regex patterns.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Electron 31, React 18, TypeScript, Recharts for timeline visualization. Backend: Node.js 20, Express.js local server, ssh2 npm package for router SSH. Database: PostgreSQL via Supabase cloud hosting for session sharing. CI/CD: GitHub Actions, electron-builder for packaging. electron-store for persisting UI preferences (dark mode, default SSH timeout) to local JSON config. Jest + React Testing Library for convergence timeline component unit tests.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Session Management: GNS3 lab topology configuration, router credential storage. SSH Instrumentation: parallel SSH connections to up to 10 routers, BGP debug output capture. Convergence Analysis: route propagation timeline, RIB processing time per router. Reporting: session export to PDF.
target_output.system_components: Electron main process, React renderer, Express.js local HTTP server on port 7429, ssh2 SSH client pool, Supabase PostgreSQL client, convergence analysis module. Convergence analysis module parses SSH show bgp summary output via regex state machine into structured ConvergenceEvent records. electron-store config persistence layer manages user SSH credential templates and UI preferences locally.
target_output.workflows: Architect configures profiling session in React UI, entering GNS3 API URL and router SSH credentials. User clicks Start Profiling; React sends POST /api/sessions/start to local Express server. Express server establishes parallel SSH connections to each router via ssh2. BGP update events captured and sent to Supabase PostgreSQL via Supabase JS client. React polls GET /api/sessions/{id}/events every 2 seconds to update timeline visualization using Recharts.
target_output.data_model: Supabase PostgreSQL tables: profiling_sessions (id, name, lab_platform, created_at), routers (id, session_id, hostname, ip), convergence_events (id, session_id, router_id, event_type, route_prefix, event_timestamp, created_at). SSH credentials stored in localStorage in the renderer process. lab_topologies (id, session_id, platform ENUM eveng/gns3/cml, node_count, created_at) for future multi-topology comparison. SSH credentials excluded from Supabase — stored encrypted in localStorage via electron-store with AES-GCM key derived from machine ID.
target_output.api_design: Express.js local REST: POST /api/sessions/start, GET /api/sessions, GET /api/sessions/{id}/events, POST /api/sessions/{id}/stop. Supabase REST API used directly by React frontend for session listing. GET /api/sessions/{id}/events supports ?event_type= and ?router_id= query parameters for filtered timeline views. WebSocket endpoint /ws/sessions/{id}/live streams convergence events to the React renderer in real time during active profiling sessions.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Electron security: contextIsolation=false for Node.js access from renderer. SSH credentials stored in React component state and persisted to localStorage. No additional security controls required for local lab tool. Note: contextIsolation=false is acceptable for a local-only lab tool with no external content loaded. SSH private keys should never be stored in localStorage; only username/password credentials used for lab router access over the LAN.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions builds Electron app with electron-builder. macOS .dmg and Windows .exe distributed via GitHub Releases. Supabase account required for database connectivity. Node.js runtime bundled with Electron. Code signing: macOS notarization via Apple Developer certificate in GitHub Actions secrets; Windows Authenticode signing via self-signed cert for internal lab distribution. Auto-update: electron-updater checks GitHub Releases API on startup and prompts user before downloading. Supabase migrations applied manually via supabase CLI before each release.
target_output.observability: Console logging in Express server and Electron main process. No metrics or alerting for local desktop tool. Sentry Electron SDK captures renderer-process unhandled exceptions with BGP session context (router_hostname, platform) attached as tags for lab platform-specific bug triage. SSH connection failure events logged to electron-log with rotation at 5MB for field diagnostics.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Free tier Supabase for individual use. GitHub Actions free tier for CI builds. No cloud infrastructure costs beyond Supabase. Supabase free tier supports up to 500MB database and 50,000 monthly active users — sufficient for a team of 10 network architects sharing profiling sessions. Upgrade to Supabase Pro at $25/month if session history exceeds 10,000 convergence events.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1: Electron + React + Express local server + Supabase cloud DB. Phase 2: EVE-NG support. Phase 3: Web UI accessible from browser without Electron. Phase 4: Cisco IOS-XR and Juniper Junos parsing adapters for multi-vendor lab environments.
