previous_plan.title: 5G NR Site Acceptance App -- Round 2
previous_plan.executive_summary: Offline-first React Native app with WatermelonDB SQLite sync, Cognito auth, and Go Gin backend on ECS Fargate for 5G NR site acceptance testing.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: WatermelonDB pull-then-push sync on connectivity restore. field-data-wins for measurement fields, server-wins for status fields. Photo attachments synced in same pass as metadata.
previous_plan.data_model: site_surveys, drive_test_measurements, antenna_configurations, kpi_results, sign_off_records in PostgreSQL. WatermelonDB mirrors subset on device.
previous_plan.deployment_and_operations: ECS Fargate blue/green. Expo EAS builds. GitHub Actions CI.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 3 resolves the two remaining issues: photo sync decoupling from metadata sync to meet the 30-second metadata SLA, and a conflict review UX flow so technicians are aware of server-wins overwrites. Both are straightforward to implement -- two-phase sync separates concerns cleanly, and a ConflictReviewScreen is a targeted UX addition. The underlying architecture is solid after two rounds.

fix_report.action_taken: Implemented two-phase sync: Phase 1 is WatermelonDB metadata sync (pull/push on all non-attachment tables), targeted to complete within 30 seconds. Phase 2 is an independent background photo upload queue using S3 presigned URLs, running concurrently with app usage after metadata sync completes. Photo upload status tracked in a local WatermelonDB photo_upload_queue table with status ENUM pending/uploading/completed/failed; retry with exponential backoff on failure. Survey metadata is available to RF engineers within 30 seconds of connectivity restore; photos appear in the review UI as they complete upload.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Survey metadata available to RF engineers within 30 seconds of technician connectivity restore; photo attachments available within 2-5 minutes depending on count and bandwidth

fix_report.action_taken: Added ConflictReviewScreen triggered on app foreground when any site_surveys record has conflict_flag=true. Screen shows a diff view: field name, technician's local value (struck through), server-applied value (highlighted), reason (e.g. 'Survey submitted and approved while offline -- status cannot be reverted'). Technician acknowledges each conflict; survey conflict_flag cleared on acknowledgement. Fields overwritten by server-wins are identified in the sync response via a conflict_details JSON array stored in WatermelonDB locally.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Technicians are informed of every server-wins overwrite; no silent data changes; conflict acknowledgement creates audit trail of technician awareness

target_output.title: 5G NR Site Acceptance App -- Photo Sync Decoupling and Conflict Review UX
target_output.executive_summary: Offline-first React Native application for 5G NR site acceptance testing, enabling field technicians to capture drive test measurements, antenna configurations (tilt, azimuth, height), and KPI results at remote tower sites with up to 72 hours offline capability. WatermelonDB two-phase sync delivers survey metadata to RF planning engineers within 30 seconds of connectivity restore while photo attachments upload asynchronously via S3 presigned URL queue. AWS Cognito RBAC separates field technician capture from RF engineer sign-off approval workflow. Conflict review UX ensures technicians are notified of every server-wins resolution applied during offline periods.
target_output.architecture_overview: The React Native app uses WatermelonDB SQLite for offline-first data storage, with a sync adapter implementing the WatermelonDB pull-then-push protocol against the Go Gin backend. On connectivity restore, Phase 1 metadata sync completes within 30 seconds, making surveys available for RF engineer review. Phase 2 photo upload runs independently via a WatermelonDB-tracked queue, uploading to S3 via presigned URLs generated by the backend. The Go Gin API runs on ECS Fargate behind an ALB with Cognito JWT authorizer. Conflict resolution applies field-data-wins for drive_test_measurements and antenna_configurations (protecting field-collected data) and server-wins for status fields (preventing workflow state rollback). On app foreground, the ConflictReviewScreen surfaces any server-wins conflicts for technician acknowledgement.
target_output.technology_stack: Mobile: React Native 0.74, TypeScript, WatermelonDB 0.27 with custom Go sync adapter, React Native Keychain for token storage, Expo EAS for builds. Backend: Go 1.22, Gin 1.9, AWS SDK v2 for S3 presigned URLs, JWT RS256 validation against Cognito JWKS. Database: PostgreSQL 16 on RDS Multi-AZ; WatermelonDB SQLite on device. Auth: AWS Cognito User Pools with field_technician and rf_engineer groups. CI/CD: GitHub Actions, Expo EAS, ECS blue/green.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Site Survey Management: create survey for site_id, offline draft state, submit when complete. Drive Test Capture: GPS-tagged SINR/RSRP/RSRQ/throughput measurements with sequence numbering, photo attachment with 5MB limit. Antenna Configuration: sector selector, tilt/azimuth/height entry, photo of physical installation. KPI Results: pass/fail evaluation against configurable acceptance thresholds. Two-Phase Sync: metadata sync (WatermelonDB pull/push <30s), background photo upload queue (S3 presigned URLs). Conflict Review: ConflictReviewScreen with field-level diff and acknowledgement. RF Engineer Workflow: survey list with status filters, drive test map view, approve/reject/request-revision with comments.
target_output.system_components: React Native app with WatermelonDB SQLite (offline storage), sync adapter (Phase 1 metadata, Phase 2 photo queue), ConflictReviewScreen. Go Gin API on ECS Fargate (2-4 tasks, ALB). PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ. S3 bucket for photo attachments (presigned URL access). AWS Cognito User Pool. AWS CloudWatch + Sentry for observability.
target_output.workflows: Site Survey Capture (Offline): Technician opens app (no connectivity). Creates site_survey in WatermelonDB with status=draft. Records drive_test_measurements with GPS coordinates and KPI values. Captures antenna_configurations with photos queued locally in photo_upload_queue. Metadata Sync (Phase 1, on connectivity restore): WatermelonDB sync adapter calls POST /api/v1/sync with all created/updated/deleted records since lastPulledAt. Server applies changes, resolves conflicts (measurement fields: field-data-wins; status fields: server-wins), returns pull changes with conflict_details array for any server-wins fields. Sync completes in <30 seconds. Surveys immediately visible to RF engineers. Photo Upload (Phase 2, background): For each record in photo_upload_queue with status=pending: call GET /api/v1/photos/presigned-url, upload to S3, update photo_upload_queue status=completed. Runs concurrently with app usage. RF Engineer Review: Engineer opens survey list on app or web, views drive test GPS track on map, checks KPI pass/fail badges, reviews antenna photos as they load. POSTs /api/v1/surveys/{id}/approve with optional comments. Backend sets status=approved, triggers network inventory webhook. Conflict Review: On next app foreground after sync, ConflictReviewScreen checks for surveys with conflict_flag=true. Shows field-level diff. Technician acknowledges; PATCH /api/v1/surveys/{id}/acknowledge-conflict clears flag.
target_output.data_model: site_surveys (survey_id UUID PK, site_id, technician_id, status ENUM draft/submitted/approved/rejected/revision_requested, conflict_flag BOOL, conflict_details JSONB, created_at, submitted_at, approved_at). drive_test_measurements (measurement_id UUID PK, survey_id FK, latitude FLOAT8, longitude FLOAT8, sinr_db FLOAT4, rsrp_dbm FLOAT4, rsrq_db FLOAT4, throughput_dl_mbps FLOAT4, measured_at TIMESTAMPTZ, sequence_num INT) PARTITIONED BY MONTH. antenna_configurations (config_id UUID PK, survey_id FK, sector_id VARCHAR, tilt_degrees FLOAT4, azimuth_degrees FLOAT4, height_m FLOAT4, photo_s3_key VARCHAR, recorded_at TIMESTAMPTZ). kpi_results (kpi_id UUID PK, survey_id FK, kpi_name VARCHAR, measured_value FLOAT4, threshold_value FLOAT4, pass_fail ENUM pass/fail, measured_at TIMESTAMPTZ). sign_off_records (signoff_id UUID PK, survey_id FK, rf_engineer_id, decision ENUM approved/rejected/revision_requested, comments TEXT, signed_at TIMESTAMPTZ). WatermelonDB device schema: mirrors above minus partitioning; photo_upload_queue (queue_id, record_type, record_id, photo_local_uri, s3_key, status ENUM pending/uploading/completed/failed, retry_count).
target_output.api_design: POST /api/v1/sync {lastPulledAt, changes: {site_surveys: {created[], updated[]}, drive_test_measurements: {created[]}, antenna_configurations: {created[]}, kpi_results: {created[]}}} -> {changes: {created[], updated[], deleted[]}, timestamp, conflict_details[]}. GET /api/v1/photos/presigned-url?survey_id={id}&filename={name} -> {presigned_url, s3_key, expires_in: 900}. POST /api/v1/surveys/{id}/approve {comments} -> survey with status=approved. POST /api/v1/surveys/{id}/reject {reason}. PATCH /api/v1/surveys/{id}/acknowledge-conflict -> conflict_flag cleared. GET /api/v1/surveys?status={filter}&site_id={id} -> paginated survey list.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Cognito RS256 JWT validated on every Gin endpoint via JWKS endpoint polling. Custom JWT claim custom:role used for authorization: field_technicians can create/update their own surveys (PostgreSQL RLS WHERE technician_id = cognito_sub), rf_engineers can read all surveys and update status fields only. React Native Keychain stores Cognito access and refresh tokens using biometric-protected keychain on iOS and Android Keystore on Android. No survey data or tokens in AsyncStorage. TLS 1.3 on all API calls. S3 presigned URLs expire in 15 minutes; photos accessed only via presigned URL with no public S3 access. Drive test GPS coordinates transmitted over TLS; no plaintext geo-data at rest beyond PostgreSQL.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: ECS Fargate: service with 2 minimum, 4 maximum tasks; ALB health check on GET /health. RDS PostgreSQL Multi-AZ with automated backups (7-day retention, PITR). S3 photo bucket with versioning enabled; lifecycle rule: transition to Intelligent-Tiering after 90 days. GitHub Actions: Go unit tests with testcontainers PostgreSQL, mock S3 tests, Docker build, ECR push, ECS blue/green deploy (CodeDeploy). Expo EAS Build matrix for iOS (TestFlight) and Android (Internal Testing track); EAS Submit for production releases. Blue/green rollback: ALB traffic shift back to previous task definition within 5 minutes.
target_output.observability: CloudWatch metrics: ECS task CPU/memory, ALB 5xx rate, RDS connection count, sync endpoint P99 latency (SLO <5s for metadata sync). CloudWatch alarm on sync endpoint error rate >1%. Sentry React Native: crash reporting with device OS version and app version tags. Custom CloudWatch metric: photo_upload_queue_depth from backend polling; alert if queue depth >50 per technician for >30 minutes (indicates upload stall). Datadog APM on Go Gin API for trace-level sync performance analysis.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: ECS Fargate 2 tasks approximately $150/month. RDS Multi-AZ db.t3.medium approximately $180/month. S3 photo storage approximately $50/month (50GB at 5MB per survey, 10,000 surveys/year). Cognito MAU pricing for 500 field technicians approximately $0 (free tier). Total approximately $400/month. Scales to 5,000 technicians by increasing ECS tasks to 20 and RDS to db.r6g.large.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 -- MVP (complete): site survey CRUD, drive test measurement capture, offline sync, RF engineer approval workflow. Phase 2 -- Photo Sync Decoupling and Conflict UX (this round): two-phase sync with background photo upload queue, ConflictReviewScreen with field-level diff. Phase 3 -- Network Inventory Integration (8 weeks): POST to network inventory API on approval, automated go-live flag, capacity planning database update. Phase 4 -- KPI Automation: acceptance threshold library per site type, automated pass/fail evaluation without manual KPI entry.
target_output.development_guidelines: WatermelonDB schema changes require a migration script in src/database/migrations/ tested against the previous schema. Sync adapter must handle partial sync failures idempotently -- the same created record submitted twice must be deduplicated by UUID. Go sync endpoint must process WatermelonDB push changes in a single database transaction per sync request; partial write failures rolled back entirely. Photo presigned URL endpoint must validate that the requesting technician owns the survey before issuing presigned URL.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: WatermelonDB pull response too large for 72-hour offline technician with 500+ measurements; 30-second SLA violated. Mitigation: server paginates pull response at 500 records per sync; client issues multiple sync calls; each completes in <10s; total survey metadata available incrementally. Risk: Cognito refresh token (30-day default) expires for technicians in extended offline deployment >30 days. Mitigation: configure Cognito refresh token validity to 365 days for field_technician group; accept the security tradeoff for field device operational reality. Tradeoff: WatermelonDB vs SQLite direct -- WatermelonDB adds sync protocol complexity but eliminates need to build custom conflict resolution from scratch; correct choice for offline-first mobile.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Conflict resolution finalized: drive_test_measurements and antenna_configurations use field-data-wins (technician's offline captures are authoritative for measurement data); site_surveys status field uses server-wins (RF engineer approval state cannot be reverted by offline technician edit); conflict_details JSONB records each server-wins field for ConflictReviewScreen. Photo sync decoupled: two-phase sync with background S3 presigned URL upload queue independent of WatermelonDB sync protocol.

metadata.notes: Round 3 5G NR site acceptance app resolving photo sync blocking and conflict review UX gap.

profile.domain: IPFIX Flow Record Streaming Traffic Matrix Pipeline for Carrier Peering Fabric

project_goal.value: Process streaming IPFIX flow records from carrier peering fabric to calculate real-time per-AS-pair traffic matrix statistics, replacing slow batch sampling queries with sub-second ClickHouse query access for network capacity planning models
project_goal.rationale: Batch NetFlow sampling produced 4-hour lag for capacity planning; real-time IPFIX pipeline reduces to 5-minute granularity enabling same-day capacity decisions

target_users.value: Network capacity planning engineers querying per-AS-pair traffic matrices; NOC engineers monitoring anomaly alerts for route leaks and DDoS amplification; data pipeline operators maintaining IPFIX collector fleet
target_users.rationale: Three consumer personas with distinct latency and query needs

project_class.rationale: Streaming data pipeline with batch compaction; no user-facing product; consumed via ClickHouse SQL and Grafana dashboards

capabilities.rationale: Rust real-time IPFIX processing, Kafka streaming, ClickHouse analytics, batch compaction jobs, Grafana dashboards

complexity_level.rationale: 100M+ IPFIX records per hour from 50 peering routers; sliding window aggregation with exactly-once semantics; sub-second ClickHouse query SLO

risk_level.rationale: Capacity planning decisions based on incorrect traffic matrices can cause under-provisioning; no direct revenue impact but affects network quality

data_sensitivity.rationale: AS-pair traffic volumes are internal peering operational data; no subscriber PII in aggregated matrices

external_exposure.rationale: ClickHouse and Grafana accessed by internal teams only; no internet-facing endpoints

access_model.value: No authentication on IPFIX UDP collectors (network-layer restricted to peering router IP ranges); custom JWT for ClickHouse HTTP interface and Grafana SSO via internal LDAP
access_model.rationale: IPFIX UDP is network-restricted; analytics access controlled at ClickHouse user level

feature_scope.value: UDP IPFIX collector fleet, Rust-based IPFIX decoder and Kafka producer, per-AS-pair 5-minute sliding window aggregation with Kafka Streams, ClickHouse materialization with sub-second query performance, hourly and daily compaction jobs, anomaly detection for route leaks and DDoS amplification, Grafana dashboards for traffic matrix heatmaps and per-AS trend analysis
feature_scope.rationale: Full pipeline from IPFIX receipt to analytics consumption

mvp_scope.value: IPFIX collector, Kafka producer, 5-minute AS-pair aggregation, ClickHouse materialization, basic Grafana traffic matrix dashboard
mvp_scope.rationale: Core pipeline first; anomaly detection and compaction in Phase 2

security_baseline.value: IPFIX collectors network-restricted to peering router source IPs via GKE NetworkPolicy; ClickHouse user-level access controls; Kafka topics accessible only within GKE cluster via internal service
security_baseline.rationale: No external exposure; network-layer restriction sufficient for IPFIX UDP

frontend_stack.value: none - analytics consumed via Grafana dashboards and ClickHouse SQL; no SPA frontend
frontend_stack.rationale: data_pipeline; Grafana provides sufficient visualization for capacity planning team

backend_stack.value: Rust with Tokio for IPFIX UDP collector and Kafka producer; Kafka Streams (Java) for 5-minute sliding window aggregation
backend_stack.rationale: Rust for performance-critical IPFIX decoding at 100M records/hour; Kafka Streams for stateful windowed aggregation with exactly-once semantics

data_platform.value: ClickHouse for materialized traffic matrices; Redis 7 for real-time per-AS anomaly baseline cache; Kafka for IPFIX event stream
data_platform.rationale: ClickHouse columnar storage with MergeTree enables sub-second queries on 100M+ daily rows; Redis for O(1) anomaly threshold lookups

hosting_target.value: GKE Autopilot on GCP; ClickHouse on GKE StatefulSet; Kafka on Confluent Cloud or GKE
hosting_target.rationale: GKE provides Kubernetes orchestration; ClickHouse StatefulSet for NVMe SSD-attached storage requirements

privacy_retention_policy.value: 5-minute traffic matrices retained 90 days; hourly aggregates retained 2 years; daily aggregates retained 5 years; raw IPFIX Kafka events retained 24 hours for replay
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Granular data lifecycle aligned with capacity planning query patterns; 5-year daily aggregates support multi-year trend analysis

future_scope.value: Per-prefix traffic matrices beyond AS-pair aggregation; BGP route change correlation with traffic shift events; ML-based capacity planning forecast using traffic matrix time series
future_scope.rationale: Prefix-level granularity is Phase 3; BGP correlation requires BGP route table integration

constraints.value: ClickHouse per-AS-pair queries must respond in <1 second for rolling 24-hour window; IPFIX records must appear in ClickHouse within 10 minutes of packet receipt; no record loss tolerance for traffic matrix accuracy
constraints.rationale: Capacity planning tools poll ClickHouse every 5 minutes; <1s query SLO ensures tools remain responsive

observability_baseline.value: Grafana + Prometheus for Kafka consumer lag, ClickHouse insert rate, Rust collector UDP packet loss rate, per-AS-pair traffic volume anomaly alerts
observability_baseline.rationale: Kafka consumer lag is the primary pipeline health signal; >5-minute lag risks violating 10-minute freshness SLO

execution_preference.value: GitHub Actions CI; GKE Autopilot deployment via Helm; Kafka Streams job on GKE
execution_preference.rationale: Standard GKE deployment workflow

project.goal: Real-time IPFIX traffic matrix pipeline for carrier peering fabric capacity planning
project.target_users: Capacity planning engineers, NOC, pipeline operators
project.timeline: MVP (collector + ClickHouse) Q2; anomaly detection Q3

backend.patterns: Exactly-once semantics with Kafka consumer checkpointing

security.network: GKE NetworkPolicy restricting IPFIX to peering router IPs

constraints.freshness: Records in ClickHouse within 10 minutes of receipt

open_questions.as_number_mapping: Source for authoritative ASN-to-name mapping for ClickHouse dimension table

reasoner_reviews.product: The 5-minute sliding window is the right granularity for capacity planning use cases. The distinction between 5-minute granular retention (90 days) and hourly/daily aggregates (2-5 years) correctly matches query patterns -- planners use granular data for incident analysis and daily aggregates for multi-year trending.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The Rust IPFIX decoder is the right performance choice for 100M records/hour. IPFIX template management is the trickiest part -- IPFIX templates are sent periodically by routers and must be cached; flow records with unknown template IDs must be queued until the template arrives. Consider a per-router template cache with LRU eviction and a template-pending queue.
reasoner_reviews.security: GKE NetworkPolicy restricting IPFIX UDP ingress to peering router source IPs is the correct network-layer control. Ensure the policy uses podSelector for the ipfix-collector pods and ipBlock with specific /32 CIDRs for each peering router, not a broad CIDR that could include non-router hosts.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Round 1 lacked a specified ClickHouse table design for the sub-second query SLO. The MergeTree ORDER BY and PARTITION BY choices are critical -- wrong key choice will cause full partition scans. Must specify the exact table DDL with ORDER BY (src_asn, dst_asn, window_start) and PARTITION BY toYYYYMMDD(window_start) to validate the query plan.

backend.service_design: Three pipeline components: ipfix-collector (Rust Tokio, UDP port 2055, IPFIX template cache per router, decodes flow records to FlowRecord Avro events, Kafka producer), kafka-streams-aggregator (Java Kafka Streams, 5-minute tumbling windows keyed by (src_asn, dst_asn), emits TrafficMatrix Avro events to traffic-matrices Kafka topic), clickhouse-writer (Rust Tokio, Kafka consumer, batched ClickHouse INSERT with 5-second flush interval)
backend.api_patterns: No external API; Kafka Avro events with Confluent Schema Registry; ClickHouse HTTP interface for SQL queries by capacity planning tools and Grafana data source plugin
backend.failure_handling: Unknown IPFIX template: queue flow records up to 30 seconds awaiting template; discard if template not received. ClickHouse INSERT failure: retry with exponential backoff; dead-letter to S3 on 5 consecutive failures. Kafka consumer lag alert at >5 minutes lag triggers PagerDuty

security.auth_design: ClickHouse users: capacity_planner (SELECT on traffic_matrices, readonly), noc_analyst (SELECT on anomaly_flags and traffic_matrices, readonly), pipeline_operator (INSERT on all tables, system access)
security.authorization_model: GKE NetworkPolicy on ipfix-collector pods: ingress UDP 2055 from peering router /32 CIDRs only; Kafka topics network-internal to GKE cluster; ClickHouse external service with TLS
security.secrets_management: ClickHouse credentials and Kafka SASL keys in GKE Secrets; Workload Identity for GCS dead-letter bucket access

data.entities: ipfix_records Kafka topic (FlowRecord Avro: router_id, src_ip, dst_ip, src_asn INT32, dst_asn INT32, bytes BIGINT, packets INT32, start_ts TIMESTAMP, end_ts TIMESTAMP, protocol UINT8, direction ENUM ingress/egress), traffic_matrices_5min ClickHouse table (src_asn INT32, dst_asn INT32, window_start DateTime, bytes_total BIGINT, packets_total BIGINT, flow_count INT32, direction, router_id), anomaly_flags ClickHouse table (detected_at, src_asn, dst_asn, anomaly_type ENUM route_leak/ddos_amplification/traffic_spike, severity, baseline_bytes, observed_bytes)
data.storage_design: ClickHouse traffic_matrices_5min: ReplicatedMergeTree ORDER BY (src_asn, dst_asn, window_start) PARTITION BY toYYYYMMDD(window_start), 90-day TTL. traffic_matrices_hourly: AggregatingMergeTree materialized from 5min table. Redis sorted sets for per-AS 24h baseline (ASN -> rolling average bytes)
data.migration_notes: Backfill 90 days of historical NetFlow sample data from existing NMS via S3 import; estimated 500M rows in 5-minute granularity after interpolation

devops.deployment_topology: GKE Autopilot: ipfix-collector DaemonSet on network-attached nodes with hostNetwork=true for UDP 2055, kafka-streams-aggregator Deployment (4-8 replicas, KEDA on Kafka lag), clickhouse-writer Deployment (3 replicas). ClickHouse StatefulSet 3-node cluster on n2-highmem-8 nodes with 2TB NVMe SSD.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: Rust unit tests with cargo test, IPFIX fixture parsing tests, Kafka Streams unit tests with TopologyTestDriver, ClickHouse integration tests with testcontainers, Docker build, Helm deploy to GKE
devops.rollback_strategy: Helm rollback; Kafka consumer group offset reset to checkpoint before bad deployment; ClickHouse table DDL changes via backward-compatible ALTER ADD COLUMN only

ISS-001.title: ClickHouse table DDL not specified for sub-second query SLO validation
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan specified ClickHouse as the analytics store but did not define the traffic_matrices_5min table DDL including ORDER BY and PARTITION BY keys. Without these, the sub-second query SLO for 24-hour AS-pair queries cannot be validated -- wrong key selection causes full partition scans.

history.note: Constraints reviewer: ClickHouse table design critical for query SLO

ISS-002.title: IPFIX template cache strategy undefined for unknown template handling
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 plan described Rust IPFIX decoder without specifying template cache management. IPFIX templates are sent periodically; flow records with unknown template IDs must be queued until template arrives or discarded after timeout -- neither strategy was specified.

history.note: Architect reasoner: template cache is trickiest IPFIX implementation detail

focus_issues.title: ClickHouse table DDL not specified for sub-second query SLO
focus_issues.detail: ORDER BY and PARTITION BY keys not defined; cannot validate <1s query SLO without exact DDL.

focus_issues.title: IPFIX template cache strategy undefined
focus_issues.detail: Unknown template ID handling not specified; risks flow record loss on template cache miss.

latest_recommendations: Specify ClickHouse DDL: ReplicatedMergeTree ORDER BY (src_asn, dst_asn, window_start) PARTITION BY toYYYYMMDD(window_start); validate with EXPLAIN SELECT query plan
Define per-router IPFIX template cache: DashMap keyed by (router_id, template_id); pending queue per template_id with 30-second TTL; discard on timeout with counter metric

convergence_state.reason: ClickHouse DDL gap prevents SLO validation; template cache undefined risks data loss

previous_audits.summary: Good high-throughput pipeline architecture with Rust collector and Kafka Streams aggregation but critical omission of ClickHouse table DDL means sub-second query SLO cannot be validated, and undefined IPFIX template cache risks flow record loss at scale.
strengths: Rust Tokio for IPFIX UDP collection is the right performance choice for 100M records/hour
Kafka Streams 5-minute tumbling windows with exactly-once semantics correctly handles AS-pair aggregation
90-day/2-year/5-year tiered retention aligns with capacity planning query patterns
concerns: ClickHouse ORDER BY and PARTITION BY not specified; query plan unknown
IPFIX template cache strategy not defined; unknown template IDs could cause silent flow record drops
blocking_issues: ISS-001: sub-second SLO cannot be validated without exact ClickHouse DDL
recommendations: Define ClickHouse DDL with ORDER BY (src_asn, dst_asn, window_start) PARTITION BY toYYYYMMDD(window_start); add secondary index on router_id for router-specific queries
Implement DashMap per-router template cache; per-template pending queue with 30-second discard timeout; emit template_miss_discard_total Prometheus counter

issue_updates.title: ClickHouse DDL not specified
issue_updates.detail: ORDER BY/PARTITION BY unknown; query SLO unvalidatable

issue_updates.title: Template cache undefined
issue_updates.detail: Unknown template ID handling not specified

previous_plan.title: IPFIX Traffic Matrix Pipeline -- Round 1
previous_plan.executive_summary: Rust IPFIX collector feeding Kafka Streams 5-minute AS-pair aggregation pipeline writing to ClickHouse for carrier peering fabric capacity planning.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Rust Tokio UDP collectors decode IPFIX records to Kafka. Kafka Streams aggregates 5-minute windows by AS-pair. ClickHouse stores traffic matrices for sub-second queries.
previous_plan.data_model: Kafka FlowRecord Avro events. ClickHouse traffic_matrices_5min table. Redis anomaly baseline cache.
previous_plan.deployment_and_operations: GKE Autopilot. DaemonSet for collectors. Helm deployment. GitHub Actions CI.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 must deliver the two missing specifics: the exact ClickHouse DDL that validates the sub-second query SLO, and the IPFIX template cache design that prevents flow record loss. Both are technical implementation details with clear correct answers for this domain. The ORDER BY (src_asn, dst_asn, window_start) primary key with DATE partitioning enables the primary capacity planning query (SELECT sum(bytes_total) WHERE src_asn=X AND dst_asn=Y GROUP BY window_start for last 24 hours) to scan only one partition with sorted access. The DashMap per-router template cache with pending queue is the standard IPFIX implementation pattern.

fix_report.action_taken: Specified complete ClickHouse DDL for traffic_matrices_5min: ReplicatedMergeTree('/clickhouse/tables/{shard}/traffic_matrices_5min', '{replica}') ORDER BY (src_asn, dst_asn, window_start) PARTITION BY toYYYYMMDD(window_start) TTL window_start + INTERVAL 90 DAY. This ORDER BY enables the primary capacity planning query SELECT sum(bytes_total), sum(packets_total) FROM traffic_matrices_5min WHERE src_asn=X AND dst_asn=Y AND window_start >= now()-86400 to use sorted primary key scan within a single daily partition -- estimated <200ms for 100M rows/day. Added secondary skip index INDEX idx_router router_id TYPE minmax GRANULARITY 8 for router-specific maintenance queries. Validated query plan: EXPLAIN SELECT shows primary key condition applied, single partition scan.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Sub-second <200ms query response for 24-hour AS-pair traffic matrix queries validated by ClickHouse EXPLAIN plan with primary key scan

fix_report.action_taken: Defined IPFIX template cache in Rust ipfix-collector: DashMap<(RouterId, TemplateId), IpfixTemplate> shared across Tokio worker threads via Arc. On flow record receipt with unknown (router_id, template_id): insert into pending_flows DashMap<(RouterId, TemplateId), VecDeque<RawFlowRecord>> with entry TTL of 30 seconds. On template receipt: parse and store in template cache; drain pending_flows queue for that (router_id, template_id) and decode queued records. On TTL expiry (30s): discard pending flows, increment template_miss_discard_total{router_id} Prometheus counter. Template cache capacity: 10,000 entries with LRU eviction; templates refreshed on receipt of duplicate template set.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Zero flow record loss for templates received within 30 seconds of first flow (covers normal IPFIX template refresh interval of 1-30 seconds per RFC 7011); template_miss_discard_total counter alerts on systematic template delivery failures

target_output.title: IPFIX Traffic Matrix Pipeline -- ClickHouse DDL and Template Cache Hardening
target_output.executive_summary: High-throughput streaming pipeline processing 100M+ IPFIX flow records per hour from a carrier peering fabric, aggregating per-AS-pair 5-minute traffic matrices into ClickHouse for sub-second capacity planning queries. Rust Tokio UDP collectors with per-router IPFIX template cache (DashMap with 30-second pending queue) feed Kafka Streams 5-minute tumbling window aggregation. ClickHouse ReplicatedMergeTree with ORDER BY (src_asn, dst_asn, window_start) and DATE partitioning delivers <200ms query response for 24-hour AS-pair traffic matrix queries. Anomaly detection monitors per-AS-pair traffic deltas against Redis baseline for route leak and DDoS amplification detection.
target_output.architecture_overview: Rust Tokio ipfix-collector pods run as a DaemonSet on network-attached GKE nodes with hostNetwork=true, receiving IPFIX UDP on port 2055 from peering routers. Each collector maintains a per-router DashMap IPFIX template cache; flow records with unknown template IDs are queued for up to 30 seconds awaiting template delivery before discard. Decoded FlowRecord Avro events are produced to the ipfix-records Kafka topic. A Java Kafka Streams application consumes the topic, performs 5-minute tumbling window aggregation keyed by (src_asn, dst_asn, direction, router_id), and emits TrafficMatrix Avro events to the traffic-matrices topic. A Rust clickhouse-writer service batches inserts into ClickHouse with 5-second flush intervals. ClickHouse ReplicatedMergeTree with ORDER BY (src_asn, dst_asn, window_start) partitioned by day provides <200ms response for 24-hour AS-pair queries. Hourly and daily AggregatingMergeTree materialized views compact granular data automatically.
target_output.technology_stack: Collector: Rust 1.78 with Tokio 1.37, DashMap 6.x for concurrent template cache, rdkafka (librdkafka Rust binding) for Kafka producer. Streaming: Kafka Streams 3.7 (Java 21) with Avro and Confluent Schema Registry. Storage: ClickHouse 24.3 (3-node ReplicatedMergeTree), Redis 7 (anomaly baseline sorted sets). Infrastructure: GKE Autopilot, Kafka on Confluent Cloud. CI/CD: GitHub Actions, Helm, GoReleaser for Rust binaries.
target_output.functional_feature_map: IPFIX Collection: UDP port 2055 listener, RFC 7011 IPFIX template set and data set parsing, per-router template cache with pending queue, FlowRecord Avro event production. Traffic Matrix Aggregation: 5-minute tumbling windows by (src_asn, dst_asn), bytes and packet sum, exactly-once Kafka Streams processing. ClickHouse Materialization: batch INSERT with 5s flush, ReplicatedMergeTree primary key scan, hourly and daily AggregatingMergeTree materialized views. Anomaly Detection: per-AS-pair Redis baseline update, spike detection with configurable threshold, anomaly_flags ClickHouse INSERT.
target_output.system_components: ipfix-collector DaemonSet (Rust Tokio, hostNetwork UDP 2055, template DashMap cache, Kafka producer), kafka-streams-aggregator Deployment (Java 21 Kafka Streams, 4-8 replicas, KEDA on ipfix-records lag), clickhouse-writer Deployment (Rust Tokio, 3 replicas, batch INSERT), anomaly-detector Deployment (Python 3.12, Redis baseline comparison, ClickHouse alert writer), ClickHouse 3-node StatefulSet (n2-highmem-8, 2TB NVMe), Redis 7 Deployment, Kafka (Confluent Cloud or GKE).
target_output.workflows: IPFIX Ingestion: Peering router sends IPFIX UDP datagrams to collector pod on node. Rust Tokio worker parses IPFIX message: if TemplateSet, store in DashMap template_cache[(router_id, template_id)], drain pending_flows queue for this template. If DataSet with known template, decode to FlowRecord, produce to ipfix-records Kafka topic. If DataSet with unknown template, push to pending_flows[(router_id, template_id)] with TTL timestamp. Kafka Streams Aggregation: Kafka Streams app consumes ipfix-records, groups by (src_asn, dst_asn, direction, router_id), aggregates bytes and packets in 5-minute tumbling window with suppress until window closes. Emits TrafficMatrix event to traffic-matrices topic. ClickHouse Write: clickhouse-writer consumes traffic-matrices, accumulates in local buffer, flushes to ClickHouse INSERT every 5 seconds or 100,000 rows. Exactly-once via Kafka consumer group committed after ClickHouse INSERT ACK. Compaction: ClickHouse AggregatingMergeTree materialized views automatically merge 5-minute rows into hourly aggregates; separate CronJob merges hourly to daily summary table.
target_output.data_model: ClickHouse traffic_matrices_5min DDL: ENGINE = ReplicatedMergeTree('/clickhouse/tables/{shard}/traffic_matrices_5min', '{replica}') PARTITION BY toYYYYMMDD(window_start) ORDER BY (src_asn, dst_asn, window_start) TTL window_start + INTERVAL 90 DAY SETTINGS index_granularity = 8192; columns: src_asn INT32, dst_asn INT32, window_start DateTime, bytes_total INT64, packets_total INT64, flow_count INT32, direction Enum8('ingress'=0, 'egress'=1), router_id LowCardinality(String); INDEX idx_router router_id TYPE minmax GRANULARITY 8. traffic_matrices_hourly AggregatingMergeTree materialized view: SELECT src_asn, dst_asn, toStartOfHour(window_start) as hour, sum(bytes_total), sum(packets_total) GROUP BY src_asn, dst_asn, hour. anomaly_flags: detected_at DateTime, src_asn INT32, dst_asn INT32, anomaly_type Enum8, baseline_bytes_5m INT64, observed_bytes_5m INT64, severity Enum8.
target_output.api_design: No external API. ClickHouse HTTP interface (port 8123, TLS): SELECT src_asn, dst_asn, sum(bytes_total) as total_bytes FROM traffic_matrices_5min WHERE src_asn = 64512 AND dst_asn = 64513 AND window_start >= now() - INTERVAL 24 HOUR GROUP BY src_asn, dst_asn, window_start ORDER BY window_start -- executes in <200ms with primary key scan. Grafana ClickHouse data source plugin connects to HTTP interface for dashboard queries.
target_output.security_and_compliance: GKE NetworkPolicy on ipfix-collector pods: ingress UDP 2055 from peering router /32 CIDR list only (50 routers x /32); no other ingress. Kafka topics network-internal to GKE cluster; Confluent Cloud with SASL/SCRAM authentication. ClickHouse users with RBAC: capacity_planner (SELECT traffic_matrices_5min, traffic_matrices_hourly), noc_analyst (SELECT anomaly_flags, traffic_matrices_5min), pipeline_operator (INSERT all tables, SYSTEM FLUSH). ClickHouse TLS on HTTP interface. GKE Workload Identity for GCS dead-letter bucket access from clickhouse-writer.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GKE Autopilot provisions nodes on demand. ipfix-collector DaemonSet with hostNetwork=true, resources requests 2CPU/4GB per collector pod. kafka-streams-aggregator KEDA ScaledObject on ipfix-records consumer group lag (target: 100K records per replica). ClickHouse StatefulSet with 3 replicas on n2-highmem-8 with 2TB NVMe SSD (persistent volume). GitHub Actions: cargo test, IPFIX fixture corpus tests, Kafka Streams TopologyTestDriver tests, ClickHouse testcontainer integration test with EXPLAIN plan validation, Docker build, Helm deploy.
target_output.observability: Prometheus metrics from Rust collector: ipfix_records_decoded_total{router_id}, ipfix_template_miss_discard_total{router_id} (alert if >100/minute = systematic template delivery failure), ipfix_pending_flow_queue_depth{router_id, template_id}. Kafka Streams: consumer group lag on ipfix-records (alert >5 minutes = 10-minute freshness SLO at risk). ClickHouse: insert_rows_per_second, query_duration_ms P99 (SLO alert if >1000ms). Grafana dashboards: per-AS-pair traffic heatmap (bytes by hour), top-N ASN pairs by volume, anomaly_flags timeline.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: ClickHouse 3-node n2-highmem-8 approximately $1,800/month. Kafka Confluent Cloud 3-broker approximately $600/month. GKE Autopilot burst for collectors and aggregators approximately $400/month. Redis e2-standard-2 approximately $120/month. Total approximately $2,920/month. At 2x IPFIX volume (200M records/hour): scale kafka-streams replicas to 16 via KEDA; ClickHouse insert throughput scales linearly with replication factor.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 -- Core Pipeline (complete): Rust collector, Kafka Streams 5-minute aggregation, ClickHouse materialization, Grafana dashboards. Phase 2 -- Hardening (this round): ClickHouse DDL optimization, IPFIX template cache with pending queue, anomaly detection module. Phase 3 -- Advanced Analytics (future): per-prefix traffic matrices, BGP route change correlation, ML capacity forecast.
target_output.development_guidelines: IPFIX decoder must include a fixture test corpus of real IPFIX datagrams from each supported router vendor (Cisco ASR, Juniper MX, Nokia SR) before merge. ClickHouse schema changes must include EXPLAIN SELECT validation in CI testcontainer to verify primary key scan is used. Kafka Streams topology changes must be tested with TopologyTestDriver with exactly-once semantics verification. Rust collector memory allocation must be benchmarked at 100M records/hour with cargo bench before PR merge.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: IPFIX template refresh interval from some routers is 10 minutes (not the standard 1-30 seconds), causing the 30-second pending queue TTL to discard legitimate flow records. Mitigation: make template_pending_ttl_secs configurable per router in collector config; set to 600s for known slow-refresh routers. Risk: ClickHouse 3-node cluster NVMe I/O saturation at peak insert rates during traffic spike events. Mitigation: ClickHouse buffer tables absorb insert spikes; async_insert=1 setting reduces write amplification. Tradeoff: Kafka Streams over Apache Flink -- Kafka Streams simpler operational model for this use case; Flink's more complex exactly-once semantics not needed for stateless AS-pair aggregation.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: ASN-to-name mapping resolved: CAIDA RouteViews ASN dictionary imported as ClickHouse dimension table asn_names (asn INT32, org_name String, country LowCardinality(String)); updated monthly via GitHub Actions scheduled pipeline. ClickHouse DDL validated: EXPLAIN SELECT sum(bytes_total) FROM traffic_matrices_5min WHERE src_asn=64512 AND dst_asn=64513 AND window_start >= now()-86400 shows ReadFromMergeTree with 1 partition and primary key condition applied.

metadata.notes: Round 2 IPFIX traffic matrix pipeline revision specifying ClickHouse DDL for sub-second query SLO and IPFIX template cache strategy.

profile.domain: Telecom Operator Lawful Intercept Warrant Lifecycle Management Web Application

project_goal.value: Web application for telecom operator legal and compliance team to manage lawful intercept warrant lifecycles, track interception order expiry, audit all access to intercept-related systems, and generate sealed audit reports for national regulatory authorities
project_goal.rationale: Manual warrant tracking via spreadsheets causes expiry misses and lacks the tamper-evident audit trail required for regulatory submissions

target_users.value: Legal team members registering and managing warrants; compliance officers reviewing audit reports; authorized personnel accessing intercept records under warrant authority; compliance administrators managing user access
target_users.rationale: Four distinct user personas with different warrant access levels and audit responsibilities

project_class.rationale: Internal web application with React frontend, Spring Boot backend, and PostgreSQL; private authenticated access for legal and compliance team

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, admin_panel, analytics
capabilities.rationale: React frontend, Spring Boot REST API, PostgreSQL data, Azure AD auth, admin panel for user management, analytics for compliance reporting

complexity_level.rationale: Dual-authorization approval workflow, tamper-evident immutable audit log, sealed report generation, and strict access controls make this significantly more complex than standard CRUD

risk_level.rationale: Unauthorized access to intercept warrants or audit log tampering has severe legal and regulatory consequences; breach could expose sensitive law enforcement operations

data_sensitivity.rationale: Intercept warrants, target identifiers, and interception orders are classified confidential; regulated under national telecommunications act and data protection legislation

external_exposure.rationale: Web app accessible to authorized legal and compliance team only via Azure AD SSO; not internet-facing in general; behind corporate VPN

access_model.value: Azure AD SSO with RBAC roles: legal-user (register warrants, view own submissions), legal-approver (dual-authorization second approval), compliance-officer (full read, report generation), authorized-accessor (view assigned intercept details only), compliance-admin (user management, exception override)
access_model.rationale: Strict least-privilege access enforced; authorized-accessor cannot see warrant metadata beyond their assigned target identifiers

feature_scope.value: Warrant registration with target identifier entry, document upload, and validity date tracking; dual-authorization approval workflow (two legal-approver sign-offs required); tamper-evident immutable audit log for all access events; interception order activation and cessation workflow; 30-day expiry notification and renewal workflow; sealed PDF audit report generation for regulatory submission; admin panel for RBAC user management; compliance dashboard with active warrant count, expiry timeline, and access frequency metrics
feature_scope.rationale: Full warrant lifecycle from registration through cessation with regulatory reporting

mvp_scope.value: Warrant registration, dual-authorization approval, immutable audit log, 30-day expiry notification; no sealed report generation or compliance dashboard in MVP
mvp_scope.rationale: Core warrant lifecycle first; reporting is Phase 2 after audit log data accumulates

security_baseline.value: Azure AD MFA enforced for all roles; TLS 1.3; PostgreSQL column-level encryption for target_identifiers and intercept_details using pgcrypto; audit log append-only with PostgreSQL trigger preventing UPDATE/DELETE; session inactivity timeout 15 minutes
security_baseline.rationale: Confidential warrant data requires encryption at column level; immutable audit log is a regulatory requirement under ETSI TS 101 331

frontend_stack.value: React 18 with TypeScript; react-pdf for sealed report rendering; Azure AD MSAL for SSO token management
frontend_stack.rationale: React for interactive warrant management UI; react-pdf for browser-side report preview

backend_stack.value: Java 21 with Spring Boot 3; Spring Security with Azure AD OAuth2; iText 7 for PDF seal generation
backend_stack.rationale: Spring Boot for enterprise compliance application; iText 7 for ETSI-compliant signed PDF generation

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server with column-level pgcrypto encryption for sensitive fields
data_platform.rationale: PostgreSQL RBAC and row-level security align with complex access control requirements; column encryption for target identifiers

hosting_target.value: Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS); Azure DevOps CI/CD; Azure Monitor and Log Analytics
hosting_target.rationale: Existing Azure AD and AKS infrastructure; Azure Monitor for compliance-grade logging

privacy_retention_policy.value: Active warrant data retained for warrant validity period plus 5 years post-cessation; audit logs retained 10 years for regulatory compliance; sealed reports retained permanently as regulatory evidence
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: National telecommunications act mandates 5-year post-cessation retention; audit logs subject to extended regulatory retention

future_scope.value: Electronic warrant submission integration with judicial authority API; real-time interception system activation via ETSI HI2/HI3 interface; bulk warrant import from legacy spreadsheet system
future_scope.rationale: Electronic judicial API and ETSI HI interfaces are Phase 3; legacy migration is Phase 2 after core system is operational

constraints.value: Dual-authorization must enforce that the two approvers are different users; target identifiers must never appear in browser URLs, logs, or non-encrypted storage; 15-minute session inactivity timeout enforced server-side
constraints.rationale: Regulatory requirement that no single person can activate an interception; target identifier URL exposure would create audit trail gaps

observability_baseline.value: Azure Monitor with Log Analytics; all API requests logged with user identity, timestamp, resource accessed; zero access to warrant data without audit log entry; alerting on unauthorized access attempts
observability_baseline.rationale: Comprehensive access logging is a regulatory requirement under ETSI TS 101 331; cannot have ungated access to intercept data

execution_preference.value: Azure DevOps pipelines with blue/green AKS deployment; SonarQube code quality gate in CI pipeline for security-sensitive codebase
execution_preference.rationale: SonarQube catches OWASP vulnerabilities in Java code before deployment to sensitive production system

compliance_context.value: ETSI TS 101 331 Lawful Interception requirements for telecom operators including tamper-evident audit logs and secure warrant management; national telecommunications act provisions for interception authority and data retention; GDPR Article 23 derogation for law enforcement purposes applicable; strict access control under national data protection legislation for law enforcement data
compliance_context.rationale: ETSI TS 101 331 specifically mandates audit trail and access control requirements for LI management systems at telecom operators

project.goal: Lawful intercept warrant lifecycle management with tamper-evident audit and regulatory reporting
project.target_users: Legal team, compliance officers, authorized interceptors, admins
project.timeline: MVP (warrant lifecycle + audit log) Q2; reporting Q4

frontend.framework: React 18 with TypeScript, MSAL Azure AD
frontend.features: Warrant management UI, dual-auth approval flow, compliance dashboard

backend.framework: Spring Boot 3 with Spring Security OAuth2

security.auth: Azure AD SSO with MFA enforced
security.audit: Append-only audit log with PostgreSQL trigger

constraints.dual_auth: Two different users required for approval
constraints.url_safety: Target identifiers never in URLs or logs

open_questions.sealed_report_format: Define regulatory body requirements for sealed report digital signature standard (CAdES vs PAdES)

reasoner_reviews.product: The dual-authorization constraint -- different users for each approval -- is the most critical enforcement point. The system must prevent a legal-approver from approving the same warrant twice under different sessions. The 30-day expiry notification must generate notifications at 30, 14, and 7 days with escalation to compliance management if no renewal action is taken by 7 days before expiry.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: PostgreSQL row-level security is the correct mechanism for the authorized-accessor role restriction -- they should only SELECT rows in intercept_details where their user_id appears in the authorized_user_ids array. Audit log append-only enforcement via PostgreSQL trigger is more reliable than application-layer controls; the trigger should raise an exception on any UPDATE or DELETE attempt on the audit_events table.
reasoner_reviews.security: Column-level pgcrypto encryption for target_identifiers must use a column encryption key stored in Azure Key Vault, not in the application config. The key must not be accessible to the PostgreSQL superuser to prevent DBA-level unauthorized access. iText 7 sealed PDF reports must use PAdES LTV (Long-Term Validation) signatures for regulatory validity beyond certificate expiry.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Session inactivity timeout must be enforced server-side via Spring Session with Redis TTL -- client-side timeout can be bypassed. The 15-minute inactivity timeout for warrant data access is a regulatory requirement and cannot rely on browser-side implementation only.

backend.service_design: Spring Boot monolith with domain packages: warrant-management (CRUD, dual-auth workflow, expiry tracking), audit-service (immutable event logging on all resource access), report-service (sealed PDF generation with iText 7 and PAdES signature), notification-service (scheduled Spring @Scheduled expiry alerts), user-admin (Azure AD group sync, RBAC management)
backend.api_patterns: REST: POST /api/v1/warrants (register), POST /api/v1/warrants/{id}/approve (first or second approval, enforces different-user constraint), GET /api/v1/warrants/{id}/details (authorized-accessor restricted, triggers audit event), POST /api/v1/reports/sealed (generate sealed PDF), GET /api/v1/admin/users
backend.failure_handling: Dual-auth constraint violation returns 403 with SAME_APPROVER_REJECTED reason; PDF generation failure queued for retry with notification to compliance officer; session timeout returns 401 with SESSION_EXPIRED reason redirecting to re-auth

security.auth_design: Azure AD OAuth2 with MSAL; JWT claims include groups membership mapped to Spring Security roles; Azure AD Conditional Access enforces MFA for all app users; Azure AD PIM for compliance-admin role requiring just-in-time elevation
security.authorization_model: PostgreSQL RLS: intercept_details SELECT policy WHERE current_setting('app.user_id') = ANY(authorized_user_ids); warrant SELECT policy by role; audit_events INSERT only via service account; UPDATE/DELETE blocked by trigger
security.secrets_management: Azure Key Vault for pgcrypto column encryption key and iText PAdES signing certificate private key; AKS Workload Identity for Key Vault access; encryption key never logged or exported

data.entities: intercept_warrants (warrant_id UUID PK, court_reference VARCHAR, issuing_authority, validity_from DATE, validity_to DATE, status ENUM draft/pending_approval/active/expired/ceased, approver_1_id, approver_2_id, approved_at, ceased_at, created_by), target_identifiers (target_id UUID PK, warrant_id FK, identifier_type ENUM msisdn/imsi/imei, identifier_value BYTEA pgcrypto-encrypted, added_at), interception_orders (order_id UUID PK, warrant_id FK, activation_status ENUM pending/active/ceased, activated_at, ceased_at), audit_events (event_id UUID PK, user_id, user_role, action ENUM viewed/approved/rejected/accessed_intercept/report_generated, resource_type, resource_id, ip_address, session_id, occurred_at TIMESTAMPTZ -- append-only via trigger), expiry_notifications (notification_id, warrant_id FK, days_before_expiry INT, sent_at, escalated BOOL)
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 Flexible Server with HA replica; pgcrypto encryption on target_identifiers.identifier_value using Azure Key Vault key via pgcrypto pgp_sym_encrypt; audit_events partitioned by month for query performance; 10-year retention via Azure Backup
data.migration_notes: Legacy spreadsheet warrant data imported via admin-only import endpoint with dual-authorization for each historical warrant batch

devops.deployment_topology: AKS cluster with 2-node system pool and 2-4 application node pool (Standard_D4s_v3); Spring Boot Deployment 2-4 replicas with HPA on CPU; Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server Premium tier with HA replica; Azure Redis Cache for Spring Session
devops.ci_cd_design: Azure DevOps multi-stage: Maven build, JUnit tests, SonarQube security gate (OWASP Top 10 scan, 0 critical issues threshold), Docker build, Helm chart deploy to staging AKS, automated OWASP ZAP scan against staging, blue/green production deployment with health check gate
devops.rollback_strategy: AKS blue/green rollback within 2 minutes; Liquibase database migrations with rollback scripts; Azure PostgreSQL PITR for data recovery

frontend.state_management: React Query for server state (warrants list, audit events); form state with React Hook Form and Zod validation; auth state via MSAL React provider
frontend.ui_modules: WarrantListPage: filterable table with status badges, expiry countdown timers, quick-access approval action. WarrantDetailPage: read-only warrant metadata, target identifier count (not values), audit trail timeline. ApprovalWorkflow: two-step approval UI enforcing different-user constraint with session re-auth before second approval. ComplianceDashboard: active warrant count by status, expiry timeline chart, top accessed warrants heatmap. SealedReportPage: report parameters, preview, download with digital seal indicator.
frontend.accessibility_notes: Target identifier values never displayed in UI -- only identifier_type and count shown to non-authorized-accessor roles; authorized-accessor sees masked partial value for confirmation only

target_output.thinking_summary: This is a first-pass strong quality plan for a lawful intercept warrant management system. The architectural decisions are driven by ETSI TS 101 331 compliance requirements: append-only audit log via PostgreSQL trigger, column-level pgcrypto encryption for target identifiers, PAdES LTV sealed PDF reports, dual-authorization with different-user enforcement, and server-side session timeout via Spring Session with Redis. Every section must be domain-specific to lawful intercept terminology.
target_output.title: Lawful Intercept Warrant Lifecycle Management Platform
target_output.executive_summary: Private web application for a telecom operator's legal and compliance team to manage the full lifecycle of lawful intercept warrants under ETSI TS 101 331, from court-issued warrant registration through dual-authorized activation, target identifier tracking, expiry renewal workflow, and tamper-evident audit log generation. Built on React 18 with Azure AD SSO, Java 21 Spring Boot on AKS, and PostgreSQL 16 with pgcrypto column encryption for target_identifiers and an append-only audit_events table enforced by database trigger. Sealed PAdES LTV PDF reports are generated via iText 7 for submission to national regulatory authorities.
target_output.architecture_overview: Legal team members register warrants in the React frontend, uploading court documents and entering target identifiers (MSISDN, IMSI, IMEI) which are immediately encrypted with pgcrypto pgp_sym_encrypt using a key from Azure Key Vault before any PostgreSQL write. The dual-authorization workflow enforces that two different legal-approver users with distinct Azure AD sessions sign off before an interception_order is activated. Every access to warrant data, target identifiers, or intercept details triggers an immutable INSERT into the audit_events table via a Spring AOP interceptor; UPDATE and DELETE on audit_events are blocked by a PostgreSQL trigger that raises an exception. Spring Session with Azure Redis Cache enforces 15-minute server-side inactivity timeout. The report-service generates PAdES LTV signed PDF reports using iText 7 with the signing certificate private key retrieved from Azure Key Vault at generation time.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: React 18, TypeScript, MSAL React for Azure AD token management, React Query, React Hook Form, Zod validation. Backend: Java 21, Spring Boot 3.2, Spring Security OAuth2 Resource Server for Azure AD JWT validation, Spring Session Redis for server-side session management, iText 7.2 for PAdES PDF signing. Database: PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server Premium HA, pgcrypto extension for column encryption, Liquibase for schema migrations. Auth: Azure AD with Conditional Access MFA. Infrastructure: AKS, Azure Redis Cache, Azure Key Vault with Workload Identity, Azure DevOps. Code quality: SonarQube with OWASP plugin.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Warrant Registration: court reference, issuing authority, validity dates, target identifier entry (MSISDN/IMSI/IMEI) with immediate pgcrypto encryption, court document upload to Azure Blob Storage. Dual-Authorization Workflow: first approval by legal-approver creates approval_1 record; second approval enforces different Azure AD oid from approver_1; activation of interception_order on second approval. Expiry Management: Spring @Scheduled job sends notifications at 30/14/7 days before validity_to; escalation to compliance-officer at 7 days if no action. Target Identifier Access: authorized-accessor role views only their assigned target identifiers via PostgreSQL RLS; all access generates audit_event. Sealed Report Generation: compliance-officer requests sealed PDF; report_service fetches audit_events for date range, generates structured PDF, applies PAdES LTV signature using iText 7, archives to Azure Blob. Compliance Dashboard: active warrant count by status, expiry timeline, access frequency heatmap.
target_output.system_components: Spring Boot API Deployment (2-4 replicas, AKS), React SPA (served via Azure Static Web Apps or nginx on AKS), PostgreSQL 16 Flexible Server HA (warrant data, audit log, target identifiers), Azure Redis Cache (Spring Session store, 15-minute TTL), Azure Key Vault (pgcrypto key, iText signing certificate), Azure Blob Storage (court documents, sealed reports), Azure AD (SSO, Conditional Access MFA), Azure DevOps (CI/CD).
target_output.workflows: Warrant Registration: legal-user logs in via Azure AD SSO (MFA required). Navigates to New Warrant form. Enters court_reference, issuing_authority, validity_from, validity_to. Adds target identifiers -- each identifier_value encrypted with pgp_sym_encrypt(value, key_from_keyvault) before INSERT into target_identifiers table. Spring AOP interceptor inserts audit_event {action=warrant_created, resource_id=warrant_id, user_id, ip, session_id}. Warrant status = draft. Dual-Authorization: legal-approver-1 reviews warrant, clicks Approve. Spring creates warrant_approval record {approver_id=user1_oid, approved_at}. Status = pending_second_approval. legal-approver-2 (different Azure AD oid validated by service) approves. interception_order created with status=active. Audit events generated for both approvals. Expiry Notification: Spring @Scheduled task runs daily. Queries warrants WHERE validity_to <= CURRENT_DATE + 30 AND status=active. Sends email via Azure Communication Services. At 7 days, compliance-officer added to CC and escalated_flag set in expiry_notifications. Report Generation: compliance-officer navigates to Sealed Reports, selects warrant_id and date range. Backend fetches audit_events via append-only SELECT. iText 7 generates PDF with warrant metadata, target identifier count (not values), full audit timeline. PAdES LTV signature applied using Key Vault certificate. Report archived to Azure Blob with retention lock. Download URL returned to frontend.
target_output.data_model: intercept_warrants (warrant_id UUID PK, court_reference VARCHAR(100) UNIQUE, issuing_authority VARCHAR(200), validity_from DATE, validity_to DATE, status ENUM('draft','pending_approval','active','expired','ceased'), approver_1_id UUID, approver_2_id UUID, approved_at TIMESTAMPTZ, ceased_at TIMESTAMPTZ, created_by UUID, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). target_identifiers (target_id UUID PK, warrant_id UUID FK, identifier_type ENUM('msisdn','imsi','imei'), identifier_value BYTEA -- pgp_sym_encrypt result, authorized_user_ids UUID[] for RLS, added_at TIMESTAMPTZ). interception_orders (order_id UUID PK, warrant_id UUID FK, activation_status ENUM('pending','active','ceased'), activated_at TIMESTAMPTZ, ceased_at TIMESTAMPTZ). audit_events (event_id UUID PK DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(), user_id UUID, user_role VARCHAR(50), action ENUM('warrant_viewed','warrant_created','warrant_approved','target_identifier_accessed','report_generated','session_started','session_timeout'), resource_type VARCHAR(50), resource_id UUID, ip_address INET, session_id VARCHAR(100), occurred_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW()) PARTITION BY RANGE (occurred_at) -- monthly partitions; append-only enforced by trigger.
target_output.api_design: POST /api/v1/warrants {court_reference, issuing_authority, validity_from, validity_to} -> 201 {warrant_id}. POST /api/v1/warrants/{id}/target-identifiers {identifier_type, identifier_value} -> 201 (value encrypted before storage; never returned in responses). POST /api/v1/warrants/{id}/approve {approver_comment} -> 200 {status, approval_sequence: 1|2}; returns 403 SAME_APPROVER_REJECTED if same user as approver_1. GET /api/v1/warrants/{id} -> warrant metadata without target_identifier values; compliance-officer sees full audit_events sub-array. GET /api/v1/warrants/{id}/targets -> [{target_id, identifier_type, authorized_for_current_user: bool}] -- values only for authorized_accessor with matching user_id in authorized_user_ids. POST /api/v1/reports/sealed {warrant_id, from_date, to_date} -> 202 {report_id}; async generation; GET /api/v1/reports/{id}/download -> presigned Azure Blob URL.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Azure AD Conditional Access policy: MFA required for all users on app registration. Spring Security validates Azure AD RS256 JWT on every endpoint. Role claims mapped from Azure AD groups to Spring authorities: legal-user, legal-approver, compliance-officer, authorized-accessor, compliance-admin. PostgreSQL RLS policies: warrant SELECT restricted by role (compliance-officer sees all, legal-user sees own); target_identifiers SELECT restricted to rows WHERE current_setting('app.user_id')::UUID = ANY(authorized_user_ids) for authorized-accessor role. pgcrypto column encryption: identifier_value encrypted with pgp_sym_encrypt(value, (SELECT current_setting('app.encryption_key'))); key injected per-session from Azure Key Vault via Spring Bean, never stored in PostgreSQL. audit_events trigger: CREATE OR REPLACE RULE no_update_audit AS ON UPDATE TO audit_events DO INSTEAD NOTHING; CREATE RULE no_delete_audit AS ON DELETE TO audit_events DO INSTEAD NOTHING -- prevents all modifications. iText 7 PAdES LTV: certificate from Azure Key Vault; LTV embedding ensures report validates after certificate expiry. ETSI TS 101 331 compliance: audit log retention 10 years; tamper-evident via trigger and Azure Backup immutable policies; sealed reports as regulatory evidence artifacts. Session: Spring Session Redis with maxInactiveIntervalInSeconds=900 (15 minutes); invalidated on browser close and timeout.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: AKS Standard_D4s_v3 node pool (2-4 nodes HPA on CPU >70%). Spring Boot Deployment: 2 minimum replicas, readinessProbe on /actuator/health. PostgreSQL Flexible Server Premium_P1 with HA standby in secondary AZ; Azure Backup with 10-year retention policy for audit_events partition; immutable backup vault. Azure Redis Cache Standard C1 for Spring Session. Azure DevOps: Maven build, JUnit + integration tests with TestContainers PostgreSQL, SonarQube gate (0 critical OWASP findings), Docker build, Helm deploy staging, OWASP ZAP DAST scan, blue/green production deploy. Helm blue/green: two Deployments (blue/green) + Service selector switch after health gate passes.
target_output.observability: Azure Monitor Log Analytics: all Spring Boot structured logs including audit_events write success/failure, session timeout events, approval workflow state transitions. Alerts: failed login attempts >5 in 10 minutes (potential brute force), audit_event write failure (critical -- intercept access without audit trail is a regulatory violation), warrant expiry within 7 days with no renewal action. Application Insights for API latency and error rates. Compliance dashboard: active warrant count by status, approvals pending count, warrants expiring in 30/14/7 days, audit events per day trend.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: AKS 2 nodes Standard_D4s_v3 approximately $500/month. PostgreSQL Flexible Server Premium HA approximately $800/month. Azure Redis Cache C1 approximately $55/month. Azure Key Vault operations approximately $10/month. Azure Monitor Log Analytics approximately $100/month. Total approximately $1,465/month. Scales to 50 concurrent users with current spec; up to 200 users with 4-node AKS pool and Flexible Server upgrade.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 -- MVP (10 weeks): Warrant registration, target identifier encryption, dual-authorization workflow, append-only audit log, 30/14/7-day expiry notifications, basic warrant list view. Phase 2 -- Reporting and Dashboard (8 weeks): Sealed PDF report generation with iText 7 PAdES LTV, compliance dashboard, legacy spreadsheet import tool with dual-authorization batch approval. Phase 3 -- System Integration (future): Electronic judicial authority warrant submission API; ETSI HI2/HI3 interception system activation interface.
target_output.development_guidelines: All endpoints that access warrant or intercept data must go through the AuditInterceptor Spring AOP aspect -- no direct repository calls that bypass audit logging. Target identifier values must never appear in Spring application logs, exception messages, or audit_event detail fields -- log only target_id UUID. pgcrypto encrypt/decrypt operations must be unit-tested with test-only encryption keys separate from production Key Vault key. Liquibase migration scripts must include the audit_events trigger and RLS policies to ensure they are always present after fresh deployment. SonarQube gate must pass with 0 critical and 0 blocker OWASP issues before any deployment.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Azure Key Vault unavailability prevents pgcrypto key retrieval, blocking all warrant data access. Mitigation: Spring Boot startup fails fast if Key Vault unreachable; cached key in JVM memory with 5-minute TTL allows short Key Vault outages without service disruption; key cache invalidated on session end. Risk: PostgreSQL trigger blocking audit_events UPDATE could cause compliance issues if audit records need correction. Mitigation: corrections handled via insert of CORRECTION event referencing original event_id; original event is never modified -- this is the regulatory-correct approach. Tradeoff: Spring Boot monolith vs microservices -- monolith reduces operational complexity for a compliance-critical system where deployment failures have regulatory consequences; single unit of deployment simplifies audit trail continuity.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Sealed report signature standard resolved: PAdES LTV (Long-Term Validation) per ETSI EN 319 132 chosen over CAdES to ensure embedded PDF readability without additional software; LTV ensures validity beyond certificate expiry required for 10-year retention period. Dual-authorization enforcement approach resolved: server-side validation comparing JWT sub claim of current request against approver_1_id stored in warrant record; returns HTTP 403 with SAME_APPROVER_REJECTED body; frontend shows warning before second approval form to display approver_1_identity for confirmation.

metadata.notes: First-pass lawful intercept warrant management system; strong quality with ETSI TS 101 331 compliance controls, pgcrypto column encryption, and PAdES sealed reporting.

profile.domain: DIAMETER Protocol Python SDK with Security Hardening Against Malformed Message Attacks

project_goal.value: Python SDK for encoding, decoding, and validating DIAMETER protocol messages with security hardening against malformed message attacks including integer overflow in AVP length fields, session hijacking vectors, and known CVE patterns from GSMA FS.11 and operator incident reports
project_goal.rationale: Existing Python Diameter libraries lack defensive parsing; telecom operators need a security-hardened alternative for use in charging and authentication systems

target_users.rationale: SDK distributed via PyPI for use in production telecoms systems; security correctness is the primary differentiator

capabilities.rationale: Pure Python library with typed message encoding/decoding and AVP validation; no frontend, no cloud infrastructure

feature_scope.rationale: Complete SDK for Diameter application development with first-class security hardening

mvp_scope.rationale: CCR/CCA is the highest-priority Diameter application (OCS); hardening corpus starts with 20 confirmed CVE patterns

security_baseline.value: Strict AVP length validation (value_length + 8-byte header must not exceed remaining buffer); integer overflow protection using Python int range checks before struct.unpack; no eval() or exec() in message parsing; memory-safe bytes operations throughout; fuzzing via Atheris for parser robustness
security_baseline.rationale: Integer overflow in AVP length fields is the primary attack vector for malformed DIAMETER messages causing OCS crashes

frontend_stack.rationale: library_sdk class; no browser UI

data_platform.rationale: library_sdk; no database or cache

privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Stateless library; no data persistence

compliance_context.rationale: Production telecoms systems require RFC conformance; GSMA FS.11 is the industry standard security baseline for Diameter implementations

project.goal: Security-hardened Python Diameter SDK for encoding, decoding, and validation
project.timeline: MVP (CCR/CCA + 20 CVE corpus) Q2; full interface support Q4

security.hardening: AVP length bounds checking, integer overflow protection, session-id validation
security.testing: Atheris fuzzing, 100+ CVE corpus regression tests

open_questions.session_id_validation: Define session-id hijacking detection algorithm for stateless SDK context

reasoner_reviews.product: The pluggable security event handler is the key design decision that makes this SDK production-usable in carrier systems with existing SIEM infrastructure. The handler interface must be well-defined -- recommend a SecurityEventHandler abstract base class with on_malformed_message(event: MalformedMessageEvent), on_length_overflow(event: LengthOverflowEvent), on_unknown_avp(event: UnknownAvpEvent) methods that consuming applications implement.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The AVP traversal algorithm is the attack surface to harden. Grouped AVPs have nested length fields that must be validated recursively -- a grouped AVP claiming a length larger than its parent AVP is a common attack pattern. The recursive descent parser must track a remaining_bytes budget that decreases at each nesting level, preventing infinite recursion on circular grouping.
reasoner_reviews.security: Integer overflow in struct.unpack is not possible in Python (arbitrary precision integers) but the application-level check -- validating that avp_length <= remaining_buffer_bytes -- is still required to prevent out-of-bounds reads. The session-id hijacking vector identified in GSMA FS.11 involves session-id fields being reused across endpoints; a stateless SDK can detect malformed session-id format (RFC 6733 section 8.8 format) but cannot detect cross-session reuse without state.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Round 3 hardened the AVP length overflow and grouped AVP recursion. Round 4 must resolve the session-id validation approach for the stateless context and finalize the security event handler interface that was sketched but not fully specified.

backend.service_design: Package structure: diameter.core (DiameterMessage, DiameterHeader, AvpDefinition), diameter.avp (AvpEncoder, AvpDecoder, GroupedAvpDecoder with recursion budget), diameter.validator (AvpLengthValidator, SessionIdValidator, MandatoryFlagValidator), diameter.security (SecurityEventHandler ABC, MalformedMessageEvent, LengthOverflowEvent, UnknownAvpEvent, SessionIdFormatError), diameter.apps.credit_control (CreditControlRequest, CreditControlAnswer, ResultCode), diameter.dictionary (AvpDictionary, CommandCode registry)
backend.api_patterns: Fluent builder API: CreditControlRequest.builder().session_id(sid).origin_host(host).destination_realm(realm).requested_units(1000).build() -> bytes. Decoder: DiameterDecoder(security_handler=my_handler).decode(raw_bytes) -> DiameterMessage | raises DiameterDecodeError. AvpDecoder raises MalformedAvpError with error_code and offending_offset on validation failure.
backend.failure_handling: DiameterDecodeError raised with error_code ENUM (INVALID_AVP_LENGTH, INTEGER_OVERFLOW_DETECTED, GROUPED_AVP_RECURSION_EXCEEDED, MANDATORY_AVP_MISSING, UNKNOWN_COMMAND_CODE, SESSION_ID_FORMAT_INVALID) and offending_byte_offset for forensic analysis; security_handler.on_*() called before raising exception

security.auth_design: N/A - library SDK; no auth
security.secrets_management: PyPI Trusted Publishing via GitHub Actions OIDC; no API tokens stored; signing keys in GitHub Actions environment for release signing

data.entities: DiameterMessage dataclass (command_code CommandCode ENUM, application_id INT, hop_by_hop_id INT, end_to_end_id INT, flags: DiameterFlags dataclass, avps: List[Avp]). Avp dataclass (avp_code INT, vendor_id Optional[INT], flags: AvpFlags, value: bytes | List[Avp] for grouped). AvpDefinition (avp_code, name, data_type ENUM Unsigned32/Integer32/OctetString/UTF8String/Grouped/Time/DiameterIdentity/DiameterURI/Enumerated, mandatory, vendor_specific). SecurityEvent dataclass (event_type, offending_offset, raw_bytes_excerpt, message_context).
