previous_plan.title: Diameter SDK Security Hardening -- Round 3
previous_plan.executive_summary: Security-hardened Python Diameter SDK with RFC 6733 encoding/decoding, AVP length bounds checking, grouped AVP recursion budget, and pluggable security event handler.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Zero-dependency Python package with AvpDecoder recursion budget, strict length validation, and SecurityEventHandler interface. AVP dictionary from bundled XML. CCR/CCA application support.
previous_plan.data_model: DiameterMessage, Avp, AvpDefinition dataclasses. Stateless library.
previous_plan.deployment_and_operations: PyPI distribution. GitHub Actions CI with Atheris fuzzing. Trusted Publishing.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 4 closes the two remaining interface-completeness gaps for this library SDK. The SecurityEventHandler ABC with typed event dataclasses is a design decision that affects every consuming application's implementation -- it must be complete and stable. The session-id validation scope clarification is important for users to know what security they get from the SDK vs what they must implement themselves. Both are API design decisions, not architectural changes.

fix_report.action_taken: Defined complete SecurityEventHandler ABC in diameter.security module: abstract base class with three abstract methods: on_malformed_message(event: MalformedMessageEvent) -> None, on_length_overflow(event: LengthOverflowEvent) -> None, on_anomaly(event: DiameterSecurityEvent) -> None for catch-all. Each event type is a frozen dataclass: MalformedMessageEvent(event_type: SecurityEventType, offending_offset: int, raw_bytes_excerpt: bytes, message_context: dict[str, Any], error_code: DiameterDecodeErrorCode). LengthOverflowEvent(event_type, claimed_length: int, available_bytes: int, avp_code: int, offending_offset: int, raw_bytes_excerpt: bytes). SessionIdFormatEvent(event_type, session_id_value: str, format_violation: str). NullSecurityEventHandler provided as default no-op implementation. DefaultLoggingSecurityEventHandler provided as convenience implementation logging to Python logging module.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Consuming applications have a complete, stable interface to implement for SIEM integration; null handler is the default for backward compatibility; logging handler covers 80% of use cases without custom implementation

fix_report.action_taken: Specified session-id validation scope in SDK: SDK performs RFC 6733 section 8.8 format validation -- session-id MUST match pattern: NAS-Identifier ';' High32bits ';' Low32bits [';' optional-value] where High32bits is current Unix timestamp at session creation and Low32bits is a monotonic counter. SDK validates this format regex on every decoded Session-Id AVP; format violations emit SESSION_ID_FORMAT_INVALID event via security_handler and raise SessionIdFormatError. Cross-session reuse detection (replay attack detection) is explicitly out of scope for the stateless SDK; documented in security_boundaries.md in the repository with recommended stateful detection patterns consuming applications should implement (e.g., Redis set of seen session-ids with TTL).
fix_report.expected_outcome: Consuming applications know exactly what session-id security is provided by the SDK vs what they must implement; security boundary is explicitly documented

target_output.title: Diameter Protocol Python SDK -- Security Event Handler Interface and Session-Id Validation Specification
target_output.executive_summary: Zero-dependency Python 3.12 SDK for RFC 6733 DIAMETER protocol encoding, decoding, and validation with security hardening against malformed message attacks. Published to PyPI with zero external runtime dependencies, supporting Credit-Control-Request and Credit-Control-Answer messages with typed AVP construction, strict bounds-checked AVP length validation, grouped AVP recursion budget (preventing infinite recursion on circular grouping), and a fully-specified SecurityEventHandler ABC with typed event dataclasses. Session-id validation enforces RFC 6733 section 8.8 format compliance with explicit documentation of stateless scope boundaries. Hardening regression test suite covers 100+ CVE corpus patterns with Atheris coverage-guided fuzzing on every CI run.
target_output.architecture_overview: The SDK is structured as a pure Python package (diameter) with no external runtime dependencies. The diameter.avp.AvpDecoder implements a recursive descent parser with a remaining_bytes budget parameter that decrements at each grouped AVP nesting level, raising GroupedAvpRecursionExceeded if budget reaches zero. AVP length validation checks claimed_length + 8 <= remaining_buffer_bytes before struct.unpack to prevent out-of-bounds reads -- not an integer overflow (Python's arbitrary precision prevents struct overflow) but an application-level bounds error. Every validation failure calls the injected SecurityEventHandler before raising a typed exception. The diameter.security module defines the SecurityEventHandler ABC with three abstract methods and three concrete event dataclasses. Session-Id AVPs are validated against the RFC 6733 section 8.8 format regex on decode; cross-session reuse detection is explicitly delegated to consuming applications with documented patterns. The diameter.apps.credit_control module provides typed CreditControlRequest and CreditControlAnswer builders.
target_output.technology_stack: Python 3.12 with dataclasses, typing, struct, re, abc, and enum from stdlib only -- zero external runtime dependencies. Optional: cffi C extension for performance-critical parsing (activates automatically if cffi available). Dev/Test: pytest 8.x, pytest-cov, mypy strict, Atheris 2.x for coverage-guided fuzzing, Bandit for security linting. Distribution: PyPI Trusted Publishing via GitHub Actions OIDC, hatch build backend, semantic versioning.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Message Encoding: DiameterMessage.to_bytes() produces RFC 6733 wire format bytes with correct header flags and AVP serialization. Message Decoding: DiameterDecoder.decode(raw: bytes, security_handler: SecurityEventHandler = NullSecurityEventHandler()) -> DiameterMessage with full AVP tree. AVP Validation: length bounds checking, mandatory flag enforcement, grouped AVP recursion budget (default depth=10), unknown AVP handling (emit UnknownAvpEvent, include as raw bytes). Session-Id Validation: RFC 6733 format regex on every decoded Session-Id AVP; SessionIdFormatError on violation. Security Event Handler: SecurityEventHandler ABC with MalformedMessageEvent, LengthOverflowEvent, SessionIdFormatEvent; NullSecurityEventHandler (default), DefaultLoggingSecurityEventHandler (convenience). CVE Corpus: 100+ malformed message fixtures from CVEs and GSMA FS.11; all must be rejected without crash.
target_output.system_components: diameter.core (DiameterMessage, DiameterHeader, DiameterFlags, CommandCode ENUM), diameter.avp (Avp, AvpFlags, AvpEncoder, AvpDecoder with recursion budget, GroupedAvpDecoder), diameter.validator (AvpLengthValidator, MandatoryFlagValidator, SessionIdValidator), diameter.security (SecurityEventHandler ABC, NullSecurityEventHandler, DefaultLoggingSecurityEventHandler, MalformedMessageEvent, LengthOverflowEvent, SessionIdFormatEvent, SecurityEventType ENUM, DiameterDecodeErrorCode ENUM), diameter.apps.credit_control (CreditControlRequest builder, CreditControlAnswer decoder, ResultCode ENUM), diameter.dictionary (AvpDictionary, diameter_base.xml bundled AVP definitions).
target_output.workflows: Encoding Workflow: Developer constructs CreditControlRequest using fluent builder, setting typed fields (session_id: str, origin_host: DiameterIdentity, requested_units: int). Builder validates mandatory AVPs present and calls AvpEncoder for each. DiameterMessage.to_bytes() serializes header + AVP list with correct 4-byte padding. Decoding Workflow with Security Handler: Application implements SecurityEventHandler (or uses DefaultLoggingSecurityEventHandler). DiameterDecoder(security_handler=handler).decode(raw_bytes). Parser reads 20-byte header, validates version=1. For each AVP: reads 8-byte header, extracts avp_code, length. Validates length <= remaining_buffer_bytes; on violation: security_handler.on_length_overflow(LengthOverflowEvent(...)); raises DiameterDecodeError(INVALID_AVP_LENGTH). For Grouped AVPs: recursive call to GroupedAvpDecoder with remaining_bytes=parent_avp_value_length and recursion_depth-1; raises if depth=0. Session-Id AVP decoded: SessionIdValidator.validate(value) checks RFC 6733 regex; on violation: security_handler.on_anomaly(SessionIdFormatEvent(...)); raises SessionIdFormatError. CVE Regression Test: pytest parametrize over 100+ binary fixtures in tests/cve_corpus/; each must raise DiameterDecodeError without crash.
target_output.data_model: Python dataclasses (all frozen=True for immutability): DiameterMessage(version: int, length: int, flags: DiameterFlags, command_code: CommandCode, application_id: int, hop_by_hop_id: int, end_to_end_id: int, avps: tuple[Avp, ...]). Avp(avp_code: int, flags: AvpFlags, vendor_id: int | None, value: bytes | tuple[Avp, ...]). SecurityEventHandler ABC: @abstractmethod on_malformed_message(event: MalformedMessageEvent) -> None; on_length_overflow(event: LengthOverflowEvent) -> None; on_anomaly(event: DiameterSecurityEvent) -> None. MalformedMessageEvent(event_type: SecurityEventType, offending_offset: int, raw_bytes_excerpt: bytes, error_code: DiameterDecodeErrorCode, message_context: dict[str, Any]). LengthOverflowEvent(event_type: SecurityEventType, claimed_length: int, available_bytes: int, avp_code: int, offending_offset: int, raw_bytes_excerpt: bytes). SessionIdFormatEvent(event_type: SecurityEventType, session_id_value: str, format_violation: str, offending_offset: int).
target_output.api_design: Public API surface (all in __init__.py): from diameter import DiameterDecoder, DiameterMessage, Avp, CreditControlRequest, CreditControlAnswer, SecurityEventHandler, NullSecurityEventHandler, DefaultLoggingSecurityEventHandler, DiameterDecodeError, DiameterDecodeErrorCode. Usage: decoder = DiameterDecoder(security_handler=DefaultLoggingSecurityEventHandler(logger=logging.getLogger('diameter'))); msg = decoder.decode(raw_bytes); ccr = CreditControlRequest.from_message(msg); print(ccr.session_id, ccr.requested_service_units). Encoding: ccr_bytes = CreditControlRequest.builder().session_id('host.example.com;1234567890;1').origin_host('ccf.example.com').destination_realm('example.com').cc_request_type(CcRequestType.INITIAL_REQUEST).requested_service_unit(RequestedServiceUnit(cc_time=3600)).build().to_bytes().
target_output.security_and_compliance: RFC 6733 compliance: all mandatory AVPs for CCR/CCA validated against RFC 6733 and 3GPP TS 32.299 AVP definitions. AVP length bounds checking prevents out-of-bounds buffer reads. Grouped AVP recursion budget (default 10 levels) prevents stack overflow on deeply nested groupings. Session-Id format validation per RFC 6733 section 8.8: regex '^[^;]+;[0-9]{1,10};[0-9]{1,10}(;.*)?$'; HIGH 32-bit field range check [0, 4294967295]. Cross-session reuse detection: explicitly out of scope; documented in security_boundaries.md with recommended stateful detection using Redis seen-session-id set with TTL. GSMA FS.11 hardening: 15 specific attack patterns from FS.11 section 7 implemented as CVE corpus test cases. Bandit security linting in CI: no HIGH severity findings allowed. PyPI package signing with sigstore for supply chain verification.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI: Ubuntu-latest and macOS-latest matrix; python 3.10/3.11/3.12 matrix; pytest --cov with >95% line coverage gate; mypy --strict; Bandit -ll (HIGH severity fail); Atheris fuzz targets for AvpDecoder and GroupedAvpDecoder run 60s on PR (4 hours on release branch); PyPI Trusted Publishing on git tag v* push via OIDC token. Release process: git tag vX.Y.Z, push; Actions builds wheels for all Python versions, publishes to PyPI via Trusted Publishing, creates GitHub Release with changelog.
target_output.observability: No SDK-level telemetry (zero dependencies constraint). DefaultLoggingSecurityEventHandler routes events to Python standard logging at WARNING level for UNKNOWN_AVP events and ERROR level for length overflow and malformed message events. Consuming applications implement custom SecurityEventHandler for SIEM integration (e.g., emit to Splunk, Datadog, or OpenTelemetry). CVE corpus test run statistics reported in GitHub Actions test summary with per-fixture pass/fail breakdown.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: No infrastructure cost -- PyPI distribution. GitHub Actions free tier covers CI. Atheris fuzzing compute cost for 4-hour release runs approximately $0.20/release via GitHub Actions. PyPI storage cost negligible. SDK scales linearly with message volume in consuming applications; AvpDecoder benchmarks show 50,000 CCR messages/second throughput on a single Python 3.12 process; cffi extension improves to 200,000 messages/second.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 -- Core SDK (complete): RFC 6733 encoding/decoding, AVP length validation, CCR/CCA support, 20-pattern CVE corpus. Phase 2 -- Security Hardening (complete rounds 1-3): grouped AVP recursion budget, 100+ CVE corpus, Atheris fuzzing. Phase 3 -- Interface Completion (this round): SecurityEventHandler ABC finalized, session-id validation scope documented. Phase 4 -- Application Interfaces (future): Gx, Gy, Ro, Cx Diameter interface modules; Rust core with PyO3 bindings.
target_output.development_guidelines: All public API changes require a CHANGELOG.md entry and SemVer version bump; breaking API changes require a major version increment. New CVE patterns added to tests/cve_corpus/ must be documented with CVE identifier, affected vendor, and attack vector description in a comment at the top of the fixture file. Atheris fuzz targets must cover all public decoder entry points; new decoder functions require a new fuzz target before merge. SecurityEventHandler on_*() implementations must never raise exceptions -- the SDK catches all handler exceptions and logs them to Python warnings to prevent handler bugs from disrupting message parsing.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: New RFC 6733 or 3GPP AVP definitions not yet in bundled diameter_base.xml cause UnknownAvpEvent on every message from new Diameter applications. Mitigation: SDK accepts user-provided AVP dictionary extension at DiameterDecoder instantiation via avp_dictionary=AvpDictionary.from_xml(custom_path). Risk: cffi optional extension builds fail on non-standard Python builds (PyPy, Alpine musl). Mitigation: cffi extension failure at import time is caught silently; SDK falls back to pure Python parser with a log warning. Tradeoff: frozen dataclasses vs mutable objects -- frozen dataclasses prevent accidental mutation of decoded messages (critical for security) at the cost of higher memory allocation for modifications; correct tradeoff for an immutable parse result.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: SecurityEventHandler interface finalized: three abstract methods on typed frozen dataclass events; NullSecurityEventHandler default prevents breaking change for consumers not using security handler; DefaultLoggingSecurityEventHandler covers common logging use case. Session-id validation scope resolved: RFC 6733 section 8.8 format validation in SDK; cross-session reuse explicitly delegated to consuming application with security_boundaries.md documentation and Redis-based reference implementation provided in SDK examples directory.

metadata.notes: Round 4 Diameter SDK revision finalizing SecurityEventHandler ABC interface and session-id validation scope documentation.

profile.domain: SD-WAN VPP/StrongSwan Branch Appliance Replacement Infrastructure Project

project_goal.value: Replace commercial WAN optimization appliances at 200 branch sites with open-source SD-WAN overlay built on VPP (Vector Packet Processing) and StrongSwan, projecting 60% reduction in recurring WAN appliance licensing and maintenance costs
project_goal.rationale: Commercial WAN appliance licenses represent $1.2M/year recurring cost; VPP/StrongSwan open-source stack with equivalent functionality eliminates licensing while improving packet forwarding performance via DPDK

target_users.value: Network operations team managing SD-WAN overlay configuration and monitoring; infrastructure engineers executing migration batches; finance stakeholders reviewing cost savings dashboard
target_users.rationale: Three internal consumer personas: ops team for day-to-day, engineers for migration execution, finance for savings tracking

project_class.rationale: Network infrastructure platform project; Ansible-based automation, Kubernetes control plane, no customer-facing product

capabilities.rationale: Ansible automation for CPE provisioning, Python FastAPI backend for orchestration, GitLab CI for deployment

complexity_level.rationale: 200 branch sites, VPP DPDK configuration, StrongSwan IKEv2 key management at scale, parallel-run validation before legacy decommission

risk_level.rationale: Branch site connectivity loss during migration directly impacts business operations at that site; 200 sites amplifies blast radius

data_sensitivity.rationale: WAN tunnel configurations and IPsec policies are internal operational data; no subscriber PII

external_exposure.rationale: SD-WAN control plane and management interfaces accessible only within internal corporate network; branch IPsec tunnels are encrypted WAN links

access_model.value: Keycloak RBAC with roles: sdwan-operator (read topology, view metrics), sdwan-engineer (Ansible playbook execution, configuration), sdwan-admin (full access including IPsec key rotation)
access_model.rationale: Separation of read access from configuration execution; IPsec key management restricted to admin role

feature_scope.value: Ansible playbook library for VPP and StrongSwan CPE provisioning, IPsec IKEv2 tunnel establishment per branch, traffic steering policy configuration, parallel-run validation with legacy appliance active, latency and packet loss metric comparison, batch migration management with per-site rollback, cost savings tracking dashboard, legacy appliance decommission runbook automation
feature_scope.rationale: Full migration lifecycle from pilot deployment through validation to legacy decommission

mvp_scope.value: Ansible VPP and StrongSwan provisioning for 5 pilot sites, IPsec IKEv2 tunnel establishment, parallel-run metrics comparison, manual rollback procedure
mvp_scope.rationale: Pilot 5 sites validates the automation stack before scaling to 200; parallel-run validation is the safety gate

security_baseline.value: IPsec IKEv2 with AES-256-GCM and PFS (DH group 20 NIST P-384) for all WAN tunnels; StrongSwan certificate-based authentication using internal PKI; Keycloak RBAC for orchestration access; no PSK-based IPsec allowed
security_baseline.rationale: Internal security policy mandates IKEv2 AES-256-GCM PFS; certificate auth eliminates PSK sharing risk across 200 sites

frontend_stack.value: none - infrastructure project; operations via Ansible and Grafana dashboards; no web UI
frontend_stack.rationale: infrastructure_project; no user-facing frontend required

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with Ansible for CPE provisioning; FastAPI for orchestration REST API enabling programmatic migration batch management
backend_stack.rationale: Ansible for idempotent CPE configuration; FastAPI for batch management API consumed by CI/CD and ops tooling

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for branch site inventory, migration batch state, IPsec policy records, and cost savings records; Prometheus + Grafana for WAN performance metrics
data_platform.rationale: PostgreSQL for structured migration state tracking with transaction guarantees; Prometheus for time-series WAN metrics

hosting_target.value: Bare-metal Kubernetes on hub site data center for control plane; CPE devices at branch sites run VPP and StrongSwan on commodity x86 hardware with Intel DPDK-capable NICs
hosting_target.rationale: Self-hosted infrastructure; CPE hardware commodity replacement for commercial appliances

privacy_retention_policy.value: Migration records and cost savings data retained 5 years for finance audit; IPsec key rotation logs retained 2 years; Prometheus WAN metrics retained 1 year
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Finance audit trail for cost savings claims; IPsec audit for security compliance

future_scope.value: BGP-based dynamic routing over SD-WAN tunnels replacing static routing; automated IPsec certificate renewal via ACME with internal CA; SD-WAN QoS policy management for application-aware traffic steering
future_scope.rationale: BGP and dynamic routing are Phase 3; current static tunnel routing sufficient for branch connectivity replacement

constraints.value: Migration must not cause branch site downtime exceeding 30 minutes per site; parallel-run must demonstrate <5% latency increase vs legacy appliance before legacy decommission approval; IPsec IKEv2 with AES-256-GCM PFS mandatory for all tunnels
constraints.rationale: 30-minute downtime window during migration maintenance; parity threshold protects against performance regression

observability_baseline.value: Prometheus Node Exporter on CPE devices for system metrics; custom VPP Prometheus exporter for packet forwarding metrics (pps, bps, drops); StrongSwan metrics for tunnel uptime and rekey events; Grafana dashboards for fleet-wide tunnel health
observability_baseline.rationale: Fleet-wide tunnel monitoring critical for 200-site operational visibility

execution_preference.value: GitLab CI for Ansible playbook validation and deployment pipelines; ArgoCD for Kubernetes control plane GitOps; migration batches executed via GitLab CI manual trigger with rollback job
execution_preference.rationale: GitLab CI integrates with existing infrastructure team workflow; manual trigger for migration batch provides human approval gate

compliance_context.value: NIST SP 800-77 Rev 1 IPsec VPN compliance required for all branch WAN tunnels using IKEv2 with AES-256-GCM and Perfect Forward Secrecy (DH group 20 / NIST P-384); internal PKI certificate lifecycle governed by internal Certificate Policy requiring annual CA audits and 90-day leaf certificate rotation; network change management requires CAB (Change Advisory Board) approval for each batch of branch site migrations; 30-minute maximum downtime per site documented as SLA commitment in migration runbooks; IPsec key rotation logs retained 2 years and migration records retained 5 years per finance audit requirements; Keycloak RBAC access reviews conducted quarterly to enforce least-privilege on orchestration API; no pre-shared key (PSK) IPsec tunnels permitted per corporate security policy — certificate-based authentication only.
compliance_context.rationale: High risk level for a 200-site WAN infrastructure replacement using IPsec mandates NIST 800-77 compliance, formal PKI governance, CAB change control, and audit log retention to satisfy corporate security policy and finance audit obligations.

project.goal: Replace 200 commercial WAN appliances with VPP/StrongSwan SD-WAN overlay, 60% cost reduction
project.target_users: Network ops, infrastructure engineers, finance stakeholders
project.timeline: 5-site pilot Q2; 200-site full migration Q4

data.db: PostgreSQL 16 for migration state and cost records

devops.cd: ArgoCD GitOps for Kubernetes control plane

constraints.parity: <5% latency increase vs legacy in parallel-run

open_questions.batch_size: Determine optimal batch size balancing risk and migration velocity for 200-site rollout

reasoner_reviews.product: The parallel-run validation gate with <5% latency threshold is the correct risk control for a 200-site migration. The 30-minute downtime window per site must include the full cutover sequence: VPP/StrongSwan activation, traffic steering switchover, validation ping, and rollback decision point. The cost savings tracking must capture appliance retirement dates to accurately attribute monthly savings.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: VPP with DPDK requires Intel DPDK-capable NICs at branch CPE devices. The Ansible provisioning playbook must validate DPDK NIC compatibility before VPP installation -- incompatible hardware would cause silent packet forwarding failures. Consider a pre-flight check playbook that runs before any provisioning. StrongSwan certificate provisioning at scale requires a certificate management workflow -- recommend certbot or strongswan-pki via Ansible with the internal CA.
reasoner_reviews.security: IKEv2 with AES-256-GCM and DH group 20 (NIST P-384) for PFS is the correct security policy. Certificate-based StrongSwan authentication requires that the internal PKI CRL or OCSP is reachable from all branch sites -- if the CRL endpoint is only on the hub network, certificate revocation checking will work. Define the certificate validity period and renewal automation before pilot deployment.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 30-minute downtime window is tight for a full VPP/DPDK initialization sequence. VPP with DPDK can take 2-5 minutes to initialize depending on NIC and driver loading. Factor VPP startup time into the maintenance window plan and test on pilot hardware.

backend.service_design: Orchestration components: sdwan-api (FastAPI, migration batch management endpoints, site status API), ansible-runner (Ansible Runner 2.x executing playbooks as Kubernetes Jobs triggered by GitLab CI or sdwan-api), vpp-config-generator (Python module generating VPP startup.conf and FIB configuration from site profile), strongswan-cert-manager (Python module invoking internal CA for site certificate issuance via EST protocol)
backend.api_patterns: REST: POST /api/v1/batches (create migration batch with site_ids[]), GET /api/v1/batches/{id}/status (per-site migration state), POST /api/v1/batches/{id}/execute (trigger Ansible playbook run), POST /api/v1/sites/{id}/rollback (reactivate legacy appliance), GET /api/v1/cost-savings (monthly savings by batch)
backend.failure_handling: Ansible playbook idempotent with --check mode pre-flight; SSH connection failures mark site as unreachable and abort batch; post-migration connectivity health check (ping + iperf2 latency) within 30-minute window; automatic rollback trigger if health check fails

security.auth_design: Keycloak OIDC for sdwan-api; Ansible Runner uses service account JWT; StrongSwan CPE certificates issued by internal SubCA with 1-year validity; CRL distribution point on hub network HTTPS endpoint
security.authorization_model: Keycloak RBAC in sdwan-api: sdwan-operator GET endpoints only; sdwan-engineer POST /batches and /execute; sdwan-admin POST /rollback and certificate rotation endpoints
security.secrets_management: StrongSwan private keys generated on CPE during provisioning (never transmitted); certificates issued via EST (Enrollment over Secure Transport) from internal CA Ansible role; Keycloak client secret in Kubernetes Secrets

data.entities: branch_sites (site_id UUID PK, site_name, address, cpe_hostname, cpe_ip, legacy_appliance_model, legacy_appliance_vendor, migration_status ENUM pending/piloting/migrated/decommissioned, migrated_at), wan_tunnels (tunnel_id UUID PK, site_id FK, hub_endpoint, tunnel_ip_range, ipsec_policy_id, established_at, status ENUM active/degraded/down), ipsec_policies (policy_id UUID PK, ike_version, encryption_algo, integrity_algo, dh_group, pfs_enabled, cert_expiry), migration_batches (batch_id UUID PK, batch_name, site_ids UUID[], planned_date, executed_at, status, latency_parity_pct), cost_savings_records (record_id, site_id FK, appliance_retired_at, monthly_license_saving_usd, monthly_maintenance_saving_usd)
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 on bare-metal Kubernetes StatefulSet; 5-year backup retention for migration audit records; Prometheus TSDB for 1-year WAN metrics retention
data.migration_notes: branch_sites table pre-populated from existing network inventory system via CSV export; 200 sites loaded before pilot starts

devops.deployment_topology: Bare-metal Kubernetes 3-node cluster at hub site: sdwan-api Deployment (2 replicas), ansible-runner Kubernetes Jobs (on-demand per batch), PostgreSQL StatefulSet (single node with backup), Prometheus StatefulSet, Grafana Deployment. CPE at each branch: x86 mini-PC with Intel i350-T2 NIC (DPDK-capable), VPP 24.02, StrongSwan 5.9, Ubuntu 22.04
devops.ci_cd_design: GitLab CI: Ansible playbook lint (ansible-lint), dry-run against test CPE VM in CI, manual trigger for production batch execution with required sdwan-admin approval. ArgoCD syncs Kubernetes manifests from Git. Migration batch GitLab CI job: ansible-runner execute, post-migration health check, rollback job triggered on health check failure
devops.rollback_strategy: Rollback Ansible playbook: reactivate legacy appliance VLAN, disable VPP forwarding, restore original default route to legacy appliance, verify connectivity via ping; complete within 10 minutes of rollback trigger

target_output.thinking_summary: First-pass strong quality plan for a 200-site SD-WAN VPP/StrongSwan migration. The key architectural decisions are the parallel-run validation gate with quantified latency parity threshold, StrongSwan certificate-based auth via EST protocol with internal CA, VPP DPDK pre-flight hardware validation, and PostgreSQL migration state management for rollback safety. The cost savings tracking model must capture appliance retirement dates accurately. Every section is domain-specific to WAN optimization, VPP, and IPsec.
target_output.title: SD-WAN VPP/StrongSwan Open-Source Branch Appliance Replacement -- 200-Site Migration
target_output.executive_summary: Infrastructure project to decommission 200 commercial WAN optimization appliances and replace them with an open-source SD-WAN overlay using VPP (Vector Packet Processing) with Intel DPDK for packet forwarding and StrongSwan IKEv2 with AES-256-GCM PFS for encrypted branch tunnels, projecting $720,000/year in license and maintenance savings. Ansible playbooks provision CPE devices at each branch site, establish IPsec tunnels to the hub, and configure VPP traffic steering policies. A parallel-run validation phase compares iperf2 latency and packet loss against the active legacy appliance before any decommission approval. Migration batches are managed via a GitLab CI pipeline with manual approval gates, FastAPI orchestration API, and automatic rollback on post-migration health check failure.
target_output.architecture_overview: The SD-WAN control plane runs on a 3-node bare-metal Kubernetes cluster at the hub data center. Ansible playbooks executed as Kubernetes Jobs provision each branch CPE: a pre-flight check validates DPDK NIC compatibility (Intel i350-T2 or equivalent), installs VPP 24.02 with DPDK, configures VPP startup.conf with NIC binding and FIB static routes to the hub, installs StrongSwan 5.9, issues a site certificate from the internal SubCA via EST protocol, and configures the IKEv2 connection profile with AES-256-GCM and DH group 20 PFS. During parallel-run, both the legacy appliance and new VPP tunnel are active; iperf2 measurements compare round-trip latency and packet loss every 5 minutes for a 48-hour window. The FastAPI sdwan-api tracks per-site migration_status and batch execution state in PostgreSQL. On parallel-run approval (<5% latency delta), the legacy appliance VLAN is disabled and VPP becomes the sole forwarding path. Cost savings records log appliance retirement dates for monthly finance reporting.
target_output.technology_stack: CPE Software: VPP 24.02 with Intel DPDK 23.11 for packet forwarding on x86 CPE hardware (Intel i350-T2 NIC), StrongSwan 5.9 for IKEv2 IPsec, Ubuntu 22.04 LTS base. Automation: Python 3.12, Ansible 9.x with Ansible Runner 2.4 for playbook execution. Orchestration API: FastAPI 0.110 on Kubernetes. Database: PostgreSQL 16 StatefulSet. Observability: Prometheus 2.50, Grafana 10.x, VPP Prometheus exporter (vpp-exporter). CI/CD: GitLab CI 16.x, ArgoCD for Kubernetes GitOps. PKI: internal CA with EST protocol for certificate enrollment via Ansible estscep role.
target_output.functional_feature_map: CPE Provisioning: DPDK NIC pre-flight check (vfio-pci driver binding validation), VPP installation and startup.conf generation (NIC worker threads, heap size, FIB routes), StrongSwan IKEv2 config generation (AES-256-GCM, DH group 20, certificate auth), site certificate issuance via EST. Parallel-Run Validation: dual-path iperf2 latency comparison (new VPP tunnel vs legacy appliance), 48-hour measurement window, automated pass/fail evaluation at <5% latency delta. Migration Batch Management: batch creation with site list, GitLab CI manual trigger, per-site status tracking, automatic rollback on health check failure. Cost Savings Tracking: appliance retirement recording, monthly license and maintenance savings aggregation, Grafana savings dashboard. Legacy Decommission: VLAN disable playbook, appliance power-off confirmation, decommission_records update.
target_output.system_components: Kubernetes control plane (hub): sdwan-api Deployment (FastAPI 2 replicas), ansible-runner Jobs (on-demand), PostgreSQL 16 StatefulSet, Prometheus StatefulSet, Grafana Deployment. CPE at branch sites: Intel i350-T2 NIC x86 mini-PC, VPP 24.02 with DPDK, StrongSwan 5.9, vpp-exporter (Prometheus), Ubuntu 22.04. Network: IPsec IKEv2 tunnels from each CPE to hub StrongSwan gateway; hub VPP router aggregating all 200 branch tunnels.
target_output.workflows: Branch Migration Workflow: Operations engineer creates migration batch via sdwan-api POST /api/v1/batches with site_ids[] (recommended batch size: 10 sites). GitLab CI pipeline triggered manually by sdwan-admin. For each site in batch: Ansible pre-flight check (DPDK NIC compatibility, available disk space, connectivity to hub). If pre-flight passes: provision VPP and StrongSwan, issue certificate via EST, establish IPsec tunnel. Start parallel-run: both VPP tunnel and legacy appliance VLAN active. iperf2 measurements every 5 minutes for 48 hours against hub measurement endpoint. At 48 hours: evaluate latency delta; if <5%: auto-mark site as parallel_run_passed, engineer reviews and approves cutover. Cutover: disable legacy appliance VLAN, update default route to VPP tunnel, verify connectivity (ping + iperf2 30s). If connectivity test passes: migration_status=migrated, cost_savings_records updated. If connectivity test fails within 30-minute window: trigger rollback playbook. Rollback: re-enable legacy VLAN, disable VPP forwarding, restore legacy default route, ping verification; complete in <10 minutes. IPsec Certificate Renewal: StrongSwan on CPE sends EST renewal request 30 days before expiry to internal CA EST endpoint; Ansible renewal playbook handles failures; Keycloak admin alerted on renewal failure.
target_output.data_model: branch_sites (site_id UUID PK, site_name VARCHAR, cpe_hostname VARCHAR, cpe_mgmt_ip INET, legacy_appliance_model VARCHAR, migration_status ENUM('pending','pre_flight_failed','provisioning','parallel_run','parallel_run_passed','migrated','rollback_in_progress','decommissioned'), migrated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). wan_tunnels (tunnel_id UUID PK, site_id FK, hub_ipsec_gateway INET, tunnel_local_ip INET, tunnel_remote_ip INET, ike_established_at TIMESTAMPTZ, last_rekey_at TIMESTAMPTZ, cert_expiry DATE, status ENUM('active','degraded','down')). migration_batches (batch_id UUID PK, batch_name, site_ids UUID[], created_by, executed_at, completion_pct INT, status ENUM('pending','running','complete','partial_failure')). parallel_run_metrics (metric_id UUID PK, site_id FK, measured_at TIMESTAMPTZ, legacy_rtt_ms FLOAT4, vpp_rtt_ms FLOAT4, legacy_packet_loss_pct FLOAT4, vpp_packet_loss_pct FLOAT4). cost_savings_records (record_id UUID PK, site_id FK, appliance_retired_at DATE, monthly_license_saving_usd NUMERIC(10,2), monthly_maintenance_saving_usd NUMERIC(10,2)).
target_output.api_design: POST /api/v1/batches {batch_name, site_ids[], planned_date} -> {batch_id}. GET /api/v1/batches/{id}/status -> {batch_id, status, sites: [{site_id, migration_status, parallel_run_latency_delta_pct}]}. POST /api/v1/batches/{id}/execute -> triggers GitLab CI pipeline via GitLab API. POST /api/v1/sites/{id}/approve-cutover -> moves from parallel_run_passed to cutover execution. POST /api/v1/sites/{id}/rollback -> triggers rollback Ansible playbook. GET /api/v1/cost-savings?from={date}&to={date} -> {total_monthly_saving_usd, sites_migrated, sites_remaining, savings_by_batch[]}. GET /api/v1/sites/{id}/parallel-run-metrics -> time series of parallel_run_metrics for site.
target_output.security_and_compliance: StrongSwan IKEv2: connection profile ike=aes256gcm128-sha384-ecp384! esp=aes256gcm128-sha384-ecp384! (AES-256-GCM, SHA-384, DH group 20 NIST P-384, PFS enabled); no PSK fallback. Certificate auth: CPE certificates issued by internal SubCA (2048-bit RSA CA, 4096-bit leaf); 1-year validity; CRL published at https://pki.internal/crl/sdwan.crl accessible from all branch sites via hub tunnel. EST enrollment via Ansible estscep role using Bootstrap PSK for initial enrollment only; renewed certificates issued via EST with existing certificate auth. Keycloak RBAC on sdwan-api: Keycloak RS256 JWT validated; sdwan-engineer required for POST /batches; sdwan-admin required for POST /rollback and certificate rotation. Ansible inventory encrypted at rest via Ansible Vault; site SSH keys in GitLab CI protected variables. Internal security policy compliance: IKEv2 AES-256-GCM PFS is the only permitted tunnel configuration; Ansible playbook enforces this via pre-task assertion before StrongSwan configuration.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Hub Kubernetes cluster (3 bare-metal nodes): sdwan-api Deployment with 2 replicas, HPA on CPU; PostgreSQL StatefulSet with daily backup to NFS; Prometheus StatefulSet with 1-year TSDB retention; ArgoCD manages all manifests from Git. CPE hardware: Intel NUC 13 Pro or equivalent with Intel i350-T2 dual-port NIC; Ubuntu 22.04 installed via Ansible bootstrap; VPP installed from VPP stable PPA; DPDK interfaces bound to vfio-pci driver via dpdk_devbind Ansible role. GitLab CI pipeline: ansible-lint, dry-run against virtualbox CPE VM, manual production trigger requiring sdwan-admin approval, post-migration health check, rollback job on failure. Batch size recommendation: 10 sites/batch based on 48-hour parallel-run window allowing 20 batches over 40 working days for full 200-site migration.
target_output.observability: Prometheus scrapes: VPP vpp-exporter (interface pps/bps/drops, FIB lookup rate), StrongSwan metrics (tunnel uptime, IKE renegotiation count, SA establishment time), Node Exporter (CPE CPU, memory, NIC queue depth). Grafana dashboards: fleet-wide tunnel health map (green/degraded/down per site), parallel-run latency comparison per site, migration progress (sites migrated/remaining), cost savings trend. Alerts: tunnel down >5 minutes (P1), cert expiry <30 days (P2), parallel-run latency delta >5% (blocks cutover approval), iperf2 packet loss >1% on migrated site (P2).
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Commercial appliance replacement: $1.2M/year current licensing and maintenance. CPE hardware: $800/site x 200 = $160,000 one-time. VPP/StrongSwan: open-source, zero license cost. Hub Kubernetes control plane: 3 bare-metal servers from existing inventory. Net savings year 1: $1,200,000 - $160,000 = $1,040,000. Year 2+: $1,200,000/year. 60% savings figure validated: $720,000/year when comparing to list price including vendor support contracts. Scale beyond 200 sites: hub VPP router supports 1,000 IPsec tunnels on 10G NIC; 200-site fleet is 20% of capacity.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 -- Pilot (8 weeks): 5 pilot sites, VPP/StrongSwan provisioning automation, EST certificate enrollment, parallel-run validation framework, rollback playbook. Phase 2 -- Batch Rollout (16 weeks): 10 sites/batch, 20 batches, 48-hour parallel-run per batch, progressive migration. Finance savings dashboard tracking appliance retirement dates. Phase 3 -- Legacy Decommission (4 weeks): Full 200-site decommission, appliance return to vendor or repurpose, final cost savings report to finance steering committee. Phase 4 -- Optimization (future): BGP dynamic routing over tunnels, automated certificate renewal via ACME, QoS traffic steering for application-aware policies.
target_output.development_guidelines: Ansible playbooks must be fully idempotent -- re-running any playbook on a site in any migration_status must not cause harm. Pre-flight check must validate DPDK NIC compatibility before any VPP installation attempt; abort with detailed error log if NIC not supported. StrongSwan IKEv2 policy template must be defined as a Jinja2 template with only site-specific variables (CPE IP, hub IP, certificate path) substituted; policy parameters (cipher, DH group, PFS) are constants not variables to prevent accidental security weakening. Post-migration health check must run iperf2 UDP test (not just ICMP ping) to validate actual throughput, not just connectivity.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: DPDK vfio-pci binding conflicts with existing kernel NIC driver at branch CPE on some hardware models. Mitigation: pre-flight Ansible task checks lspci output for supported NIC models against a tested compatibility list; abort provisioning if unsupported NIC detected. Risk: StrongSwan IKE renegotiation causes 1-3 second tunnel blackout if not handled gracefully by VPP FIB. Mitigation: configure StrongSwan make-before-break IKE renegotiation (IKEv2 CREATE_CHILD_SA before deleting old SA); test renegotiation behavior in pilot. Risk: 48-hour parallel-run window per batch limits migration velocity to 200 sites in 40 working days minimum. Tradeoff: Acceptable given the risk level; cutover risk reduction outweighs schedule pressure. Tradeoff: VPP vs Linux kernel networking -- VPP DPDK provides 10x higher packet forwarding throughput than kernel stack but requires Intel DPDK NIC and increases CPE hardware specificity; correct for a WAN optimization replacement where forwarding performance is the primary value.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Batch size resolved: 10 sites/batch balances risk (limited blast radius per batch) and velocity (20 batches x 48-hour parallel-run = 40 working days for full rollout); batch size configurable in sdwan-api. IPsec certificate validity resolved: 1-year certificates with automated EST renewal triggered 30 days before expiry; 90-day grace period before Ansible alert triggers manual intervention. EST bootstrap PSK approach: single bootstrap PSK per batch, revoked after all sites in batch complete initial enrollment.

metadata.notes: First-pass SD-WAN VPP/StrongSwan 200-site migration plan with parallel-run validation gate, EST certificate provisioning, and cost savings tracking.

profile.domain: A desktop application that helps NOC engineers migrate legacy SNMP-based monitoring configurations to a modern gNMI/OpenConfig telemetry stack, providing a graphical interface to map OID-to-path translations, validate YANG model coverage, and export Telegraf or gNMIc collector configs.

project_goal.value: Desktop application for NOC engineers to map SNMP OIDs to OpenConfig gNMI paths, validate YANG model coverage, and export gNMIc and Telegraf collector configurations
project_goal.rationale: NOC team is migrating 200+ device types from SNMP to gNMI telemetry; manual OID mapping is error-prone without tooling

target_users.value: NOC engineers and network reliability engineers performing gNMI telemetry migration
target_users.rationale: Primary users identified as the 8-person NOC team and 3 NREs

project_class.rationale: Packaged Electron desktop app; no server-side hosting required

capabilities.rationale: Electron frontend, embedded Python backend for YANG parsing, SQLite for migration project state

complexity_level.rationale: YANG model parsing, OID-to-OpenConfig mapping database, config template rendering

risk_level.rationale: Internal tooling with no network exposure; processes only network configuration metadata

data_sensitivity.rationale: SNMP OID lists and device profiles are internal network configuration data

external_exposure.rationale: Desktop app used on NOC workstations; no network endpoints exposed

access_model.value: No authentication required; single-user desktop application on NOC workstations
access_model.rationale: Low-risk internal tool; no multi-user access model needed

feature_scope.value: MIB file import and OID resolution, OID-to-OpenConfig path mapping with bundled mapping database, unmapped OID manual assignment, YANG model coverage validation, gNMIc subscription config export, Telegraf inputs.gnmi config export, Grafana dashboard template export, migration project management
feature_scope.rationale: Full migration workflow from OID discovery to collector config generation

mvp_scope.value: MIB import, OID-to-OpenConfig lookup against bundled database, manual OID mapping for gaps, gNMIc config export, migration project save/load
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum to handle first 10 device type migrations by NOC team

security_baseline.value: No credentials stored; app reads local MIB files and config files only; no network calls
security_baseline.rationale: Local-only tool with no network endpoints reduces attack surface to zero

frontend_stack.value: Electron with React and TypeScript
frontend_stack.rationale: Electron provides native file dialogs for MIB import and config export; React for complex mapping UI

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 embedded via subprocess
backend_stack.rationale: pyang library for YANG model parsing is Python-native; invoked as subprocess from Electron main process

data_platform.value: SQLite for migration project state
data_platform.rationale: Portable project files; each migration project is a self-contained SQLite database

hosting_target.value: Local desktop installation on NOC workstations (Windows and macOS)
hosting_target.rationale: Distributed as Electron installer; no cloud hosting

privacy_retention_policy.value: Migration project files stored locally by user; no data sent to any server; no retention policy required
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Local desktop tool; data governance is user responsibility

future_scope.value: YANG module browser, live gNMI path validation against test device, Nautobot integration for device inventory import
future_scope.rationale: Post-MVP features requested by NOC team after initial deployment

constraints.value: Must bundle pyang and OID-to-OpenConfig mapping database; no internet dependency at runtime; Windows and macOS installers required
constraints.rationale: NOC workstations may be air-gapped; bundled dependencies mandatory

observability_baseline.value: Local application log to %APPDATA%/snmp-gnmi-migrator/logs; Python subprocess stderr captured and displayed in app error panel
observability_baseline.rationale: NOC engineers need visibility into pyang parsing errors for invalid MIB files

execution_preference.value: Iterative releases with NOC team testing each sprint milestone
execution_preference.rationale: Close feedback loop with primary users during development

project.goal: Desktop tool for OID-to-gNMI path mapping and collector config export
project.timeline: 3-month MVP

frontend.ui_components: MIB import panel, OID mapping table, coverage heatmap, config export wizard

backend.libraries: pyang for YANG parsing, jinja2 for config template rendering
backend.invocation: Electron main process spawns Python subprocess via child_process.spawn

devops.packaging: Electron Forge for Windows NSIS installer and macOS dmg

constraints.offline: fully offline, bundled pyang and mapping DB

open_questions.yang_source: Confirm which OpenConfig YANG modules to bundle: public openconfig-interfaces, openconfig-bgp, or full openconfig-yang repo

reasoner_reviews.product: The OID-to-OpenConfig mapping database is the core value-add of this tool. It should be bundled as a pre-built SQLite file populated from the openconfig/public-cloud-vendor-mappings repository, updated with each app release. The migration project management feature is important: NOC engineers work on 200+ device types across multiple sessions and need to save and resume work.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Python subprocess invocation for pyang is the right choice given pyang has no Node.js equivalent. The Electron main process should spawn a persistent Python subprocess on app start rather than spawning per-operation to avoid startup latency. Inter-process communication via JSON over stdin/stdout is clean and debuggable. The bundled OID-to-OpenConfig SQLite database should be treated as read-only; project-specific custom mappings stored in the project SQLite file.
reasoner_reviews.security: No authentication or encryption needed for this low-risk internal tool. Ensure the Python subprocess is spawned with the bundled Python binary from app resources, not the system Python, to avoid dependency version conflicts. MIB file paths should be sanitised before passing to pyang to prevent directory traversal in the subprocess argument.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Electron Forge with @electron-forge/maker-squirrel (Windows) and @electron-forge/maker-dmg (macOS) is the standard approach. Bundling Python requires packaging the venv inside app.asar resources. electron-builder alternative also acceptable. Air-gap requirement is satisfied by bundling all assets.

backend.service_design: Python subprocess (migrator_backend.py) started at Electron app launch as persistent child process. IPC protocol: newline-delimited JSON over stdin/stdout. Commands: parse_mib (path), lookup_oid (oid), lookup_oids_batch (oids[]), validate_yang_coverage (project_id), render_gnmic_config (project_id), render_telegraf_config (project_id). Responses: {command, status, data, error}.
backend.api_patterns: Electron main process sends JSON command line to Python stdin; Python responds with JSON result line. Timeout per command: 30s for parse_mib (large MIB files), 5s for lookups. main.ts wraps subprocess into a BackendClient class with async sendCommand(cmd, args): Promise<BackendResponse>.
backend.failure_handling: Python stderr captured to app error log. Subprocess crash triggers automatic restart with 3-second delay. pyang parse errors returned as structured error objects with line number and error type. MIB path sanitised via path.resolve() and validated to be within allowed directories before passing to subprocess.

frontend.app_structure: Electron renderer process (React SPA) with views: ProjectDashboard (list of migration projects), MigrationWorkspace (OID mapping table + coverage panel), ExportWizard (config export options), Settings.
frontend.pages_and_flows: MigrationWorkspace: NOC engineer imports MIB file via Electron dialog, app calls parse_mib, displays OID table. Each OID row shows: OID string, MIB description, resolved OpenConfig path (green if found in bundled DB, yellow if manually mapped, red if unmapped). Engineer clicks unmapped OIDs to open path assignment dialog. Coverage panel shows percent mapped per YANG module. ExportWizard: engineer selects target format (gNMIc YAML or Telegraf TOML), configures device address and credentials fields (not stored), copies export to clipboard.
frontend.state_management: React Query for IPC command calls (treated as async data fetching). Zustand store for project state (active_project_id, oid_mappings map, coverage_stats). OID table virtualised with react-window for performance with 1000+ OID lists.
frontend.performance_notes: OID lookup calls batched to Python subprocess using lookup_oids_batch command to avoid per-row IPC overhead. OID table rendered with react-window virtualization; only visible rows rendered.

security.auth_design: No authentication; local desktop tool. No credentials stored by app; export wizard collects device address and subscription paths only for config template rendering, not persisted.
security.abuse_prevention: MIB file path validated to user-selected file only; subprocess argument sanitisation prevents path traversal. Python subprocess runs with dropped privileges (no subprocess.shell=True).

data.entities: Bundled read-only: oid_openconfig_mappings (oid VARCHAR PK, openconfig_path VARCHAR, yang_module VARCHAR, yang_revision VARCHAR, notes VARCHAR). Per-project SQLite: migration_projects (id INTEGER PK, name VARCHAR, device_vendor VARCHAR, device_model VARCHAR, created_at DATETIME, updated_at DATETIME). snmp_oids (id INTEGER PK, project_id FK, oid VARCHAR, mib_name VARCHAR, mib_description VARCHAR, mapping_status ENUM unmapped/auto_mapped/manual_mapped, openconfig_path VARCHAR nullable, mapped_at DATETIME nullable). openconfig_paths (id INTEGER PK, project_id FK, yang_module VARCHAR, path VARCHAR, coverage_status ENUM covered/custom). collector_configs (id INTEGER PK, project_id FK, config_type ENUM gnmic/telegraf, rendered_at DATETIME, config_content TEXT). device_profiles (id INTEGER PK, project_id FK, vendor VARCHAR, model VARCHAR, os_version VARCHAR).
data.storage_design: Two SQLite databases: oid_openconfig_mappings.db bundled in app resources (read-only, opened via URI mode ?mode=ro). Project file {project_name}.mproj is a SQLite file stored in user-chosen directory. Each migration project is a portable self-contained file.
data.schema_notes: project SQLite uses WAL mode for concurrent React Query reads during OID import. oid_openconfig_mappings bundled DB updated with each app release via database refresh script.

devops.deployment_topology: Electron app packaged via Electron Forge: @electron-forge/maker-squirrel for Windows NSIS installer, @electron-forge/maker-dmg for macOS. Python 3.12 bundled venv included in app.asar resources. Bundled oid_openconfig_mappings.db updated from openconfig public mapping data in CI.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: npm run lint (eslint+prettier), npm run test (vitest for renderer, jest for main process), python -m pytest for backend unit tests, npm run make (electron-forge build for platform matrix). Artifacts: Windows .exe installer and macOS .dmg uploaded to GitHub Releases.
devops.rollback_strategy: GitHub Releases provides previous installer versions for manual rollback. Migration project .mproj files are portable; engineers can open projects in any app version with schema migration on open.

target_output.thinking_summary: First-pass architecture for an Electron desktop tool for SNMP-to-gNMI migration. Key decisions: persistent Python subprocess for pyang avoids per-operation startup latency; dual SQLite (bundled read-only mapping DB + per-project file) keeps the mapping database updateable independently of project data; react-window virtualisation handles 1000+ OID lists without performance degradation. Plan quality is intentionally weak per manifest - thin on risks and deployment sections.
target_output.title: SNMP-to-gNMI Migration Desktop Tool
target_output.executive_summary: An Electron desktop application for NOC engineers to migrate SNMP monitoring configurations to gNMI/OpenConfig telemetry. Engineers import MIB files, resolve OIDs against a bundled OID-to-OpenConfig mapping database, manually assign gaps to custom YANG paths, validate coverage, and export gNMIc YAML or Telegraf TOML collector configurations. Migration projects are saved as portable SQLite files. Runs fully offline on Windows and macOS NOC workstations.
target_output.architecture_overview: Electron desktop app with React TypeScript renderer communicating with an Electron main process that owns a persistent Python subprocess (migrator_backend.py). The Python process uses pyang for YANG model parsing and handles OID lookup, YANG coverage validation, and config template rendering via Jinja2. IPC uses newline-delimited JSON over stdin/stdout. Two SQLite databases: oid_openconfig_mappings.db bundled as a read-only resource, and per-project {name}.mproj SQLite files storing migration state. All assets bundled; no network calls at runtime.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Electron 31, React 18, TypeScript strict mode, React Query for IPC async state, Zustand for project state, react-window for OID table virtualisation. Backend: Python 3.12 bundled venv, pyang 2.6 for MIB/YANG parsing, Jinja2 for config template rendering. Database: SQLite via better-sqlite3 (main process) and sqlite3 (Python subprocess). Packaging: Electron Forge with Squirrel (Windows) and dmg-maker (macOS). CI: GitHub Actions.
target_output.functional_feature_map: MIB Import: Electron file dialog opens .mib or .my file; main process passes path to Python subprocess parse_mib command; pyang parses OIDs and descriptions; results written to snmp_oids table. OID Mapping Table: displays all OIDs with mapping_status colour coding (green=auto_mapped, yellow=manual_mapped, red=unmapped). Batch OID Lookup: lookup_oids_batch queries oid_openconfig_mappings.db for all OIDs in project; auto-mapped OIDs receive openconfig_path. Manual Mapping: engineer selects unmapped OID, types OpenConfig path with autocomplete from openconfig_paths table. Coverage Dashboard: percent-mapped per YANG module displayed as horizontal bar chart. Export Wizard: selects gNMIc or Telegraf format, configures subscription parameters, renders config via Python Jinja2, displays for copy.
target_output.system_components: Electron main process: BackendClient (Python subprocess manager, JSON IPC), ProjectStore (better-sqlite3 project file CRUD), MappingDB (read-only oid_openconfig_mappings.db queries). Python subprocess migrator_backend.py: pyang MibParser, OIDResolver (queries bundled mapping DB), YANGCoverageAnalyser, ConfigRenderer (Jinja2 templates for gNMIc YAML and Telegraf TOML). Electron renderer: React SPA with MigrationWorkspace, OIDMappingTable (react-window), CoveragePanel, ExportWizard. Bundled resources: oid_openconfig_mappings.db, Python 3.12 venv, Jinja2 config templates.
target_output.workflows: MIB Import and OID Mapping: Engineer opens or creates migration project (selects device vendor and model), imports MIB file via dialog, app spawns parse_mib command to Python, receives OID list, calls lookup_oids_batch against bundled mapping DB, renders OID table with colour-coded mapping status. Engineer selects unmapped OIDs and assigns OpenConfig paths manually; mapping_status updated to manual_mapped. Coverage Validation and Export: Engineer views coverage panel showing percent-mapped per YANG module (e.g., openconfig-interfaces 94%, openconfig-bgp 78%). Switches to ExportWizard, selects gNMIc output, enters device address and TLS cert path (not persisted), app calls render_gnmic_config, displays rendered YAML in copy panel. Engineer saves rendered config to file via Electron saveDialog.
target_output.data_model: Bundled DB (read-only): oid_openconfig_mappings (oid VARCHAR PK, openconfig_path VARCHAR, yang_module VARCHAR, yang_revision VARCHAR, source VARCHAR, notes VARCHAR). Per-project .mproj SQLite: migration_projects (id INTEGER PK, name VARCHAR, device_vendor VARCHAR, device_model VARCHAR, os_version VARCHAR, created_at DATETIME, updated_at DATETIME). snmp_oids (id INTEGER PK, project_id FK, oid VARCHAR, mib_name VARCHAR, object_type VARCHAR, mib_description VARCHAR, mapping_status ENUM unmapped/auto_mapped/manual_mapped, openconfig_path VARCHAR nullable, mapped_at DATETIME nullable). device_profiles (id INTEGER PK, project_id FK, vendor VARCHAR, model VARCHAR, gnmi_port INT DEFAULT 57400). collector_configs (id INTEGER PK, project_id FK, config_type ENUM gnmic/telegraf, rendered_content TEXT, rendered_at DATETIME).
target_output.api_design: Python subprocess IPC commands (JSON over stdin/stdout): parse_mib {path} -> {oids: [{oid, mib_name, description, object_type}]}. lookup_oids_batch {oids: [string]} -> {mappings: {oid: openconfig_path|null}}. validate_yang_coverage {project_id} -> {coverage: [{yang_module, mapped_count, total_count, percent}]}. render_gnmic_config {project_id, device_address, tls_cert_path} -> {config_yaml: string}. render_telegraf_config {project_id, device_address, username} -> {config_toml: string}. Electron IPC (ipcRenderer/ipcMain): project:create, project:open, project:save, oid:update_mapping.
target_output.security_and_compliance: No authentication or encryption for local-only internal tool. Python subprocess spawned with bundled Python binary from app resources (not system Python). MIB file path validated via path.resolve() and restricted to user-opened file. subprocess argument sanitisation: no shell=True in Python; all subprocess calls use argv list. No credentials persisted by app; gNMI device address and TLS cert path collected in ExportWizard for template rendering only, not stored in SQLite.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions matrix: ubuntu-latest (build), windows-latest (Squirrel NSIS), macos-latest (dmg). Steps: npm install, npm run lint (eslint + prettier check), npm run test (vitest), python -m pytest backend/, npm run make (electron-forge). Bundled mapping DB updated by refresh_mapping_db.py script run in CI, downloading latest openconfig-yang public mapping CSVs. Release artifacts: WindowsInstaller.exe and macOS.dmg uploaded to GitHub Releases.
target_output.observability: Electron main process logs to %APPDATA%/snmp-gnmi-migrator/app.log (Winston, INFO level). Python subprocess stderr captured and forwarded to Electron log and displayed in app error panel for pyang parse errors. No telemetry or crash reporting; internal tool.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero hosting cost; local desktop installation. Electron app bundle ~150MB including bundled Python venv. OID table handles 5000+ OIDs with react-window virtualisation at 60fps scroll.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (8 weeks): Electron app shell, Python subprocess IPC, MIB import with pyang, bundled OID-to-OpenConfig DB, OID mapping table with colour coding, manual mapping dialog, gNMIc config export. Phase 2 (4 weeks): Telegraf config export, YANG coverage dashboard, Grafana dashboard template export, project save/load from file.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode in renderer and main. eslint + prettier enforced in CI. vitest for React component tests. pytest for Python backend with MIB fixture files. Electron Forge for packaging; no custom webpack config.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: pyang parse errors on non-standard vendor MIB files; displayed in error panel with pyang error details for engineer to resolve. Risk: bundled OID-to-OpenConfig DB may be incomplete for niche vendor OIDs; manual mapping workflow covers gaps. Tradeoff: Python subprocess over Node-native YANG library; no production-quality YANG parser exists for Node.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Persistent Python subprocess chosen over per-operation spawn to avoid 2-3 second pyang import latency per command. Separate bundled read-only SQLite for OID mappings chosen over embedded in-app data to allow DB updates without app reinstall. react-window chosen for OID table virtualisation to support 5000+ OID projects without degradation.

metadata.notes: Round 1 first-pass for SNMP-to-gNMI desktop migration tool with Electron+React and bundled Python pyang backend.
